Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the market developments during the intermeeting period, using the charts that Carol Low has shared. The economic growth forecasts were somewhat tempered, leading to mixed reactions in various market segments. Some were in a risk-reduction mode, while others were seeking risks. In the foreign exchange market, the dollar appreciated after a prolonged period of weakness, with a significant focus on the dollar-yen rate due to continued high levels of intervention by the Japanese authorities.

Let's begin with the US interest rates. The three-month deposit rate and its forward rates, as shown in the top panel of my handout, have been declining since January due to disappointing employment data. The green tripwires on the graph represent employment report dates and the differences between expected and actual payroll numbers. The November 7 report showed a stronger-than-expected growth of 61,000 nonfarm payrolls, but the December 5 report disappointed with a 93,000 shortfall, marking the start of four consecutive disappointing reports. Treasury yields have been falling since then, with the ten-year note yielding around 3.75% this morning. Central bank buying has also been a factor, as discussed in the Bluebook.

The middle panel of my handout graphs the ten-year note yield and the aggregate amount of Treasury securities held in custody by the New York Fed. Foreign central bank holdings of Treasuries grew from $700 billion to about $800 billion between January 2003 and November 2003. The ten-year yield fluctuated widely during this period, from about 3.10% to a high of 4.60%, and then declined by about 50 basis points since November. The direct link between the two variables is hard to quantify, but other factors, such as disappointing data and expectations of low short-term rates, have also influenced yields.

Besides reacting to employment data and central bank buying, the Treasury market has been preoccupied with the impact of these purchases on Treasury yields. The yield curve has flattened but remains steep by historical standards. The bottom panel graphs the spreads of the ten-year note less the three-month bill since 1990. Some investors believe that yields should be higher at the long

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I noted in the chart on the Fed custody holdings of Treasury securities, shown in the middle panel on page 1, that there are a couple of periods of very significant rise. If one tries to compare them with the Japanese intervention data on page 4, it obviously doesn’t square in the slightest. Indeed, it looks as though the intervention of very early 2004 didn’t show up in the custody holdings until the third week in January. Is that the typical time lag?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Well, there are a lot of moving parts here. The aggregate custody holdings are for all countries.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, I assume the Japanese are dominant.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: In terms of the delta, Japan is obviously a key contributor. But there are a lot of major players and many ins and outs in these figures. Also for Japan, their cash has actually been rising over the last few months. That is published on their web site. I believe almost a quarter of their reserves, if I have my numbers right, are now held in cash. So they are not automatically–

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Cash meaning deposits?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Deposits, right.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Where? In domestic banks or foreign banks? In dollars?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: In banks in dollars. So it’s not that they are automatically buying dollars and immediately putting them into Treasuries. There has been a lag, and in fact that lag actually has been growing.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I thought the lag was just two or three days.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Well, it is if they are immediately buying securities. But again, if we look at their deposit data, we see that their cash has been rising, so they are not immediately putting all of their intervention dollars to work.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Do the securities go into custody at the point of purchase?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: At the point of settlement.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I’m sorry, I meant at the point of settlement. So it’s not an issue of their purchasing a security, taking it home, and then sending it back, so to speak. They purchase it, and at settlement it goes into custody as a book entry item, and that’s it.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Exactly. Again, though, one of the missing pieces is how much of their dollar purchases are staying as deposits rather than going directly into securities.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: On the bottom chart of page 1, everybody uses that spread between the ten-year note and the three-month bill. But what the comparison in that chart is supposed to capture is better captured by using a one-year maturity nine years out because clearly the ten-year note is picking up an average of everything from one day out to ten years. I understand that it’s not a market rate. It’s a work of art, based on first differences in the chart. Is there any reason that we don’t do that other comparison instead? That’s the rate we really want to see.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Yes, this is used because I think people tend to be more familiar with this relationship. I was not trying to be precise, but I wanted to show that there is a sense in which the people who have been saying that yields ought to be a lot higher are really fighting this in part. That is, the spread is so high that—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The spread is actually 5 points, not 3.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: The contours of the chart would be the same if one used the ten-year- ahead forward rate less, say, the one-year-ahead forward rate. The amplitude would be higher, and the spread would wind up at something like 5⅛ percent.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: In the short term it would be, but not as the slope of the yield curve changes. The spread is affected by that slope. The point is that what you’re trying to measure here is being muddied by the fact that the numbers are already partially in the numerator. With respect to the chart at the bottom of page 3, those IMM commitment data can’t have anything to do with the dollar because the euro is unchanged. It’s a technical issue with respect to the yen and has nothing to do with anything else as far as I can see.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Well, that’s a good point. In the interest of full disclosure, I must say that I put the euro in there just to point out that there wasn’t a universal response from this group. Now, one of the anecdotal stories that we keep hearing is that there’s a group of market participants—if not hedge funds then CTAs and model-driven accounts—that are becoming more and more important in the marketplace. A lot of these funds use the same kinds of models, and when their moving average triggers get hit, they all tend to move at the same time. So what we seem to have had is that kind of move—in the yen in particular.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes, it looks as though 100,000 contracts just got—is it yen delta hedging? It’s curious.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: It was a dramatic move. Again, it seemed to be very much a position- adjustment story, and that was the point I was trying to convey. It seemed to be a change driven not by fundamentals but by technical factors—and those technical factors were most powerful in the yen.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Other questions for Dino? President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Dino, I was intrigued by your thoughts regarding the beginning of a risk-reduction phase. How strongly do you feel about that? I may be slightly behind the curve in terms of the way market participants are looking at this; but from people in the investment business around Boston, I seem to be getting more comments about the amount of risk that is out there.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Yes, we’re really seeing some crosscurrents. On the one hand, if we talk to people who are active in, say, Treasuries, we hear a lot about the carry trade and about an expansion of risk and leverage.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Right.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: On the other hand, there are pockets where risk-reduction moves do seem to be occurring—where people have had some losses and then reduced their other positions. It’s not universal. This may be a very short-term phase that was localized or limited to a couple of market segments. We may not be talking about this in the next week or next month. So it seems as if we have some conflicting forces going on here.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: I wondered if you had any more thoughts on the dollar, Dino. The dollar’s rise is rather surprising given the disappointing data we’ve had on the U.S. economy and the decline in U.S. interest rates. Do you have some sense that attitudes have changed from the pervasive expectations of further declines? Or is this the end?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Well, explaining exchange rates is always a risky business. The rebound in part seems to have been technically inspired. It’s hard to pinpoint, but I think a number of things were going on that had some effects. I didn’t spend much time talking about economic developments in Europe, but there has not been as much of an improvement in Europe as people thought there would be three or four months ago. If Europe had performed better over the last few months, then we might not have had this rebound in the dollar. On the other hand, as I pointed out, the underlying sentiment among the strategists on the Street really hasn’t changed. When the euro got to 1.29, a number of people thought that the market was ripe for a correction. One fellow on our foreign exchange committee, which is a private-sector group, said when the euro was at 1.28, “We’ll see 1.20 before we see 1.30.” So this may be a natural process that we’re going through of a flushing out of positions, and we may have a revival of the trend we were seeing. But that’s one guess.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: As I understand it, Mr. Chairman, someone has to move for approval of the Desk’s transactions, and I’ll volunteer for that duty.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It’s not a great burden, I hope! @laugh

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: It’s a lot easier than talking about the exchange rates.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Any objections? Does anyone else want to speak before we close the questions? If not, I will accept your motion and assume, without objection, that the Desk’s transactions are approved. David Stockton and Karen Johnson.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the data we've received since the January FOMC meeting. We've had a mixed bag of information, but this time around, it's been more of a disappointment than a surprise. The labor market, in particular, has been a letdown. Private nonfarm payrolls were flat in February, and the pace of hiring since last September has been much slower than we anticipated. Initial claims for unemployment insurance have been lower, but we haven't seen a corresponding increase in new hiring.

There have been a few positive signs, such as a trend of increasing temporary-help employment and a rise in the average workweek. However, these indicators don't fully offset the labor market weakness.

In terms of production and spending, we've seen some softness in manufacturing and spending, which led us to revise down our projection for real GDP growth in the first half of the year. The largest revision came from construction activity, where residential, nonresidential, and state and local sectors have all come in below expectations.

Excluding motor vehicles, consumer spending was actually a bit stronger than we anticipated. Business outlays for equipment and software were also stronger than projected. High-tech spending has continued to increase rapidly, and spending on capital equipment outside of high-tech and transportation has shown a marked acceleration.

Despite some negative surprises, we still believe the economy is expanding at a fast enough pace to absorb resource slack. However, there are risks to the outlook, and we've included confidence intervals around our projection to illustrate the uncertainty. These intervals are wide, and there's a 30% chance that the actual outcome will lie outside the bounds. We've also included stochastic simulation results to provide a better perspective on the sources of uncertainty.

In conclusion, the data since the January FOMC meeting have been a mixed bag, with some positive signs but also some disappointments, particularly in the labor market. We've revised down our projection for real GDP growth in the first half of the year, but we still expect the economy to expand at a brisk pace through the end of next year. The uncertainty surrounding the outlook is higher than it has been in the past, and we've included confidence intervals and stochastic simulation results to help illustrate that uncertainty.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to share some insights on the economic outlook for this year and the next, focusing on foreign economic activity. The forecast suggests a moderately robust growth with contained inflation. This optimistic picture is driven by the rebound of the global high-tech industry, positive spillovers from the U.S. economy expansion, and accommodative monetary policies in many foreign economies.

Although growth in the second half of last year did not match that of the U.S., it rebounded sharply, with an average real GDP growth of over 4.25%. However, there were significant differences across countries, with emerging Asia leading the way and the euro area lagging behind. Nevertheless, by the end of last year, the expansion became widespread and more firmly established.

Looking at the current quarter, indicators confirm that this process has continued. In some economies, such as Japan and China, we anticipate some moderation in growth. Output in the euro area is expected to accelerate a bit further this quarter and next to approach its potential rate of growth.

However, there are two issues that merit further discussion. First, recent developments in oil and other primary commodity prices have been a concern. Despite forecasts of declining oil prices, spot prices for WTI have risen from around $30 per barrel to $37 over the past six months. Higher oil prices have been partially offset by the appreciation of many foreign currencies against the dollar. However, the balance of demand and supply in the global oil market could push up dollar oil prices during a general dollar depreciation. Other factors supporting spot oil prices include low inventories, OPEC production policy, instability in some oil-supplying countries, and strong demand as the global economy expands.

Second, the ever-expanding need to finance the U.S. external deficit has been a topic of debate. The TIC data on January capital flows and the balance of payments data for the fourth quarter provide some insight into how this deficit has been financed recently. Foreign private net purchases of U.S. securities remained strong, and financial market concerns about a shift in foreign preferences away from U.S. assets have abated. The balance of payments data reported a significant increase in U.S. net investment income in the fourth quarter, which implied a current account deficit that was $

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Do you have any breakout on foreign affiliate earnings in foreign currencies and the extent to which earnings are converted to dollars—resulting in much larger amounts of dollars at the prevailing exchange rate?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I asked my staff if they could make that decomposition, and I basically got “no” for an answer. @laugh The dilemma is that we’re talking about looking at this across all the different currencies and all the different—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: There’s a lot of hedging going on.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I understand. And that is shifting a bit when this valuation effect shows up and when there were offsetting gains on the one hand versus losses on the other. Remember we’re talking now about U.S. earnings abroad, not foreign earnings here. At any rate, the staff did not seem hopeful that they could disentangle the earnings in euro, yen, Canadian dollars, and pounds and the exchange rate effects as reported in these data. We know the general tendencies, but we don’t have enough data to do the actual accounting that would disentangle that.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I know you eventually get it, but at this stage do you have the foreign affiliate earnings by major geographic areas?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I raised that question. I think what we’d have to do at this point is to look at annual reports or something. At some later point we may have those data.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: In other words, Commerce publishes the data but with a significant lag?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Right.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I ask because theoretically one could take the dollar earnings and literally convert them back to their foreign currencies.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Back to their foreign currencies.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Of course, you don’t know what’s hedging and what isn’t hedging, but at least it gives you a rough idea. The problem is that you need to wait for the earnings data.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: And we would have to do it over time in order to track foreign earnings versus the imputed exchange rate effects that we would have injected into that calculation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes, a significant part of that is the oil earnings of U.S. companies. I must tell you, though, that if you try to reconcile company reports with the Commerce numbers you will think you’re looking at two separate universes. The bookkeeping that goes on there makes Enron’s bookkeeping look like Accounting I. President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: I have a question about page 10 in Part 1 of the Greenbook, which has to do with the assumptions on labor productivity through 2005. You have productivity growth dropping to only 1.6 percent from 3.0 percent in 2004 and 5.3 percent last year. I asked Bob Rasche to look at the time series properties, and it looks as if labor productivity growth is pretty close to a random walk. Obviously it can’t be literally a random walk. But you have a major drop in 2005, and perhaps you could explain where that comes from.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: It’s a deceleration, not actually a decline, in productivity. That deceleration in productivity is a function of the fact that by our reading, even with what we consider to be a fairly optimistic view on the growth of potential output, the level of productivity is above its long-run trend. This pattern looks to us like standard cyclical behavior. What we have is a deceleration in productivity as it goes from above trend back toward trend. That results in a slowdown in the growth of productivity and accounts for the step-up in payroll employment that we believe will occur. Again, that looks to us to be consistent with the cyclical time series performance of productivity around a spline function type trend, which is basically the way we put together the longer-run productivity forecast. So as many of you have noted over the past year or so, that forecast incorporates a significant element of risk. It certainly has been an area where we have been wrong and wrong rather consistently in one direction over this particular period—that is, in anticipating that some of what we perceive to be cyclical increases in productivity are going to abate going forward. As I think I responded to President Minehan at the last meeting, we really just don’t have a lot to go on in terms of gauging how much of a backlog of one-time efficiency improvements still exists out there. So in writing down these figures in our forecast, we are trying to blend some judgment and some econometrics. But this is indeed an area where our confidence is not terribly high.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: An implication would be a major swing in unit labor costs next year compared with this year, and presumably that might affect the inflation outlook. At any rate, I think this is a fairly important part of the outlook for 2005. Growth in productivity of 1.6 percent, of course, is equivalent to the miserable 1974-95 average. I understand the argument for it.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: That would be an average over a long period of time whereas this would be a relatively brief period of somewhat lower cyclical growth. On the inflation point, I would just note that we don’t think there is a big effect on prices from swings in unit labor costs that are associated with cyclical movements in productivity. We tend to believe that firms in their pricing behavior try to look through cyclical movements. So the implicit acceleration in unit labor costs that is incorporated in this projection does not exert much upward pressure on inflation.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: But, of course, it also feeds into the employment outlook.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Right, employment and profits both. I think in some sense in the forecast we have a cyclical slowing in profits that also looks to us to be fairly typical. What we typically see is a big increase in profits when productivity is soaring in the early stages of a business cycle and then, as productivity slows down, profits growth slows as well and the labor share begins to recover. Admittedly this cycle has been one where everything has been more drawn out, so to speak, than has been the case in the past. And because of that we feel much less confidence in gauging the timing of that cyclical slowing in productivity.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Except for the second half of last year, we haven’t had the typical recovery surge. So in one sense we may not be very far into the expansion phase of the cycle.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: That’s a very real possibility.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: This is obviously part of the ’05 projection, but I was very interested in where that came from. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Parry.

Speaker: MR. PARRY
Statement: Dave, there is a simulation called “rising inflation expectations” in the Greenbook in which inflation expectations increase gradually by 1 percentage point. I don’t know from what level to what level, but I do know that some of the surveys that we see, such as the Blue Chip survey, suggest that inflation expectations are quite high—around 2¼ or 2½ percent. I think it would be interesting to know what the results of a declining inflation expectations scenario would be because, if indeed the market expects 2 percent, it’s possible that view could change. If that were to occur, I would assume that conceivably we could see inflation rates moving down from current levels. So I’d find that to be a more interesting alternative. But I just don’t know what the levels were in the scenario where the inflation expectations number rose 1 percent. It went up from what to what?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Actually, I’d have to go back and ask our model folks because it’s in terms of the implicit inflation expectation that is embedded in that model.

Speaker: MR. PARRY
Statement: I bet it’s a lot lower than 2½.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: I’m sure it’s lower than 2½ percent.

Speaker: MR. PARRY
Statement: You could exogenously make it 2½ percent.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: We highlighted this scenario in part because the model probably incorporates lower inflation expectations than those of some market participants. And in light of the decline in the dollar and the acceleration of commodity prices, there are at least a few straws in the wind that suggest that perhaps inflation will not be as sanguine as we are forecasting. So we wanted to highlight that possibility to some extent. But I think you’re absolutely right: Around our baseline forecast we see that risk balanced against a risk that inflation expectations could come down further and maybe even considerably further. If our inflation forecast begins to materialize in the data—surprising people in terms of how benign the behavior of prices will be—inflation expectations could decline. And if the increase in commodity prices and the decline in the dollar really are not providing much upward impetus to inflation, one could see a break to the downside as well. As we noted in the Greenbook, another downside risk that we see on the inflation side is that the markup over unit labor costs looks very high to us and that’s associated with strong profitability as well. We have that only tipping down over the forecast period, in part owing to the assumed deceleration in productivity. It’s certainly possible, though, that we could get a sharper decline as competitive pressures put more downward pressure on the price markup. That would be another source of significantly lower inflation going forward. So while we view the risks as reasonably balanced, we certainly think we could have declining inflation expectations as well as increasing expectations.

Speaker: MR. PARRY
Statement: Okay, thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I wanted to ask a question about the labor markets. The labor force participation rate has been declining, as has been widely described in the press. Some of us were talking about this earlier, noting that a significant part of the decline is due to a reduction in the teenage labor force participation rate. There has been a big increase in school enrollment rates for teenagers as well. I was wondering if you had any thoughts on these developments and their implications for the tightness or weakness of labor markets and also for inflationary pressures.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: We have interpreted this downturn in labor force participation as principally a cyclical phenomenon. Nevertheless, we have revised down the level that we think the labor force participation will eventually return to; we’ve revised that down in light of the employment data. But the basic message of our forecast is that a lot of the people who have withdrawn from the labor market will make themselves available again when the economy and job prospects begin to improve. Admittedly, we can’t see that from the discouragement data because, as you point out, the reasons people give for leaving the labor force include leaving for school or to take care of home responsibilities. And an increasing number of people are reporting that they are retired. So it’s possible that the reduced labor force participation will prove to be more persistent than we are anticipating. If that’s the case, then in essence there is less slack in our baseline forecast. If we get our growth forecast along with less of a bounceback in labor force participation, there will be a bigger decline in the unemployment rate than we’re showing, and the labor markets could tighten more immediately. The result would be greater pressure on inflation than we’re showing. I was going to say that I feel reasonably confident, but I guess I should strike that word from my lexicon, so I will say that we feel comfortable in our belief that a significant part of this downturn in participation will prove to have been cyclical. That’s in part because of the timing of the downturn. The peak in participation coincided so closely with the peak in the tightness of the labor markets and the deterioration coincided so closely with the period of persistently weak labor markets that it’s hard to think that it was not cyclical. It would be difficult to consider it a coincidence that the trend turned significantly downward starting right at the peak of the business cycle. But obviously that remains a risk. We see the risks in this aspect of the projection more as one in which labor markets could tighten more quickly than we’re forecasting.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: So the increase in school enrollment is something that we’ll see washing out as the labor market tightens?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Yes. Part of the complication that has arisen in doing this analysis is that the questions in the household survey on labor force participation were changed in the mid-1990s, as you know. Consequently, we have no cyclical experience in terms of how people actually were responding to these questions about being in the labor force and why they were in or out of the labor force. So we’re uncomfortable with our ability to look at past cyclical patterns and draw a lot of inferences from that going forward.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Bill Wascher and his group, as you may know, are trying to simulate the old breakdown. If they succeed, we’ll probably learn a few things about this issue.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Yes, Bill has been working on the issue. But ultimately, though we may be able to draw some inferences, it’s going to be hard to believe—given that the questions were changed—that we’re getting something that tracks very closely with past cyclical behavior. But he is attempting to do that decomposition.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Any further questions for our colleagues? If not, who would like to start the Committee discussion? President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from our latest economic assessment in the Seventh District. Our contacts remain optimistic about current conditions, but there's a growing sense of unease about the future. Household spending remains strong, with retailers reporting robust sales across various product categories. However, automakers are underperforming, expecting sales to pick up later in the year due to increased incentives.

Despite the strong household spending, job growth remains weak. Businesses are hesitant to hire, preferring to meet demand through process improvements, stretching their workforce, using temps, and just-in-time hiring. Some are concerned about the potential for an economic downturn, but they're not providing specific reasons.

One positive sign is the hiring plans of large firms like Caterpillar, which plans to add 1,000 net new employees in the US this year. Temporary workers are also seeing growth, but the gains are not as significant as expected given the overall economic strength.

Business investment is improving, but much of the spending is focused on productivity enhancement and automation. There's also a significant amount of financial capital looking for investment opportunities, leading to higher prices for riskier assets.

The international economy is having a significant impact on local businesses. Foreign demand and a weaker dollar have boosted orders and shipments, but import prices and shipping costs have also risen sharply. Some firms have been successful in passing these cost increases on to their customers.

Regarding the labor market, our contacts believe the rhetoric about offshoring jobs is overblown. Domestic call center jobs, for example, continue to increase in demand despite reports of these jobs moving overseas. These jobs are more sophisticated and require more nuanced language skills than the routine jobs leaving.

The recent jobs data is disappointing, with firms reluctant to hire new workers. The weak job market hasn't dampened consumer spending, but the fundamental indicators suggest decent job gains are on the horizon. However, we're still unsure when and where the corner of decent job growth will appear.

In summary, our outlook remains positive, with output growth expected to remain above potential, eliminating resource slack sometime next year. However, given the soft labor market and low inflation, we don't need to rush to adjust interest rates.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President McTeer.

Speaker: MR. MCTEER
Statement:
During our recent meeting, I'd like to share some insights from our latest board reports and data on the economy in the Eleventh District. The economy is growing, but it's expanding more slowly than the national average. Home prices have softened in major metropolitan areas, and apartment rents have dropped significantly. Landlords are offering incentives like free rent for up to two months to attract tenants.

The office market, particularly in Dallas and Austin, is equally overbuilt. Dallas has a surplus of office space that could last up to ten years, and new projects are still being announced. The low-interest rates are driving the investment in existing properties. However, when rates eventually rise, there's a risk of a wave of foreclosures and bankruptcies.

Our agricultural sector has been sustained by low-interest rates, but when rates rise, marginal producers may face challenges. The high-tech sector around Austin is showing signs of improvement, with venture capital flowing and IPOs resuming. The Mexican economy is also looking better, leading to increased Texas exports to Mexico.

However, there are emerging pricing pressures in our District, particularly for primary and fabricated materials, steel, and scrap. Retail prices are declining but at a slower rate. Several manufacturers have reported the ability to raise prices and increase profits, which is causing excitement in the industry.

The construction industry has shifted from a buyer's market to a seller's market, which is unprecedented in its scope and impact. The rising steel and other building materials prices have led to the term "double ordering" being used for the first time in many years.

The national economy is continuing to expand, but employment growth has been resistant to improvement. Productivity gains are helping to restrain inflation, but I believe we've crossed a tipping point where the prospects for increasing inflation far exceed the prospects for declining inflation. While I don't think a policy shift is necessary yet, we should keep a close eye on the situation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some insights to the current economic situation in New England. While we're seeing positive signs of growth with increasing sales, profits, and optimism, the labor market remains a concern. Massachusetts, in particular, is experiencing a significant employment downturn. There's growing pressure for legislation to prevent outsourcing or at least to discourage companies from moving jobs overseas.

The latest employment benchmark revisions suggest that job losses in 2003 were more severe than initially thought, especially in Massachusetts. However, the pace of job loss may be moderating slightly. New England has had the sharpest percentage drop in employment of any region during the recovery, with Massachusetts employment levels more than 6% below pre-recession peaks.

Despite this, businesses report a shortage of skilled workers, particularly in the hospitality industry and in high-end software support, healthcare, and biotech. New England's inflation rate is rising faster than the national average, driven largely by a 6.3% increase in shelter costs. High housing costs are making it difficult for businesses to attract the skilled workers they need, especially in industries like healthcare.

On a brighter note, the District's economic situation appears to be improving. Businesses are reporting increased sales and profits, and plans to increase capital spending and hire new staff. High-tech firms are experiencing solid growth, with Boston area software job postings up 16% from a year ago.

However, businesses are also reporting rising insurance costs and concerns about a lack of pricing power. Our Bank's directors note an improving situation, but one that remains uneven. Some industries, like commercial real estate, are still struggling, while others, like truck and semiconductor manufacturing, are optimistic.

Nationally, the slow rate of employment growth remains a puzzle. Business spending is up, corporate earnings are strong, and businesses have increased borrowing. Yet, firms aren't hiring. Our projections suggest that employment growth will improve "sometime soon," but I'd like to see more concrete evidence before making any assumptions.

In conclusion, New England is growing, but the employment situation remains a concern. I'd like to see more data before making any assumptions about when employment growth will pick up. Additionally, I'm concerned about the potential for financial instability if rates remain too low for too long. We

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Broaddus.

Speaker: MR. BROADDUS
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on our District's economic situation, which seems to align with Cathy's assessment to some extent. We've received positive news recently, both from data and anecdotal reports. The manufacturing sector, in particular, is showing signs of improvement, with robust factory shipments and new orders in January and February. Consumer spending has also been relatively strong, as indicated by our monthly survey and anecdotal information. Our banking directors have reported increased loan demand from both businesses and households. Additionally, our broadcasting company CEO mentioned a sharp increase in ad revenue.

However, while there are positive indicators, the job market remains a concern. Manufacturing employment is stabilizing but at a very low level, and there's no clear evidence of significant job market improvement overall. The soft February jobs report and the widening output gap are also cause for concern. The Greenbook's revised GDP growth projections for the first half of the year and the increased output gap could put downward pressure on core inflation, but this effect might be offset by higher energy prices and a weaker dollar.

The employment gap, as measured by the difference between actual and potential employment, has been widening based on recent job growth reports. The Greenbook doesn't provide an estimate of potential employment and the employment gap, but if potential employment grows at the rate of the working-age population, the narrowing of the gap might not be significant. Productivity growth estimates also vary widely, which could impact the employment gap and inflation.

While I agree with the Greenbook's projections of aggregate demand growth, the labor market considerations suggest that we may not be giving enough attention to the downside risk on inflation. I believe it's important for us to consider this in our policy setting later in the meeting.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share my perspective on the current demand situation. In essence, we're seeing demand come in stronger than anticipated, though not excessively so. My contacts at Wal-Mart have expressed confusion, as their internal data suggests weaker demand than what's actually materializing. Sales are running about 200 to 225 basis points ahead of expectations. Wal-Mart has reluctantly increased their sales guidance to 4 to 6 percent but remain concerned.

Prices for general merchandise are falling at a slower rate than before, around 1 percent, compared to the previous 3½ percent. My contact in the trucking industry reported a much stronger January-February period, with a combined increase of about 8 percent. Availability of drivers remains tight, leading to increased capital spending.

FedEx is the most upbeat of my contacts, reporting a clear strengthening of demand. Their domestic express business is up 4.0 percent in March, while ground business is up 8 percent. UPS also reported stronger business than anticipated, but security-related costs are becoming a significant burden. My UPS contact spends about 20 percent of his day on security matters.

Regarding inflation, the core inflation measure excludes temporary price changes, but food and fuel prices, which make up a significant portion of the measure, are no longer temporary. Long-term oil futures have risen substantially, and food prices are influenced by the dollar. The best guess on the dollar is that it will remain stable, but the staff believes further depreciation is in order.

Most inflation shocks, such as fuel, commodity prices, health care costs, property and liability insurance, and security costs, are in the same direction and may not be fully absorbed. Therefore, we should consider the possibility of a gradually worsening inflation environment. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Parry.

Speaker: MR. PARRY
Statement:
Chairperson, the economy in the Twelfth District is experiencing robust growth with some areas seeing significant expansion. Retail sales are on the rise, although retailers remain cautious due to intense competition keeping prices in check. Housing markets in the West, particularly Southern California and the Central Valley, have experienced significant price increases. Low interest rates and local economic conditions are contributing factors. However, IT centers like Silicon Valley have seen little to no home value appreciation.

Manufacturing sector conditions have improved, with a pickup in exports, including semiconductor sales to Asia, contributing to the boost. Despite this, IT producers continue to face competitive pressures, leading Intel to move production of flash memory chips and 300 jobs to Oregon from California.

Some parts of the District have experienced notable job growth, but overall job creation has been sluggish. Health care costs are a major concern, as evidenced by the recent five-month-long grocery workers' strike in Southern California, which resulted in employees bearing more health care costs. Workers' compensation costs are also a significant issue, with Governor Schwarzenegger pushing for reforms by the end of March.

California voters recently passed Propositions 57 and 58, allowing the state to issue $15 billion in bonds to address accumulated deficits and supplement revenues, while barring additional borrowing once the funds are depleted. The bond issuance is planned for early June, and rating agencies have responded positively.

Nationally, economic data have been mixed, with payroll employment and consumer spending being particularly concerning. While the economy is still expected to grow, there are heightened concerns about downside risks. Our forecast for the current quarter has been revised down to 4.3%, and the projection for the year as a whole has been reduced by 0.4%, to 4.7%. Unemployment is projected to fall below 5.5% by the fourth quarter.

Recent data on payroll employment and labor force participation raise questions about the amount of labor market slack going forward. Core inflation remains close to price stability, with our expectations for core PCE and CPI inflation in 2004 being 1% and 1¼%, respectively. The latest Blue Chip economic indicators suggest that the Fed may allow inflation to rise to around 2¼ to 2½% over the next

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Third District's economic outlook since our last meeting. Our Philadelphia staff's leading indexes suggest a pickup in growth across all three states over the next three quarters. Current conditions show continued improvement in most sectors, except for a moderate pace in retail sales, which was affected by last year's Presidents' Day weekend snowstorm. Residential construction remains high, but there's been a pullback in the first few months, possibly due to weather. Commercial construction is still hampered by high office market vacancy rates.

Manufacturing activity continues to expand, with an index of general activity in our March business outlook survey showing a reading of 24.2 percent, indicating continued expansion, albeit a decrease from February's 31.4 percent. The more forward-looking indexes of new orders and shipments have trended down since their post-recession highs but remain higher than during the first 1½ years of the recovery.

Despite the expansion in manufacturing, it's not yet creating jobs. Our survey revealed that nearly three-quarters of firms had job openings in the last three months and expected more in the next six months. However, nearly two-thirds of these firms reported difficulties filling these positions due to a lack of qualified applicants. This is a structural issue, possibly related to skill mismatches and productivity increases.

The labor market remains the weak spot for the District economy as a whole. Recent payroll data revisions have resulted in job level declines in Pennsylvania and New Jersey, with no job growth since the recovery began. Delaware has fared better, but still no job growth since the recovery. Before the revisions, reported job growth since the start of the recovery had been slightly better in our three states than in the nation.

On a national level, economic conditions and the outlook have not changed much since our last meeting. Activity continues to expand at a strong pace, but employment remains soft. Business investment has strengthened, supported by strong profit growth. Consumer spending is expanding moderately, and core inflation remains quiet despite commodity price increases.

The missing piece is employment growth, but it's difficult to see how it can remain missing given the pace of GDP growth. Productivity growth cannot continue at the extremely rapid rate we've seen. I believe employment growth will pick up

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some updates on the economic situation in our Southeast region. Retail sales remain strong, with reports of positive trends in tourism and hospitality, particularly in South Florida. Unusually cold weather in the North has led to an influx of snowbirds and foreign visitors, while low mortgage rates continue to support our single-family housing markets. We're seeing small improvements in the nonresidential sector, mainly due to institutional construction.

However, vacancy rates remain high in most large office space markets, and both office and industrial markets have only stabilized. Business leaders remain optimistic, but borrowing for business expansion has been disappointing, with much capital spending being financed from profits.

Employment growth is primarily coming from Florida, with most of the jobs being low-skill positions in the hospitality industry. There are reports of a shortage of highly skilled workers, such as engineers and computer specialists, and even minimum-wage jobs are difficult to fill in some areas.

We're hearing concerns about potential overbuilding and speculation in the real estate markets, especially in Florida, where entire condo projects and upscale residential lots are being pre-sold before construction. Prices for goods and services are increasing, with lumber, steel, and insurance being particularly affected.

On a national level, I remain optimistic about the economic outlook, with broad-based growth expected in the coming quarters. However, the lack of solid job growth and the unsustainable fiscal situation are concerns. The growing accumulation of U.S. securities by Japan and China, which now account for over a quarter of outstanding Treasuries, is also worth noting.

Inflation risks are currently balanced, but with some uncertainties and drags on activity, we may not be able to be as patient as some people think. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement:
Chairperson, good afternoon, I'd like to update you on the economic conditions in the Tenth District since our last meeting. Business sentiment remains positive, with consumer spending and tourism performing well during the ski season and into the present period. Housing activity has eased slightly, but it remains robust in our region. Manufacturing activity continues to strengthen, with production, new orders, and employment all on the rise.

Moreover, manufacturing firms have reported an increase in capital spending and spending plans. Business leaders express optimism about economic growth for the rest of the year, with stronger feelings in the eastern part of our region, particularly in Kansas City. Commercial real estate remains weak, and firms are less enthusiastic about new hiring than capital spending.

One anecdotal example of this trend is a major propane distributor in our region. By investing in new technology, such as call centers, global positioning, and communications technology, they anticipate reducing their workforce by at least 10% in the short term and up to 25% in the long term due to improved efficiency. This is indicative of the return on investment in capital spending over labor costs.

However, there are some concerns. Delays in receiving orders for goods used in manufacturing have been reported, and anecdotal evidence suggests that price increases may be on the horizon. With both monetary and fiscal policies delivering significant stimulus, the economy is showing signs of growth, and it's essential that we remain vigilant to potential inflationary pressures.

Although I'm not advocating any immediate policy changes, I believe that conditions are developing that could lead to price pressures emerging before the job market turns around. We should keep a close eye on this situation as capital spending continues to drive productivity gains.

At the national level, economic activity remains strong, and our growth projections are consistent with the Greenbook forecast, averaging around 4.5% GDP growth for 2004-05. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on the current economic trends in our District. Inflation remains a non-issue for now, with only select products and services experiencing price pressures. However, there are concerns regarding certain benefits, such as health benefits, in the ongoing transit strike in the Twin Cities.

A notable bright spot is the increase in spending on equipment and software. This trend has been ongoing for several months and continues to strengthen. The state and local government budget situation has also improved, thanks to policy changes and revenue surpluses.

Manufacturing and mining sectors are expanding, leading to modest employment growth. The labor market is stabilizing, with little talk of significant payroll reductions. Consumer spending has been strong, but rising energy prices are causing concerns, which may impact discretionary spending.

Housing remains a bright spot in the construction sector. On the national front, the economy is in a positive state, with incoming data showing growth, albeit not as robust as initially anticipated. I believe there's a risk in overinterpreting high-frequency data, as they can be noisy and subject to revision.

Regarding productivity, I've had reservations about the deceleration projected in the Greenbook. While the deceleration has been delayed, it's crucial to distinguish between cyclical and secular trends. If the slowdown is more fundamental, it could result in the same GDP growth with smaller employment gains or more rapid real GDP growth. I, for one, hope for continued productivity gains.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent interactions with businesses in the Fourth District. The economic conditions have improved since our last meeting. My contacts are reporting an increase in orders and filling pipelines for commercial loans. Productivity enhancement through capital equipment spending is a common theme among business executives. While there's been a lot said about IT investments, a major steel producer is restarting operations in Cleveland to meet global demand for steel. The market for nearly new machine tools is drying up, and businesses are becoming more creative in increasing productivity without adding to their workforce.

Outsourcing to foreign suppliers is gaining popularity among large and medium-sized companies, not just for manufacturing components but also for services like software development, call centers, back office operations, and research. I'm not overstating the trend, but the growing volume of such instances is noteworthy.

However, I've also noticed a trend of rising prices, primarily in commodities, construction materials, and metals. Some businesses are now able to pass on these price increases, which could be a sign that the prolonged period of disinflation might be coming to an end. Although it's unusual in an economy with significant labor market slack, the combination of rising commodity prices, falling dollar, and accommodative macro policy is causing concerns among my directors. I believe monetary policy is on the right track, but we should be mindful of potential inflationary signals. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It’s 11:05 a.m. Why don’t we recess for a short coffee break.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: 2 Mr. Chairman, in front of you is a chart of the forward rate yield curve that you spoke about during Dino’s briefing. The red line is the ten-year-ahead one-year- forward rate less the one-year rate, and the black line duplicates the line that Dino showed on his chart. And, yes, it is the case that the spread is quite high when we look at it in forward rate terms, and the amplitude of the swings is greater.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It’s interesting that the red line in 2001-04 is rising whereas the other one is flat, which of course reflects our moving the funds rate down. That alters the meaning of the chart. Anyway, Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the current monetary policy discussion. While demand has been stronger than anticipated, it hasn't quite met expectations, and the labor markets have been slower to respond than we'd hoped. This has resulted in a larger output gap than expected and a slower closure than desired. Furthermore, recent developments such as declining equity prices, widening risk spreads, and subdued consumer and business confidence suggest that private-sector optimism is waning.

Despite these concerns, I believe we should remain patient with our current monetary policy strategy. The underlying inflation rate may be flattening out, but the evidence is inconclusive. While housing has cooled down faster than anticipated, consumption remains relatively strong. Business spending on capital equipment remains robust, and the manufacturing sector is continuing to recover, albeit at a slower pace than anticipated.

Some argue that our patience should be wearing thin, citing concerns that policy is too accommodative and that asset prices are being distorted. While it's true that we've attempted to lower interest rates and boost asset prices to stimulate demand, I don't believe the current degree of accommodation is sufficient to warrant a change in policy.

The uncertainty surrounding the economy's potential growth rate and the size of the output gap argues for a cautious approach. We should wait for clear evidence that labor market slack is declining and that inflation has stabilized before considering any policy action. Additionally, maintaining accommodative policy is consistent with managing risks and avoiding potential deviations from the most likely outcomes.

In conclusion, while I understand the concerns regarding asset prices and the potential for future instability, I believe that the macroeconomic imperatives outweigh these risks. Until we see clear evidence of a stronger labor market and stabilized inflation, I would be hesitant to alter our current monetary policy stance.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to express my appreciation for the team's new uncertainty calculations. The formal statistical estimates of uncertainty are crucial as they help us go beyond comparing forecasts, which can be misleading due to the conversational approach and potential biases towards means. I find the graphics particularly useful as they provide a visual representation of where alternative simulations fit within the uncertainty bounds.

Regarding the forecast itself, we may be nearing the end of an upswing in upgrading. For the past six months, most data have come in ahead of expectations, leading to steady upgrades. However, this meeting marks the first time the Greenbook has downgraded the forecast, albeit slightly, due to a disappointing employment growth number and some other indicators showing weakness. The Blue Chip forecasters, on the other hand, are still upgrading. This suggests that we should consider not only group mean square errors but also the alertness of forecasters in picking up turning points. I believe the Greenbook team has shown great alertness during my tenure, but we should further investigate this matter.

As for the macro implications of the potential widening income distribution, it's an issue that has recently gained political attention. In recoveries, the income distribution typically closes, but this time it seems that the pattern will be reversed. Evidence suggests that income disparities will widen significantly in this recovery. Profits are high relative to wages, and the price markup on unit labor costs is at a record high. The profit share of total output is at a cyclical peak, while wage growth remains sluggish.

The question then arises as to what impact this will have. We see widening disparities among households, with the wealth-income ratio rising but financial obligation rates remaining high and loan-delinquency rates at a local high. Labor force participation rates are dropping, mainly among groups likely to be affected by the cycle, and there's a sharp rise in quit rates for those who have given up looking for work. The duration of unemployment is rising even as the overall unemployment rate falls. There's also a significant income-related disparity in consumer sentiment.

While it's easy to find evidence that income disparities are widening, it's harder to identify macro implications. A dollar shifting from wages to profits would have minimal overall macro effects, apart from the marginal tax rate differences. However, these conclusions

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to provide an update on the economic situation in the Second District. Business and consumer confidence have dipped slightly, employment has decreased some, and our Empire State survey shows reduced optimism for the future. However, the numbers remain strong. Manufacturers are reporting increased pressure on input prices, particularly in steel and energy. They are also seeing higher prices received and expecting more pricing power. The national and global businesses in New York's financial community remain positive, but are more cautious due to uncertainty about employment growth, political debates, and terrorism risks.

Productivity growth is not instilling as much confidence in the future as one might expect. The expansion's fundamental elements remain on the same path as our last meeting. Monetary policy's powerful support, accommodative financial conditions, and productivity growth acceleration should keep the economy expanding at a healthy pace for the next few quarters. Consumer spending may moderate, but real income growth should continue to support expenditures. Investment is expected to strengthen rapidly, driven by demand growth, competitive pressures, ample profit margins, and favorable financial conditions.

Our forecast has been adjusted slightly, with lower GDP growth forecasted for 2004 and 2005 compared to the Greenbook. The main differences are in the expected path of consumption and a larger negative contribution from net exports. We anticipate a gradual rise in the private saving rate and less impetus from asset prices, inflation, and interest rates. Productivity growth is expected to stay elevated longer and fall less than the Board's staff forecasts, leading to more moderate employment growth.

Core inflation numbers are likely to remain low, but the rate of increase may have stopped falling. Unit labor costs should start to rise modestly as productivity growth moderates, but the increases should be modest for a while. The surveys on pricing pressure and the ability to pass on price increases suggest a diminished probability of further falls in inflation.

The risks to this outlook for growth and inflation are close to symmetric. The chances of a substantial upside surprise on output growth have diminished. Until employment growth strengthens and unit labor costs turn, there is a chance of a further decline in core consumer price inflation. Even if these conditions materialize, it is unlikely that inflation will accelerate over the forecast period to a level above our comfort level.

Given these conditions, we believe monetary policy

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement: In preparation for this meeting, I focused again on business C&I loans, and for the first time in the two-and-a-half years that I have been following that information source, we are starting to see some measurable growth. It would be one thing simply to report that, but it’s quite another, at least in my mind, to go back and think about how long it has taken for us to get here. When I first began looking at these numbers, business loans were in a decline. From a decline and a sense of very negative feedback, they went to no new information and then to early indications of some bottom fishing and options taking. After that there was some talk of activity but no measurable activity and then some activity but it was still not showing up in the C&I numbers. Finally it is in the C&I numbers. In addition, we have gone from a time when banks and the securities industry were clearly cutting back on marketing and on their parameters for loan approvals to a point where they’ve become very aggressive. So, I think in a real sense this measure reflects not only the underlying economy but to a certain extent the psychology. And as Presidents McTeer and Moskow have indicated, there are indications that both have picked up. So if one were to use the trend in C&I loans as a barometer, it would seem to reinforce the Greenbook analysis of the strength in the underlying economy, with the lag in employment growth being the anomaly. We made a significant shift in our communication six weeks ago when we moved away from the “considerable period” language and expressed the need for patience. It seems to me that patience is still in order in our communication at this time.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some thoughts on the recent economic developments and their implications for policy. I'll discuss three key areas: first, my interpretation of what's happened during the intermeeting period; second, potential reasons for these trends; and third, the policy implications.

Firstly, the data we've seen since the last meeting has been mixed, with some positive signs but also some disappointments. The labor market has been weaker than anticipated, and demand for new vehicles has been sluggish. Dealer inventories are rising, and housing activity, while still strong, is below expectations. The "surprise index," which measures how data come in relative to expectations, has been negative since December, suggesting either overly optimistic expectations or a slower-than-expected economic recovery.

Secondly, the reasons for these trends are subject to debate. One theory is that the rapid increase in benefit costs has discouraged businesses from hiring new workers. However, I believe this is less of a factor since benefit costs are relatively small compared to overall hiring costs. Another theory is that structural productivity growth may be stronger than previously thought. I find this argument more compelling, as we may be in the midst of a prolonged period of productivity growth that started around nine years ago. This could explain the relatively weak job creation despite a strong economic recovery.

A third theory, which hasn't been discussed much, is the idea of "business gloom." Businesses may be hesitant to hire and increase labor income due to uncertainty about the sustainability of consumption growth. Consumption has been supported by stimulus measures, but the expectation is that this support will wane during the year. Real labor income growth has been slowing, and this could be contributing to the weak demand for consumer durables.

Thirdly, the policy implications of these trends are significant. One issue is the potential for inflation and pricing pressures. While there are some supply and demand imbalances in certain industries, I believe these are unlikely to lead to broad-based inflationary pressures given the weak labor market. Another issue is the risk of overstaying our welcome at historically low interest rates. While monetary policy may not be as accommodative as we thought due to the lower real equilibrium interest rate, we should be cautious about making significant changes to our statement given the fragile state of financial markets and the potential downside risks to the economy. Finally

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I will align myself with the Kohn axis—

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: As long as it’s not an axis of evil!

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to bring up the topic of monetary policy patience and potential financial imbalances. While some, including Vice Chairman Geithner, believe that our current policy stance is appropriate, there are concerns that it may lead to financial imbalances in the future. The history of using monetary policy to correct financial imbalances has had its challenges.

Given this, I'd like to propose that we consider alternative tools or instruments to address potential financial imbalances. We've all received numerous questions and concerns regarding financial stability, such as the housing market, corporate bond spreads, household and firm balance sheets, government-sponsored enterprises, and regulatory developments. These are important issues, but as individual FOMC members, it's difficult for us to respond in detail.

Central banks in industrialized countries have responded to this challenge by issuing regular financial stability reports. These reports provide a systematic and objective way to communicate information about financial conditions across various sectors. I asked the staff for examples, and they provided me with reports from thirteen institutions, including the Bank for International Settlements, the European Central Bank, and the Central Banks of Australia, Austria, Canada, the United Kingdom, Finland, France, Germany, Norway, Spain, and Sweden.

These reports are valuable resources, and I encourage everyone to check them out. They cover a range of topics, from macroeconomic backgrounds to data and qualitative information on banks, households, firms, security markets, international finance, and regulatory developments. While much of the material is publicly available, the reports provide an analytical overview and include special topic boxes, research notes, and articles.

By presenting assessments of financial conditions objectively, these reports help draw attention to emerging financial problems and focus policymakers' attention on them. I believe we should consider providing the public with a regular financial stability report, possibly as part of the Monetary Policy Report to Congress or in some other existing venue or as a stand-alone document.

The staff could likely gather much of the necessary material during regular briefings in the Greenbook cycle. However, we should assess the marginal cost of providing such reports before moving forward. If there's interest in pursuing this idea, I'd be happy to work with the staff to develop a more detailed proposal for the FOMC to consider. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to begin by expressing my appreciation for the diverse forecasts presented by our staff. I believe this range of perspectives will help us better understand the uncertainties in our economic outlook. As someone who frequently advises banks on the importance of acknowledging the variability of outcomes, I'm pleased to see that we're practicing what we preach in our FOMC materials.

Since our last meeting, the data have been a mixed bag. Our forecast shows a slight decrease in GDP and employment growth, with inflation remaining stable. The pace of the recovery, while still robust, is slowing down. I've spent a considerable amount of time examining the corporate sector in light of its strong profit levels and cash flow. Despite this, businesses have not increased spending in all areas as expected.

I believe the disparity in spending on various aspects of their operations could be due to a few intriguing aspects of their financials. Corporations have been reducing their equity on net, resulting in stronger balance sheets, but they are generating more profits than they can utilize and, as a result, are retiring stock. Another observation is that corporate net debt issuance has been substantial until recently. While a significant amount of debt has been issued, we've also seen a paydown in bank loans and commercial paper. Companies have been taking advantage of the prolonged period of low interest rates to strengthen their financials by lengthening their liabilities.

However, in the last two months, we've observed some changes. Commercial loan growth in the banking system and commercial paper growth in the financial markets have emerged for the first time in a while. There has even been a net expansion of debt so far this quarter. Some of this could be attributed to the start of inventory building, but it has been quite modest. Corporations are attempting to maintain their high profit margins, as the Street anticipates double-digit earnings growth for corporations in the coming years.

However, what concerns me is that, among the government sector, the household sector, and the business sector, we currently have net savings in only one sector, and that's the corporate sector. Until the corporate sector shifts from a net saver to a net investor, I remain pessimistic about achieving rapid growth in the future.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you very much. We’ll turn to Vincent Reinhart.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the material we've received for today's meeting, which appears to focus on the language we'll use to describe the economic situation and potential risks. Based on recent market movements, it seems likely that participants are expecting the federal funds rate to remain at 1% for the rest of the year, as indicated by the data in the first exhibit. This consensus view is shared by primary dealer respondents, who anticipate no action until 2005.

Market participants are also confident about the near-term path of short-term rates, as evidenced by the low volatility implied by options on Eurodollar futures. Given this sense of market expectations, it's worth noting that words seem to have a significant impact on how markets react to your decisions, as shown in the bottom panel of the exhibit.

The economic outlook has been disappointing of late, but the market reaction to these disappointments has resulted in a decline in investment-grade corporate yields that has offset the decline in share prices and appreciation of the dollar. Financial conditions, therefore, may not have changed significantly since the January meeting.

While encouraging expectations of more accommodative policy could support spending going forward, the lags in monetary policy might lead you to believe that such effects would mostly be felt when the economy is in less need of support. The period when any action taken today would have the most leverage on the economy is next year, when real GDP is expected to grow at 4%.

If the Committee's characterization of the economy leads market participants to expect a tighter stance of policy, adverse outcomes for aggregate demand could materialize, leading to a higher cost in terms of lost output. While investors do not expect action at this meeting, the distribution of possibilities for later this year implied by options on Eurodollar futures rates has a noticeable portion below 1%.

If the Committee feels that it may be keeping policy on the easy side longer than currently priced into financial markets, it might want to highlight recent economic releases and emphasize that resource utilization is likely to remain below levels consistent with the economy operating at its productive potential for some time. This could help tip the balance of inflation risk to neutral and qualify the Committee's patience.

It's also worth noting that the real federal funds rate has been negative for the past ten quarters, and the estimate of the equilibrium real short rate

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to raise some points regarding the current economic situation. The productivity growth rate is showing signs of deceleration, as indicated by high-frequency data. While this slowing hasn't resulted in significant employment growth yet, it's important to consider whether the recent weakness in employment is due to statistical noise or something more substantial.

Moreover, there's a growing concern that the persistently low interest rates are encouraging financial markets to reach for higher yields. This could lead to an increase in risky securities on business balance sheets, which might not be sustainable in a rising interest rate environment.

Although the economy is exhibiting considerable strength, I'm hesitant to adjust our risk assessment of inflation to a balanced stance. Doing so might create momentum toward a policy action if the economic expansion were to slow down. I recommend leaving the inflation risks sentence unchanged for now.

Regarding the language in our statement, I suggest updating the first sentence in the operative paragraph to reflect the ongoing support of underlying productivity to the expansion. The rest of the paragraph would remain the same. Your thoughts?

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I support your recommendation for minimal change. I would, however, move to balance on the inflation statement, and I’ll tell you why. In my view, that is certainly an accurate reflection of the probability distribution. In fact, while I believe that the risks are close to balanced, my own judgment is that the probability of a ½ point surprise on the upside is a bit higher than that of a ½ point surprise on the downside. I also think, though, that the effect of that change in our statement is going to be totally dominated by the next employment report. We saw how large the reaction was in the bond market to the February report. If we have another weak jobs report—or whichever way the number goes—I think it’s going to overwhelm the effect of that minor change in our inflation assessment. In short, to me balance is an accurate reflection of the risks with regard to inflation, and I think the impact on the markets of our stating that is likely to be quite transitory.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: I support your suggestion, Mr. Chairman. I’m actually not altogether averse to going to balance on the inflation assessment. But given that we’re coming off a period of downgrading our forecast of economic activity, I think I’d rather leave the statement alone for another month. I’m not entirely averse to that minor change, however, if that’s all we do.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I just want to clarify that I was not advocating that we move rates at this meeting or even at the next meeting. But I do think there are developments that we ought to keep in the back of our minds as we consider the stance of policy. Clearly, there continues to be an output gap. And clearly, the incoming information suggests that economic activity has been less strong than it was earlier, though it continues to be strong. And like everybody else, I don’t know why the pace of hiring has been as slow as it has been or whether it will get slower or speed up more quickly than we expect. I could go along with your recommendation, Mr. Chairman, but I fall more into President Poole’s camp, and to some extent I agree with what Ned just said. I’d rather say that the probability of a rise in inflation is about equal to that of an unwelcome decline—use the phrasing in alternative C—because to me that more closely describes what the markets and others are seeing. I think it also conveys a very slight edging up in our concern—not a promise of a move the next time or the time after that or even the time after that—about how long interest rates have been as low as they are. So, on the margin, I would come down with President Poole on this one.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I think it’s a close call on whether to balance our inflation concerns. But after hearing your comments, with which I tend to agree completely, I have a slight preference for staying where we are. I think we’re in a situation today where the fewer changes in the operative paragraphs, the better.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Parry.

Speaker: MR. PARRY
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I certainly support your proposal. I also think that it’s a very close call with regard to the balance on inflation. I must admit that recent developments in energy prices and the value of the dollar have led me to conclude that it probably would be better to talk in terms of balanced inflation risks. But for now, I could support your recommendation. I think a change in our statement about the inflation risks is imminent; it’s just a matter of exactly when it will be made.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I, too, would have at least a mild preference for going to balance on the inflation risks. The reason is that, as I listened to the discussion today, it struck me that we heard more anecdotes that seemed consistent with the possibility of building inflationary pressures.
