So on that basis, as well as other factors, balance seems genuinely appropriate to me. That’s where I would come out on the issue.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I could support your recommendation, but I share the view of President Stern and others in that the discussion around the table today sounded, at least to my ear, as if we do see the inflation risks as balanced. In fact, I think we may have stayed with the phrase “unwelcome decline in inflation” too long. Those words in the statement don’t seem to be reflective of the discussion we had this morning. So I would have a preference for a balanced risks statement on inflation. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. It will come as a surprise to no one that I have at least a mild preference for staying with the inflation risk toward the downside. In my mind, we haven’t yet actually seen evidence—we have a lot of anecdotes but no real evidence—that inflation has even stabilized. It is true that core PCE has moved up, though from extremely low levels. But the core CPI continues to decelerate even on a three-month basis. So I’d prefer to be more certain that actual inflation has stopped falling before I can be convinced that the outlook calls for moving to balance on the inflation risks. My second point is that I think we all expect total inflation to decline relative to last year. At least the FOMC members projected inflation of 1 to 1¼ percent this year, which is about ½ point less than it was in 2003. Now, a lot of that reflects a projected decline in energy prices, and perhaps it’s not unwelcome, but the total is expected to fall. And finally, from my perspective, in a risk-management sense, any decline in inflation from current levels—if it’s more than a tenth or so—is more unwelcome than any increase. I think inflation is at such a low level that a decline has more welfare costs potentially over time than a rise. I don’t expect much to happen, but in my cost–benefit analysis, the downside risks get a little more weight than the upside risks.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I have just a couple of points. First, I can support your recommendation, although I had a marginal preference for the wording “about equals.” I think “about equals” is shaded somewhat to the left of “appears almost equal.” That is to say, we have lots of words in the English language, and “about equals” is an epsilon to the right of “almost equals.” My guess is that we could come up with another word if we wanted to. We could do this ad infinitum on a scale between “really equal” and “sort of equal.” @laugh So my preference on the wording is not a strong one. Let me take this opportunity, however, to make a comment about the Bluebook. One of the changes this time in the structure of the Bluebook was the elimination of the three options on changes in the funds rate. I think that made absolute sense, given where we are, but I also believe it could cause us a problem down the road. Other things being equal, I would prefer to have the three options presented, and we can dismiss them if we don’t find them relevant. I say that because I can see a world sometime in the future, perhaps with a different Chairman, where there’s a minority view for either increasing or decreasing rates and the opportunity to support such a move is made difficult if it’s not in the Bluebook. This is not to take away from the obvious decision we’re making this time. But as a general matter, I think that it makes some sense to leave that structure in the Bluebook and spend the extra page on it.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I also can support your recommendation on the language, given your comment that, if we make a change, the markets are going to view that as the first step in a sequence and will expect changes at our subsequent meetings. But I do have to admit that I also have a mild preference for going to a balance of risks on inflation. Again, though, I can support your recommendation on the language.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I’d prefer that we move to a balanced statement on the inflation risks because I think the risks in fact are relatively balanced now. But I would not choose to disagree with the stronger preferences that might be held by others around this table who believe that we really should not be making a change at this time. That’s the more important decision at this point, I think.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. While I am sympathetic to the concerns that others have expressed, my view is that we shouldn’t make any changes, and I’d keep this slight shading in the balance of risks language as it is. I have that view for a couple of reasons. One is that the market seems to be expecting very little change and I see no reason to surprise the market by doing something else at this stage. Last time we made what to everyone in this room was a relatively small change—moving to the “patience” language—to give us some flexibility. The market reacted quite strongly. I’m not quite sure why we have to do anything like that this time around. For reasons that you pointed out, Mr. Chairman, we’re not exactly starting on a new path just yet. Second, I would find it hard to explain, in the context of data that have been weaker than expected, why the Committee would alter its view of the inflation picture in the opposite direction. That seems inconsistent with some of the other language changes we want to make. Third, a point I made earlier, I think one’s assessment depends to some degree on one’s view of where we’re starting in terms of the inflation dynamics. As I said, I’m sympathetic to the concerns expressed about rising inflation; we’ve heard a lot of stories and anecdotes about markets where prices seem to be picking up. But I’m not sure we need to start signaling a greater worry about inflation at a point when our own forecast is for output growth to slow and for the output gap to widen. Again, that seems internally inconsistent. Therefore, I would leave the wording in the statement as it is, at least at this stage, and wait to see if the incoming data tell us how the forecast might change. If we wait until May to make this language change, I don’t believe people will think that we are behind the curve. So, for those three reasons, I would support your recommendation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Broaddus.

Speaker: MR. BROADDUS
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to align myself with Tony's appreciation for the thoroughness of the Bluebook. However, I'd feel more assured, Vincent, if you included all three options to demonstrate that we should anticipate having multiple action alternatives moving forward.

Regarding policy, Mr. Chairman, I might be considered an outlier within the Kohn camp. In fact, after I finish my remarks, I may even be ousted! I understand my perspective might be extreme, but I believe it's essential to voice it. Based on the current economic situation, I believe the labor market slack is not decreasing but increasing, which is fundamental.

I've pondered the inflation rate and where the risks lie on inflation going forward. Don made a valid point that there's limited evidence, at best, that disinflation has ceased, and some evidence suggests it may still be ongoing. I was also struck by Roger's comment during the economic discussion about the potential for price increases due to the imbalances we're experiencing. I acknowledge these price increases, but I don't entirely discount them. However, I still believe we're underestimating the disinflation risk.

After careful consideration, I've come to the conclusion that we should give more weight to the disinflation risk. I understand I'm expressing an extreme view, but I wouldn't be content with the language in alternative A regarding the assessment of risks. The statement there suggests that the probability of an unwelcome fall in inflation has diminished in recent months, but I'm not convinced that's accurate. Therefore, my preference would be to acknowledge the possibility of a policy easing or at least recognize it as an option. We've kept this off the table for a long time, and I believe it's time to reconsider. So, if it were up to me, the sentences would read something like this: "The probability of an unwelcome further fall in inflation has risen modestly in recent weeks and remains somewhat greater than the risk of a rise in inflation. Consequently, while policy eventually will need to become less accommodative as the economy improves, somewhat greater accommodation may be appropriate in the interim."

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: Thereby making the Chairman’s recommendation seem terribly moderate! @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I appreciate the anecdotes regarding price pressures, but I’m somewhat wary of them. I think many of them reflect the salience of the commodity and energy price changes we’ve seen. But first, we know that those are a small part of total costs. Second, there may be some confusion between high costs and rising costs, and of course, only rising costs contribute to inflation. Also, I want to associate myself with Governor Ferguson’s point that the news since our last meeting has been primarily of a weakening in the expansion, particularly in terms of the employment statistics, and of a strengthening in the dollar. The latter has not been talked about a great deal, but the downward trend in the dollar has been arrested. So, Mr. Chairman, I would like to support the statement you have proposed, with the bias downward, understanding that we may well want to come to a balanced assessment on inflation sometime soon.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President McTeer.

Speaker: MR. MCTEER
Statement: I agree with your recommendation for no change in policy. I would prefer balanced risks on inflation. I think most of the weakness we’ve seen recently has been on the real side rather than the inflation side. It’s just too much of a Phillips curve type of analysis for me to view the two as a tradeoff in that way. I would even suggest changing the word “patience” this time. I agree with Tony that there are a lot of words we can use, and I fear that if we start using that one time after time, we'll get in the same bind we did with the “considerable period” phrase. So, I would be happier if you had found a word that was equally as strong as “patience”; I think it would be helpful just to change that word so we don't get locked in. Back when I played tennis, if you were about to receive a serve, you didn’t stand flatfooted. You would keep moving your feet so you’d be able to go either way. And I think the word “patience” leaves us flatfooted.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I’m comfortable with your recommendation both on policy and on the statement. I think it’s true that the anecdotal information on prices is worse since our last meeting. But the numbers haven’t moved. And our statement already reads that the risks on inflation are quite close to balance. “Almost equal” is quite close. @laugh On that basis, I’m comfortable with the suggestions you proposed.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I support your recommendation, and I would prefer to keep the slight bias emphasizing that there is a possibility of disinflation. One of the things that makes me uncomfortable about making a change—going back to my comment that I like the fan charts—is that inflation is “fanning” around 1 percent, and I personally think 1 percent is a low number. Also, there are economic consequences of what is going on. How companies manage their earnings statements and how financial institutions operate at extremely low interest rates have implications for the economy. That, plus the fact that we haven’t seen in the data actual evidence of a stabilization in inflation, leads me to prefer to retain the bias toward weakness.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement: Mr. Chairman, if it is a close call as to whether or not we would bring the inflation risks into balance—and I think it is a close call—then I have a strong preference for changing the statement as little as possible. It seems to me that, in the past when we have made significant changes in the statement, the market has overread their significance and has been confused about what we were trying to communicate. The changes that have been proposed for the statement today in my view reflect the minimal change in the outlook that has taken place in the intermeeting seven-week period. And I think we ought to go with a statement that suggests that the outlook has changed only minimally. So I support your recommendation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: This is a very close decision actually—not so much in the numbers but in the fact that all of us, as best I can judge, could go either way if we had to. It’s just too close to call. I think Governor Olson probably correctly summarized where we stand. And there’s a lot to be said for President Poole’s argument that it’s really not going to matter all that much because what will matter is what the data show. So in a way I think it’s probably best for us to do nothing and let the market essentially move the expectations on the federal funds rate. Therefore, let’s have a statement read that essentially involves no change.

Speaker: MR. BERNARD
Statement: I’ll begin with the directive wording, which is at the bottom of page 11 in the Bluebook: “The Federal Open Market Committee seeks monetary and financial conditions that will foster price stability and promote sustainable growth in output. To further its long-run objectives, the Committee in the immediate future seeks conditions in reserve markets consistent with maintaining the federal funds rate at an average of around 1 percent.” Moving on to the risk assessment, which is part of the formal vote, that would be the language under “B” on page 12: “The Committee perceives that the upside and downside risks to the attainment of sustainable growth for the next few quarters are roughly equal. The probability of an unwelcome fall in inflation has diminished in recent months and now appears almost equal to that of a rise in inflation. With inflation quite low and resource use slack, the Committee believes that it can be patient in removing its policy accommodation.”

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Since we will be approving the statement as well, could you distribute the draft statement, please, just to be sure? It’s in effect what Vincent had indicated, so I trust there are no surprises. [Pause] If there are no comments, please call the roll.

Speaker: MR. BERNARD
Statement: Chairman Greenspan Yes Vice Chairman Geithner Yes Governor Bernanke Yes Governor Bies Yes Governor Ferguson Yes Governor Gramlich Yes President Hoenig Yes Governor Kohn Yes President Minehan Yes Governor Olson Yes President Pianalto Yes President Poole Yes

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: We now have to go back to the agenda item we deferred at the beginning, which was a discussion of the minutes of the previous meeting. There were important issues raised. Vincent, do you want to comment?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to address some feedback I've received regarding the minutes from our last committee meeting. In response to some tough questions President Parry has asked over the years, I'll be delaying the start of his lunch. @laugh

I've circulated a memo last night detailing the comments we received on the January draft minutes. One policymaker suggested trimming the first four paragraphs discussing the committee's communications policy. Four others recommended shortening only paragraph 3, which reviewed issues related to expediting the minutes. I've included all comments, with potential additions in bold and potential deletions as italics, starting on the second page of the memo.

When drafting the minutes, I assumed transparency was the goal. Providing a full and accurate record seemed necessary, covering the different aspects of your discussion about risk assessments, minutes, and economic forecasts. Paragraph 3's contribution was to present the pros and cons of expediting the minutes' release, which hasn't been well articulated publicly before.

However, two changes seem appropriate. The first is moving the last sentence of the first paragraph, which concerns the working group, to the second paragraph, focusing on risk assessments. This will prevent readers from interpreting the working group's formation and dissolution too heavily. The second change is revising the last sentence of the third paragraph, which implies that minutes will eventually be expedited, to a more neutral description, indicating no decision has been made.

These changes address four out of the five objections regarding that part of the minutes. The remaining changes have policy implications, which is why I'm bringing it back to the committee. We should consider shortening the description of discussions about the risk assessment formulas, minutes, and economic projections. While procedural issues might not be worth discussing publicly, the main points have already been reported, and this offers an opportunity to frame the issues in a balanced way.

Additionally, reducing the discussion to its bare bones would give commentators ample opportunity for irony about the committee's views on transparency. If we decide to shorten the minutes, please consider this as direction for preparing the next set. In other news, given your decision to experiment with accelerating the production of minutes, we won't have the luxury of extended discussions for this meeting and the rest of the year. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let me first follow up on your transparency assessment. I think Cathy Minehan has raised an interesting point. I would say this: We run the risk, by laying out the pros and cons of a particular argument, of inducing people to join in on the debate, and in this regard it is possible to lose control of a process that only we fully understand. We have a ratchet in here where, if we were to move forward, we can’t go back. So the concept of transparency is a very important concept but one that should be approached with a recognition that we cannot move back and forth on it. I’m a little concerned here that by raising certain issues we may not be able to backtrack. I hadn’t thought about it when I originally read the draft minutes, but in seeing the concerns that other people had, I think there’s something here that we have to consider. I do not recall so many people raising questions about the minutes before because I think most of us read the minutes passively. That suggests to me that, if there were really a strong focus on them, we’d find a greater degree of disagreement among us about their content. Now, I don’t know whether what I just said is true. I suspect it is just on the basis of my experience. What was it—four or five people who commented?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Five.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I was not one of them, so I would make the count six. It would be useful to discuss this even at the expense of delaying our tribute to our guest of honor—is that what we’re calling you today?

Speaker: MR. PARRY
Statement: The old guy!

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Does anybody have any thoughts on this? I must admit that I didn’t think about some of these issues until I saw the comments others made, but I’m a little concerned about other people getting into the debate when they know far less than we do. I don’t know what Michelle has to say on this but my impression is that, if we go in that direction, we may find ourselves coming to a conclusion that is not based on our best judgment. She’s nodding in agreement.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: Mr. Chairman?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: I do have a concern about paragraph 3. It’s not just a question of hiding or reducing the information available; I think that paragraph actually misrepresents the substance of the conversation in that it seems to suggest that the reason for not releasing the minutes earlier is purely logistical, which is certainly not correct. There were arguments of substance on both sides, and to suggest that the arguments against are purely logistical risks giving the impression that our convenience is more important than revealing information to the market.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: May I make a suggestion? This could get to be a very long discussion, and we don’t need that. Do you have time to make changes?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I have all the time you want. The Committee, however, will have to vote on the minutes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Oh, I understand that. Let me make the following suggestion: Those who haven’t commented should provide comments to Vincent. The reason I say that is that we may very well find that those who didn’t comment actually strongly support the minutes as written, and it’s important to know that. We could then do—we’ve done this on numerous occasions on other matters—a notation vote. If there is a problem in trying to find out who is where or what the issues are, Vincent can get on the phone and talk to each of you and make a judgment. In my view, that’s the best way to resolve this, and I think it’s worthy of being resolved. It’s an important issue.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: For the Reserve Bank members, this will be the first experiment with notation voting electronically. You’ll have to do that kind of vote in two weeks anyway for the minutes of this meeting, which we will be circulating at that time.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Okay. Any comments relevant to this?

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: I have a question. We log in our preferences to Vincent. Then what does he write down as the final word, the majority vote?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: He will try to infer from your comments, and I think we all trust him in this respect, a way to rewrite the minutes that he thinks will gain a majority vote. Then we will find out whether he is accurate. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement: A quick question prompted by Vincent’s last comment that we won’t have the luxury to have this type of discussion in the future. A couple of meetings back, when we were talking about expediting the minutes, you suggested that one way to deal with resolving comments to the Secretary was to have a subset of the Committee to help with the editing. Are we going to talk some more about that? As we go through this experiment, are we going to use that kind of process, or are we simply going to leave it entirely to Vincent?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: No, at this time the only thing Vincent will be involved with is finalizing the minutes for the previous meeting. That has nothing to do with how we proceed going forward on this.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: President Guynn, in the experiment with expediting the minutes, we’re going to post drafts of the minutes every evening so you’ll be able to see how the comments evolve. Then we’ll deal bilaterally with Committee members if people have specific comments. When we talked about the possibility of forming a subgroup of the Committee, we ran into the legal issue that the FOMC cannot delegate matters of monetary policy. If the minutes are viewed as a part of monetary policy, you can’t—

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement: Will we see each other’s comments? This is the first time I think we’ve ever had the luxury of seeing who made what kinds of comments. Will we see each other’s comments, and by attribution, in the future?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Now, wait a second. May I ask a question? If we have a subcommittee that is putting out a draft of the minutes to be voted on, none of us is in any way forgoing our legal commitments. It’s the delegation of the vote that’s the legal issue. A subcommittee to facilitate that process is, in my judgment—I don’t know what the General Counsel has to say on it—

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement: I thought that’s what you suggested in earlier conversations. The idea was to have some group look at the comments on the staff’s draft and make some shared judgment about whether the suggested changes should be made.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It doesn’t matter how it’s done because the ultimate decision is made finally by a notation vote. On this one, I think it’s probably not necessary to appoint such a group, but that doesn’t change the previous discussion.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement: Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Any further comments? If not, the next meeting is May 4.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Good morning, everybody. This is the last FOMC meeting that Al Broaddus will be attending. I believe he attended his first meeting in 1973.

Speaker: MR. BROADDUS
Statement: In July 1973, I’m told, Mr. Chairman. But I don’t remember. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: You don’t remember? Well, I guess the FOMC didn’t make a big impression on you at that time!

Speaker: MR. BROADDUS
Statement: It made a big impression; I was just too nervous to remember.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: We will miss you, but of course we will have a luncheon for you after our next meeting. Your official date of retirement is August 1?

Speaker: MR. BROADDUS
Statement: Right, August 1.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, whatever you have to say for the future to the FOMC, we will give you another 32.4 seconds to say it at this meeting.

Speaker: MR. BROADDUS
Statement: I’ve got more time for a last shot then! @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Would somebody like to move to approve the minutes of March 16?

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection, they are approved. Mr. Reinhart, do you want to make some comments relevant to this set of minutes?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I’d just like to report on the first dry run of our experiment in expediting the production of the FOMC minutes. We were able to produce a proposed final version of the minutes and get all twelve voting members of the Committee to indicate by our deadline that they were inclined to vote to adopt the minutes at the next meeting. There was not a lot of slack in our schedule, though, and we received the last of the members’ responses as to their inclinations on the vote pretty close to the wire. Two factors contributed to the success of the experiment. One was contracting for an outside professional to help provide a typed transcript of the meeting on an expedited basis. That sped up the process of preparing the initial draft of the minutes considerably. For another, there weren’t all that many comments on that first draft, so we were able to incorporate them and turn out a revised draft in fairly good order. One problem, which we had anticipated, was getting information to and from Committee members who were out of their offices. Needless to say, we learned a few things about the process, and we will make some minor adjustments, but they will be in the background from your perspective. Once the minutes were ready, we asked Dave Skidmore of the Public Affairs staff to prepare some hypothetical headlines and the first few paragraphs of a news wire story on the minutes. Then Board and New York staff members independently looked at that information as well as the minutes and tried to estimate the likely market effects of an early release of those minutes. The results, which should be taken with a grain of salt, suggested that there could have been a fairly substantial market reaction, and that was because of the timing. These minutes would have been released on the afternoon of the outsized employment report, and perhaps would have reinforced the market reaction to the numbers released earlier that day. I’m sure you noted that in this exercise we asked you to indicate whether you were inclined to vote to approve these minutes but not actually to vote on them. The reason was that if we had asked you to vote, we would have to indicate in the next set of minutes that the Committee had taken a notation vote on that decision. That’s why two and a half weeks after the meeting we’ll continue to ask you to give us your inclination as to whether you would approve the draft minutes but actually have you vote on them at the next meeting. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Any questions for Vincent?

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Mr. Chairman, may I ask a question? It seems to me that we ought to consult with counsel as to whether the so-called inclination to vote could be regarded as being the equivalent of a vote and could create a problem in the future. I think that’s something on which we should get some expert legal advice.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: We did.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Okay. So it’s perfectly okay to put it that way?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Virgil thought that the wording of the request we sent around was perfectly appropriate.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Geithner is proposing the election of Joseph Tracy as an associate economist of the FOMC. If there are no objections, I will assume that his request is approved.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Dino Kos, would you start us off?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the economic data and market movements that have occurred since the last meeting, using the charts circulated by Carol Low. The stronger economic data and shifting expectations have brought forward the anticipated timing of the tightening cycle, affecting various asset prices.

The top panel on page 1 shows the three-month deposit rate in black and the three-month deposit rates three, six, and nine months forward in dashed red lines. The first move up in forward rates followed the strong employment report on April 2, and the second move was due to higher-than-expected CPI data on April 14, along with other indicators suggesting inflationary pressures. The three-month forward rate moved about 35 basis points higher, while the nine-month forward rate moved up about 100 basis points. As a result, the Treasury curve sold off, and yields moved sharply higher as well. The two-year yield, which is most sensitive to policy changes, rose about 82 basis points between late March and today.

The middle panel shows the spread between the two-year note and the target fed funds rate, which widened to 130 basis points, its widest level since the spring of 2002. There has been much discussion about what would happen to bond yields if the fed funds target were raised. However, it's important to note that yields have already risen significantly in the last five weeks just from the discussion of a possible tightening.

The bottom left panel graphs the two-year to ten-year Treasury spread. If the market were truly worried about inflationary pressures, one would expect the curve to steepen as the long end rises faster than the short end. But the spread has not widened and has even narrowed slightly in the past two weeks. Similarly, breakeven rates from TIPS have not widened and have also narrowed slightly in the past two weeks. The market is certainly discounting higher interest rates, but it's not clear that higher inflation is also being priced in.

The mortgage market provided another element to recent price changes. The middle left panel graphs the ten-year yield in red and an estimate of the duration of outstanding thirty-year fixed-rate mortgage-backed securities (MBS) in blue. With the ten-year yield going from 3.75 to 4.5 percent, the

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The volume of financing in the mortgage markets has come down very sharply and has stayed down for quite a long period now. I would assume, and indeed you implied it, that hedges have already been taken on a goodly part of the positions held. So the impact of a change in yields clearly has been far less than it was last August when the maturity, as you said, went from one-half year to three and one-half years, or whatever it was. What is left out there to be hedged? And what are the vulnerabilities in the event, for example, that we get another 50 basis point rise in the ten-year note?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Well, I think it’s hard to identify what the vulnerabilities are. On the downside, it’s the refinancing risk that is being hedged; on the upside, it’s the extension risk. As the duration is extending because higher interest rates are pushing off the day of possible refinancing, there’s a certain amount of selling that portfolios will naturally do.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: But that doesn’t move as rapidly as when we get a big interest rate move and there’s a huge option takedown of mortgage positions.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Exactly. We had a flavor of that in ’94 when an unexpected change in interest rates caused a huge amount of extension hedging. So, that’s always the risk. Now, what will trigger it is difficult to pinpoint ex ante. But certainly there would be some amount that would need to be hedged as rates increase.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Presumably there will be a lot of delta hedges moving in with that type of operation.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Yes. I’m looking at Governor Bies, who may have some direct experience in this area. But again, my guess is that there would be a certain amount of hedging that would still need to be done.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Mr. Chairman, you can see in Dino’s chart on page 2 that, when rates are unchanged and are expected to be unchanged, the duration of a thirty-year conventional mortgage levels out at around four and one-half years. So we have at most about another half year to go if rates were to rise.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, that’s what history tells us—that at the lowest levels of refinancing, duration has been about four and one-half years. But can that change in any way? There’s nothing mathematically necessary about it, is there?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: No, that’s more a reflection of the mobility of our population. When rates are unchanged for long enough, the economic incentive to refinance goes away, but people still do prepay their mortgages because they move or they trade up. So, I think the four and one-half years is not immutable, but it’s a reflection of deeper demographics.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: A reflection of the house turnover rate?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I’m a little surprised that in your charts New Zealand has all of a sudden become a major international power. Considering the fact that the yield spreads between New Zealand and Australia are locked, you must be trying to convey some subtle message here.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Well, the only message is that, with the euro having brought together a lot of currencies into one, the number of currencies out there with floating rates has been reduced. We basically have the euro and a number of fixed-rate currencies, which are not worth showing, so the number of alternatives in the population is smaller.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: You ought to try the zloty or something.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: It may be too calm.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: There are indications that inventories are building up on the docks in China and that warehouses are beginning to fill, which I presume is related to the recent weakness in commodity prices, especially for steel. Prices for cold rolled sheet, which is the premier steel—especially in China—for car making, have come down quite significantly. And that’s a classic inventory play. Are you picking up anything that suggests that there’s more to this than just temporary gluts? The data are, of course, nonexistent, but what does one hear about the inventory situation in China?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: We tend to talk to traders rather than to shippers and factory managers, so I would not hold myself out as the expert in this area. Certainly among trading and investment types, I would say that there’s a lot of confusion. The sentiment about China has been pretty volatile in terms of whether the economy is overheating or not, what the inventory situation is, and what the policy stance is. Some of the volatility that we’re seeing in markets may be reflective of the fact that people change their minds about those issues from day to day. Now, in terms of inventories, I don’t know if Karen has a better handle—

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: We don’t have any actual real-time data on inventories at that level.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: There is an old classic adage that suggests that, when everybody says “nobody has any inventories,” it is telling you that the rate of accumulation is horrendous and that the level is already very high by historical standards but you haven’t sated the demand for the still higher level that occurs just before it turns around. And the first sign is a break in price. What has happened to the copper price in the last three or four days, by the way?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Copper prices are down.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: They’re down a bit.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: In the last three or four days?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Well, they’re off their peak. When we put the Greenbook to bed, they had come down from their peak level, and they have come down another 10 cents, I would say, since then. That was largely on news of the macro monetary policy coming out of China rather than on news about real economic activity or inventories or anything else. So, it was in some sense an inference—in effect, that if China’s boom cooled, copper would become less viable—on the part of those who trade in futures markets rather than a direct read on what was going on. Now, over the same interval—since we put the Greenbook to bed through Friday, which is the last date for which I have quotes—lumber prices, for example, both spot and in the futures market, went up not down. That’s partly because what is driving the lumber speculators or purchasers or hedgers or whoever is U.S. homebuilding as opposed to Chinese construction. And these market players thought they were getting different signals, so the futures curve shifted the other way. Soybean prices, which are being driven by expectations of the beginning of harvesting, also have gone up a bit since the Greenbook rather than down. But cotton is down a lot off its peak; copper is down noticeably from its peak; and wheat is down from its peak. So a more informed and discriminating shifting seems to be going on, but it is tied to what people perceive to be news about the factors that are driving demand in these markets.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Industrial activity or more broadly a goodly part of the GDP is better measured by metals prices than by lumber, whose demand is fairly concentrated, or certainly by agricultural products.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Right. And for metals, I think the story isn’t necessarily the same for all of them. But copper in particular was thought to be driven by developments in China.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Aluminum is peaking, as is zinc and I think tin. Somebody still buys tin, but that’s down, too, I believe.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I have a less detailed picture in front of me regarding aluminum. It appears to have stopped rising as opposed to falling.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It has flattened out.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Yes, but copper has already started moving down.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Okay. Any other questions for Dino? If not, would someone like to move the ratification of the domestic market transactions?

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection they are approved. Dino, would you like to talk about the swap lines?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: It’s May, which means it is time to consider the renewal of the swap lines. I did send a brief memo around which covered the renewal of the swaps, and I’m recommending that the Committee renew them. There were also a couple of other items that I mentioned briefly regarding some things that we’re doing with the euro reserves. I’d be happy to answer questions about either item.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: If there are no objections, I will assume that your request is granted. We now move on to the staff report, and I call on Dave Stockton and Karen Johnson.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman and esteemed colleagues, I'd like to begin by expressing my disappointment when I arrived at work last Thursday to find that our economic forecast had taken a turn for the worse. The BEA reported a real GDP growth rate of 4.2 percent in the first quarter, a full percentage point less than our previous forecast. We've spent the past few days analyzing the implications of this data, focusing particularly on inventory investment.

Our initial assessment indicates that our error was largely due to an overestimation of nonfarm inventory investment's contribution to real GDP growth. About two-thirds of our miss was due to truck inventories, which proved more complicated to estimate than we anticipated. The BEA does not use a straightforward translation of data on the number of vehicles held in inventory to estimate inventory investment for trucks.

However, we do not believe that this will significantly impact our forecast for truck production for the year as a whole. We've adjusted our second-quarter growth projection to 5 percent, up from 4.5 percent, to account for a smaller drag from the motor vehicle sector.

Other inventory investment also came in below our expectations, and we've adjusted our forecast accordingly. We now anticipate a more gradual rebound in inventory investment throughout the year and into 2024.

Despite this setback, we remain optimistic about the overall economic outlook. Monetary and fiscal policies continue to provide a substantial boost to growth, and the labor market is showing signs of improvement. Private payrolls rose 160,000 per month on average in the first quarter, and hiring intentions have been trending upwards.

However, we must remain cautious. The employment situation is still tentative, and the ongoing liquidation of inventories could be a sign that business caution has not yet evaporated completely. We'll continue to monitor these developments closely.

Inflation has also been a concern, with recent data coming in higher than expected. We've revised our core consumer price inflation projection accordingly, raising it by between 0.25 and 0.5 percentage points for both this year and next.

Despite these challenges, the economy is showing signs of strength. Low real interest rates, tax cuts, and large increases in government spending are fueling aggregate demand, and final sales are advancing briskly. We'll

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to bring up the recent and anticipated behavior of import prices, which may be of particular interest given the recent surge in import deflator. In the first quarter, import prices for goods and services rose at an annual rate of 10 percent, up significantly from the 2.6 percent increase in the last quarter of 2003.

A significant contributor to this increase was the rise in oil prices, which led to a large increase in the prices for computer and semiconductor imports. However, this was partially offset by declining prices for computers. Another factor was the continued increase in service imports, which rose strongly in 2003 due to the depreciation of the dollar.

For core imports, the annual rate of price increase in the first quarter was 6.6 percent, higher than our Greenbook estimate but less than the 1.8 percent recorded last year. Commodity-intensive categories, such as foods and non-oil industrial supplies, experienced the greatest jumps, with the latter rising at an annual rate of nearly 24 percent.

Our model analysis suggests that the exchange rate and commodities, including natural gas, were the two most important factors behind the rapid inflation in core import prices last quarter. Looking ahead, we forecast a deceleration of core import prices to an annual rate of about 3.5 percent for the remainder of this year and 0.5 percent next year.

The recent and projected path of the nominal exchange value of the dollar, which paused around the turn of the year after a two-year period of depreciation, is a significant factor in this forecast. We expect the exchange rate to contribute a small negative to zero to import price inflation starting with the current quarter. Commodity prices are expected to continue to exert significant upward pressure on import prices over the rest of 2004 due to lagged effects from previous increases, but will hold down import price inflation in 2005 due to declines expected on average and in most categories.

The extremely rapid increases in commodity prices during the fourth quarter of last year and the first quarter of this year, particularly for minerals and metals, are linked to the rebound of real output growth in some regions, especially emerging Asia. China, which has averaged nearly 15 percent growth over the past three quarters, is a major reason

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Incidentally, has General Motors reported yet?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: No, we don’t have the report on General Motors yet.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Questions for our colleagues? President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I just wanted to ask Karen a question about China. You mentioned that China’s growth had been 15 percent and you expected it to slow to 7½ percent but not collapse, as you put it. There has been a lot of speculation about the risks and potential difficulties associated with a slowing Chinese economy. I was wondering if you could talk about that a bit in this context: We all are familiar with the Asian financial crisis of the late ’90s, and I was wondering what magnitudes we would have to see in China in order to come close to what we saw in the late ’90s and what kind of spillover effects would stem from that.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Well, if China’s growth fell below, say, 5 percent, I think we’d see fairly significant spillover effects. Seven percent used to be a sort of “target” rate of growth; then it became 8 percent and then it was around 10 percent. But China growing at 7 percent would be a return to what we had seen for quite a few years in the 1990s, which would probably allow for a better allocation of resources than growth of 15 percent is likely to be generating. The fact that China is not in fact a fully market-based economy is, I think, the bad news and the good news. The bad news part is that it probably is leading to a misallocation of resources and, therefore, to the risk of some over-accumulation of certain items—and the sudden redirecting of effort because somebody may well wake up eventually and realize that the warehouses are full of air conditioners, say, that aren’t moving. On the other hand, it’s partly good news because there are no phenomena like CEO risk aversion or tendencies on the part of markets to overshoot or the need for depreciation or balance sheet corrections to work their way through before companies make decisions. If the authorities in China decide that too many resources are being used to produce air conditioners and what they really want are snow blowers, the desired reallocation of resources will happen pretty quickly. So, to my mind, the risk in China is more that resources are being wasted. And given that standards of living in China and much of the world aren’t as high as we’d like them to be, that’s a very significant and important cost. But I don’t think that involves the same systemic cyclical behavior yet that a truly market economy would have. That said, if the Chinese run into economic problems in terms of their ability to meet the demands of the population for rising standards of living and jobs—or if they fail to deal in an orderly way with the transition from a mostly rural to a more industrialized economy—I don’t know what the effects would be. If those efforts hit a rough spot, I don’t know that I have any special expertise to suggest what might politically happen in China or how that situation would resolve itself. I do think the spillover to the rest of Asia would be very severe—at least as bad as in the late 1990s.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: May I just add a comment? One difference to note between Asia ’98 and China now is that a major issue in the earlier situation was an outflow of capital, but China has a closed capital account. So a capital outflow is something that we’re just not going to see to the same degree in the current case. That’s one difference that may mitigate the possibility of a sudden financial crisis similar to what occurred in Asia in the late ’90s.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: And in places such as Korea and a few other countries, we’re not seeing the current account deficits and the need to attract capital; so if the capital were to stop flowing, it wouldn’t have exactly the same consequences. On the other hand, outside of China, domestic demand is not really the driving force in Asia; it is export demand. So in a macroeconomic sense, the Asian countries are vulnerable to development in China and in the United States.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Further questions for David or Karen? If not, who would like to start our Committee discussion? President Moskow?

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some positive updates from the Seventh District economy. We're seeing a more widespread pickup, and contacts are less nervous than they were in March. Many businesses report increased bookings, growing backlogs, and low inventories, indicating that momentum will continue. Household spending has been strong, and now we're hearing about increases in business spending, particularly in machine tools and heavy equipment.

Our shipping contacts, from trucking to warehousing to cardboard boxes, have all reported strong numbers. Air travel is also picking up, with both United and Boeing reporting traffic above September 11, 2001, levels and high load factors. Boeing even announced increased production schedules last week.

Advertising is picking up in the magazine industry, suggesting that the business may be emerging from a "pageless" recovery. Labor markets are responding to the increases in demand. Temporary-help firms report stronger growth in billable hours, and permanent placements and forward-looking orders have increased.

Price increases are becoming more common. Firms are passing on input cost increases to their customers, and retailers are able to offset cost increases by raising their own prices. In the auto industry, inventories are high, but some manufacturers are comfortable with these levels as they help cover planned production shutdowns.

The data on real economic activity since our last meeting have been encouraging, with robust household and business spending. We think we've turned the corner on employment growth, but we'll need to see another month or two of strong numbers before declaring victory.

The latest readings on inflation were surprising, and we may need to reevaluate the size of the output gap. While we still believe there's a sizable output gap, we can't dismiss the possibility that it's smaller than we've been thinking. We'll be closely monitoring the data in the coming weeks to determine if inflation is a greater concern than we currently believe. Depending on the data, we may need to tighten more aggressively than the path laid out in the Greenbook.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Broaddus.

Speaker: MR. BROADDUS
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to shift our focus today to the national economy and provide some thoughts on the overall forecast. The economic situation in our district aligns with the national trend, so I won't delve into specifics about the regional picture. Instead, I'd like to share my perspective on recent developments at the national level.

The data we've received since the last meeting has significantly altered my view of the near-term outlook and the balance of risks. The release of the March jobs report and the first-quarter GDP report suggest that the economy may be moving towards a balanced growth path and reasonably full employment. Aggregate demand appears to be broadly based, and productivity growth has slowed to a point where further demand increases will stimulate additional job growth. Moreover, recent core inflation data indicate that the disinflation risk that has been a concern for many of us may have passed.

However, it's important to note that the employment and output gaps are still substantial. Unit labor costs are behaving reasonably well, so I'm not yet alarmed about inflation pressures. But we must remain cautious, as things can change quickly in this business.

I'd also like to discuss the potential risks to the U.S. economy from recent developments in the foreign sector, particularly in China. I've had some insightful conversations with Marvin Goodfriend, who recently returned from an IMF mission to the Peoples Bank of China.

China's recent trade deficit and the strong global demand for manufactured goods, especially in China, have led to effects on industries like textile production in the Carolinas. This is due to both business going to China and business coming from China to U.S. companies. However, China's boom is unsustainable, with money and credit growing at an unsustainable rate. This could lead to inflation, which is currently around 3 percent, but could reach much higher levels if the boom continues.

Unfortunately, China lacks the institutions to effectively deal with this situation, such as a strong independent central bank, a healthy commercial banking system, and fully flexible interest rates. The authorities have used controls in the past, but these have not been effective, and their impact may be even less so now that the economy is larger and transitioning to a market economy.

In conclusion, while inflation in China may be slowing, the boom could continue and create

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent conversations with key industry contacts. My contacts at FedEx and UPS have expressed optimism about the economy, with FedEx planning for a significant increase in capital expenditures for capacity expansion in fiscal '05, up around 20-25% from the previous year. They're also closely monitoring fuel prices and availability. UPS reported a 9.2% increase in domestic air freight in the last twelve months and a 6% increase in the first two months of this year compared to last. International business was up 10% and they expect strong growth in March.

FedEx's contact mentioned that check traffic from China to the US is growing exceptionally fast, up 60% in the first quarter of this year. UPS isn't planning to increase capital expenditures at this time. J.B. Hunt reported upward pressure on prices for truck shipments, with rates up 18% in the last three years and 6% in the last four quarters. Driver supply is extremely tight, leading to higher driver rates.

My Wal-Mart contact shared that sales are growing about 5%, but the March-April sales data can be ambiguous due to the varying date of Easter. He noted that lower-income individuals are financially stressed, but higher-income individuals are doing well. Wal-Mart has been growing about 8% per year in square footage and new stores are performing well.

Regarding the national economy and inflation outlook, I asked my staff to calculate inflation using a different method, focusing on the ex-energy part of the PCE data. This calculation shows an inflation rate that is about ¾ percentage point higher than the core inflation measure, suggesting that inflation was not as low last year as we thought. I believe the inflation risks are skewed to the upside and money growth is rising again. I'd like to emphasize that the inflation risks are rising, as there's a lot of commentary in the marketplace to that effect. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President McTeer.

Speaker: MR. MCTEER
Statement:
Chairperson, this morning over breakfast, I was pondered to pen an economy haiku. Surprisingly, our Dallas meeting commenced with this theme. My haiku reads, "Growth resumes, prices climb higher." I believe this succinctly encapsulates both our District and the national economy. Since last September, Texas unemployment has seen a steady decline, with border employment rates returning to pre-recession levels. Retail sales have fully recovered, and high energy prices, although a national burden, remain a boon for Texas.

Our District's petrochemical industry, which had suffered due to high energy input prices and weak demand, is now experiencing relief. The industry benefits from strong demand from China and southern Asia, leaving us in a favorable position. The high-tech sector, a notable laggard, has seen a return of net employment gains in March. This is a result of rising orders and production, with semiconductor industry contacts expecting sales growth of 19% this year.

Price pressures are evident across the board, with more producers passing on higher costs to consumers. Building materials, fabricated metals, petrochemicals, and oil field equipment have all seen price increases. Increased orders for packaging from retailers have driven paper prices higher. Steel and galvanized metal are in short supply, and railroads are operating at capacity.

However, industries such as airlines and homebuilders have not enjoyed increased pricing power. The labor market still holds a large pool of unskilled workers seeking employment, with little upward pressure on wages. Rapid healthcare cost increases have discouraged firms from hiring new permanent full-time employees.

On a national scale, the latest CPI, PCE, and GDP statistics suggest that the price pressures reported earlier in the year have become widespread. I remain hopeful that this uptick in inflation is a one-time adjustment marking the end of disinflation rather than the beginning of a significant upward threat. However, we must remain vigilant as core and median inflation measures are also higher. Capacity utilization and output gap measures are subject to large after-the-fact revisions, and profit margins have shown a strong inverse relationship with inflation pressures. Lastly, the significant amount of policy accommodation currently in place warrants our attention. The latest Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey shows a significant swing

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some positive updates on the economic situation in New England. Manufacturers have expressed optimistic views, and while business confidence in Massachusetts is slightly lower than previous highs, recent surveys indicate positive assessments of national business conditions. Consumer confidence is up significantly from a year ago, retail sales are improving, and residential real estate markets remain strong.

I should note that job growth has been a topic of concern in our previous discussions, but there have been some encouraging signs. Nonfarm employment rose in March, marking only the fifth monthly increase in the last three years. The unemployment rate for the region is now below 5 percent, and contacts are reporting increased hiring, particularly in manufacturing and related services.

One of our tenants, a large intellectual property law firm, has reported a surge in patent work in the biotech and computer services sectors, as well as an increase in legal work related to patent litigation. They've taken more space in our building and are adding to their staff. Small businesses are also reporting increased activity, even in commercial real estate.

However, there are concerns about rising costs for metals, wood, plastic, rubber, fuel, and other petroleum products, as well as health care and other insurance costs. Some firms believe they can pass on these cost increases, but others are uncertain. Planned salary increases are moderate, at around 3 percent, and capital spending is increasing at a moderate pace.

One surprising finding from our contacts is the number of times the upcoming presidential election was mentioned as a concern regarding the economic future. Some people believe that the economy is being artificially pumped up for the October election and will slide afterwards. While I'm sure that's not the intent, it does reflect concerns about the sustainability of the recovery.

Despite these concerns, the national data since our last meeting have been encouraging. Unemployment insurance claims have been stable, and firms expect to pick up their hiring rates. Personal income and expenditures are rising, retail sales are strong, and the housing market remains solid. Consumer confidence is high, and business strength is evident in strong profit growth and accommodative financial markets.

Given this information, the question is whether the recovery is sustainable, especially after the fiscal stimulus wears off. Our forecast in Boston is less optimistic for the second half of this year, but the basic trajectory of the data is the

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some positive economic developments in our Southeast region that have continued since our last meeting. Florida is leading the way with a robust economy, and tourism is a significant contributor. Occupancy rates are rising, and there's an increase in foreign tourists coming to the US. Atlanta's convention bookings are up 20% from last year, and business travel has also increased. Our manufacturing sector is experiencing a pickup, which is reflected in new orders and production. This improvement is partly due to stronger export activity.

Residential construction continues to be vigorous, but nonresidential construction has yet to show any significant improvement in most markets. Some industries, such as apparel and textiles, are still struggling due to structural adjustments. However, we're seeing positive signs in the banking sector with significant improvement in C&I lending.

There have been reports of rising prices in various sectors, including steel, energy, food, chemicals, plastic, metal, wood building materials, fertilizers, tires, ice cream, and agricultural products like soy, coffee, and meats. Prices are increasing due to low inventories, lack of capacity, increased demand, higher OPEC prices, and regulation.

On a national level, the economy is showing strong growth, and business spending is making a nice contribution. While there is uncertainty regarding employment growth, recent data and anecdotal reports are encouraging.

We need to pay more attention to price pressures and price prospects and their implications for policy. While inflation is not yet alarming, we should not dismiss the signals we're getting from both the data and anecdotal reports. We may be able to start taking back some of the extraordinary policy accommodation without causing serious unintended consequences. We should also be careful about introducing new language into the statement as we approach the time for a policy shift. Pre-commitments about the timing or path of policy changes could mislead markets or limit our flexibility.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Third District regarding our current economic situation. While economic activity is on the rise, job growth remains elusive. Our Philadelphia Fed's leading indexes suggest a pickup in growth across all three states over the next three quarters. Retail sales have seen an uptick in March and April, with some executives reporting larger average purchases and increased sales of luxury items. However, auto dealers are experiencing modest sales growth, with expectations of maintaining current levels for the rest of the year.

Residential construction remains high, but permits and contract values have eased recently. Commercial construction, on the other hand, is hampered by high vacancy rates, particularly in the office market. Manufacturing activity continues to expand, with our Business Outlook Survey (BOS) index of general activity reaching 32.5% in April. Employment in the sector has remained flat, with some firms reporting difficulty finding skilled labor.

The labor market issue was a major focus in our April BOS, with 67% of firms reporting open positions and 66% of them having trouble filling them due to a lack of qualified applicants. This issue is not just limited to production machine operators or those with special plant and systems skills, but also extends to applicants lacking basic reading, writing, and arithmetic skills.

Wages and goods prices are increasing at a slightly faster rate in the Philadelphia region compared to the nation. Industrial price pressures have risen, and some firms are passing on these increased costs to their customers. Our survey index of future prices paid has also increased.

Despite these challenges, business leaders and bankers I've met with during my April travels report signs of improvement and remain optimistic. At the national level, economic conditions have improved since our last meeting, and the recovery may now be self-sustaining. Consumer spending, house-price appreciation, and healthy consumer balance sheets are contributing to this growth.

However, there are risks to economic growth, including the potential for renewed but still modest pricing power among businesses and the impact of rising interest rates on consumer lending. I believe we are approaching the time when we will need to adjust monetary policy, but not yet to the point of tightening. Instead, we should focus on increasing our flexibility regarding future actions. I propose we acknowledge the improvement in the labor market, re-evaluate our inflation

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement:
Chairperson, good afternoon, I'm pleased to report that the Tenth District economy has shown remarkable improvement since our last meeting. Most sectors have exhibited increased strength, with manufacturing activity accelerating in March and April. Production and new orders have surged, resulting in substantial backlogs at numerous firms we surveyed. Employment growth has been modest but consistent, with three consecutive months above year-ago levels. Optimism about future activity has boosted capital spending plans, while hiring plans have remained unchanged.

Our region's production index edged up to 31 in March and jumped to 46 in April, while the new orders index fell back to 29 in March and then rebounded strongly to 54. Notable news includes the revival of demand for business jets in Wichita due to improved corporate profits and an aging fleet.

Consumer spending and housing activity have strengthened, with mid-level department stores experiencing growth after lagging behind. High-end goods and second homes have also seen increased demand in several parts of our region. Summer resort bookings have been very strong, although there are concerns about the impact of higher gas prices on tourism.

Energy activity has continued to expand due to high oil and gas prices, but there are shortages of workers, rigs, and materials in the industry. Commercial real estate remains in a slump, the only exception to the generally positive news.

Year-over-year job growth in the District turned positive in March for the first time since 2001. Wage increases remain minimal, but manufacturers have raised their output prices due to surging raw materials costs. Some firms have successfully passed these increased costs onto their customers by implementing price surcharges, which are now being institutionalized into their base price programs.

Our agricultural sector is benefiting from bullish commodity markets and optimism in the sector. The national economy is expanding at a solid pace, with GDP growth projected between 4½ and 5 percent this year. However, there are rising concerns about inflation, as both consumers and businesses expect further price increases. Core inflation measures have risen, and there are signs that inflation expectations are increasing.

While I don't anticipate a significant inflation outbreak in the near term, the upside risk is increasing. The current real funds rate is negative, and the dollar's depreciation and higher energy, commodity, and input

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to align myself with Al Broaddus' perspective on the national economy and the fragility of the China situation. I regret that I won't be able to repeat this agreement at future meetings, Al. I share his concerns about the Chinese authorities' ability to slow down their economy in a controlled manner, and we must keep a close eye on that situation.

Regarding the output gap, recent labor market data, particularly the March employment report, suggest that the long-awaited economic strengthening is finally underway. The data have reduced the risk that productivity would remain strong enough to absorb robust demand without creating inflationary pressures. Productivity growth has slowed to an average of 3% in the fourth and first quarters, but we should exercise caution when interpreting these figures.

On employment, we've only seen one month of significant growth, and the first quarter as a whole showed a shrinking output gap, but not by much. Capacity utilization in manufacturing rose, and the unemployment rate dropped 0.3 percentage point, but this decline was partly due to a decrease in labor force participation. The unemployment rate in March was actually higher than the first-quarter average. With actual growth exceeding estimated potential, the output gap measure fell only 0.2 percentage point to 2%. The staff projects potential growth at around 3½% this year and next, meaning we'll need demand growth above 4% to make a dent in the slack.

Inflation concerns have subsided, but we must consider whether we're seeing a more persistent strengthening of inflation pressures. I believe inflation will remain low and stable, despite the recent uptick, due to ongoing slack, productivity increases, and current profit margins. Core consumer inflation fell significantly between 2001 and 2003, indicating substantial excess capacity in labor and product markets. With resource utilization still below levels from that period, I believe substantial excess capacity remains.

In conclusion, the economy appears to be on a path toward full employment, but the evidence is tentative. The trajectory so far has been gentle, and even if we realize the more robust growth projected, slack is likely to erode gradually over coming quarters. I agree that we need added flexibility in our statement and that the path of tightening will likely be gradual. We must ensure markets understand this as we prepare to tighten to

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the ongoing discussion about the economic trends in the Ninth District. I've had the opportunity to meet with our Advisory Council on Small Business and Labor, Twin Cities financial services leaders, and our board of directors. The stories I've heard have been overwhelmingly positive.

The manufacturing sector is experiencing significant expansion. Employment is growing, exports are up, and firms are investing in equipment and software. The labor market is tight, but there's a skills mismatch issue that's being discussed. Housing remains strong, and large construction projects have recently started. While office space construction remains weak, absorption is picking up, which could lead to renewed activity in that sector. Consumer spending is robust, and there's a common theme of companies being able to raise prices and have those increases stick. This trend is widespread and has boosted business attitudes and confidence.

Regarding the national economy, my assessment remains positive, but there are a few noteworthy changes. The economy is performing well, as Dave mentioned, with virtually all cylinders firing. However, there's more inflationary pressure than I anticipated. I don't foresee a significant acceleration of inflation, but the likelihood of further declines or a leveling off at the current pace has diminished. I also question the reliability of using the output gap to predict inflation. In my view, it's not a reliable indicator.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: First Vice President Moore.

Speaker: MR. MOORE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share an anecdote that resonated with me during a recent family dinner. My daughter expressed her delight at being seated at the adult table, even if it was just for that one occasion. And I must admit, I can identify with her sentiment, sitting here today.

The Twelfth District's economic expansion has gained considerable momentum, with manufacturing activity on the rise and the IT sector leading the charge. Technology product sales have surged, and IT exports to Asia have seen significant growth. Our contact in California summed it up succinctly—it's all about China.

The aircraft sector, which had been struggling, is showing signs of stabilization. Although Boeing's production levels are still below their 1999 peak, recent delivery rates and order backlogs suggest that the company will produce roughly the same number of planes this year as last. Other sectors are also performing well, such as the housing market in parts of the San Francisco Bay area, which is experiencing double-digit price appreciation and record sales.

Consumer spending has been strong, as reflected in growing sales tax revenues. Job gains have been significant in most areas of the District, except for California, which has taken steps to address high labor costs by reforming its workers' compensation system. California employers face workers' comp costs that are more than three times the national average, but reforms are underway, with two major bills passed since last summer.

California is also making progress on its budget, with plans to issue approximately $12 billion worth of deficit finance bonds. The first issuance, consisting mostly of fixed-rate bonds, is scheduled for this week. The second issuance, focusing on variable-rate bonds, is expected in late May or early June. The improved revenue outlook and emerging spirit of compromise bode well for California's ability to eliminate its structural deficit in the future.

Turning to the national economy, our forecast for economic activity aligns with the Greenbook, but we anticipate a steeper trajectory for the funds rate through the end of next year. Recent employment data suggest that the labor market may be turning the corner, but the evidence is still tentative. Inflation data are a concern, with some uncertainty regarding whether the recent increase reflects transitory movements or a moderately higher underlying trend. We take comfort in the fact that some of the recent increase can

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some updates on the economic outlook for our region since our last meeting. The risks have shifted in a favorable direction, and the growth outlook appears stronger with less downside risk. Inflation risks are more balanced around a higher but still moderate path. The Second District economy has continued to improve, with an increased pace of expansion. Employment is growing rapidly, and businesses plan to hire more in the coming months. However, there are reports of skills mismatches and difficulty filling certain positions. Inflation in our region is higher than the national average, and much of it is due to housing prices. Businesses report higher input prices and the ability to pass on these costs to consumers.

For the nation, we expect the economy to expand at a rate of around 4.5% this year, even with monetary policy following the current market interest rate path. The improving labor market, moderate compensation growth, and high consumer confidence levels suggest a reasonably strong pace of growth in consumer spending. Investment spending is also expected to continue its rapid pace, supported by growing demand confidence, strong profit margins, and favorable financial positions.

However, the inflation outlook has changed more significantly. The recent acceleration in inflation suggests a more broad-based shift than just transitory effects. Surveys of establishments show a more generalized increase in the ability to pass on cost increases. Unit labor costs have likely shifted into positive territory in the first quarter, and TIPS spreads show a significant rise in inflation expectations over the medium term.

Despite these developments, we do not believe there is a significant risk of sustained acceleration in inflation in the near term that would be inconsistent with our price stability objective. Factors such as profit margins, labor market slack, the fading effect of the dollar decline, recent commodity and energy price surges, and competition pressures are expected to contain the degree of acceleration in core consumer prices. Our forecast is for the core PCE to increase roughly 1.5% in 2023.

However, we need to be more attentive to the risk of a sustained increase in prices materializing earlier than expected. The financial markets seem to reflect a high degree of confidence in our ability to contain inflation risks, but this confidence is tied to the substantial degree of monetary policy tightening already priced in over the next twelve months. We should welcome

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let’s break for coffee and be back in ten to fifteen minutes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some positive updates on business conditions in the Fourth District. We've seen a noticeable improvement in recent weeks, with a broad-based acceleration across various industrial sectors. Here are my observations:

First, businesses are ramping up their capital spending plans for this year and next.
