The reason is that the error term embeds so many different influences, including inflation expectations, which President Moskow referred to, commodity price pass-throughs that may occur through channels other than just energy prices, competitive pressures, and regulatory influences. It’s the bucket into which everything in the “all else” category goes. So I would not want to characterize our view as just two channels—gaps and these other variables. On the absence of a speed effect, we looked pretty hard for a speed effect and disposed of a lot of degrees of freedom in the statistical world in that search. We think there is a discernible speed effect in the prices of items that are in relatively inelastic short-term supply. But for final core PCE prices, we can’t find it. And we’re reassured in that by reference to some particular historical episodes like the recovery from the very deep recession of 1980-82, when the unemployment rate was rising very rapidly and yet inflation continued to decline. Now, if there were a strong speed effect, it should have shown up during that period, and it didn’t. So we’ve looked hard on the econometrics, and we’ve done some case studies, and neither approach leads us to think that there’s much there. With respect to direct monetary influence—whether money itself shows up in inflation—we think it’s important, but we think it’s important in setting the background factors that we believe influence the inflation dynamics more fundamentally. We think that what monetary policy is doing is altering the balance of factors like the gap and resource utilization, for example. When we have moved to test a direct influence of money in our price equation, it’s a little like a mirage on the highway: Sometimes it’s there a little bit, and sometimes it disappears. We can’t find a reliably strong statistical relationship. So we think this is a good, albeit imperfect, framework to use for the entire analysis.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: The other question I have deals with chart 3, on housing prices. My question is about the footnote, which says that the rent–price ratio is adjusted for biases in the trends of both rents and prices. Is that where you pick up demographics and lifecycle factors? What are these biases in the trends, and how does one think about changing demographics and the relative attractiveness of owning a home versus renting? Give me some sense of whether or not the shape of the curve that you show here is likely to reverse, as you imply, or likely to stay relatively low.

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: The biases referred to in that footnote were really technical biases in the construction of the two measures shown here, the rent measure and the price measure. Had we not adjusted for them, the rent-to-price ratio would have been much lower at the end point. So it would have looked more alarming. In part we think the published data have some technical problems that need to be taken care of before this analysis can be done in a way that is meaningful. With regard to the question of owning versus renting, it depends to some extent on what is happening to interest rates because that changes that calculation at the margin. So it’s really important to plot any kind of valuation measure relative to an opportunity cost. Just showing the rent-to-price ratio I think would have been somewhat misleading; it’s really that gap that we think is the meaningful measure of valuation. And it looks somewhat rich, taking account of the fact that interest rates are relatively low and income growth has been relatively strong. I don’t want to leave the impression that we think there’s a huge housing bubble. We believe a lot of the rise in house prices is rooted in fundamentals. But even after you account for the fundamentals, there’s a part of the increase that is hard to explain.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: I, too, thought the presentation by the staff was very good this morning. I must say, as one who is going to talk about similar things in my own remarks, mine is going to sound a little lame in comparison! But there was one issue that you didn’t talk about this morning, and not much was said about it in the briefing on Monday or in the Greenbook either. And that is, despite the low utilization rate, wages have kept up. Does that worry you? Is this something that is a reversion to normal in a regression equation, or is this the start of something that we ought to worry about?

Speaker: MR. WILCOX
Statement: We think wages are being supported by the rapid productivity growth that we’ve had over the last few years. We think that is adding a tenth this year, for example, compared with last year and another couple of tenths next year. On the other hand, overall we don’t see ECI compensation accelerating greatly. One of the influences on the ECI that we believe will hold it down is health insurance costs, which we expect to contribute less to the rise in ECI this year. Also, we actually think that in 2003 the growth of ECI was a little stronger than we could account for in our models, and we project that that puzzle will go away. So the balance of all those factors leaves our projection just slightly flat this year compared with last year and up only a little next year. There are risks. One of the risks to that compensation projection, which we’ve highlighted, is the decline in the markup. If workers bid more aggressively for their share of the pie than we built into the baseline or if firms are more eager to ramp up their production than we’ve assumed, that compensation trajectory could turn out to be too optimistic.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Governor Gramlich, those figures did get our attention, though. There were upward revisions to the nonfarm business comp hours starting in the fourth quarter of last year, and we also missed on the ECI in the first quarter—it came in higher than we had projected. That was one of the factors that prompted us to go back and look at our estimates of both the NAIRU and potential output. We made a very marginal, almost cosmetic, upward revision to our estimate of the NAIRU—we raised it by just a tenth—but we also revised down potential output just because we thought the overall constellation of wage and price data was more negative than we had anticipated, given our previous estimates of the levels of potential output and the gap. So I think we’ve tried to respond to that, but it was something we found troubling vis-à-vis the views that we held in April.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement: I want to follow up on what you just said, David. You mentioned that less slack wasn’t the major contributor to the bulk of the rise in inflation, but you didn’t comment on exactly how much your estimate for slack has changed. Can you tell us how much you reduced that estimate from the time of the May Greenbook? You made a few remarks about that, but is that the sole explanation for the change in the fed funds rate path from the May Greenbook to the current Greenbook? There is a significant change in that path. Is it all a reflection of the change in that estimate for slack?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: I’d say that two factors are behind the revisions we made to the federal funds rate assumptions that underlie our forecast. One, importantly, was the downward revisions we made to potential output. In essence, those downward revisions suggested to us that you needed to be closer to neutral by the end of our forecast period than we had previously assumed. A piece of that—and these are all simultaneously determined within our forecasting system—was that the inflation figures were higher as well. We didn’t think that revising up our inflation forecast as much as we did, without making a corresponding adjustment to the federal funds rate—and therefore allowing the real federal funds rate to be on a weaker path than we had in the last forecast—would be warranted. So the higher inflation and the lower estimate of potential output suggested to us that the underlying path for the funds rate probably needed to be steeper than we had been assuming previously.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Dave, one thing that surprised me about the baseline forecast and also about most of the simulations was the flat ten-year Treasury rate. If I want to take, say, the market-based funds rate scenario and assume that the ten-year rate goes up about half as much as the funds rate over that period, then as a first approximation should I just extrapolate or interpolate the real growth and unemployment numbers based on that? Or do you think there’s more going on there?

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: Maybe I’ll just start off by talking for a moment about how we got the assumption for the ten-year rate. We begin by asking ourselves where we think the ten-year rate would go by the end of our forecast period under the market-based assumption for the funds rate. Our guess is that it would be up about ½ percentage point from where it is now, just using the usual term structure arguments that relate short-term rates to long-term rates. So we deviated from that by holding the ten-year rate essentially flat. That reflects our belief that, if our view for inflation turns out to be correct, there will be a favorable market surprise that will keep the ten-year rate from rising along the path that it otherwise would have taken.

Speaker: MR. WILCOX
Statement: In our alternative simulation for that market-based funds rate, we have the ten-year Treasury bond rate coming up 20 basis points by the end of the forecast period.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Right. I was speculating that it might be more than that. Presumably you’ve got a greater impact on the ten-year rate in the market-based scenario.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Obviously, it depends a little on whether rates move up earlier in our projection period or later. My guess is that if there were a significant surprise on long-term interest rates next year, most of the impact of that would probably be felt late in the year and moving into 2006.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Okay.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: President Stern, I asked the staff to calculate for me if we followed exactly the path of the funds rate that is now built into markets, where the ten-year rate would be a year from now. And their answer was 30 basis points higher than it is today. So, it is not 50 percent of the rise in the funds rate.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement: I have a question on chart 3 on the commercial real estate valuation. Having lived through the 1986-91 period when the NOI(net operating income)–price ratio was down to the levels it is today, I look at that period as abnormal because we had tax changes that were retroactive, which basically put a lot of real estate projects and ownership under water. So we had a tremendous upheaval in the real estate market, with people filing for bankruptcy and dumping properties. It worries me that we’re back down in a sense to a situation that is comparable to that severe turn in the real estate market. I really look at that as a good example of how retroactive taxes affect people’s return targets when they enter into long-lived projects.

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: I was actually here at the Board during that period, and I remember that we were concerned as well in the late ’80s about real estate valuations and the amount of construction still going on given that the tax benefits had been withdrawn in 1986. We did not really understand what the rationale was in the market. Ultimately it turned out that there was a massive amount of over-construction and lending standards that were too lax. I think there are some reasons to be watchful of developments in the commercial construction sector at this point. It is clear that the compensation for risk has gotten somewhat thinner in this market than it was throughout the 1990s, presumably after market participants had learned some lessons from the very thin compensation that they accepted through the ’80s. On the other hand, we do see some rather substantial differences in the structure of the commercial real estate market at this time. Lending standards do seem to have held much firmer. And there’s a lot more transparency in the market now because of the more widespread availability of data on prices and rents to market participants. So we think the decisionmaking is better. I wouldn’t say it is perfect. We do feel that there is some reason to be alert to developments in that market.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Just to follow up on what Roger was asking about the panel in chart 3 on housing valuations. In that panel the relative movement of the two measures is somewhat key to at least the intuitive persuasiveness of the argument that housing might be overvalued. I understand the units of the real long-term Treasury yield. I don’t understand the units of the rent-to-price ratio.

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: The rent and the price data come from different sources; they are not part of an integrated system. The rent data are from the CPI, which is itself only an index number. So there is no way to say the rent–price ratio is 8 percent—like an earnings–price ratio, for example.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It is proportional to whatever that number would be.

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: Yes. We could make the level of the red line anything we wanted to, but we could only move it in a level adjustment up and down.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: But you set the scale, too, right? You could set it so that the zeroes are the same on both axes?

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: Right. With creative charting we could make the relationship between the two series shift up and down however we wanted. That’s why we’re stressing—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: But that’s not so for the ratio between those two series. That is invariant to the scale.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Yes, that’s true; they ought to have the same zeroes it seems.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: No, no. If you have a relative measure that is an actual ratio, the ratio of the two numbers is invariant to what the relative number is.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: That’s right. But in the chart, the staff has the zero set at very different places on the two scales.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: You can’t trust them to do it right! @laugh

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: I’m just wondering, how did you decide where to put the zero? If you put it much closer to where the zero is for the long-run Treasury rate, the squiggles in the red line would be a lot smaller.

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: You can make the squiggles as small or as large as you want.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Right. And your argument has to do with the size of the squiggles?

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: No, I think the argument has to do with the size of the gap between the two. And the current gap relative to its average over history—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The scale will not change the fact that the gap is closing. The conclusion is independent of the scale. You could try any variation you want, and that gap will always close.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: The gap between these two lines?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: No matter what we used for our charting convention, the gap would be relatively narrow now compared with its historical average.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Just take the ratio of the two ratios, and it will be going down.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: That’s fine for now.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Further questions? I noticed in some of the monthly data that the productivity numbers in the second quarter seem to be coming in under the 2.4 percent Greenbook forecast. Is that your sense? I note that the compensation per hour seems okay, but the figures implicit in the personal income data at least suggest that we would be getting a somewhat higher unit labor cost than is in the Greenbook.

Speaker: MR. WILCOX
Statement: In the Greenbook we had nonfarm business productivity at 2.4 percent. A quick update of that based on the information that we received on Friday and Monday would put it at 1.9 percent.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: So it’s a somewhat higher unit labor cost?

Speaker: MR. WILCOX
Statement: Unit labor costs are up 0.4, right.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Okay. Who would like to start the Committee discussion? President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some positive updates from the Seventh District. The expansion here is broad-based, with only a few sectors experiencing weakness. Several of our contacts have described the business climate as exceptionally strong. One director, who oversees around forty businesses, mentioned that he's never seen so many companies in various industries performing well at once. Temporary-help firms based in our district have reported a classic recovery growth path. The Chicago Purchasing Managers' report, released this morning, shows continued expansion, although there was a significant decline from 68 in May to 56.4 in June.

At our recent automotive outlook symposium, industry experts forecasted that light motor vehicle sales will increase to 17 million units in the second half of this year and maintain that pace in 2005. These numbers are lower than the Greenbook forecast, particularly for 2005. A truck industry analyst predicted that medium- and heavy-duty truck production would rise from about 300,000 in 2003 to 370,000 in 2004 and 460,000 in 2005.

However, we've also heard concerns about emerging inflationary pressures. Many firms are reporting price increases, with euphemisms like "price restoration" and "fuller pricing" being used. Some price increases are due to higher energy costs and strengthening worldwide demand for commodities. While commodity prices have decreased recently, it's too early to tell if they've peaked. Reports suggest bottlenecks in sectors where sales have increased rapidly, such as construction equipment, agricultural equipment, and fiberboard packaging. In some cases, low inventories of raw materials and parts led firms to use higher-cost alternatives like air freight to avoid parts shortages and production delays.

It's unclear whether these price increases will become permanent. Some contacts have accepted price surcharges from their suppliers but are resisting base price increases. They plan to keep pressure on their suppliers and expect surcharges to come off once commodity prices fall. Most contacts report good profit margins, so they may have room to absorb cost increases without raising prices.

Turning to the national outlook, labor markets are firming, and the expansion appears to be on a self-

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some positive news about the economic activity in New England. We've seen a notable uptick in economic growth over the past few months. Employment has grown for three consecutive months, bringing us back to the same level as last year, except in Massachusetts. The unemployment rate in the region is below both its high and the national average.

Consumer confidence has been low recently, possibly due to slower wage gains and spending growth compared to other regions. However, business confidence is high, with solid business spending and strong merchandise exports. The regional stock indexes have outperformed their national counterparts.

Despite these positive signs, there are challenges. Input costs, such as steel, oil, and transportation, have been rising, and labor supply remains a problem, particularly in the hospitality industry due to H-2B visa limits. Small businesses are experiencing these rising costs, and some have even reported a revolt against FedEx and UPS for high delivery costs and service restrictions.

Despite these challenges, even smaller firms report having more pricing power and are able to pass on cost increases. The situation in New England is improving, and we expect it to catch up with the rest of the country this summer or later in the year.

The national economic data also shows solid consumer and business demand, with hiring surpassing expectations. Fiscal policy is likely to remain expansionary this year, but may be less so next year. The forecasts for New England's economy are positive, with solid growth, continued productivity trends, and slowly falling unemployment.

However, there are risks. Inflation data for the first and second quarters shows a significant uptick, and it's uncertain whether this is temporary or not. The risks around the forecast are substantial, and there's a possibility of higher inflation than expected. Therefore, it's important that we start moving the fed funds rate up and get real short-term rates to a positive position, then to a more neutral level. The markets expect this, and if we don't act, the yield curve may steepen with a rise in the inflation premium. We should also avoid making any implied commitments about the pace of our actions going forward and instead focus on what we are doing and the risks involved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to update you on the economic activity in the Third District. We've seen a notable improvement since our last meeting, with solid job growth in all three states. Payroll employment increased by 0.6 percent from the first quarter to the first two months of the second quarter, matching the national average. New Jersey and Delaware have fully recovered from the recession, but Pennsylvania still has some ground to cover.

The unemployment rates in our region have dropped to 5.1 percent in April and May. However, we're hearing reports of labor shortages, which some firms in the Boston area have mentioned. The other sectors in our District are performing well, with retail sales and general merchandise continuing to grow, albeit at a slower pace for autos. Commercial real estate markets remain soft, but residential construction and home sales have been strong.

Manufacturing activity in the region is expanding, with our business outlook survey index of general activity reaching 28.9 percent in June. The more forward-looking indexes of new orders and shipments also improved. However, transportation costs are rising significantly due to fuel surcharges and labor shortages. The prices-paid and prices-received indexes in our manufacturing survey eased a bit in June but remain at nine-year highs.

Nationally, the recovery is now self-sustaining, with production activity expanding, business investment accelerating, and consumer spending rising at a moderate pace. The economy has left the recovery phase and is now in an expansion.

Our forecast differs from the Greenbook's in terms of growth and inflation. We expect a steeper funds rate path, faster growth this year and next, and higher inflation. We believe that extended periods of negative real rates are followed by rapid real growth and rising inflation. However, there are risks to both growth and inflation. On the growth side, we see risks as balanced, with the potential for oil prices to drop or rise. On the inflation side, we need to take action to prevent the recent acceleration in inflation from becoming embedded in inflation expectations.

In conclusion, I recommend that we start the process of putting the funds rate on an upward trajectory to ensure that the recent acceleration in inflation does not become embedded in inflation expectations. We should maintain maximum flexibility with respect to policy going forward and avoid committing to a specific speed of adjustment. Language as policy has

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the business landscape in the Fourth District, which has shown noticeable improvement since our last meeting in May. My contacts are now dealing with higher sales volumes and are responding by installing new equipment, reorganizing processes, and in some cases, adding to payroll. The overall outlook remains positive, but I don't anticipate a significant acceleration in economic activity during the second half of the year.

Productivity growth continues to be a key driver in the manufacturing sector, with many companies implementing lean manufacturing strategies to increase throughput and reduce inventories. This has allowed them to produce more volume with minimal additional employees. CEOs are focused on maintaining productivity gains and are not allowing stronger demand to distract them from their goals.

Despite the strong productivity growth, I expect to see an increase in the equilibrium real rate of interest due to the close correspondence between productivity growth and the rate of return on capital. While I'm less certain about the magnitude and timing of this rise, I believe it's important to address it given the low domestic saving rate in the US.

Regarding inflation concerns, I've spoken with business leaders about the recent upsurge in commodity and consumer prices. While some price increases will persist, others will not. Energy prices have decreased, and some raw material price increases can't be passed along the supply chain. Unit labor costs have not risen, and business contacts do not anticipate any worsening in the inflation climate. I believe we can keep the trend core PCE around 1.5% for the next several years if we get monetary policy back toward neutral within a reasonable timeframe.

In conclusion, I think the risks facing us have shifted toward an environment where inflation could gradually creep up if we're not vigilant. I recommend raising the fed funds rate today and continuing to do so at a pace consistent with getting policy back toward neutral to address these risks. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement:
Chairperson, I'm pleased to report that the Tenth District economy has maintained a robust growth trajectory since our last meeting. The labor market is showing significant improvement, with employment figures in May and June being exceptionally strong and above year-ago levels in all seven states. Manufacturing activity has also rebounded, with production and new orders continuing to increase. Most manufacturers have reported that their capacity utilization rates have returned to pre-recession levels.

Looking ahead, we anticipate a slight cooling off during the second half of the year, but activity is expected to remain historically strong. Year-over-year production and new orders indexes are setting new highs as we move through June. Consumer spending is rising at a moderate pace, with retail sales steadily increasing, and we're optimistic about the summer outlook. Housing activity remains strong, with little impact from the recent mortgage rate increases.

Energy activity has continued to expand due to high oil and gas prices, although labor shortages in the sector have caused some drilling to be constrained. Commercial real estate remains our weak spot.

Regarding inflation, wage pressures remain contained, but manufacturers are raising output prices due to higher energy and raw materials costs. While many expect these costs to moderate, some manufacturers are reporting that strong demand has made it easier for them to pass on price increases.

Turning to the national outlook, the economy is expected to expand at a solid pace, with GDP growth projected to be around 4¾ percent this year and slightly below 4 percent next year. The continued strong growth is largely due to accommodative monetary and fiscal policies and robust global growth.

However, we anticipate a slowdown in growth next year due to a back-off in fiscal policy and a presumed tightening in monetary policy. Core inflation has increased, and while some of the increase can be attributed to transitory factors, there is also an embedded inflationary updrift due to earlier accommodative monetary policy.

Given this, I believe we should consider moving back toward a neutral policy stance more deliberately and systematically to avoid having to raise rates above neutral to contain inflation in the future. We should let the economy define our future policy course rather than our words today.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Shall we break for coffee? See you all shortly.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It took me a while to figure out how to address you! @laugh

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to share some insights about the Twelfth District economy and its alignment with the national economy. In recent months, our district has gained momentum and expanded in line with the nation. Consumer spending has risen significantly, and price discounting for various small retail items has decreased as demand has grown. Housing markets have seen strong price appreciation and sales, although some of this activity may be a response to anticipated mortgage rate increases.

The manufacturing sector is also experiencing a continued general recovery, with rapid growth in exports, particularly to East Asia, leading to strong gains in overall output and sales. Job growth has picked up in the West, especially outside California, which has lagged a bit in recent months. Employers in some areas report finding it harder to fill job openings, particularly in the mountain region, where job vacancy rates have increased significantly over the past year and a half. This has been associated with a pickup in the growth of labor compensation relative to the nation.

However, it's too early to tell whether this recent pickup is transitory or the leading edge of a broader trend. Historically, such divergences have occurred due to regional labor demand shocks and have eventually disappeared as workers moved into the region to fill those jobs. Labor compensation in the West then returned toward the national pace.

Regarding the national economy, our views are in line with the Greenbook. Economic growth in the second half of the year is expected to be reasonably strong, and the pace of growth is likely to exceed potential, bringing the economy gradually back toward full employment. The strong employment growth reported for April and May raises confidence that consumer spending will remain strong enough to support these projections.

However, to keep output from overshooting potential, policy will need to tighten, given its current highly accommodative stance. A path of the funds rate reasonably close to that currently embodied in the fed funds futures is appropriate.

In terms of the inflation outlook, there are substantial risks on both the upside and downside. On the upside, there is a possibility that the role of transitory factors in explaining the recent uptick in core inflation is overestimated, and this could affect future wage bargains. The dollar could fall further, and oil prices could jump or remain elevated. We have also seen a small uptick in compensation growth, although there is still significant labor market slack.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President McTeer.

Speaker: MR. MCTEER
Statement:
During our recent meeting, I'd like to share some insights from the Eleventh District's economic expansion. While it started later and progressed more slowly than the U.S. expansion, recent reports from our directors, Beige Book contacts, and others suggest that the gap is narrowing rapidly. In fact, the tone from our board meetings has become more optimistic in the past few months.

Instead of focusing on the fluctuations of various sectors in our regional economy, I'd like to discuss the emerging mindset of pricing power. One of our Houston-based directors reported that petrochemical operating rates have been increasing significantly, with some product lines nearing capacity limits. He was surprised by the quick turnaround and mentioned that his company had raised prices twice since the last board meeting with minimal resistance. Customers were even expressing surprise that the price increases weren't larger. This trend seems to indicate a growing concern among businesses about the potential underestimation of capacity utilization in the Federal Reserve's data. Many businesses are closely monitoring not only their industries but also their suppliers' capacity utilization.

Another director from the high-tech sector shared the results of a survey of chief information officers. Over the past two years, infrastructure spending, such as maintaining computer networks, remained stable despite economic downturns and accounted for 80 to 90 percent of their budgets. However, as business has improved, they now plan to allocate only 60 percent of their budgets to infrastructure spending in the second half of the year, with the remaining 40 percent going towards new applications and emerging technologies. This shift in attitude is quite bullish, as businesses are focusing more on staying competitive rather than just considering payback periods for investments.

Looking at the national economy, I generally agree with the changes and the overall staff forecast for this year. When I submitted my projections in January, I believed there was a good chance that we would end 2004 with lower inflation than at the beginning of the year. However, recent developments have led me to revise my inflation projections for this year and next year, as anecdotal evidence suggests that capacity constraints are becoming more prevalent.

One of our economists attempted to simulate the Federal Reserve's manufacturing capacity utilization rate based on ISM survey data. While these series and concepts of capacity utilization differ, the simulation suggests that the Fed's measure may

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. The expansion in the Ninth District is broadly based. It has accelerated recently, and we’re hearing scattered reports of labor availability issues in select locations. With the improvement in employment, conversations and concerns about offshore outsourcing have diminished, as one would expect. Among the sectors that are strong are manufacturing, mining, energy, and agriculture. Housing construction remains a source of strength as well, although the inventory of unsold homes appears to be building in some major locations; and nonresidential construction is starting to improve, albeit off a low base. Consumer spending was strong earlier in the spring. Recent reports have been more mixed, and that has been attributed essentially to lousy weather. We will see whether or not that in fact turns out to be the case as we get additional information. As far as prices are concerned, reports are continuing to come in about both current and prospective price increases. Clearly, as I’ve said before, there’s more going on there than I had anticipated earlier. As far as the national economy is concerned, like almost everybody else I think the outlook for growth in the national economy is unquestionably favorable. My own forecast is for modestly higher real growth both this year and next than in the staff forecast. The differences aren’t large. It seems to me that the fundamentals for growth are clearly positive. Maybe equally important or even more so, earlier uncertainties about employment gains have diminished, as have earlier uncertainties about the global recovery. I do expect inflation to remain low, but not as low as I previously anticipated. And I do think there is an obvious danger in attributing the acceleration we’ve seen in inflation to temporary or one-off factors. In any event, given the confidence intervals that were presented in the chart show and in the Greenbook, it doesn’t seem to me to be a stretch to expect core inflation to be 2 percent this year and even a bit higher next year.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent conversations with my contacts at Wal-Mart and other companies. Wal-Mart's June sales came in weaker than anticipated, with sales growth now projected to be between 2-4%. My contact attributes this to cool weather affecting sales of seasonal supplies. Wal-Mart's sales growth forecast is now 3-5%.

A Gallup poll conducted by Wal-Mart revealed that rising gasoline prices are the top concern for consumers, cited by 23% of respondents. Health care, the general economy, and fear of war followed closely behind. Unemployment fears dropped significantly to 13% of respondents, a dramatic change from previous months.

My contact at FedEx reported strong earnings, projecting a 25% increase in fiscal '05 over fiscal '04. They attribute this to gains in volume, pricing, and cost management. Outbound traffic from Asia to the rest of the world is up 18%, and U.S. ground business is up 12%. FedEx is expanding capacity in response to high demand.

My UPS contact described the economy as shifting into higher gear, with outbound Asia volume exceeding expectations. European business is also strong, but U.S. traffic is exceeding expectations as well. The express business is strong, and international capacity is tight. UPS is replacing smaller aircraft with larger ones and is in negotiations with the pilots' union for a new contract.

My J.B. Hunt contact views the economy as warm, with business up and down. Hunt trucking volume in the intermodal business is up 15-17%, possibly due to rail service problems at Union Pacific and CSX. Pricing is strong on the truck side, with a 7% year-over-year increase, and the supply of drivers is extremely tight.

My Wall Street contact believes the general view on Wall Street is that the Fed may have fallen behind the curve and will closely monitor inflation reports. I share his view that there is a solid upward momentum to real activity, but transportation demand has taken many companies by surprise. Inflation risks are skewed to the upside over the next few quarters, regardless of current policy decisions.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to update you on the economic expansion in our Southeast region. The retail sales, autos, housing, tourism, travel, banking, and manufacturing sectors have all shown positive developments. However, I won't delve into each sector's specifics. The main concerns remain in nonresidential construction and chemicals.

Recently, there have been imbalances in certain sectors leading to significant price increases. For instance, steel and cement shortages, along with rising prices in other building materials, are impacting big projects in Florida. Concrete prices have risen by 10 percent, with more increases expected. Developers are reporting higher costs for projects due to these price hikes.

Moreover, a shortage of truck drivers, caused by increased demand and new regulations, is increasing shipping costs and causing some firms to stockpile materials like concrete, steel, plywood, and sheetrock. One Florida contractor even holds a three-month inventory of steel to mitigate future cost increases.

These price increases are being passed on to consumers. Companies in various industries, such as UPS, waste management, concrete, agriculture, trailer manufacturing, auto seat manufacturing, lumber, and numerous small trucking and delivery companies, have reported imposing fuel surcharges without customer complaints. A box manufacturer passed along a 10 percent increase in paper prices with little resistance and plans to raise prices again in September.

Small grocery stores, hotels, and restaurants in Florida, as well as Tennessee hotels and resorts, have raised prices due to strong demand and higher food and energy input prices. Fast food companies are experiencing chicken supply problems due to the trucking shortage, leading some grocery stores to ask their poultry suppliers to "name their price." Finally, many personal service firms, like barbers and spas, have raised prices with little resistance.

However, it's important to note that not all firms are passing on input price increases to consumers. Some are absorbing the costs due to competition, weak demand, or the belief that many of the increases will prove to be transitory.

Despite these concerns, the national economic data has been positive overall, with reasonably strong real GDP growth, benign inflation, and low unemployment. The Greenbook forecast suggests GDP growth in excess of 4 percent in 2004, with inflation stabilizing in 2005 below that of

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement:
At the Fifth District meeting, I'd like to share some insights on the economic recovery. Manufacturing activity has strengthened in May and early June, with expansion continuing, albeit at a slower pace. Shipments and new orders are up, and there are reports of increased export demands from Europe and China for niche technology products. Employment in manufacturing has increased in Maryland, Virginia, and South Carolina, but continues to decline in North Carolina, particularly in textile, apparel, and furniture industries.

Outside of manufacturing, hiring is picking up, and labor markets are showing signs of tightness. Our manufacturing wage diffusion index increased sharply this month. However, the pace of materials price increases eased in May and June, but firms still face higher prices for commodities like steel and concrete.

Retailers report moderately higher revenues, but sales growth in early June has slowed from May's pace. Elsewhere in the service sector, revenue growth has accelerated. Commercial real estate reports a moderate uptick in retail leasing activity, but office and industrial leasing remain sluggish. Residential construction remains strong, and commercial loan demand is increasing.

The national economy's recovery is deepening and broadening, particularly in business spending and hiring. The Greenbook projects real GDP growth at around 5% for the remainder of '04 and slowing to the rate of potential growth thereafter, closing the estimated output gap by early '06.

Headline inflation has been higher than desired, but it's likely to moderate in the near term. The output gap is still significant, and the markup of prices over unit labor costs is above its long-term average. Inflation expectations are reasonably anchored, and year-over-year consumer inflation remains moderate. However, short-term inflation expectations have risen, as have market-based inflation expectations at longer horizons. The market's reaction to Monday's inflation figures suggests continued nervousness about upside inflationary risk.

Overall, we're in a good position coming into this meeting. The labor market recovery is on track, inflation is expected to moderate, and markets anticipate us to raise interest rates at a steady pace. The main challenge for monetary policy in the coming months will be determining the speed at which we need to increase interest rates to maintain price stability.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from our latest economic analysis. The Second District economy appears to be strengthening, with rapid employment growth and strong retail sales. Manufacturers' optimism remains high, and credit demand is increasing. Core inflation in the region has decreased slightly but remains above the national average. Wages are rising to attract skilled workers, and there's pressure to increase compensation.

At the national level, we anticipate continued growth in demand, driven by strong consumer confidence, income growth, and employment gains. Investment is expected to continue growing rapidly, with strong corporate balance sheets, high profit margins, low inventories, and high orders. However, we expect a negative contribution from net exports this year and next.

Our forecast projects real GDP growth of around 4.5% this year and under 4% in 2005. The risks to this forecast are balanced, with the main downside risks coming from a significant slowdown in household spending, a sharp reduction in productivity growth, or a decline in the willingness of foreigners to buy US financial assets.

We're more concerned about inflation, which has accelerated significantly above our comfort level. There's a risk that higher inflation expectations could lead to higher compensation growth. The persistent reports of pricing power and supplier delays suggest a pattern of inflation dynamics that's hard to reconcile with the remaining slack in the economy. We've raised our core PCE inflation forecast to 1.75% for this year and a lower rate for next year.

We believe the risks to this forecast are balanced around a higher path for underlying inflation and a larger bulge in the near term. The uncertainty in the outlook reinforces the need to maintain our commitment to price stability. The costs of being perceived as complacent about inflation are now greater than the alternative.

As we begin to move towards a more neutral fed funds rate, we're encouraged that financial markets seem prepared for this transition. However, we cannot measure or know everything about how the world will react to the initiation of rate increases, and we're likely to encounter some surprises.

We're comfortable with the extent of the tightening that's priced in, but we may need to act more aggressively if circumstances warrant. We should qualify our previous statements and emphasize that we will act as necessary to keep expected inflation low. It would be helpful

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share my perspective on the economic outlook, which aligns with many of the forecasts presented here, but with some adjustments due to recent developments. My projection shows the economy closing the output gap gradually, reaching full utilization towards the end of 2005, while keeping core PCE inflation below 2%. I plan to assume a measured pace of tightening, similar to what's currently in the market.

However, I anticipate slower growth and higher inflation this year compared to my January projection. The primary reasons for this are the increase in energy and other commodity prices, which have dampened growth and raised prices. Core inflation seems to be significantly influenced by these price shocks.

Looking ahead, I expect the effects of these shocks to fade as oil and commodity prices come down, and policy stimulus takes effect. Next year, the erosion of fiscal and financial stimulus should help slow growth, while inflation remains contained due to continuing slack and elevated profit margins.

There are uncertainties regarding the pace of the output gap closure and the sustainability of core inflation below 2%. Recent data suggest contradictory signs, with some indicators pointing to a quick disappearance of slack and others suggesting a slower approach. I believe the risks around both the output gap and inflation are roughly balanced, but we need to closely monitor the inflation data to confirm my expectation of stabilization and moderation of core inflation.

In conclusion, I propose that we communicate a measured pace of policy tightening in our announcement, while acknowledging the need for flexibility to address any unforeseen circumstances. This language has been effective in recent months, and markets have responded appropriately. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share my perspective on the upcoming Fed meeting and the anticipated rate hikes. Based on recent polls and market surveys, it's widely believed that the Fed will start raising rates at this meeting. However, the real concern seems to be about the pace and extent of these rate hikes and whether the Fed is falling behind the curve.

Despite the market chatter, I find it challenging to see substantial evidence supporting this notion. For instance, the Greenbook projects a funds rate increase of 2 percentage points over the next six quarters, which is below the market's expectations and the Fed funds futures curve. This pace leaves the funds rate below equilibrium at the end of 2023. I believe such an approach might be insufficient to prevent inflation from increasing.

However, models are just one perspective. Two potential issues that could disrupt these simulations are the pass-through effect and output gaps. Regarding the pass-through effect, exogenous price increases, such as commodity and oil prices, could lead to inflationary pressures. However, commodity prices have already started decreasing, and oil prices have backed off recently. Moreover, the demand-supply analysis suggests that oil prices and futures prices are as likely to fall as rise.

Another concern is the potential underestimation of output gaps. While the staff believes the current 5.6% employment rate represents full capacity, I argue that it does not. I recall a few years ago when the unemployment rate was at 4%, and there was no significant inflationary pressure. Additionally, employment as measured by the employer survey is still below its peak, labor force participation is low, and the workweek is still low. Wages have increased slightly but have not accelerated, and unit labor costs are still low, though they are picking up. The overall picture does not indicate an overfull macroeconomic capacity, and I still believe the NAIRU is around 5%.

Considering both the pass-through channel and the output channel, the inflation risks seem somewhat balanced to me. As the FOMC has been stating for some time, the statement "The statement has the added advantage that it actually seems to be true." @laugh

As a best guess, I remain comfortable with the strategy of gradually increasing rates starting now. However, we cannot solely rely on best guesses. I shared my concerns about the upside risks last

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some thoughts based on the economic developments during the intermeeting period. I've been taken aback by the contradictory surprises we've encountered. We've discussed the downside surprises, so I'll try to keep it brief. First, real GDP growth was revised down by about half a percentage point. New orders and shipments in May were stronger than expected but weaker than anticipated. Consumer spending was also weaker than expected. Furthermore, the staff has consistently revised down their outlook for both 2004 and 2005 in almost every forecast round.

These downside surprises don't change my belief that the economy is on a self-sustaining growth path. Strong job growth and consumer confidence will continue to support economic momentum. However, the pattern of these surprises leaves me concerned that the economy's path may be shallower than initially thought and potentially below the growth rate of potential.

On the positive side, there have been surprises regarding inflation. While we're still within the zone of price stability, we're at the upper end of it. The causes of these surprises are debatable, with some arguing that we're just dealing with the natural catch-up, while others suggest that the pass-through for oil, commodity, and import prices is higher than historical norms. Some even argue that the commonly held view of inflation dynamics is incorrect.

Inflation is not yet a pressing concern, but we must consider the upside risk. The models we have on price level changes have limited explanatory power, so humility is essential.

Given these uncertainties, the implications for monetary policy are that we should maintain our commitment to our mission while being pragmatic. We should make as few changes as possible to our general message, while holding open the option to adjust our plans as incoming data warrant. As Barry Goldwater once said, "Gradualism in the face of uncertainty is no vice. Undue haste in defense of credibility is no virtue." @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Did he say that? @laugh

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: Forty years ago, I was thirteen! @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective on the current economic situation. I'm encouraged by the broad-based and sustainable growth we're seeing. Capital expenditures, which were a major cause of the last recession, are strong. Employment growth has picked up significantly in the last few months, and manufacturing is showing real strength for the first time in a long while, with rising capacity utilization and employment growth for four consecutive months. Retail sales continue to grow steadily, and inventories are being managed effectively, with inventory-sales ratios at new lows every month.

Corporate profits have increased by over 31 percent in the last year, and forecasts indicate continued growth in 2004. Debt-equity ratios for nonfinancial corporations have improved significantly since their peaks in the third quarter of 2002 and are now at levels last seen in the 1970s. Our recent flow of funds data for 2004:Q1 shows a fourth straight quarter of a surplus funding gap for businesses, indicating that companies have the financial strength to fund expansion internally and access capital markets and financial institutions for favorable terms.

Regarding the profit share of GDP versus GNP, as the Chairman mentioned earlier, I've been tracking this number for some time. The profit share of GDP hit a 75-year high of 8.5 percent in the first quarter, which is about 2.5 points above the long-term average. Historically, profit margins tend to revert to the mean over time. However, analysts forecast that earnings per share for 2005 will show a marked slowdown but still growth. They also assume that companies will have to absorb more costs that are not passed through to customers.

Productivity growth tends to slow as expansions progress, and competition for workers intensifies as strong employment growth continues. Increased compensation to retain the best employees in more competitive labor markets, as well as slower productivity growth, will likely contribute to a larger share of income going to labor. Therefore, I'm less optimistic about labor costs than the staff forecast.

Inflation measures have shown surprising strength lately. While some unusual price jumps in items such as used cars, apparel, or lodging can be identified, the list of exceptions is getting broad enough to question whether pricing power has returned in

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from Larry Meyer's Rules of Forecasting during our discussion today. Rule 1 is to hold onto your forecast as long as possible, Rule 2 is to make a new forecast when the old one becomes untenable, and Rule 3 is knowing when to switch from Rule 1 to Rule 2.

Regarding the real side of the economy, I believe we've made significant progress since our forecasts in June 2003 and January 2004. The economy has shown remarkable improvement, and the Federal Reserve's actions and communication strategy have played a crucial role in supporting the recovery. However, there have been some revisions to the inflation narrative from last year, with some arguing that our concerns with deflation were overstated. I believe it's essential to understand last year's inflation behavior, but it's not directly relevant to the current policy challenge.

The most critical issue is the recent increase in inflation, which has led me to question my previous forecast. While I'm still inclined to believe that inflation will moderate in the second half of the year, I acknowledge that my past forecast misses have taught me a valuable lesson. The recent increase in inflation was driven primarily by nonmarket prices, particularly in the financial services sector. Excluding these prices, the acceleration in core PCE inflation can be explained by higher goods prices, particularly durable goods prices.

The key to moderating inflation is controlling unit labor costs. Labor supply remains elastic, and the recent addition of over a million jobs has barely affected the unemployment rate. Wage trends are more challenging to assess, with total compensation increasing at a faster rate than expected, but average hourly earnings growing more slowly. The true marginal cost of labor is likely somewhere between these two figures.

Given the uncertainties surrounding inflation and output forecasts, I suggest we adopt a cautious approach. We should embark on a program of gradual rate increases but remain flexible and ready to adjust in response to new information. Regarding communication, I believe it's essential to be mindful of the market's expectations and avoid destabilizing them unnecessarily. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent meetings with three banks: a New York money center bank, a Southeastern regional bank, and a national bank with a Midwest base and a twenty-four-state footprint. I've come away with two key takeaways.

First, asset quality has been a standout. Two out of three bankers used phrases like "the best ever" and "getting better." They believe asset quality is stronger than suggested in Steve's chart, even surpassing what could be achieved through good credit standards alone. They attribute this to management discipline, which aligns with the discipline Sandy Pianalto mentioned, and which we've seen across the business community.

Second, lending activity has been slow, but there's a shift happening. Bankers' expectations have caught up with our forecast for a recovery. While commercial real estate is the only significant lending activity to date, this is mainly driven by small regionals and large community banks that rely on interest rate spreads for profitability. C&I lending remains soft, but business confidence has fully recovered, and the partial expensing will provide an additional incentive for the second half of the year. Based on my contacts in the banking industry, I anticipate increased activity from the sector in the second half of 2004.

Our communications have signaled a sequence of how we expected the recovery to unfold. We anticipated inflation would be quiescent for a considerable period, followed by caution and patience in removing policy accommodation. Now, it seems we've reached the point where we can begin the process of raising rates at a measured pace. I see little reason to change our anticipated sequence of events.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Mr. Reinhart.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: [ERROR]

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: [ERROR]

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Do you want to start a go-around on this issue?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: No, we don’t need a go-around on this. If you have an objection to the statement, please indicate it.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: I guess I can accept this statement. I must say that I’ve been sitting on a very pointed fence for a long time on this issue about language. Would it be reasonable to ask Vincent to circulate in advance suggested language for our August meeting—to give us some idea about what we are going to do about this so that we can think about it ahead of time? I think there’s a sense around the table—certainly I feel this way—that we’ve got to get out from under this language somehow. And I think we need to spend some time on this.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think that’s an excellent suggestion, and there’s no reason that it can’t be done. Vincent could give alternate suggestions. Even though we have six weeks to do that, there’s no reason that we can’t send out early versions of different possibilities if we decide to drop the last two sentences.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I would be tilling some of the ground of the two prior Ferguson working groups, but I can give you alternative wording on different rationales that the Committee considered in its previous discussions of communication policy.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: If we adopt the proposed statement, I think it would also be appropriate— perhaps in the monetary policy hearing, Mr. Chairman—for you to in some way emphasize this last sentence that has been added to the statement. I don’t know how to do that exactly. But I just worry that an implied commitment to the market of moving 25 basis points per meeting could leave us with continuous declines in the real federal funds rate.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: This does not say that; it doesn’t make a commitment. If you read it very closely, it says that we are forecasting that our moves are likely to be measured but that we will act on the basis of the evidence.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: All I’m saying is that I think the way this plays out is going to be influenced by the degree of emphasis placed on the one sentence rather than the other sentence.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I agree with that.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: I hope that we can put a lot of emphasis on the importance of the data in driving what we do and try to back off from that implied commitment.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: That is clearly my inclination. Remember, we started with “considerable period,” then we went to “patience,” and now we’re coming to various forms of unwinding the whole process.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: This is sort of the great-grandchild of “considerable period!” @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It has turned out better than we had any reason to anticipate. And I think we need one more turn of the screw to go back to our old-fashioned balance of risk statement. But I don’t think it has to be done today. We don’t need it. Are there any further comments? Yes.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: I have a question about one particular phrase. We have in this statement “hiring has picked up.” On consideration, I’m worried about how that will be interpreted in the event that we get something like a month with 200,000 in job gains.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: But hiring has picked up.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: But if we see a month with 200,000 of job creation people may say, well, hiring is not picking up. So I was thinking about something more generic like “labor market conditions have improved.” That would be more expansive and therefore less susceptible to second-guessing. It’s just a question. I think both characterizations are correct, but one is more ambiguous in the fuller sense.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I have no objection to that language if people would prefer that. Is that satisfactory to everybody? Let’s make that change.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: What were the words—“labor market conditions have improved”?

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: “Labor market conditions have improved.” It’s a bit more ambiguous, so we don’t have people overreacting to an individual labor number.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think that’s a useful addition. Yes?

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I feel very much the same as President Poole does about this. I thought we should take the opportunity now that we’re increasing rates to go back to the old formulation. I probably should have responded to the Bluebook sooner with some alternative language, which I worked on a little. I can go along with what you are suggesting. I take your point that things have gone pretty well with this whole process. But I think there’s going to be some scrutiny of the fact that we’re saying “measured” and then trying to take it away in the next sentence.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Actually, I started that process.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I know you did.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I don’t think we got a negative reaction to that.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Maybe, maybe not. I’m not sure I agree with that. But that’s your interpretation, and I’m willing to go with it. Nevertheless, I do think it would be good for Vincent to figure out some ways in which we might say what we did and why we did it and then characterize the risks in a simple fashion the way we used to. I hope we’ll go back to statements that are a little shorter and more formulaic and stop trying to create great grandchildren.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: That’s where we’re headed. Any further comments? Yes.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: We may be headed there, but remember this: When we had all of these working groups that Roger chaired regarding language to describe the balance of risks, we had very difficult problems shaping that into clear communication. First, we have an issue that we’ll never resolve on exactly what we mean by the output gap. And when we’re talking about inflation risk, what are we assuming about rates? Are we assuming that the funds rate equilibrium is 1 percent or 2 percent or what? We had horrible problems. I think we got into this process partly because the balance of risk language didn’t really do the job. And I actually think this statement is clear.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, let’s put it this way. We can drop those two sentences and put in additional sentences. I think there’s general agreement within the Committee as to where we want to go and it’s merely a question of finding the appropriate language. So far, this innovation of putting it in the Bluebook I think has worked surprisingly well.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: I’m just trying to caution everyone not to think that the balance of risks formulation is going to be a panacea because it really wasn’t before and I don’t think it will be now.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, that’s something we are about to find out. If there are no further questions and this is acceptable, would you kindly read the appropriate language?

Speaker: MR. BERNARD
Statement: I’ll begin with the directive itself, which is on page 14 of the Bluebook: “The Federal Open Market Committee seeks monetary and financial conditions that will foster price stability and promote sustainable growth in output. To further its long-run objectives, the Committee in the immediate future seeks conditions in reserve markets consistent with increasing the federal funds rate to an average of around 1¼ percent.” Then with regard to the portion of the statement in the press release that is part of the vote, which is the third paragraph, the wording is: “The Committee perceives the upside and downside risks to the attainment of both sustainable growth and price stability for the next few quarters are roughly equal. With underlying inflation still expected to be relatively low, the Committee believes that policy accommodation can be removed at a pace that is likely to be measured. Nonetheless, the Committee will respond to changes in economic prospects as needed to fulfill its obligations to maintain price stability.”

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The last item on our agenda is to confirm that the date of our next FOMC meeting is August 10. Before we adjourn, I’d like to request that the members of the Board of Governors adjourn to my office to address the issue of the discount rate. Today we have an extraordinary situation in that all twelve Reserve Banks are requesting an increase in the discount rate.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Good morning, everyone. Would somebody like to move approval of the minutes of June 29 and 30?

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection. Will somebody move the election of Scott Alvarez to serve as General Counsel until the election of a successor at the first meeting of the Committee after December 31, 2004?

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Is there a second?

Speaker: SPEAKER

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the economic outlook and market trends based on the charts circulated by Carol Low. During the intermeeting period, economic growth forecasts were revised down due to rising oil prices, leading to expectations of a more gradual trajectory for short-term rates, lower bond yields, and lower stock prices.

The top panel shows the three-month deposit rate (black line) and the three-month deposit rates three, six, and nine months forward in dashed red lines. The three-month cash deposit rate had been rising but forward rates traded in a range and then declined sharply on Friday due to the employment report. The six- and nine-month forward rates have both declined about 50 basis points since late July.

The middle panels display the yields on two- and ten-year Treasuries, which both fell in recent weeks and especially on Friday. The two-year yield is about 40 basis points lower than at the last meeting, and the ten-year yield is about 60 basis points lower than in early June. With yields at these levels, there's talk of a mortgage-induced wave of buying that could push yields even lower.

The yield curve has continued to flatten, as shown in the bottom panel. Bond traders had been worried about a wave of selling by MBS investors facing extension risk, but those worries have reversed with the move down in yields. The thirty-year MBS index's duration has narrowed sharply since early June and now stands at just above 3½ years, with a six-month contraction on Friday alone.

MBS-related buying in recent days is difficult to track, but a further move in the MBS yield toward 4 percent could trigger hedge-related buying by accounts positioning for a new wave of refinancing. The spread between MBS and Treasuries has narrowed from 55 basis points in early June to about 40 basis points. Part of this outperformance by MBS is due to a reach for yield in an environment where the market believed it had comfort about the pace of tightening going forward.

Investment-grade corporate debt spreads were little changed and appeared nonplussed by the June tightening, while swap spreads were also little changed. The high-yield market performed well in the aftermath of the tightening but widened more recently as the

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Is it possible that the presumption in the market that the federal funds rate will follow a certain pattern—prior, let’s say, to the market’s move last Friday after the employment report—has created an anchor to the structure of rates that, by its very nature, delimits the extent of volatility? That could be an explanation of the low volatility. The issue really gets down to whether the market believes that is exactly what is going to happen and it is taking that presumed pattern for the funds rate as a given. And they believe that because we’re telling them that. That automatically will lower the volatility, which raises another obvious problem—that the system is less resilient to shocks from unexpected events. That makes me a little nervous if that is indeed the explanation. Second, on the issue of potential endeavors to adjust duration in the mortgage market, I have the impression that the last big waves of refinancing took out a very big chunk of moderate interest coupon mortgages. While it may very well be true that, when interest rates fall or duration falls back, there would be inclinations to hedge—essentially buy fixed-rate mortgages. But if you clean out the whole base, there’s nothing left. There’s a very delimited amount of potential hedging activity that becomes required, except for the idiosyncratic shift in duration that has nothing to do with refinancings. This is not a convexity issue so much; it’s more an endeavor to judge where there is potential for refinancing. Do we have a judgment on this?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Let me answer that in two ways. First, guessing where convexity-related hedging might kick in is as much an art as it is science. What some of the dealers are talking about is that at a rate of about 4 percent we might see some kind of buying or hedging kick in. On the second aspect—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The answer is that that’s likely. Obviously, if the duration is falling, investors would be getting increasingly uncomfortable. But that’s not where the big change is obviously.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: No. On the second point of where the rate might need to be to trigger a refinancing wave, the universe is pretty limited, as you say. In the spring, when the ten-year rate got to about 3.9 percent, a good deal of refinancing occurred. So, really, the candidates for refinancing are the mortgages that were originated over the last few months at higher rates; those now might become attractive candidates for refinancing. As for how many of those there are outstanding, I don’t have the data here, but I agree with you that it’s probably not a huge number.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Those are the only candidates.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Yes, exactly. And the number is probably not huge; it’s nowhere near as large as what we saw, say, in late ’02 and the spring of ’03.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Home purchase originations have been high, but they haven’t been high long enough to make a big difference.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Again, might it have some effect? Yes. Would it have the same kind of effect that we saw in those earlier periods? Probably not.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Another way of putting it, Mr. Chairman, is that the wave of refinancing has made the distribution of mortgages more concentrated, and it isn’t until rates get down to the levels they were a year or so ago that we would really hit the mass of that distribution. While it is true that the amount of new financing in the last year hasn’t been all that great, a lot of it has been in ARMs. And if rates were to decline some, we might see refinancing out of variable-rate and into fixed-rate products. So, there is some scope for refinancing. With regard to the first point you made about the anchoring of the term structure made possible by the Committee’s measured pace of policy action, I’d note—and this is something you’ve noted in the past—that the term structure is also anchored at the long end. The ten-year- ahead one-year-forward rate has moved in a very narrow range, and that has very powerfully lowered the volatility of the longer-term instruments that Dino showed. I think we have other manifestations of a sense of lower volatility—perhaps also bearing on risk aversion. The equity premium is in the middle of its historical range, and risk-neutral bond spreads also seem to be in their typical range. And while volatility is low, the market reaction to news has been rather high. Just think of what happened in response to the employment reports and the CPI.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: That’s true. You know, on the chart where Dino has those red dots in the regression, the latest observation was right on the new curve.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: It really does look as if that line has rotated. So although volatilities are low, that isn’t preventing the market from repricing when news comes out. That may say something about market functioning, and that would be a more favorable interpretation than the lesson you took away.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Also it’s a difference between historical observed volatility and the implied volatility.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: On this same subject though, Vincent, isn’t it true that the distribution, derived from options, about the expectations for the fed funds path doesn’t appear to have narrowed substantially? There’s still a fairly broad distribution in expectations, a fair amount of uncertainty still priced in around the path. I don’t think it’s true, just looking at how that’s changed over the past nine months or so, that the band of uncertainty is narrower.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: But the slope going forward has not changed.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: No, that has not changed. I’m just talking about whatever concerns some people here may have about market participants displaying excessive reassurance. If market participants feel reassured, it hasn’t produced substantially more confidence around that band. I think that, combined with the fact that expectations are moving around in response to the data is, again, good countervailing—

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: Mr. Chairman? In fact, there’s a chart in the Bluebook that shows that uncertainty about near-term policy has risen over the last few months. It has not risen a lot, and the uncertainty is still pretty low, I think, but chart 1 shows that it has gone up; it hasn’t gone down.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: An interesting point is that the implied volatility on the S&P 500 is really startling. Whenever we see things of that nature, we say, well, what goes down must go up—the inverse law of gravity.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: But that’s much more pronounced in the sustained interest rate—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The interest rate is essentially anchoring the equity premium. What we’re really saying is that there can be an element of too much good news. I think that’s where we have to be careful. Further questions for Dino? Would somebody like to move approval of the Desk’s transactions?

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection, they are approved. We’re now at the staff reports. Dave Wilcox and Karen Johnson.

Speaker: MR. WILCOX
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to begin by acknowledging the significant shifts that have taken place since our June and August Greenbook forecasts. In the June Greenbook, we anticipated payroll employment gains of around 300,000 per month in the second and third quarters, with GDP growth averaging approximately 5 percent at an annual rate. By the end of the year, we expected the GDP gap to narrow to just ½ percent. However, by the time we published the August Greenbook last week, we had revised our growth assessment and projected a full 1 percent gap at the end of the year.

Despite these changes, we still believe that overall macroeconomic conditions will support a return to above-trend growth. Although we have revised our payroll employment forecasts for August and September, we still anticipate gains of around 300,000 in the fourth quarter. We have also trimmed our forecast for third-quarter GDP growth by only 0.6 percentage points, from 3.9 percent to 3.3 percent, due to the deficit in hours relative to our expectation.
