One positive note from the latest employment report was the performance of the manufacturing sector, which showed signs of improvement. This sector has historically been a reliable bellwether of cyclical conditions, and its resilience in the face of recent economic challenges is encouraging.

Our inflation outlook remains relatively unchanged since the June Greenbook. The most recent CPI report indicates that the desired moderation in inflation is underway, leading us to revise our core CPI inflation projection for both this year and next by a tenth. However, we have not revised our forecast for core PCE inflation due to the BEA's faster pace of inflation in non-market-based prices.

Despite these reassuring signs, we are still grappling with the question of what is driving the recent economic weakness. Our large-scale econometric model, while valuable, does not fully explain the current situation. We are considering several hypotheses, including the possibility of temporary noise in the data, the impact of higher energy prices, and the potential for faltering fiscal stimulus.

In conclusion, while some aspects of our economic outlook have changed, others have remained constant. We will continue to monitor the situation closely and provide updates as new information becomes available. Karen will now continue our

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement:
During our meeting today, David brought up some concerns regarding the U.S. economic activity in the second and third quarters, which appears to be less robust than initially anticipated. Given the importance of U.S. demand for many regions of the global economy, we need to consider whether we should revise our forecast for foreign economic growth. However, the data we currently have does not suggest any major revisions, and our projection for foreign economic growth remains similar to that in the June Greenbook. However, there is a risk of some spillover from the softness in U.S. activity.

We were pleasantly surprised by the real growth of U.S. exports in the second quarter, which came in much stronger than expected. Exports of real goods and services expanded almost 12½ percent at an annual rate, a full 5 percentage points higher than our previous forecast. The nominal data shows strength across most categories, particularly capital goods and industrial supplies. However, there is weakness in economic activity in China, with nominal exports to China and Hong Kong declining from the first quarter.

We expect real GDP growth abroad, particularly in the major foreign industrial countries, to moderate on average over the remainder of this year. This means that real exports will decelerate some through next year. However, our story is less satisfactory with respect to real imports of goods and services. Although U.S. real GDP growth for the second quarter came in below our expectations, real imports of goods and services also surprised us on the upside. We now expect real import growth to slow some in the second half of this year, to an annual rate of 9 percent, about ¾ percentage point weaker than the figure in the current Greenbook. For 2005, we expect further deceleration in imports, to an annual growth rate a bit less than 8 percent.

Another major global economic development over the intermeeting period was a further rise in oil prices. We had expected the spot price of WTI to begin moving down, but since June 29, the spot price has moved up nearly $9 per barrel, to close above $44 yesterday. Our forecast for the average spot price of WTI this quarter has been revised up $5 per barrel. Although we and the futures markets still expect the price of oil to move down over the forecast period, our projected path shows a decline of only $4 per barrel

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: In evaluating the oil price impact in the United States, we tend to look at the issue from the crude price; essentially a change in the value of crude imports is viewed as a tax effect. But I wonder if there is not something a little different going on at the moment, or at least through the spring, with the very significant rise in refinery and marketing margins. That drove the gasoline price up the equivalent of an additional $5 per barrel on the price of oil. The reason we talk in terms of net imports only is the presumption that domestic energy earnings are recycled with a propensity to spend not significantly different from consumer spending. But that clearly has not been the case here because, despite the huge rise in refinery marketing margins during the second quarter and indeed partly through the first quarter, there has been no evident pickup in capital investment on the part of oil companies based in the United States. So in a sense, the tax so far as households are concerned—or if you want to put it this way, the propensity to save—has risen. And hence the multipliers have changed. I wonder whether that doesn’t put a slightly different timing configuration on this because in the last several weeks we’ve observed that, as crude prices have risen, gasoline prices have come down. Essentially that gap has now been restored to where it was. If that is indeed the case, the impact of the oil effect on the third quarter is not going to parallel the actual change in the price of crude. But how much different it will be is hard to judge. Also, with respect to the question of the large inventory accumulation going on worldwide, in one sense, I guess we don’t really care whether there is excess unused capacity or capacity used to increase inventories above normal. We add the two of them; it’s the combination of the two that’s really involved here.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: But it matters if demand is being underestimated.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes. And that’s where this missing barrel question comes from. It seems extraordinarily odd that after remarkably flat world consumption—at a little over 80 million barrels a day for a long period—suddenly demand has just taken off. We didn’t see that during the 1990s, as I recall.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: That is where all the anecdotes about China come in, I think. Our information about what is really going on in China is poor, at best. We know, for example, that the electricity grid in that country is not up to the task of meeting current demands, and China’s economy is growing rapidly. And we know that, in response to that, many manufacturers or other producer units—or for that matter households, for all we know—have purchased generators. But the use of numerous small generators burning kerosene or heavy fuel oil or whatever they burn is a very inefficient way to supply electricity. Their use is lowering, if you will, the average efficiency of producing electricity to support economic growth in China, perhaps in an important way.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Hasn’t their oil consumption gone from 4 million to 6 million barrels a day in a couple of years, or something like that?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: The story, though, is that to build capacity you need to stock it with an original endowment and some of that is happening.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It’s the in-process inventories that are building up. If that is indeed the case, then it’s not consumption.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Right, but we don’t know. All these anecdotes are out there, but no one has real data.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: In large measure they don’t have room to store more oil. In fact, they’re behind schedule on building their strategic reserve because they don’t have storage capacity. Where is it all going? Maybe there’s a big hole in the ground that they’re filling in.

Speaker: MR. WILCOX
Statement: Mr. Chairman, another uncertainty that I’d highlight with respect to the timing of how this plays out is that the sources of the increase in the price of oil may have a different profile recently than was the case over the first half of the year. My sense qualitatively is that strong world demand was perhaps more a front-loaded story in the first part of the year and that supply concerns maybe have emerged as an issue later.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Well, certainly in the intermeeting period, not only the spot price but I think the farther-out futures have reacted enormously to the Yukos story, which is not really about production today. Actually, it might be about production today because overnight the Russian authorities seized control of a big production unit of Yukos, which only added credence to the view that the Russian government was trying to re-establish control over those assets or wanted to gain some perceived political benefit by selling them at below true value to favored purchasers.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Is there any credible scenario in which the Russians would allow that oil production to decline at these prices?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: It is hard to imagine that they would allow that to happen for any period of time, but I’m sufficiently skeptical about their business competence that I wouldn’t assume that they can do all this shifting and achieve their political aims and not disrupt supply temporarily. But there is certainly no incentive for them to cut back production.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think they have an incentive to make believe they are cutting back to get the price up but to keep selling the oil.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: To exert some leverage over the rest of the oil producers or for a host of reasons.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. WILCOX
Statement: May I just finish the thought? The consequences for U.S. activity could be quite different, depending on the mix of supply and demand factors. If that mix changed over the first and second halves of the year, it could add to the uncertainty about the timing that you were highlighting in your comment, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think that’s a good point to make.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: I also wanted to follow on this discussion about oil prices. It seems that every time I’ve looked at this futures market for oil—and I don’t look at it on a regular basis—when the spot price is in the 30s or 40s, the price of oil is always shown as coming down in the future. Now it is projected to be coming down, but not by very much—only about $4 or $5 a barrel—by the end of 2005. I was just wondering if you view this market now as typical. Does it look the way it always does, or has it changed because of terrorism in Russia and all the other things that are going on? And does it change at different levels, as the level of the spot price varies?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Well, you’re asking someone whose expertise in this matter is more limited than I wish it were. It’s normal for this curve to display a downward slope, but it’s not always the case that it does. There have been times when the front portion of the futures curve has sloped up; that would not be considered an unprecedented event by any means. Oil is costly to store. So that figures into the value of buying now to meet a future demand versus buying it in the future. I’ve expressed to the people who study this market in great detail my frustration about the fact that this curve slopes down all the time and rides up to the actual spot price. I’ve asked why it is that the people in the futures market always continue to believe that the oil price is going to start coming down “tomorrow” despite six months’ worth of evidence that that hasn’t happened. Those who analyze the oil market confront me with this supply and demand story: They say that as best they—and I assume all the people trading in the real markets—can tell, the data they have available suggest that the current supply exceeds consumption. Now, we may someday learn that these data were just wholly inaccurate, and that may explain what was misleading all of us. But the people trading in the futures markets have the same data, and they, too, believe that current production exceeds consumption by a comfortable margin. So they are always expecting that supply–demand imbalance to show through to the oil prices. Now, these people have become a bit battle scarred. And the curve, at least to my eyes, has flattened a little from some of its previous shapes. I think that’s because, even though the production data, taken literally, suggest that production exceeds global consumption, people are pricing in either some expectation of a Russian foul-up of one sort or another, or a terrorist event, or the Venezuelan situation getting ugly, or something of that sort. So either they are attaching some probability to supply being below global capacity in the future, or they have come to believe that the data aren’t entirely reliable. Something, however, has caused them to bid up the far futures more than a straight reading of what the market’s knowledge of demand and supply would create but not so much as to take away completely this notion that inventories must be building somewhat and that it’s going to show through to prices at some point.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: There’s an interesting history here. Incidentally, there have been significant periods of a so-called contango curve.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Pickup, yes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to discuss some interesting observations about the oil market, specifically regarding the price trends over the past few decades. In 1985, the oil price curve had a noticeable upward slope. However, around three to four years ago, something unusual occurred. Prior to this period, six- to seven-year futures were clustered around $18 to $20 a barrel, which was considered the long-term marginal supply price outside of OPEC. These futures remained in a tight range as the spot price fluctuated significantly.

However, a few years ago, the long-term futures price began to rise and is now at $30 a barrel. Some argue that this shift was due to the deteriorating assumption of OPEC crude supply security, starting with the Intifada and the Middle East issues. As a result, noncommercial interests, such as investors and speculators, saw an opportunity to buy long-term futures to secure oil inventories.

Commercial interests, who own the physical barrels, are the only ones who can sell to these noncommercial interests. By selling net claims to them, commercial interests reduce their supply, effectively increasing demand. This trend can be seen in the Commodity Futures Trading Commission's (CFTC) net position on the New York Mercantile Exchange (NYMEX). The noncommercial interest is now net long, meaning they have been buying for some time and have been increasing their position.

This presents an intriguing question: what would happen if 4 or 5 million barrels a day were knocked out of Saudi crude supply due to an uprising or similar event? The premium for this risk is already in the market, and it's unclear how quickly it will disappear if enough inventories are not accumulated worldwide to meet both commercial and speculative interests' needs.

At some point, these speculative buyers will have enough oil, at which time they will stop their hedge buying. This could potentially lead to a decrease in the oil price.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Or it will come down just with the passage of time. If among those purchasers are people who envision some reason for using oil, they don’t need to buy in the spot market because they will have already bought forward. If they are just speculating, as it gets close to the time when they’re going to get delivery of this oil that they don’t really want, they’re going to sell it spot. Ultimately, it’s only the people who actually consume the oil who can sustain the price. That’s what makes this global demand story have to be the context in which this trading is taking place.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think the best way of visualizing it is this: When what I’d call the OPEC premium rises, the underlying ex-ante demand to hold inventories worldwide rises. If supply exceeds consumption, inventories will rise to the level at which that demand is sated, and at some point it will be sated. The only issue is, Where is that number?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: And somewhere out there are alternative energy sources—tar sands, other kinds of materials, or whatever. They often used to be cited as putting a limit on how high the oil price could be.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: If oil goes to $80 a barrel you’ll be surprised at what—

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Even ethanol looks good.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I guess the staff’s estimates are that the supply of oil is close to unitary elasticity over the long run in the United States and that it’s very inelastic in the short run. But with enough time, we just price ourselves out of the market.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: But if one imagines that the citizens of India and China, which would total about 3 billion people, raise their per capita consumption of energy to, say, half the average of that in industrial countries—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: That’s a very important point.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: That would result in an altogether different picture of the long-term energy supply–demand balance in the world. And the effect of that has to feed back at some point. Now, if that were really what was driving everything, one might think that investment in the energy sector would just be taking off, and I don’t see that either. And part of it is because this miserable curve slopes down all the time. In the short run it always seems as if the price of oil is going to fall any day and, therefore, that such investment is not really going to pay off. There’s a certain inconsistency in that, I realize.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: I don’t want to prolong this discussion too much. The Chairman made the point I wanted to make—that how the marginal cost of energy is determined has moved from a secure low-risk environment to a very high-risk environment. One of the things I think we’re seeing with the news regarding Yukos and other developments that are moving the market around is that many people in the market—in a situation where I believe supply and demand are actually pretty closely in balance now—are looking to Russia or Venezuela or other places as the marginal producers and no longer to Saudi Arabia, for example. The latter always has a lower cost of production but a higher risk profile. Is that a possible explanation?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: It’s certainly true that the role of Saudi Arabia as the swing producer was a deliberate and explicit policy adopted by the Saudis. They would restrict production or they would expand production in an attempt to set a sort of horizontal supply curve for oil at a specified price. That has just broken down. It has broken down for a variety of reasons. What I think we’re seeing is the transition to some new structural pattern in global oil and related sources of energy that hasn’t really settled out yet. And it’s not clear exactly how it is going to settle out.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Further questions for our colleagues? If not, who would like to start the Committee discussion? President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. The two big questions facing us are whether the recent soft patch in economic activity is temporary and to what degree underlying inflation has picked up. On the first question, we get a sense from our Seventh District contacts that their business is good but apparently not robust enough to generate substantial hiring. We have received mixed reports on employment. Our contacts at temporary-help firms say that they are placing temporary workers across a wide range of industries. In addition, they are increasing their placement of workers in permanent jobs. Some of our more general business contacts even report scattered labor shortages in a few specialized areas. Nevertheless, few say that they are permanently increasing their head counts even though business is pretty good. In terms of spending, some sectors that had weak activity in June showed improvement in July. In particular, retailers told us that they saw something of a bounceback; and looking ahead, merchants sound quite bullish about the upcoming holiday season and are planning to build their inventories accordingly. As you know, light vehicle sales also bounced back in July. So far, reports suggest that sales in the first week of August continued to be strong. One manufacturer even told us that they are looking for sales in the month to be about 18½ million units.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Excuse me—was that for light vehicle or total sales?

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to share some insights I've gathered from our district contacts regarding the current economic situation. The automotive industry is facing a challenge to clear out their '04 model year cars before the arrival of '05 models. This means they'll need strong sales in August and September or risk reducing production schedules. However, other sectors, such as construction, agriculture, mining, IT equipment, and advertising, have remained strong with new orders continuing to come in.

Air travel has also seen an uptick during the summer, with solid bookings for the fall. In contrast, there are concerns about weakness in the IT services sector.

Regarding inflation, our contacts are reporting cost pressures but no significant pass-through to prices yet. Wages remain a concern due to rising healthcare costs, and material inputs like construction materials, steel, and energy continue to be elevated. These costs are expected to ease, but the resolution is taking longer than anticipated.

Supply chain bottlenecks, particularly in transportation, are also causing concerns. There's a risk that these could persist and cause problems during the peak fall shipping season. Despite these cost pressures, there are limited reports of price increases downstream.

Looking at the national outlook, we believe the economy's soft patch is temporary. The July employment report is concerning, but it doesn't change our assessment. One possible risk suggested by the recent payroll data is a return to last year's environment of uncertainty holding back hiring despite continued growth. However, our business contacts have not explicitly mentioned increased uncertainty as a reason for holding back hiring.

The fundamentals of the economy remain strong, with real income growth maintained, underlying productivity growth, and accommodative monetary policy. We are, however, concerned about persistently high oil prices, which are driven by geopolitical concerns and act as a wild card for both growth and inflation. Core inflation remains contained, and at this time, continuing to remove policy accommodation at a measured pace seems appropriate.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Almost all my contacts said that they had nothing new to report; essentially the story was the same as last time. I would mention two things that are perhaps a little different. My Wal-Mart contact said that the pace of sales in August has definitely downshifted. The Wal-Mart public story is an expectation of 2 to 4 percent growth in same-store sales, but apparently internal estimates are much closer to 2 percent than 4 percent. Wal-Mart officials were very reluctant to revise their estimates publicly to 1 to 3 percent, which is what they might otherwise do, because they thought that might give too pessimistic a picture. They are expecting some pickup in September and October, more to the 3 to 5 percent growth range. Several of my contacts spoke about the rail congestion. There seems to be a real bottleneck in U.S. railroads these days, and the congestion has actually reduced capacity. Because the average train speed is down, that means that the capacity to move freight is down. My contacts expect the problem to continue through this year because, of course, as we move into the fall more goods are moving on the rails. So the situation really won’t get straightened out until the early part of next year. I think the staff outlook for the economy makes good sense; I agree that interpreting current developments as temporary is right. As for the employment report, the back-of-the-envelope calculations I’ve done on the seasonal factors suggest that, if we had used last year’s seasonals, the number of jobs created in July would have been 80,000 higher than reported. I’ll just throw that out as something to think about. Obviously, that doesn’t fundamentally change the picture, but if we had gotten 110,000 new jobs instead of 32,000, the picture would have looked a little different. That’s all I want to report. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent conversations with businesses in the Fourth District. While the economic activity has been solid, it's been slower than anticipated this summer. Business leaders I've spoken with remain optimistic about growth for the rest of the year, but at a slower pace than expected.

Previously, I believed that my district was lagging behind the rest of the country. However, recent national economic statistics and Beige Book reports suggest that the slowdown is more widespread. Confidence in the economy, which was growing in June, has waned somewhat, even among successful businesses. One global manufacturing company, for instance, is experiencing peak order levels, but the executive I spoke with expressed concern that this pace won't last.

The labor market is another area of concern. While wage pressures aren't hindering hiring, rising healthcare costs are. Companies are becoming increasingly aware of the impact of labor costs on their bottom line. Productivity gains are also a factor in the weak demand for labor.

Inflation is a complex issue. While the market-based core PCE price index shows a moderate increase, people are concerned about the large headline CPI numbers. Some businesses report that previous spikes in commodity and energy prices haven't been fully passed through to final goods. However, I haven't heard anything to suggest substantial inflationary pressures are building across a range of final goods prices.

I still believe that dominant economic forces are pushing towards a higher equilibrium real rate of interest, and monetary policy should adjust accordingly. However, recent developments have me questioning whether the economy might be growing more slowly than anticipated. If that's the case, today's fed funds rate might be closer to neutral. I'd like to maintain flexibility in our policy decisions to respond to changing economic circumstances.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some positive news about economic activity in the Third District. Contrary to the nation and my neighboring regions, our economic expansion is picking up a bit more now than it was at the beginning of the year. Job growth in our area had been lagging behind the nation, but it has strengthened in recent months, with all three states experiencing employment gains. New Jersey, in particular, has seen the most significant growth, especially in the southern part of the state, where payroll employment is now above its pre-recession levels.

Delaware and Pennsylvania, however, still have employment levels that are 1 percent and 1.25 percent below their pre-recession levels, respectively. The tri-state unemployment rate edged down to 5.1 percent in the second quarter, and we've received reports that some firms are struggling to find qualified workers.

Our business outlook surveys suggest that wage increases are becoming more common as firms compete for workers. Thirty-eight percent of manufacturers and 50 percent of South Jersey firms reported raising wages in the past three months. Among those who raised wages, the most common increase was in the 2-4 percent range. These findings are consistent with the BLS data indicating that wages and salaries in our region have been growing faster than the nation as a whole.

Retail sales moderated a bit in June but rebounded in July, and retailers expect strong back-to-school sales. Commercial real estate markets remain soft, but residential construction activity remains high. Manufacturing activity continues to expand and has outperformed the nation in recent months. Our business outlook survey index of general activity rose to 36.1 in July, and the more forward-looking indexes on new orders and shipments improved considerably. Service-sector activity has also strengthened, with professional and business services experiencing the fastest growth among service-sector industries.

We continue to see rising prices in our region, but the pace of increase has slowed slightly. The prices-paid index in our manufacturing survey declined in the past two months but remains at a high level. Firms seem better able to pass on some of the price increases to their customers, as evidenced by the fifteen-year high in the prices-received index in July.

In summary, the outlook for our region remains positive, with our leading indicators signaling

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the ongoing discussion about the recent dip in consumer spending and employment. Consumer spending has been a crucial driver of the economy during tough times and recovery periods. We've been anticipating solid employment growth, which would lead to increased income and spending power. However, some external factors may have contributed to consumers holding back.

High energy prices, election season concerns, and other uncertainties might have chilled consumer confidence. But recent data, such as auto sales, suggest that consumer spending hasn't completely dried up. Employment data can be volatile, and we've seen similar patterns in the past.

Business leaders I've spoken with are still focused on maximizing efficiency and cutting costs, rather than hiring new staff. This mindset suggests a more moderate employment growth rate. Other sectors, like manufacturing, homebuilding, sales, investment spending, and exports, are holding steady.

Our regional data and anecdotal reports confirm this underlying strength in the expansion. Tourist destinations are doing well, air travel is strong, and there's new commercial construction on the horizon.

On the inflation front, we're not currently facing a significant threat of higher inflation rates. However, I'm less optimistic about the transitory nature of some price pressures. While many price increases related to commodities will eventually reverse, cost pass-throughs are still prevalent in areas with strong demand.

Trucking companies are announcing price increases, fuel surcharges are holding steady, and truck driver wages are on the rise. A national temporary employment service company has raised its rates for the first time in a few years. These trends indicate ongoing price pressures and pricing power.

In conclusion, while the confidence intervals around our forecasts are wider now, the risks seem more balanced than they were at our last meeting. We should continue to gradually remove our extraordinary policy accommodation. I propose we hold off on any significant changes to our communication practices until our January meeting. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement: Mr. Chairman, conditions in our District reflect much of what I’ve heard so far around the table today, but I think a couple of developments are worth noting. Our labor markets have shown further signs of improvement, actually. Employment rose in June for the fourth straight month. Major hiring announcements stayed at a high level in June and July, and local labor market surveys indicated an increase in hiring plans for the rest of this year. Layoff announcements did turn up a little in July but remained lower than the hiring announcements and were largely confined to our telecommunications firms, which are continuing to cut staff. Manufacturing activity in our region continued to grow solidly in July. Production and new orders rose further, and employment and hiring continued to expand according to all our survey results. Firms remain highly optimistic about future activity and still plan moderate increases in hiring and capital spending. Consumer spending is flat in our area, as others have noted for their regions. Energy activity continued to expand in response obviously to the price environment. As before, though, some of the individuals we talked with on the producer side say that they are being constrained to some degree by shortages of labor and equipment and difficulty in obtaining permits for some of the drilling activities they would like to pursue. I would say that on the national level the question we are struggling with is whether this recent news is a pause or a more systemic issue. I am unable to tell that for sure, but I’m inclined to suggest that it’s a pause and not something fundamental. But given the uncertainties around energy, I think continued high or rising energy prices pose some risks on that front. One thing that does concern me is that, based on my conversations with some of our business people—even those in the manufacturing sector—the news that has come out recently has surprised them as well. So they are rethinking how they are looking at the situation going forward, and that is a bit of an issue for us. But I do believe that the economy is fundamentally strong, and I think our current monetary policy stance is consistent with that moving forward, as is fiscal policy as well. So I think that the lull is temporary and that we will see activity continue to increase through the remainder of this year. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on the current economic situation in New England. While the regional economy is still expanding, the job market didn't improve from May to June, and some sectors, particularly technology and consumer products, experienced weaker demand than anticipated. One director described it as a pause in technology spending. However, growth remains strong in areas like heavy equipment, paper and packaging, and medical products. Consumer and business confidence remain high, and economic indicators remain positive.

Despite high energy prices, expectations of higher interest rates, and the Democratic National Convention, we believe this dip in demand is temporary. The national economy has also shown some softness lately, but the baseline forecast, including ours in Boston, anticipates excess capacity remaining at the end of 2005.

Housing markets remain strong, and recent readings on business spending and profits are positive, although not overly optimistic. The increase in spending on nonresidential structures and recent implied increases in manufacturing activity and employment are intriguing. Businesses might be taking a wait-and-see approach to hiring.

Consumer confidence remains good, employment claims are down, hours worked are up slightly, and layoffs are down. Surveys of consumer attitudes and business hiring plans are positive. The recent surge in jobs as measured by the household survey is hard to interpret, but it could be a positive sign.

The big question moving forward is whether this lull in activity, as represented by the payroll employment data and June consumption, will be transient or a sign of slower growth ahead. Factors like higher oil and gasoline prices, rising healthcare costs, and required retirement plan contributions have contributed to the decline in consumption and moderation in wage growth and business spending. Some of these factors have begun to ease, but oil prices are likely to stay high.

Productivity growth remains solid, business profits are healthy, and markups are higher than trend. Fiscal policy remains stimulative, and monetary policy accommodation is significant. We expect growth at a pace of 3½ percent or better to resume, and inflation pressures to continue to ease.

Regarding policy accommodation, both the Greenbook and our Boston forecast anticipate excess capacity remaining by the end of 2005. However, the level of uncertainty surrounding this calculation is significant. The current stance of policy, with real federal funds rates remaining negative, was appropriate during the recession

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. There has been little change in the District economy recently; that is, a broad-based expansion is under way and is continuing. Labor demand appears to be strengthening. Manufacturing has been a particularly bright spot recently, especially for larger firms that operate and sell internationally. We have seen the anticipated improvement in tourism activity. In talking to contacts in the hospitality industry, including some people who operate a chain of upscale, upper-end hotels around the country, they say they’ve seen steady improvement throughout the year. Finally, I would reiterate Bill Poole’s comment about bottlenecks in the railroad industry and, for that matter, in other forms of transportation as well. That is something that was called to my attention some time ago, and it apparently persists today. As far as the national economy is concerned, there are two issues on my mind. One is energy prices. The other is equity prices, although my concern about equity prices is offset at least to a degree by the decline in intermediate- and longer-term interest rates that we’ve seen. But the primary question to me is, have recent developments fundamentally altered the economic outlook? Or put another way, would I disagree substantially with the Greenbook forecast? My tentative answer to that question is, no, I wouldn’t. I’m fairly comfortable with the fundamental outlook expressed there, and for reasons that have already been cited here—the expansionary stance of both monetary and fiscal policies, the persistence of a significant advance in productivity, and improving economies around the world. All those factors continue to give me some confidence that the U.S. economy will continue to perform reasonably well. Let me make one further comment about energy prices. At least according to the anecdotes that I’ve been getting, higher oil and gasoline prices seem to be having mostly what I would call “second-order” effects. That is, nobody is saying that they have laid off people or ended a second shift or something like that because of higher energy prices. The effects are all showing up in either explicit surcharges or, more implicitly, through pass-throughs. So whatever effects those higher prices are having are coming through those channels.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on the Twelfth District economy. We've seen a steady expansion in recent months, with consumers resuming spending on various sectors like entertainment, travel, and services. However, the pace of new building in the housing market has slowed due to higher material costs and anticipated interest rate increases. Businesses, on the other hand, are focusing on cost containment and productivity improvements.

The IT sector, which had been in decline, is showing signs of improvement. Production capacity is increasing, employees are working longer hours, and firms are adding jobs. In some areas, the demand for IT workers is outpacing the supply, leading to significant wage increases.

However, the national outlook is cause for concern. Disappointing data for June and the July employment report have led us to revise down our real GDP forecast. Consumer spending, particularly in the form of personal consumption expenditures, has been weak. While higher energy prices are a factor, they may not fully explain the decline in spending.

Additionally, the saving rate is at a historically low level, and households may be responding to rising interest rates and declining equity prices by increasing their savings. This could lead to a significant decrease in real GDP growth.

Despite these concerns, I believe the economy will rebound from the recent slowdown. Labor markets are improving, and labor force participation has increased slightly in many District states. However, downside risks to the outlook have risen, and we should be cautious in our forecasts.

Regarding inflation, I've seen encouraging signs of moderation in core consumer inflation and wage growth. However, there are significant upside and downside risks to the inflation outlook. On the upside, oil prices continue to be a wildcard, while on the downside, the high markup of goods prices over unit labor costs could lead to a noticeable decrease in inflation.

Given these considerations, I recommend tightening the stance of policy at this meeting in line with market expectations of a 25 basis point increase in the federal funds rate. Going forward, we should adopt a wait-and-see approach, adjusting the pace of policy accommodation based on labor market and inflation developments.

Considering the recent economic weakness and downgraded forecast, I suggest modifying the final sentence in our statement to reflect our commitment to promoting both price stability and sustainable

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement:
At our recent gathering, I'd like to share some insights regarding the national economy's growth trajectory. It appears that the underlying pace of expansion has slowed down since our last meeting, and inflation risks have diminished. Based on current market assumptions, we anticipate a more subdued growth path than our June projections, with growth hovering around 4 percent above potential for the remainder of '04 and '05. The core PCE inflation rate is expected to remain below 2 percent.

We view the risks as evenly balanced around this growth path, but there's a greater degree of uncertainty and caution regarding the growth outlook. Conversely, we're more confident about the inflation outlook. Our overall assessment is that the economic fundamentals remain favorable, despite the recent deceleration in spending. Productivity numbers are holding up well, income growth is strong, and corporate balance sheets, profit margins, and cash flow remain robust. Consumer and business confidence numbers are also reasonably good.

Our upcoming Empire survey indicates a more significant moderation in the overall outlook compared to national surveys of business confidence. However, the numbers still suggest continued expansion, and the six-month-ahead numbers have fallen less than those related to the present.

The primary risks to this outlook include a large, sustained rise in energy prices, a substantial terrorist event in the United States, or an abrupt change in the willingness of nonresidents to acquire U.S. assets. Even without these events, there's a possibility that households and businesses may exhibit increased caution due to the threat of their occurrence or concerns about weak demand and employment growth.

Regarding inflation, with monetary policy moving back to neutrality, we're reasonably confident that the core PCE will stay below 2 percent. The recent moderation in the data supports this view, but we haven't seen enough confirmation of a downward trend yet. The recent large, unanticipated rise in underlying inflation should serve as a reminder of the need for caution. If monetary policy does not continue to adjust toward a more neutral position, there's a risk that near-term inflation expectations could feed through to a higher rate of underlying inflation.

Our Empire survey respondents report some moderation in cost pressures and pricing power, with a more substantial reduction in expected pricing power six months out. Market expectations about the path of the fed funds rate are prov

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, at our previous gathering, several of us expressed apprehension about potential inflation risks, even with a gradual policy tightening. However, I'm now less worried about these upside risks, despite the continued rise in energy prices. Recent data indicates core inflation remains contained, and labor compensation growth has been modest, despite pension contributions and anticipated productivity gains. This strengthens the belief that earlier core price increases were influenced by transient factors and that significant labor and product market slack persists. Inflation expectations have remained stable, with short-term expectations and the Michigan survey decreasing, while long-term expectations have remained constant. The TIPS market's long-run inflation compensation is 50 basis points below its May peak, offering additional reassurance. Furthermore, activity has been weaker than anticipated, and the output gap is larger than expected, which should continue to restrain inflation.

However, rising oil prices are a concern, as they impact both domestic incomes and profits through import taxes and elevate uncertainty about future prices. The financial markets interpret these increases as more likely to affect output than inflation, and they have been associated with decreases in intermediate-term interest rates. Nevertheless, it's unclear that oil prices account for the entire output shortfall and the inability of fiscal and financial stimulus to close the output gap.

The greatest downside risk to growth is the behavior of the household sector, as indicated by the increase in the saving rate in the second quarter. While a moderate increase in saving is consistent with good economic performance, a more significant rise could hinder growth if other sectors lack sufficient strength. Households and businesses continue to be confident, supported by low interest rates and readily available credit, which should encourage expansion above the growth rate of potential.

Incoming data have left us with less inflation risk, a larger output gap that is closing more slowly than anticipated, increased uncertainty, and puzzling expansion dynamics. The uncertainties are skewed towards the possibility of a slower or even stalled closure of the output gap, partly due to the potential for further energy price increases. Despite these concerns, I believe we should continue our course of removing policy accommodation but with caution and flexibility in our future actions.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some insights to the discussion regarding the recent energy price shock and its potential impact on the economy. I was planning to distribute a chart during my turn after the break, but given the urgency of the situation, I'll share it now.

Our economy was on track for a gradual tightening of monetary policy when an energy shock hit us. Let me briefly outline the key developments in the oil, natural gas, and gasoline markets. The spot price for oil has risen from around $25 a barrel in 2002 to $45 a barrel currently. The far futures price, a better indicator of the permanence of this price shock, has risen from its long-term average of about $20 a barrel in 2002 to about $35 a barrel now. Similar upward movements are occurring in the markets for natural gas and gasoline.

These energy price shocks could have significant implications for the macroeconomy. An analytical approach focusing on reduced form correlations, as shown in the chart I'm distributing, suggests strong effects. Since 1975, every upward spike in real oil prices has been followed by a recession, though not all recessions can be solely attributed to oil price shocks. The model simulations conducted by the staff, as mentioned in the Greenbook and the French, Horvath, and Mauskopf memo, indicate that a permanent energy price shock could result in an implicit energy tax of about $75 billion.

The model findings suggest an increase in next year's unemployment rate of more than ½ percentage point, core inflation by ⅓ percentage point, and a long-run aggregate supply impact of about 1½ percent. While these effects might seem substantial, it's important to consider any extra-model effects, such as consumer confidence and business scrappage of capital equipment.

Consumer confidence seems relatively stable despite the energy market worsening, and there's no clear evidence of significant business scrappage yet. However, there are other uncertainties, such as the recycling point and the potential impact on investment demand and employment demand.

Assuming the model's basic story holds, the optimal strategy for the Fed in the face of a permanent energy price shock is to keep the real funds rate constant, allowing the nominal funds rate to rise on the order of 40

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let’s break for coffee and return in ten or fifteen minutes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some insights to the current economic situation. The economy seems to be losing steam, and the recent slowdown might be more severe than what can be attributed to the rise in energy prices. For instance, households usually respond to higher energy prices by reducing their savings to maintain their non-energy consumption. However, the recent increase in the household saving rate suggests that people are focusing on rebuilding their wealth due to a stagnant or declining stock market. Similarly, businesses are investing and hiring less than expected, despite having order backlogs, indicating a renewed business caution.

Moreover, the second-quarter productivity growth estimates, which account for a significant portion of the growth, were 2.9% in the nonforeign business sector and 7.5% in manufacturing. These figures suggest that firms are still focused on cost-cutting.

Our gradualist approach to tightening monetary policy looks appropriate under these circumstances. It allows us to move towards rate neutrality while giving the economy some breathing room and allowing us to observe economic developments.

However, there's a complication in the form of the divergence between core and headline inflation measures this year. While we generally focus on core inflation measures, some members have warned against ignoring headline inflation. I've worked with the staff on this issue, and our findings suggest that core measures of inflation are better predictors of long-term inflation expectations. However, short-term inflation expectations are better predicted by headline inflation.

These results support our focus on core inflation measures in making monetary policy. However, they also indicate that long-term inflation expectations in the US might not be as firmly anchored as we'd like. In such a scenario, it's crucial that we continue to demonstrate our commitment to price stability. If the economy weakens further, we may need to reconsider our current plan, but only if we're convinced that both core inflation and long-term inflation expectations are under control.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President McTeer.

Speaker: MR. MCTEER
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to share some insights about the economy in the Eleventh District. The district's economy is showing signs of improvement, but there was a pause at the national level in June, which we also observed there. Additionally, we noticed a deceleration of price pressures that had been building up. Our regional economists anticipate a 1.8% job growth for the year, which is below average and much lower than what we'd expect during a recovery period.

Since last December, Texas has seen steady employment growth. However, the gains have been unevenly distributed, and job growth slowed during the second quarter. The education and health services sector has experienced the most significant employment gains, while manufacturing continues to shed jobs, and construction has seen no growth. Construction employment even fell in June, which was the wettest month on record in Texas.

The high energy costs are a growing concern for many industries, and we've seen some signs of this in the trucking industry. However, airlines are not passing on these costs to consumers, as they're being squeezed by higher fuel costs, which offset the boost they've been getting from solid summer traffic. American Airlines attempted to recoup some of its higher fuel costs with a fare increase but had to withdraw it. The protracted bankruptcies of some airlines may be delaying a much-needed restructuring of the industry.

High energy prices have not led to any increase in drilling activity in Texas due to limited in-state prospects and the lack of international drilling, as most oil prospects seem to be concentrated in politically unstable regions. Texas may have been "sucked dry" by now.

On a positive note, the Mexican economy continues to show strong growth, with exports to the United States increasing as our recovery gains momentum. Domestic consumption has also been growing, helped by strong remittances from the United States.

I was surprised by last week's employment report, and it makes me wonder about the duration of the soft patch. However, I take comfort in the recent strength in business spending on capital equipment and the accelerating activity in the economies of many of our trading partners.

That being said, the recent volatility of spot prices may have distracted us from the more worrying long-term developments in the futures markets, which have disturbing implications for the possible duration of higher energy prices. Hig

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, at the previous meeting, I raised concerns about the uncertain economic outlook, and unfortunately, the majority of the new data has confirmed those worries. I agree with President Moskow's initial questions: are we experiencing another prolonged period of weak growth, or is this just a temporary soft patch? Although the answer is unclear, I'm willing to accept the latest staff forecast. However, I place more emphasis on the downside risks than the upside risks.

Three reasons explain my skepticism. First, the trend in all forecasts this year has been downward, with forecasters consistently revising their predictions downward. Second, business signals and financial market conditions suggest caution. Earnings growth has slowed, major stock market indices have declined, and financing conditions are favorable for borrowers. Lastly, the conditions for a return to sustained growth are not yet in place. The expected rebound in the automobile industry may not materialize, and consumer spending could be challenged by rising energy prices, weaker wealth creation, and a potential return to normal saving rates.

While keeping inflation low and stable is our primary goal, the upside risk of inflationary pressures seems to be subsiding. With economic slack increasing and productivity gains and unit labor costs performing well, a significant upsurge in inflation appears unlikely.

Given the uncertainties we face, I believe it's crucial that we maintain our policy flexibility. If a measured move was appropriate at the last meeting, it is equally so now. While markets anticipate a pause in future meetings, it's premature to assume that outcome. We must wait for the data to guide our decision-making.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent conversations with a major regional bank and two large community banks in the Southeast and Midwest. Commercial lending activity is on the rise, but it's primarily for acquisitions rather than capital purchases. The reason being, these businesses have excess capacity, and the incremental sales dollar goes mostly to the bottom line, improving profitability and debt service capacity. This, coupled with the existing liquidity of corporate America, has resulted in a weaker demand for C&I loans than we might expect.

Mortgage demand for purchasing new and existing homes remains strong. However, there's a noticeable flattening of demand for home equity loans. This could be due to the fact that homeowners have already maximized their home equity usage, particularly with the recent increase in home valuations.

Banks are also being more cautious with their commercial real estate lending. One banker, who's also a director of a major gasoline retailer, shared an interesting anecdote. They've found that a gas price of $1.80 per gallon is the point at which they see a flattening, if not a cutback, in gasoline purchases. This suggests that consumer behavior might be changing, but it's unclear if this is a broader trend.

I've started to hear some concerns among banking contacts about potential over-extension in consumer lending. There's a heightened concern about bankruptcies and a slight indication of softness in that market. However, the information I've gathered over the past two years has historically lagged behind other indicators. This could suggest some limits on the consumer side, but it doesn't necessarily mean we should alter our monetary policy direction at this point. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my thoughts to the discussion about the current economic soft patch. Many of you have brought up valid concerns, and as I prepared for this meeting, my primary worry was the duration of this economic downturn. The consumer has been a driving force behind the economy's recovery during this business expansion. However, we've seen soft retail sales in recent months, which is a cause for concern, although they have come down from elevated levels.

Furthermore, it's not just energy prices that are causing unease for consumers, but food prices as well. Retailers have shared with me that when the price of milk and gas both increase, it raises questions about how much disposable income consumers have left for discretionary spending. If these prices stabilize, it could signal the end of this soft patch.

On a positive note, home sales remain strong, which is a good indicator of long-term commitment and optimism. While sales have plateaued, they have done so at record levels. This suggests that the housing market may indicate a degree of optimism that isn't evident in the retail sales sector.

However, we need to be cautious when comparing third-quarter sales to last year. Last year, consumers received tax rebate checks, which led to an unusual boost in sales, particularly in lower-end retail sectors.

The employment data has been confusing, with two weak reports in a row, including the revisions. While initial claims remain stable, suggesting that new jobs are not being created, the household survey indicates substantial employment growth, which could signal consumer optimism.

Lastly, inflation numbers have moderated in the last couple of months, which, along with the reasons for the soft patch, makes me more optimistic that we can maintain the "measured pace" language in our latest statement. However, given the recent data surprises, I want to reiterate that our commitment to removing policy accommodation at a measured pace is dependent on the data as they evolve.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Fifth District economy for the month of July. Manufacturing saw a slower expansion, with our diffusion index for shipments falling but remaining positive. Employment in manufacturing was positive as well, with job losses in textiles and furniture offset by gains in other industries. Our new orders index for manufacturing picked up, suggesting some improvement ahead. However, manufacturers reported accelerating prices for raw materials, particularly steel and copper.

Our service sector, on the other hand, continued to grow strongly. Retail sales revenue slowed, but there were reports of strong demand for credit in the mid-level corporate market and positive consumer credit quality trends. The commercial real estate market in D.C. is now "white hot," and a national media company reported extremely strong TV ad revenues for the first half of the year.

At the national level, I was disappointed by the weakness in the payroll employment reports and consumer spending, which has elevated my concerns about downside risks. However, I still believe these negative developments will be reversed before too long. The change in corporate profit numbers due to NIPA revisions suggests that markups are less elevated than previously thought, reducing the risk of disinflation. Consumer inflation continues to rise due to food and energy prices, but inflation expectations have fallen, indicating that inflation will remain contained.

The most significant risk we've faced this year has been the apprehension of financial markets about the Fed falling behind in raising interest rates. I believe the Committee was correct to communicate its intentions to lift the funds rate this year and next to maintain credibility against inflation. We followed through on this commitment by raising the funds rate in June and indicating our intention to do so again today. While we may have received disappointing data since raising rates, it's essential to maintain our commitment to price stability. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let’s turn now to Brian Madigan.

Speaker: MR. MADIGAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the economic indicators presented in the material distributed during the break, specifically the "Material for FOMC Briefing on Monetary Policy Alternatives." The recent economic data, as shown in exhibit 1, has had a significant impact on financial markets. The yield on ten-year Treasuries dropped nearly 45 basis points to around 4.4 percent, while equity prices plunged, with the S&P 500 falling more than 6 percent and the Nasdaq nearly 13 percent.

Despite the economic slowdown, credit spreads on investment-grade issues remained relatively unchanged, and yields on such securities declined in tandem with Treasury bonds. Spreads on speculative-grade issues widened slightly but stayed narrow. Market expectations for the federal funds rate, as shown in the middle left-hand panel, have shifted significantly, with investors now anticipating a more gradual pace of rate increases.

The signs of economic weakness have led to a significant tilt down in market expectations for the path of the federal funds rate. Market participants now believe that it will take two years for the funds rate to reach 3½ percent. However, recent policy announcements and statements suggesting that the economic slowdown is temporary have led investors to expect another ¼ point increase at this meeting.

The options on federal funds futures indicate that there is a very high probability of a ¼ point increase in the federal funds rate today. The Desk's survey of primary dealers also indicates that all dealers expect a ¼ point increase. The solid blue line in the bottom right-hand panel shows the current path for the expected funds rate derived from futures contracts, which assumes policy moves will be made only at meetings.

The economic data has led some to question whether the Committee should consider leaving policy unchanged at this meeting, as discussed in exhibit 2. The surge in payrolls earlier this year has tailed off sharply, and economic growth for the first half of the year was ¾ percentage point slower than previously estimated. The staff forecast suggests that a considerable amount of economic slack will persist over the coming quarters, with the output gap projected to shrink slowly and still be around 1 percent at the end of next year.

Both core inflation and overall inflation are forecast to move lower over the second half of this year and during 2005.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
During our meeting, I'd like to address some concerns regarding the monthly employment estimates and their potential variance. With over 130 million workers in the US, even a 1% change represents a significant number. Volatility in employment and productivity can lead to substantial statistical changes without much impact on the overall economic activity growth pattern.

Moreover, we've discussed before the lack of business enthusiasm in this recovery. Anticipatory business spending, which used to be common during economic upswings, is notably absent. This is due to the shallowness of the recent recession and the resulting weak momentum. The absence of an inventory turnaround and aggressive business activity is evident in the data, with capital and inventory investment falling significantly short of cash flows.

Business executives express caution due to corporate scandals, risk aversion by boards of directors, and increased hurdle rates of return. This high degree of caution, not classifiable as business optimism or pessimism, is affecting the recovery's momentum.

While the employment data is the first real indicator of economic activity we see each month, it may not contribute much to our estimate of actual GDP when productivity growth is unstable. Instead, income, which is a function of GDP, is the critical factor.

Despite some softness in May and June, the July numbers show signs of improvement, such as a rise in motor vehicle sales and chain store sales. Corporate borrowing has also picked up, indicating the beginning of pressure consistent with the financing gap.

Initial claims, with their low error rate variance, are an important statistic. While the payroll figures suggest a decline in new hiring, other data, like increased job turnover and consumer survey indications, point to a more positive job market.

The markets' negative response to the latest employment numbers seems disproportionate to the underlying evidence. I propose an "opportunistic disaccommodation" policy approach, taking advantage of favorable economic conditions when they arise. @laugh

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: It has a nice ring.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to emphasize that, given an equal balance of risks at this moment, we should consider raising interest rates and returning to a neutral policy stance as soon as possible. The quicker we achieve this, the more effective our policy will be. I have some concerns about the timing of our moves, as it assumes we can accurately predict future events. We've been fortunate with our communications to the markets in the past year, but we cannot guarantee this consistency. If we are overwhelmed by data, our policy discretion may be lost. Therefore, getting back to neutral will better prepare us for future decisions.

We eased aggressively in response to the events of 2000 and 2001, bringing rates down to historically low levels. Reversing those decisions is not an easy task. When we have the opportunity to reverse course, as I believe we do now, we should take advantage of it. Any action other than raising rates at every meeting requires a higher threshold of evidence.

However, I acknowledge that there may be circumstances that could warrant halting the rate hikes. These circumstances would need to be significant enough to prevent us from reaching a neutral policy position. Our risk assessment will ultimately determine our direction.

Our goal is to make adjustments that do not create market disruptions. While this may not be achievable frequently, I believe we can manage it in the current environment. I propose we raise rates by 25 basis points and issue a statement consistent with Brian's revision. Any thoughts?

President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I support your recommendation completely. I think, as Ben put it, that we need to stay on track. The only suggestion I would have in terms of the statement is that I think there a number of things besides energy prices that may be responsible for what we’re seeing in the data. I have a whole list of them written down here. Therefore, I would strike the sentence that says “this softness likely owes importantly to the substantial rise in energy prices.” In my view there’s a laundry list of things including, for example, uncertainties about the situation in Iraq. Of course, that’s tied up with energy prices, but it goes beyond just energy. My only other comment would be that it wasn’t clear to me from what you said what happens to the language under the assessment of risks in alternative A, which adds the phrase “and sustainable economic growth” at the end of the last sentence. Were you proposing to include that?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I would argue against it.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Okay. I have a preference for including it on the grounds that, if we get another employment report and perhaps other data of the sort that we’ve already had, we might want to pause in September.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: There’s nothing that prevents us from doing that.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: I understand that. But my preference would be to condition the market to that possibility and read that as it stands. I don’t feel strongly about it, but I’m explaining my preference.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, it’s another side, but there is an interesting reason for my position. If we’re going to make the statement that accommodation can be removed at a measured pace and then we say, “Nonetheless, the Committee will respond to changes in economic prospects as needed,” that is clearly related to price stability on the upside. It makes no sense to talk about putting both price stability and maximum sustainable growth in that particular context. If you’re going to do that, you might as well just take that sentence out completely. The internal coherence of that, in my judgment, is questionable. In any event, to be sure, there are lots of different things that could be causing the softness, but at least in my judgment, the major one is very clearly energy. Not to acknowledge energy as an issue in this statement, with all the events that are going on, suggests that we’re working on a different planet than the one on which we live. That is not to say, though, that there aren’t other factors involved. In fact, the word “importantly” qualifies the role of energy prices; the statement doesn’t say they are the “sole” reason for the softness we’re seeing. Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Number one, I support your approach. I agree also with the notion that, at least this time, we should not fiddle with this statement very much. We are on track to have a more thorough discussion of the language in our statements in the near future, and I think the minor adjustment approach is the right decision for this meeting. Second, I agree with you very much in that I, too, would be reluctant to leave gaps in our moves toward policy neutrality. I think almost methodically raising the funds rate is the right thing to do. If we do it at some meetings and not at other meetings, that’s just going to cause a lot of attention. Of course we have to look at the data, but as long as we’re in a position where the level of the funds rate is so highly accommodative, in general I would lean toward the methodical approach of taking baby steps at each meeting unless we get some jarring new information. Third, I would keep in the reference to energy prices, as my remarks indicated. I think they are important. In my view you are right that, if we don’t mention them at all, it will look as if we’re vaguely out of touch. On the statement itself, you may be right on the employment side. Essentially, the way I’m interpreting what you say is that you’re criticizing employment in the first difference sense because the 32,000 is a number that is estimated with error on the base of millions of people being employed. I still look at employment a lot for output gap purposes, just to see how far the expansion has to run. Our statement focuses on the first differences part of employment, not on the levels. Maybe we ought to commission our drafting committee to think about a different way of referring to the employment picture. I wouldn’t do it this time, but in general we might want the statement to focus more on levels of employment and not so much on recent changes, for the reason you suggested. That’s one of the things I’d put on the agenda to deal with in the statement as time goes on, but for this meeting I would go right down the line with alternative B.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I certainly agree that your proposal is the right thing to do at this time. I like your concept of “opportunistic disaccommodation.” I don’t know exactly what it means, but I like that concept! We are in a position to move now, and a move today will be well understood. It’s well priced into the market. It won’t have a big market effect, and it takes us in the direction we want to go. You mentioned toward the end of your remarks “going to neutral,” wherever that might be. That’s a really interesting question right now. Where is neutral? I find the chart in the Bluebook, with the wiggly shaded area that goes up and down again on the estimated ranges of equilibrium rates of interest, somewhat of a puzzle. I know I need to think about that more, but it doesn’t affect how I feel about the policy decision today. I think a ¼ point increase in the funds rate target is the right thing to do. I don’t know what to do about the statement. I have some sympathy for trying to get out of giving the market too much confidence about what we’re doing. You seemed to reflect a bit of concern about that in your questions earlier. I’m worried about that because I think we need to look at the data as they come in and we need to have some flexibility in how we assess conditions moving forward. I worry about the market continually believing that it knows exactly how we’re going to act. But at this stage of the game, maybe it’s okay that the market knows what to expect in terms of our policy. So I guess I’d go with your recommendations on the alternative B formulation. I certainly hope that we can get ourselves to a situation in which Vincent’s recommended language, or something like it, is where we end our statements. I’d prefer to leave people hanging a little as we go forward and not be quite so confident about what we’re going to do. But for now this approach is good.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I agree completely with your recommendation. I think the sooner we get back to neutral, the better off we are, and I agree that we should have a high hurdle for not raising rates at meetings while we’re in this period of removing accommodation at a measured pace. And I think it’s important not to leave gaps in the timing of our policy moves. Also, I believe it’s important for us to mention energy prices in this statement. There is one point that I just want to mention. I agree with the statement as written, but in section 3 of Brian’s table, the rationale sentence says, “The economy nevertheless appears poised to resume a stronger pace of expansion going forward.” That is a little different from what we’ve done before. I agree with the sentence, but it is forward-looking, and I don’t think we’ve had forward- looking sentences before in the rationale section of the statement. I don’t disagree with it, but I think we should recognize that.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, that thought is implicit. If we don’t believe that, why are we moving the funds rate up 25 basis points? So, essentially, it is implicit in our actions.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: As I said, I don’t disagree with it, but I think it is different from what we have been saying in the past.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Oh, absolutely, it is. And one of the things that I think we are finding with the statement is that the increased flexibility we have created for ourselves by working through the phraseology in the Bluebook gives us greater latitude to do these statements in ways in which each meeting is handled idiosyncratically. From an exposition point of view, I think that is essential.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I might just point out that the sentence before that— where we say that a portion of the rise in inflation is transitory—is itself predictive. So, it isn’t unprecedented to be giving some sense of an outlook in the rationale part of the statement.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: I support your recommendation, Mr. Chairman. I think we might encounter circumstances under which we’d want to skip a meeting in our measured pace of removing policy accommodation—perhaps to “undisaccommodate.” @laugh But I agree with Governor Gramlich that a moderate rise in energy prices would not be a rationale for doing that. That’s a circumstance in which it’s important to maintain the anti-inflationary dimension of this Committee’s policy.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: I support your recommendation, Mr. Chairman. I like the basic strategy of keeping the pressure on to try to get us back to neutral. In answer to President Minehan’s question of where is neutral, my answer has always been “north of here,” so I’d keep going in that direction. Neutral may not mean a single number, and the definition of neutral may not be fixed. But as long as we’re moving north of where we are currently, I think it’s a good idea. On the wording, I know four versions of this matrix have been handed out. Ordinarily, that would be problematic. I think each actually improved the statement. I like the wording of the last version and the reference to energy. The way it is posed here actually fits better my way of seeing the situation, so I’m comfortable with the wording as it is presented.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: I support your recommendation, Mr. Chairman. I think it is appropriate that we maintain a high hurdle as we go forward before skipping a beat. I believe this measured approach is important, as Governor Bernanke said, to maintain the credibility of our commitment against inflation. Even with a few more soft patches as we go forward, I think that’s going to be the ever-present danger in the near term. I also share your opposition to including the phrase “sustainable growth” in the last sentence, but for a slightly different reason. The way it is worded there makes it sort of a twin pillar of our obligations.
