I find myself in an internal argument about policy. On one hand, the expansion has regained vigor, but there's uncertainty about where labor market indicators will settle. Productivity growth and industrial production are strong, and capacity is in short supply in some areas. Consumers are willing to spend on autos and houses, but this could turn around and pose downside risk. Businesses have added enough inventory to last, and will likely increase non-high-tech equipment purchases before the investment tax credit change next year.

On the other hand, there are concerns about the fiscal and external deficits, and whether the economy still needs negative real fed funds rates as a stimulus. I'm leaning towards removing policy accommodation slowly, as real funds rates are marginally negative and will be less so after a 25 basis point move. However, if employment data don't improve soon, a pause may be necessary. Markets expect a rate increase today,

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. The tone of the reports that I’m getting from my business contacts in the Fourth District is positive, but it’s hardly reminiscent of the reports of robust growth that I was hearing earlier this year. Still, both order books and profits are reported to be fairly strong. Business leaders across a broad array of industries tell me that they expect to see steady sales growth through the end of 2004. Despite these positive reports, business confidence remains less upbeat than order books and profits would appear to warrant. My contacts still say that, if the economy continues to grow and stays on its present course, they will persevere in their search for more productivity improvements rather than hire additional workers, at least for the foreseeable future. And this is perhaps still the most remarkable aspect of this expansion—the ability of businesses to continue to squeeze out more production with the same number of workers. It can’t persist, of course, but I’m beginning to wonder how long I’m going to keep saying that. At a recent joint meeting of our boards from the three offices in the District, the directors made several observations about energy prices. Although most directors expect energy prices to retreat somewhat from their current high levels, they are basing their future business plans on energy prices that they expect will be higher than those of a year or two ago. In response to energy prices, though, several of our directors were commenting that they are adjusting—or at least trying through innovation to adjust—the composition of the products that they’re producing. They’re using innovations to develop products that are less energy-dependent. The Fourth District economy, like the nation’s, seems to have a lot going for it except considerable employment growth. The incoming data, though, suggest to me that the risks we face remain balanced and may actually have narrowed a bit in their scope since the summer. By that I mean that the prospects for either a resumption of the exceptionally strong business expansion that we saw in the winter and spring or a slipping back to a tractionless recovery— both of which seemed possible in the summer—look considerably more remote to me this time. Similarly, the extreme inflation or disinflation outcomes seem to me less likely as well. In summary, I believe the economy is expanding at a sustainable rate, and I think we should continue with our plan to gradually remove our policy accommodation. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Fifth District's economic landscape. While growth has slowed down, we don't foresee any major deviations from the expansion path. Manufacturing is still expanding, but employment seems stagnant or declining. Retail activity has been weak, aligning with national trends. However, residential construction remains strong, albeit with concerns over rising material costs and a shortage of heavy-equipment operators.

Our contacts report expanding economic activity across various sectors, but there's a sense of apprehension about the recovery's pace. Many firms are hesitant to hire, attributing this to uncertainty about the economic recovery's longevity.

Regarding the national economy, the August job growth was encouraging, but the September Greenbook has revised down expected job growth for the year. The output gap has widened, and core consumer prices are projected to be lower than anticipated. I believe the labor market will gradually improve, and the output gap will close by the end of 2006. However, recent data suggest downside risks to the outlook.

Financial markets anticipate a higher funds rate than the Greenbook forecast, keeping long-term interest rates elevated. The Greenbook assumes long-rates will decrease as the funds rate rises. However, if the economy requires more stimulus, markets might become anxious about inflation, causing long-rates to rise instead. We should be cautious about sending such signals to maintain our credibility for low inflation.

In conclusion, while the economic recovery is progressing, recent data have introduced uncertainties. We should remain vigilant and consider the potential implications of our actions on financial markets.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I appreciate your caution regarding the District economy and its close correlation with the national economy. I'd like to add a positive note, however, about a recent development that may have gone unnoticed. The Minnesota Twins clinching the Central Division title for the third consecutive year is a testament to the success of a small market team. @laugh

Turning to the national economy, I acknowledge that the forecast has seen some changes this year. While growth may be slower than anticipated and inflation lower, we should also consider the broader trends. Last year's growth was around 4.5%, and we're likely to see something similar this year. Unemployment has declined, and inflation remains low, which is a positive sign.

Moreover, I'm optimistic about the economy's resilience and flexibility, as demonstrated by the long expansions in the 1980s and 1990s. Expansions tend to last, and we're in a good position overall.

Regarding employment, I've expressed skepticism about the "reluctance to hire" explanation. Based on my conversations with businesspeople, I haven't sensed any unusual hesitance to hire. If we're referring to the slow improvement in employment, I agree that there may be factors at play, but I'm not convinced there's anything special about it.

Lastly, I understand the importance of considering exchange rates and our role in the global economy. However, we should aim for orderly movements in exchange rates by adhering to sound policies.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I gather your bottom line is to recommend to those of us who root for the Baltimore Orioles that we move to St. Paul! @laugh

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: I’m not sure. That move may be necessary but not sufficient. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Third District's economic landscape. Contrary to other regions, our district experienced steady expansion in economic activity during the summer months. However, we've noticed a slight easing in the pace of growth recently. For instance, our manufacturing sector's business outlook survey index of general activity dropped from a robust 28.5 in August to 13.4 in September. Yet, the index of new orders strengthened, and shipments remained high. This disparity in our indexes is unusual, but the positive comments from respondents align more with the readings on new orders and shipments.

Moreover, about a quarter of our firms reported that the special tax depreciation allowance led them to increase their capital equipment spending this year. Half of these firms anticipate increasing their spending further in 2005, with a quarter expecting significant increases. Employment growth in our region has slowed but remains positive, with all three states experiencing job growth in the first two months of the third quarter.

Retail sales of general merchandise have been rising, although the year-to-year gains have eased. Commercial real estate markets remain soft, but nonresidential construction is showing a slight improvement, mainly in warehouse construction. Residential activity is moderating but remains strong, with house appreciation continuing at a high rate.

Prices are on the rise in our region, with firms expressing concerns about fuel prices and healthcare costs. The business community's outlook remains positive, with continued improvement anticipated this year. Our staff's leading indexes signal output growth and stronger employment growth in the three states over the next three quarters.

Nationally, incoming data suggest that the economy is recovering from the soft patch. Consumer spending has picked up, although not as strongly as earlier in the year. Manufacturing activity has rebounded, and business investment continues to expand. Payroll employment gains improved in August, and inflation has eased somewhat from earlier in the year.

However, I'm not as optimistic about the inflation outlook as the Greenbook. The Greenbook forecasts GDP growth above potential for the next two years, with the output gap eliminated and monetary policy remaining accommodative. I find it challenging to believe that the inflation picture could be as favorable as the Greenbook suggests without the fed funds rate being at or above neutral at

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Shall we break for coffee?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Twelfth District's economic landscape since our last meeting. While expansion continues, it's happening at a slower pace than earlier in the year. Housing markets remain robust, but some areas are showing signs of cooling. International trade has kept seaports busy, but the warehousing and distribution chain is feeling the strain. Fortunately, bottlenecks have been minimal.

Job gains in the District have been modest and far from the pace of earlier in the year. Our contacts anticipate increasing hiring in the coming months to meet sales and output levels. However, they express concerns about finding workers with the necessary skills and experience. On the other hand, relatively unskilled labor is readily available.

Turning to the national economy, recent data suggest that the economic slowdown in June was short-lived. Consumer spending, including auto sales, is rebounding, and the drop in oil prices should help. However, we're yet to see significant job growth or a return to last year's robust output growth rates.

The Greenbook presents a cautious economic outlook, assuming persistently low real interest rates. I agree with this assessment. While the oil shock may have contributed to the recent economic weakness, its impact might not be as large as some think. Additionally, equipment investment is getting a boost from partial expensing, which could mean the underlying recovery momentum isn't as strong as believed.

Consumer spending, which was a critical support during the recession and its aftermath, remains a concern. Despite low income growth, households might increase savings to more normal levels as interest rates rise. This could restrain growth further. Additionally, mortgage refinancings have been low, which could negatively impact spending.

Inflation data for July and August have been favorable, suggesting that the winter's price increases were temporary. It's puzzling why this expansion hasn't resulted in faster average growth and a larger reduction in slack, given easy financial conditions. Continuing caution due to September 11, Middle East wars, and corporate governance scandals are plausible explanations.

Given the current low real funds rate, monetary policy must remain accommodative. A pause in the rate-raising process is likely in the near future, especially if inflation remains under control. However, a modest tightening at this meeting seems justified due to

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement:
At the current meeting, I'd like to share my perspective on the economic outlook, which largely aligns with our last discussion, except for the external imbalance concern. The inflation news appears improved, and the growth news is reassuring, though not overly exciting. We don't foresee any significant signs of a deeper and prolonged deceleration in growth that would raise concerns about the expansion's sustainability.

Regarding growth, we anticipate a solid 4% real growth rate for the remainder of this year and a 3.5% pace for '05 and '06. Core PCE is expected to remain around 1-1.5% during this period. The labor market continues to improve, with healthy wage growth supporting consumer confidence and consumption. Productivity growth reverts gradually to the '90s average. Global demand growth remains robust, though not strong enough to positively impact U.S. exports.

Our optimism level is slightly higher than some in the room, but the uncertainty lies in the external imbalance, household saving behavior, business spending, and the reversal of the external imbalance's deterioration. Household saving may increase due to rising interest rates, lower future housing returns, higher health care costs, and pension risks. Businesses remain hesitant to invest and hire, and we're unsure if this trend will continue.

Our Empire survey shows a recovery in business sentiment, which is a positive sign. However, the deterioration in our external imbalance leaves us vulnerable to confidence shocks. The dollar's stability and falling long-term rates in the context of the large upward revision to the current account deficit may suggest reduced risks, but I believe this is not the case.

We should be content with the current path of the fed funds rate as priced into the markets and pleased with its responsiveness to data changes. We don't see any upside risks at present that warrant a steeper path. It's crucial not to induce expectations of a more gradual tightening until we've established a more definitively positive real fed funds rate and moved closer to neutrality.

In summary, while the economic outlook remains positive, there are uncertainties surrounding household saving, business spending, and the external imbalance. We should remain cautious and closely monitor these factors.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement: Mr. Chairman, on the national outlook, our view is similar to the Greenbook’s in that we think the expansion has regained some of its traction and will continue as we move forward. Discussions with business contacts throughout our District tend to support this notion as well. For example, most retailers that we’ve contacted in the last few weeks reported that sales have improved in August over earlier in the summer. In addition, the chair of our board, whose firm is in the manufacturing area on a worldwide basis, reported to us just last week that capital spending budgets finally seem to have been, to use his word, “unleashed.” Another director noted that low interest rates and available capital have enabled firms to find capital for acquisition and expansion fairly readily. In our most recent survey of all thirty of our head office and branch directors, about two-thirds of those who expressed an opinion about the next six months—and that was most of them—thought conditions would improve, while about a third thought the economy would just, in their words, “muddle along.” Finally, District hirings continue to exceed layoffs and by a fair margin now. The ratio of announced planned hirings to planned layoffs was 2½ to 1 in the third quarter, greatly surpassing the previous high of 1¼ to 1 earlier in the year. Based on recent evidence like this and on forecasts by our staff, I believe the most likely outcome is continued good growth in the 3½ to 4 percent range, as suggested by others. The uncertainty seems to center on whether hiring will pick up, and I agree that the most likely outcome is that it will strengthen as we move through the remainder of this year and into next year. On the inflation outlook, it appears that the Committee and others were correct in that the spring increases were temporary and price inflation has settled back to more-modest levels. In that environment, my judgment is that what I would describe as our careful, systematic movement toward a more neutral fed funds rate is serving us well. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to echo Bill Poole's sentiments and call our latest economic developments "ho-hum." While we've had some mixed readings since our last meeting, there haven't been any major positive or negative surprises. Perhaps we should take a moment to appreciate the stability. I'm generally content with the projected growth and spending outlined in the Greenbook. If we manage 3.5 to 4% growth in the coming quarters, we'll continue to make progress in lowering the unemployment rate, and lagging sectors should show improvement.

Regarding inflation, recent data doesn't suggest an imminent threat of a significant price increase. However, some mainstream inflation forecasts still indicate an upward trend. I'd advise against emphasizing the recent positive inflation data too much in our statement.

The most intriguing aspect of our recent economic data and projections for the coming quarters is the employment and labor market situation. My labor economists have considered the possibility of a permanent decline in the labor force participation rate, which could mean that employment growth of only 100,000 per month would be sufficient to maintain the current unemployment rate. This is below the Council of Economic Advisers' claim of 300,000 new jobs per month and the Greenbook's baseline projection for 2005. There's a significant margin of error around our job growth estimates, which could impact notions of economic slack, room for growth, and ultimately, price pressures.

In our region, UPS's corporate executive office in Atlanta reported solid, though not as strong growth as earlier in the year. Small business owners I met with shared similar concerns about spottiness and uneasiness in the economy. The economic disruptions from the hurricanes are expected to be short-lived, with reconstruction outlays offsetting any temporary slowdown. Agricultural areas may take longer to recover, but the slow tourism season in Florida should help mitigate the impact.

Looking ahead to policy considerations, I believe our recent developments meet the threshold for staying on the policy path we established at our last meeting. Assuming the economy continues to evolve as described in the Greenbook forecast, I propose we gradually remove more policy accommodation. We should consider the potential for unintended consequences and distortions from prolonged policy stimulation. If we find that the

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the discussion regarding the current state of the economy. While it's clear that our expectations of a soft spot have not materialized, and economic growth has been good this year, we've also noticed that underlying demands and the future path are not as strong as anticipated. This has led to downward revisions to both actual and projected growth.

The economic growth we've seen this year is less than expected given the significant fiscal and monetary stimulus. The rise in oil and energy prices is a factor, but it doesn't fully explain the shortfall. Inflation and output have also come in below expectations since June, suggesting a demand shock.

The unexplained shortfall seems to be primarily in business investment spending. We've attributed this to "business caution," but it may be due to heightened geopolitical uncertainties, risks of further oil price increases, and the fallout from governance scandals. However, the longer business caution persists, the more I believe we're missing something more fundamental.

The bottom line is that we don't fully understand what's dampening the economy's response to accommodative policy. This has implications for the strategy going forward. Rates need to rise, but the path after this meeting should be decided flexibly based on incoming information.

Markets have reacted to recent data by reducing the expected pace of tightening beyond this meeting. They expect us to skip meetings or slow down the pace of tightening if the data warrant it. These expectations could reverse quickly if data on activity or prices strengthen.

Flexibility is still most likely to be called for around a very gradual path of tightening. Incoming data suggest that the risk of accelerating prices has diminished, and relatively low intermediate- and long-term interest rates may be required to sustain solid growth. Both markets and the staff have marked down their expectations for policy.

In conclusion, we need to pay attention to inflation and growth rates to avoid policy mistakes, as uncertainty about the natural rate of unemployment or interest rates makes this approach useful. The Bluebook table depicting policy rules illustrates this approach, suggesting a shallow upslope to rates. However, this will not be the appropriate path for policy, and we will need to look at the data to determine the direction of the path's deviation. Thank you

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the economic situation at hand. We've encountered a soft spot in the economy that seems to be more complex than previous ones. While we've had soft spots before, blaming them on the 1990s hangover, the Iraq War, Enron, and Sarbanes-Oxley doesn't explain the current slowdown.

At our last meeting, I believed the problem was an oil shock. However, I now think there's more to it. Looking at our forecasts from earlier in the year and recent revisions from the Greenbook and Blue Chip, both inflation and output forecasts have decreased. This could indicate a demand shock in addition to the oil shock.

Moreover, there's a strong international dimension to this issue. The International Finance Division's forecasts suggest that without the US and China, many countries would experience deficient domestic demand. Those doing well are primarily those trading extensively with the US or China, and their success is largely due to exports, not domestic demand. While it's not accurate to say there would be a world recession without the US and Chinese economies, there would certainly be global sluggishness. This sluggishness could be amplified if the dollar weakened, which is a possibility.

Given this situation, should we continue our plan of gradually raising or re-equilibrating rates? I believe yes for this meeting, but I'm becoming less certain about future meetings. The re-equilibration process has gone well so far. Since we began raising rates in late June, the funds rate has risen 50 basis points, while ten-year bond rates have fallen 65 basis points, and even the TIPS long-term rate has dropped 30 basis points. This decline in long-term nominal rates can be explained within orthodox macro theory. However, the 30 basis point drop in real interest rates is more complex. The most likely cause is the real economy weakening. Regardless of the explanation, why fight it? We have an opportunity to bring the financial system closer to equilibrium, lessen the chances of inordinately low interest rates if inflation suddenly increases, and potentially even lower real long-term rates due to expectations effects.

However, this win-win situation can't last indefinitely. We should keep in mind that re

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some thoughts on the economic developments we've seen so far in 2004. This year, the economy has faced a medium-sized shock to aggregate supply, primarily due to the decline in the dollar, rising commodity prices, and the sharp increase in energy costs. These factors made interpreting economic data this spring particularly challenging.

As a Committee, we believed that these supply shocks, particularly the energy cost component, were the main drivers behind the unexpected rise in inflation and the soft patch in economic activity in the spring. Both of these forecasts have held up well, indicating that supply shocks were indeed the primary reason for the first-half economic surprises.

Fortunately, the effects of this shock may be subsiding. The dollar has stabilized, and import prices have decelerated as a result. Commodity prices peaked in March, and while oil prices continue to rise, their volatility and level may have a dampening effect on the economy. About three-quarters of the increase in oil prices since late last year had already occurred by May, and refinery margins have fallen.

We can see the waning effects of the supply shock in the moderation of core inflation and the rebound in spending in the third quarter. However, we must remain cautious as further rises in oil prices or declines in the dollar are always possible. If no such developments occur, the ebbing of the supply shock will provide us with greater clarity about the underlying strength of this expansion.

My assessment is that the recovery is proceeding at a solid but somewhat less vigorous pace than we had hoped or expected. While expansion at the current pace is unlikely to create new inflationary pressures, we must closely monitor the jobs data. If job growth continues at the current pace, we may have cause for concern about business confidence and household spending.

Our strategy of removing accommodation at a measured pace has worked well, providing support to the economy, avoiding financial market surprises, and allowing us time to assess ongoing developments. I support our current plan of measured withdrawal of emergency stimulus. We can raise the funds rate today without harming the recovery. However, we must remain flexible and adjust our pace as needed based on incoming data. Financial markets are prepared for this flexibility, and it aligns with our declared strategy of removing accommodation at a measured but not mechanistic pace. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I've had some productive conversations with contacts in the banking sector leading up to this meeting, and their sentiments align with what we've heard in this room. The economy's growth is solid but not as robust as anticipated, as former Federal Reserve Chairman Ben Bernanke noted. My discussions with banking sector representatives didn't reveal anything new on this front.

Given the upcoming presidential election, which is now six weeks away, I couldn't help but consider the potential implications for the economy. Deficit levels and spending restraints haven't emerged as significant issues in the campaign, as we had anticipated. However, concerns about Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid have been elevated, not by the parties but by interested observers, including our Chairman and Pete Peterson.

As we discussed earlier, the dollar's value is a topic of interest, and I encourage members of the Committee with concerns about deficit spending, Medicare/Medicaid, and certain tax policy issues to address these matters promptly following the election.

The economy, as James Carville famously said, is the key issue in elections. Economic uncertainty is the most pressing concern this time around, and a clear solution isn't immediately apparent. However, monetary policy has not become a political issue during my tenure on this Committee. While today's statement will be scrutinized for political advantage, I believe there will be no significant political fallout for two reasons. First, the consistent implementation of our policy maintains our credibility, and second, clear communication is essential for the effectiveness of our actions.

Over the past few years, we've transitioned from an accommodative stance to a period of patience, and now we're moving toward a return to neutral at a measured pace. This policy shift is not only appropriate but also unlikely to be a political issue.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the discussion about the U.S. economy's gradual recovery from the recent soft patch. While we anticipate a pick-up, it seems that growth may be more subdued than previously expected. In light of this, the Greenbook has been revised to reflect a more modest growth outlook with stable inflation.

I share the concern of my colleagues about the slow pace of recovery, especially given the significant stimulus measures in place. However, I'm less worried about downside risks to the baseline than I was during our last few meetings. The range of uncertainty remains large, but the risks appear more balanced.

The question on everyone's mind is why, with all the stimulus, we're facing low inflation and a resource utilization gap that doesn't close until late 2006. I'd like to propose a few hypotheses.

First, the bursting of the asset bubble and the subsequent shocks may have had more profound effects than initially thought. As we've seen from Japan's experience, dealing with the fallout from an asset bubble can take years. It's possible that it could take longer than anticipated to fully address the impact of both the high-tech bubble and the subsequent shocks.

Second, there's a growing sense of business caution. Several surveys, such as the NFIB survey and the Duke University survey of CFOs, indicate that businesses expect growth to be lower than our staff forecast. This business malaise issue is worth monitoring as firms enter their capital planning cycles.

Third, global imbalances may be a factor. However, the imbalances are not between the U.S. and the rest of the world, but rather within other economies. Many industrial economies, including Japan, the euro area, the United Kingdom, Switzerland, and Australia, have seen their financial indicators marked down and growth expectations lowered. Domestic demand in these economies has been weak, making the U.S. the primary engine of economic growth.

Fourth, investment in new capital goods, such as high-tech equipment, may be less urgent than we thought. There could be a deeper pool of productive capital than we envisioned, and it could be longer-lived than originally assumed. This would support highly productive and profitable businesses but also indicate a lower demand for new investment

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add to the discussion about the economic indicators. While they've shown continued expansion, the pace has slowed down compared to the second half of last year and the beginning of this year. Business attitudes, as Governor Ferguson mentioned, have become a bit less optimistic, especially among small businesses.

I'd like to share a couple of anecdotes that might explain this trend. First, CEOs have been focusing on implementing audit adaptations and internal controls to comply with Sarbanes-Oxley legislation since June. This has taken up a significant amount of their time and resources, leaving less time for business expansion decisions. The reputation risk for non-compliance is substantial, and some companies have already issued 10Q warnings that they may not be fully compliant by year-end.

Second, large company CFOs anticipate only 2.8% GDP growth in the next twelve months but expect earnings growth of 13.4% at their own companies. This discrepancy highlights the fact that CFOs still operate in a nominal world, and their caution in spending and hiring is due to their concern about delivering double-digit earnings growth to the Street despite modest sales and low inflation.

Despite these concerns, overall, companies are stronger. We've seen more debt ratings rise from below investment grade to investment grade than fall from investment grade to below it this year. This trend, along with moderating inflation rates, indicates that the expansion is likely to continue, albeit at a slower pace.

Given this context, I believe our current policy stance, which allows for a measured removal of accommodation, is the appropriate signal to send to the market. This signal has helped extend the expansion by keeping long-term debt rates low and accessible to companies when they're ready to invest. Our credibility in this regard will continue to support capital availability and affordability.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you very much. Mr. Reinhart.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to bring your attention to some significant developments in financial markets over the past six weeks that could impact your policy decisions. According to short-term interest rate futures, market participants expect the Committee to gradually tighten monetary policy over the next year and a half, with the funds rate reaching 2.875% by early 2006. This path is slightly firmer in the near term and shallower later on compared to market sentiment at the time of the August meeting.

Investors have interpreted recent statements by policymakers as a confirmation of your intent to continue removing accommodation and have been influenced by subdued inflation readings. The shift in market expectations relative to the August Greenbook is about 0.5 percentage point lower one and a half years from now. The top right panel shows that market participants expect a near certainty of a 0.25 percentage point hike today, and the funds rate is expected to be near 2% at year-end.

Market participants anticipate a pause in the process of removing policy accommodation, likely in December, as shown by the lower implied volatility on near-term Eurodollar futures rates. The middle left panel indicates that the average response to employment surprises in the last year has been more marked than in the prior ten years, as shown in the middle right panel.

The release of the August employment report caused a puzzle, as rates rose considerably despite a touch weaker-than-expected monthly gain in payrolls. The net effect of other data over the intermeeting period resulted in a considerable relaxation of financial conditions, as shown in the bottom panel, with corporate yields lower, equity prices posting sizable gains, and the exchange value of the dollar moving sideways.

The decline in yields and the concomitant rise in share prices could be viewed as the dawning recognition among investors that a lower track for the real risk-free rate of interest will be necessary to support economic growth. However, a decision to hold the funds rate at 1.5% could encourage an even more substantial revision to the outlook for policy.

The Committee may be concerned that the pace of output growth will not be sufficiently vigorous to ensure satisfactory progress in reducing economic slack. The advance in payrolls has been fitful in recent months, and persisting slack has rolled back a portion of the turn-up in inflation

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. Questions for Vincent?

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I see that you made a slight change in B5 where the proposed language is “to be roughly equal” as opposed to “are roughly equal.”

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: That was at the suggestion of the house grammarian, who complained about the wording in the August and previous meeting statements.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Okay. So that really is unchanged, so to speak, from the current statement? I was reading it and wondering, What am I reading here?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I don’t think my maneuverings are that subtle! @laugh

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: One never knows. Another change, which I had submitted as a suggestion and I guess no one likes it but I’ll raise it anyway, is whether or not we want to make B6 look more like A6 by referring to sustainable growth as well as maintaining price stability. Now, maybe I should leave that to the Chairman to recommend. That may be a subject for the next part of the meeting.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I would note that some market analysts have pointed to such a possible inclusion as an explicit signal that policy will pause.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: We would stop?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Yes. So that would have more import than just aligning the statement to the goals in the Federal Reserve Act. It would be taken as a market signal.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Okay. I sort of figured that, but I also thought that might not be a bad thing. But, again, that’s a subject for our discussion a few minutes from now.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
At the table, I'd like to add a few remarks if there are no further questions. I believe we've covered the general outlook quite well. However, I'd like to address President Minehan's comment regarding encouraging lower ten-year interest rates. This is a significant consideration, as mortgage refinancings could lead to a drop in mortgage market duration with potential economic consequences.

While the economy has shown some softening, the Greenbook numbers suggest strong employment growth. Indicators like hires, job openings, and initial claims support this. If employment numbers surprise us, financial conditions could change dramatically.

A concern is the return of the carry trade and potential duration drops. In the event of an employment surprise, this could lead to collateral damage for American businesses and negative economic repercussions.

Given the uncertainty, it might be risky to convey a message of pausing or ending our current policy course. Instead, keeping our statement unchanged but open to policy changes seems prudent.

I recommend a 25 basis point increase in the federal funds rate and an unchanged statement as in alternative B. We have seven weeks until the next meeting, and much can change during that time. Let's all read the draft release and discuss. President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I support your recommendation for increasing the intended funds rate by 25 basis points and for the statement as written. I’m reminded of something my mother used to say when I was a child. I grew up in a family of four children with lots of cousins around, and she would often say, “Children are to be seen, not heard.” And I think that’s true of monetary policy today. We should be as quiet as we can possibly be. In fact, as a general rule, I think one of our goals should be to make policy very noncontroversial and predictable, and we should not raise issues that don’t have to be raised. So I support this proposal completely.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: I support the recommendation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: I support your recommendation, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: I, too, support your recommendation, and I would largely echo Bill Poole’s remarks.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement: I support the recommendation and the press release.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I support your recommendation as to the policy change. I take your point about the language of the statement. As I said in my comments—and other people have mentioned it as well—the big unknown is how much employment is going to grow. I certainly hope your predictions are on the mark. I’m rooting for us to be successful.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let me put it this way, if the Greenbook projections actually turn out to be right, that would be extraordinary.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Oh, yes. I’ll buy the Greenbook employment projections.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: I support your recommendation, particularly about the last sentence of the statement.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement: I support your recommendation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: I support your recommendation, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: I support your recommendations, Mr. Chairman. I think the current structure of rates is close enough to what we need, and I would hesitate to do something that would cause a lot of fluctuations in expectations. If we get to the point in November or December where we pause, I think rates will go down. It will trigger some of that refinancing we’ve talked about. I’m not really worried about the financial system in that situation; it’s pretty robust. It proved that in the summer of ’03. There wasn’t that much collateral damage, but we don’t have to inflict it until we have to.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: I support your recommendation. I would note that if we get to the end of this year and feel we have to take a pause then, we actually may want some of that refinancing if that’s going to help consumers. So let’s not be afraid of getting all the help we can get to keep the economy growing as long as inflation is under control.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: I support the recommendation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement: I support the recommendation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement: I support your recommendation, Mr. Chairman.

Statement: President McTeer.

Statement: I support it.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement: I support it as well.

Statement: I support it.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Mr. Secretary, will you read the appropriate language?

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: I support it, too—even though you didn’t care! @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Sorry, I didn’t mean to skip you. I realize that silence would not have been your position.

Speaker: MR. BERNARD
Statement: Let me begin with the directive wording, which is on page 15 of the Bluebook: “The Federal Open Market Committee seeks monetary and financial conditions that will foster price stability and promote sustainable growth in output. To further its long-run objectives, the Committee in the immediate future seeks conditions in reserve markets consistent with increasing the federal funds rate to an average of around 1¾ percent.” With regard to the wording in the press statement that is formally voted on, the second full paragraph of the draft statement: “The Committee perceives the upside and downside risks to the attainment of both sustainable growth and price stability for the next few quarters to be roughly equal. With underlying inflation expected to be relatively low, the Committee believes that policy accommodation can be removed at a pace that is likely to be measured. Nonetheless, the Committee will respond to changes in economic prospects as needed to fulfill its obligation to maintain price stability.”

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vincent Reinhart, you wanted to mention something about the minutes?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I just wanted to give a quick report on the experiment with the expedited minutes. Once again, we got the Committee to indicate its inclination to approve the minutes in time to meet our deadline. That is, we would have been able to publish the minutes two and a half weeks after the August meeting. Once again, it was a close call. The Chairman has asked me to put a discussion of the minutes experiment on the agenda for December. I’ll brief you then on some of the lessons we’ve learned and ask for guidance going forward. Now I think the Chairman has a report on other developments relating to the Secretariat.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Not at the moment. I want to put the FOMC meeting on pause, as we like to say, and ask that the Board members proceed to my office.

Speaker: MR. BERNARD
Statement: Let me mention that, as soon as the Board members come back, there will be a picture-taking session. And unless there is a change in plans, you will be asked to move to that end of the room.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: When is this? Today?

Speaker: MR. BERNARD
Statement: Yes, before lunch.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Would everyone be seated please? I would like to report that by unanimous vote the Board of Governors accepted the requests of the twelve Reserve Banks for an increase of ¼ percentage point in the discount rate—much to everybody’s surprise! As we close today, I would like to take a moment to recognize and to thank the gentleman sitting on my right because this is Normand Bernard’s final meeting as Deputy Secretary of the Committee. He has announced his intention to retire from the Federal Reserve effective at the beginning of November. It is the end of an era. Norm has worked at the Federal Reserve for more than forty-two years. As far as we can tell, today’s meeting is the 345th that Norm has attended in serving this Committee, and I want to emphasize the word “serve.” His dedication, his integrity, and his willingness to do whatever needed to be done have meant that Committee members could always count on him. His institutional memory, as you well know, is prodigious, as of course are his files! @laugh From a personal perspective, he and his team have been a pleasure to work with throughout our meetings and in the preparation of the minutes and transcripts. And Norm always has an amusing story to contribute. I have here in my hand a piece of paper, which is a bit yellowed, signed in 1962 by William McChesney Martin, granting Norm access to the minutes and other confidential material of the Federal Open Market Committee. As the fourth Chairman of the Federal Reserve since then, I regret that I am the one to witness the end of Norm’s extraordinary forty-two years. Today, Norm, we hope you will consent to join us in having a group picture taken because we would like to have it inscribed and presented to you as a token of our sincere appreciation. Moreover, I understand that there is a cake in the next room prepared for us as part of the celebration. It has the number “345” outlined on its frosting. That, I can assure you, is not the calorie count. @laugh It’s commemorating the record number of FOMC meetings that you have attended. I know I speak for the entire Committee when I express our deep gratitude for your hard work, your good humor, and your steadfast support over the years. We wish you the very best in the future.

Speaker: MR. BERNARD
Statement: Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman. @applause Well, this is a surprise. I don’t know what to say. Obviously, I’ve stayed on about a dozen years beyond the time when I could have retired because I liked the work. I joined the FOMC Secretariat in 1968 and have enjoyed the experience immensely ever since. I’ve especially liked the work atmosphere and the people I have been working with. In some cases, I’ve known you for quite a long period. For example, as I look around the table, I’m reminded that two of you were fellow staff members in the 1960s—President Poole and Governor Gramlich. And several of my former colleagues from the 1960s became members of the Board of Governors, including Nancy Teeters, Martha Seger, Lyle Gramley, Chuck Partee, and Bob Holland. Governor Kohn came somewhat later. @laugh Presidents Yellen and Minehan worked here at the Board as well. The one constant was the high quality of the staff and the Committee members with whom I have worked. I’ve enjoyed my association with all of you over these many years, and I thank you for the friendship that you have expressed and you, Mr. Chairman, for your very kind words this morning. Again, thank you all very much. @applause

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the recent market trends based on the charts we've received. During the interim period, asset prices mostly traded within the year's range, but some trends were maintained, such as the yield curve's continued flattening, narrowing spreads, and the dollar's depreciation against major currencies. However, the focus shifted to signs of a potential growth slowdown until Friday's unexpectedly strong employment data.

The top panel on the first page shows the three-month deposit rate in black and forward rates in red. Forward rates dipped after the September employment report but rose again when oil prices fell and after the October employment report. Two-year note yields have increased by over 35 basis points since the last meeting, with most of the increase occurring in the past few days. Ten-year yields have risen about 20 basis points and remain in the 4 to 4¼ percent range. As a result, the yield curve has continued to flatten, with the two-year to ten-year spread narrowing further since the September meeting.

Some market participants interpret this flattening as a sign that inflationary concerns are contained. However, ten-year TIPS breakeven rates have widened since the September meeting, as shown in the top left panel of the next page. This recent widening may be due to the last CPI report, which rose 0.4 points, or the recent fall in oil prices, which could dampen upcoming CPI reports.

Spreads have continued to narrow, with the investment-grade spread at its lowest level since July 1998, and high-yield and emerging-market spreads also at multiyear lows. While some of the narrowing can be attributed to a search for yield, the strong corporate cash positions, improved balance sheets, and improved situations in some major emerging markets suggest that it can't be solely categorized as a liquidity story.

Equity prices have risen in recent days but are only slightly higher for the year, and implied volatilities are slightly higher but not materially so.

After the Committee's September meeting, OFHEO released its report on Fannie Mae's accounting methodology, causing Fannie's stock price to fall and spreads to widen initially but quickly retraced. The effect on Fannie

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Why don’t you do that?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Shall we do that now?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes, why don’t you get it out of the way?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Okay, fine. At the last meeting, the Committee asked Vincent and me to be a “committee of two” to review how the Desk operates during a period when the market strongly expects the Committee to change the target fed funds rate. I should say that most of the work was conducted by Spence Hilton, who is sitting to my left, and by Jim Clouse from the Board’s staff, who is also here today. I won’t repeat everything that’s in the memo, which I think speaks for itself, but I’ll summarize our primary conclusions. First, we found that the volatility of the fed funds rate does not seem to correlate with any major asset class. Therefore, to the extent that there is volatility in this narrow sector, it has no observable adverse spillover effects. We also believe that over time the Desk has the tools to deal with such circumstances. Indeed, we think we learned something from the August experience that helped us manage the funds rate around the September FOMC meeting. And over time, as both the Desk and bank reserve managers adapt to the new environment, I would expect this type of volatility to dissipate. If the Committee did wish to change the regime, the choices fall in two categories. First, FOMC meeting dates could consciously be aligned to coincide with the end of the reserve maintenance period. A variation would be for policy changes to take effect in the next reserve period. A second possibility would involve publishing reserve forecasts. Both of these regime-change options have some disadvantages, and the solutions themselves may create new problems. Thus, at the risk of seeming to push for the status quo, our recommendation is to maintain the current operating framework. Vincent, I don’t know if you have anything to add.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let me just say up front, since I was the one who raised the issue originally, that I thought you reviewed it quite effectively, and I personally concur with the position that you’ve come to. With respect to the TIPS ten-year rate, the oil price has had a very unusual effect on it over the years, and one of the reasons is that the spread of the crude oil seven-year futures has behaved rather differently relative to the spot price in recent years than it had previously. Since the spread between light sweet crude prices, for which the WTI is the best measure, and gasoline and home heating oil prices is reasonably flat, can’t we take the forward WTI price, convert it into a gasoline and home heating oil price and subtract that from the CPI—with the CPI weights—to get effectively a judgment as to what core rate is implied in the CPI, presuming that the food component is average? That gets around the question of trying to answer to what extent the recent oil prices affect the forecast of the CPI that is embodied in the TIPS yield. Is that something you can do readily?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: I know I haven’t done it.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: It’s something that we regularly do, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: You take the long-term forecast?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: We take the structure of WTI futures rates, use the 6 percent weight in the overall CPI and apply it to the non-seasonally-adjusted figure, and then subtract off to get a measure of the core.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: You don’t take the crude price? You take the crude plus markup, I assume. Is that what you’re saying?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: In that context, how would this very latest run-up since the end of October look?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Well, one thing I would note is that because of the backwardation of the futures curve, it’s in fact—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It’s lower, but it’s less lower than—

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Yes. So you have the two effects. You have the spot effect of the whole futures curve shifting up, which gives you the immediate increase in inflation compensation, but you also have the forecasted subsequent decline, which actually subtracts from it.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: And because the spot prices have moved so dramatically, though the spreads have not changed all that much, there has been a significant closing of the percentage gap in prices even though the absolute gap has opened up.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Right.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I was curious how all that turns out because it’s pretty evident that the ten-year TIPS compensation is giving us a quite significantly different reading. And it would be useful to know whether or not it’s merely a statistical issue—with one based on core inflation and the other based on the total CPI—or if there’s something more deep-seated there.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I would make two observations. One is that I haven’t actually seen the time series. My recollection is that it’s only a couple of points in the intermeeting period. It’s not a large number—on the order of 10 to 15 basis points. The second point is that five-to-ten-year forward inflation compensation is actually lower. So this is something that happened in the first five years of the term structure of inflation compensation. But we can do this adjustment for you in the time series perspective.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It’s terribly important for us to get judgments on what the TIPS is showing. We can always talk about its liquidity or say that it’s X or Y. Some day we’ll look back and say that the TIPS market was giving us signals and we had too many excuses as to why we weren’t looking at it.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: When we look at these special factors, which included the smaller-than- expected auction and the change in oil futures prices, it really is hard to get those special factors as a large component in the parsing. It’s on the order of 10 basis points out of the 40 basis point rise.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think that’s useful to know.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: We took some consolation from the fact that the five-to-ten-year forward inflation compensation really just moved sideways. And the ten-year forward rate, given the flattening in the term structure that Dino noted, basically also moved sideways.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Questions for Dino either on the presentation or on Vincent and Dino’s conclusions with respect to the discussion we had last time? Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I concur with Dino's assessment and your agreement that our current approach to open market operations is appropriate. The Desk's protests are intended to signal that Dino isn't predetermining the FOMC's decision, but rather aiming to prevent excessive reserve growth and potential volatility.

Regarding changing meeting dates, I've learned from my time in Vincent's seat that there are numerous factors influencing the selection of meeting dates. I don't believe we need to add another complication at this moment. The system is functioning well as is.

Dino, I'd like to add a comment to your point about the Fed's commentary on the current account deficit and its impact on the dollar. While I agree that the deficit is a significant economic imbalance, I believe the markets perceived your comments as an attempt to depreciate the dollar. I understand the need to acknowledge the deficit, but we must be mindful of the potential perception that the Fed is trying to manipulate the dollar.

The Treasury Department is responsible for commenting on the dollar. This division of labor has proven beneficial to us. We may not be able to accurately predict the dollar's future direction, and monetary policy has limited influence over the current account deficit, which is more of a fiscal policy issue.

The dollar is already experiencing downward pressure, and it's likely to continue falling in the long term. However, the risk of a disorderly decline is low, and we should exercise caution in our statements to avoid fueling unnecessary market volatility.

There have been concerns that the Fed and the Treasury are at odds regarding dollar policy. I don't believe this is a productive approach, especially in a sensitive market environment. Therefore, while we cannot avoid discussing the current account deficit, we must be particularly careful with our language regarding the dollar at this stage in the cycle.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let’s stay on the subject. Are you on this subject?

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Yes, I was going to stay on this subject. It’s always a pleasure to echo Don. I don’t know that I fully internalize Fed doctrine on imbalances and exchange rate policy and things like that, but let me just say something from the perspective of my previous life. I always had the sense sitting there in my position at the Treasury that U.S. policymakers should always be careful not to convey the sense that they actually know the desirable direction for the dollar and that they should not in any way suggest that they were going to try to induce that move. I think that’s true in general, but it’s much more important when we’re talking about a prospective decline. Once established, the sense that a decline is desirable is very hard to reverse, and it makes it very hard to manage the risk of a more acute decline causing broader collateral damage to asset prices. So I just wanted to make the obvious point. Even if we’re very careful and even if we allude only very generally and indirectly to our concern about the size of the imbalances and their unsustainability—and that an adjustment in relative prices likely has to come as part of the unwinding—I think our musings in public will make it harder for the Administration to discipline its own ranks, which is always a formidable challenge. Let me also echo the conclusion of the committee of two. I thought the analysis was nicely framed and did a good job of looking at the alternatives. And those alternatives met that critical test, which was to illustrate why they were not particularly compelling against the regime in which we operate now.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: During our meeting, I'd like to bring up a complex issue we need to address. As we work on monetary policy and link it to the global economy, we can't dismiss the current account deficit as insignificant. We can't claim it will correct itself without market forces playing a crucial role. However, we must avoid giving the impression that we're intentionally devaluing the dollar to fix the imbalances. This is a delicate issue, and I've been invited to speak on a European panel about the euro next week. I plan to discuss the current account balance, its causes, and the historical evidence that high deficits in developed countries have adjusted without crisis. I'll also emphasize the uncertainty surrounding exchange rate forecasts and the importance of fiscal policy in addressing government deficits. We have limited success in encouraging private saving, and there's a growing recognition within the Administration that this issue needs addressing. By focusing on fiscal policy, we can make a difference. Let's try to avoid implying an imminent dollar decline, as this could harm the FOMC's credibility and potentially create instability in the markets. Any thoughts, colleagues?

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Could I ask Karen a question?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Sure, by all means.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Karen, I have a question on whether we’re seeing a change in emerging Asia in exchange rate policies and practices. I’m curious about whether you think there’s a common factor behind it in the following sense: Do we see across these countries a significant change in underlying inflation that has caused them to decide that they want more independence in monetary policy? Or are we seeing something that is more idiosyncratic, with different sets of forces at work?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Well, as I will repeat in just a minute, I’m afraid, emerging Asia actually is faring comparatively less well than the other regions of the world at the moment on two scores: higher oil prices and a perception that at least for a time there’s a bit of a pause in the strength of the high-tech sector. And these countries see themselves vulnerable to the fact that they import more primary products than anybody else in some sense. Few of them have primary product resources domestically. So the inflation consequences of developments over the last few months have loomed larger for them than for others, and I think in that sense they are reassessing how unfortunate an exchange rate appreciation would be for them. At the same time, their real performance is impaired as well, so there’s sort of a juggling act involved, as with any negative supply shock. But I think seeing that they are more vulnerable than most has altered a bit the calculus of how important it is to stabilize the exchange rate vis-à-vis each other and vis-à-vis the renminbi. And they probably are more vulnerable than China, if that’s the elephant in the room, because China is a much larger and much more closed economy and it is domestically able to deal with some of these issues better than the smaller satellite emerging Asian countries are.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Further questions for Dino or Vincent?

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Mr. Chairman, do we need to move approval of the domestic operations?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes, let’s first move approval of the Desk’s domestic operations. Would somebody like to do so?

Speaker: SPEAKER
Statement: Yes. I so move.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection, they are approved. Now, we’ve had three comments on the memo that was circulated and that Dino has discussed. Unless I hear significant negatives, I will assume that those three “votes” so to speak capture the consensus of the group. So if any you would disagree with the recommendation of Dino and Vincent, please speak up. Or if anyone wishes to add something relevant to their conclusion that reinforces it, speak up. @laugh

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Other than good job?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I guess you win on both counts. We will move on then to David Stockton and Karen Johnson.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: [ERROR]

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement:
During our recent meeting, I wanted to share some updates on the economic developments we've seen abroad since our last session. The news has been somewhat disappointing, leading us to revise down our outlook for real GDP growth in other countries. The largest downward revision, amounting to 0.4 percentage points, is for the third quarter, as growth in Japan, Europe, and emerging Asia, excluding China, fell short of expectations. In contrast, China and some Latin American countries have surprised us on the upside.

Oil prices have been a significant factor in our decision to revise down our outlook for foreign growth. Although the sharp increase in spot WTI prices attracted attention, the far futures price for WTI rose on balance by $5 per barrel over the period. This upward shift in the entire path of oil futures prices has led us to project that oil prices will remain higher for longer than we previously anticipated.

The industrial countries have experienced small negative revisions to their growth outlook due to the erosion of confidence caused by elevated oil prices and weakened domestic demand. In Canada, growth has remained strong, but we've made a slight downward adjustment due to changes in the U.S. forecast. For emerging-market economies in Asia, the prospect of sustained, elevated oil prices has more negative consequences than for Latin American countries, as they generally import oil and other primary commodities.

Despite the slowing growth in some countries, we expect a rebound in the current quarter, particularly in Japan, the United Kingdom, and emerging Asia. However, we anticipate that average growth abroad will weaken slightly over the next two years. This projection is due to the need for some rapidly growing economies, such as Canada and the United Kingdom, to bring growth in line with their potential, as well as China's efforts to slow credit creation and investment spending.

The depreciation of the dollar over the intermeeting period and the further depreciation we've incorporated into the forecast will also restrain growth in the other industrial countries. In our baseline scenario, the combined effect of higher oil prices, the lower dollar, and revisions to output growth here and abroad is a slight easing in the projected drag from real net exports in this quarter and during the next two years.

This morning, we received data on September trade, which showed a noticeably narrower trade

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Questions for our colleagues? President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Karen, there are two themes in your discussion that I’d like you to comment on a bit more to try to reconcile them. On the one hand, you say that the increase in energy prices in the last year to year and a half has been driven primarily by increases in worldwide demand. Then on the other hand, you say that the price increase we’ve seen is a reason to mark down forecasts of demand. So, it’s a question really of whether the demand curve is shifting—and prices are high because the whole demand curve has shifted out as a consequence of higher growth—or whether we’re sliding along the demand curve.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I guess we’re learning as we go along the consequences of having global demand push on resource limitations the way it has been. And we are making adjustments of a tenth here and a tenth there. So, it’s not as if analytically my demand curves are well enough defined that I can even tell the difference between moving along a curve versus the curve shifting at this point. I think down deep that strong demand is the central factor in explaining why oil prices in particular, but commodity prices more broadly, have been high. There is a self-limiting nature to the price-adjustment process that occurs, but it’s hard to say that those higher prices are then a cause for weaker growth. It is just a matter of both growth and prices being resolved in the marketplace. But I think that recent events, particularly the events in the past intermeeting period, have conveyed an awareness that the supply conditions that this higher demand is confronting are a bit less robust and a little less certain than people previously thought. In the energy case, this mix between sweet and sour oil suddenly seemed more important. Whereas we often talked—I myself did—about total production versus total consumption and questioned where the missing barrels were and so forth, now we need to think about the possibility that the important issue may not be just total production but the composition of production and the composition of refinery capacity. So it may be that the elements of demand in the different economies are going to determine how this works out. We’ve learned, for example, that hurricanes in the Gulf of Mexico have a much greater potential to disrupt supplies than we understood to have been the case. So I think this extra uncertainty and the somewhat greater volatility and risk on the supply side are leading me in an expected-value sense to say that the outcome of these demand pressures and what I know about supply may involve a little less growth than I had previously thought.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: I had a question on the auto outlook. The people that we talk to in the industry are rather conservative about the outlook for next year. They’re expecting relatively flat sales. Now, they’re not always right, of course. But the Greenbook has sales going up 4 percent next year to a record level and then going up another 3 percent in 2006. I was wondering if you could comment on the rationale for that and how it compares with the more-conservative forecasts that we’re hearing from those in the auto industry.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Well, our projection is certainly above those forecasts, as you point out. Certainly one feature of our stronger outlook for motor vehicles is that we have lower interest rates—and noticeably lower interest rates—than are currently built into market expectations. So the financing environment that we think is going to prevail is quite favorable. Another factor is that we’re still a little more optimistic on longer-term potential output growth than the consensus forecast. So in some sense we think there’s going to be greater wherewithal to purchase motor vehicles. Now, whether we have it right in terms of how that splits up between the numbers of units produced and the value per unit could certainly be open to some question. But in general in an economy in which the unemployment rate is continuing to come down, energy prices are reversing some of this year’s run-up, and interest rates surprise on the low side, we don’t think our forecast is too much of a stretch. We are aware, however, that it is higher than the automakers are currently saying and higher than most other outside forecasters are currently projecting.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Remember, that a flat forecast—because scrappage is rising—implies a significant slowdown in the rate of increase in the number of vehicles on the road. That probably has to occur at some point because we’re running out of space. But that’s a forecast that was more confidently made a decade ago, as the market continued to rise. I think it has become a very tough forecast to make. In my view, David is just as likely to be right as the whole structure of the research operation of GM.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: It is a tough forecast to make, and of course, the automakers may also be running out of room to provide incentives or price cuts and still be viable.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I’m just worried about running out of street space.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: That’s another constraint.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: I just wondered, David, if you would give us some sense of the sensitivity of the baseline forecast to changes in the budget outlook that we made reference to earlier. What happens if the tax cuts and everything else are made permanent? What is the implication of some of the fiscal actions that people have been bandying about? How much does it matter in terms of the forecast?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Well, obviously, it could matter considerably. We are anticipating the budget deficit basically to remain at $410 billion or thereabouts in fiscal year 2005 and to edge down only to $390 billion in 2006. If one were concerned that the current political configuration were going to result in less fiscal discipline, or at least had the possibility of producing an environment with less discipline, one could imagine more spending. And on the tax side, certainly making the tax provisions permanent would be likely to boost interest rates, and there’s already some expectation of that built into current interest rates. So once it happened or if that uncertainty were removed, we might get somewhat higher long-term interest rates coming about from that, but it wouldn’t be a complete innovation in the market’s thinking at this point. We are not very good political prognosticators. So in essence, in this forecast we are anticipating continued supplementals to support ongoing operations in Iraq. We have incorporated the tax law changes that have occurred to date, but we aren’t anticipating any further significant cuts, and there is implicitly some restraint on nondefense nondiscretionary spending in here. That is an area where it seems to me the uncertainty could perhaps be the greatest. Having said that, I must say that I can think of a lot of other things that would have a bigger and more immediate impact on the economy. We have shot a whole lot of fiscal bullets off here, and it’s rather hard to imagine another couple of years of stimulus that would average the 1 percent that we’ve seen over the last three years. One could hope or imagine that there might be more discipline than we’ve built into our forecast, though I don’t see the distribution of risks as strongly supporting worries about too much fiscal restraint at this point. So I think somewhere between the basically neutral fiscal policy that we’ve built into the forecast and the stimulus that has occurred over the last three years is probably in the right neighborhood. We have something that averages a little less than ¼ point in 2005 and 2006 as extra stimulus from fiscal policy.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: David, maybe I misperceived this, but your model seemed to have trouble generating a high inflation outcome. What is it that produces a “high inflation” scenario with almost no material increase in inflation? The difference in the monetary policy assumption that underpins that scenario is not that large. So is there some failure of imagination in the forecast that we could correct for?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: I certainly welcome your imagination @laugh if you wish to apply it to this particular problem. My colleague, David Wilcox, who presented some of this material in his chart show presentation in July, might want to jump in here. But it isn’t aggregate demand, as you point out, or policy assumptions because inflation in the United States is very inertial and not terribly sensitive to changes in the output gap. Now I know when telling our stories that, because we have so few hooks on which to produce inflation in the forecast, the monetary policy assumption can often sound as if it features very prominently when, in fact, it doesn’t. There’s obviously considerable uncertainty on the supply side, both with respect to things like oil prices or commodity prices that could, if they doubled again in the coming year—and one could imagine such a development—provide significant upside surprise or an even more pronounced slowing in structural labor productivity than we are projecting. We’re concerned about that latter possibility as well, and we showed an alternative simulation with a productivity slowdown. Again, one could use even more imagination and think that maybe it would slow even more dramatically than we’re showing, in which case cost pressures on businesses are going to manifest themselves more intensely than we’re currently anticipating in the projection. So those are a few stories that one could tell. The honest truth about the width of the fan charts that we show with regard to our forecast is that a lot of that is just unexplained residual in the model. The models are explaining only about 60 percent of the year-to-year variations in the inflation rate. We have been wrong in the past on inflation, and we will be wrong in the future. Our models seem to explain only a portion of the variation in inflation, and we recognize that our ignorance is a big source of uncertainty in the outlook.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Two of the questions I had teed up to ask have been asked and answered. Let me just focus on one. The staff continues to have an assumption of less tightening in the fed funds rate than is expected in the markets. I’m not sure if that has changed a lot since the September meeting on either side. You are saying, I think, that with last Friday’s labor market report in hand you would have raised the forecast in this month’s Greenbook a bit. I was wondering if you would also suggest a different trajectory for the fed funds rate embedded in your forecast

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Well, I think the employment report would not change our funds rate forecast very much. One of the things that I tried to highlight in my remarks, though, is that there are a couple of really major pieces of the outlook—such as movements in the exchange value of the dollar and movements in the stock market—where as a matter of joint strategy we are embedding in the forecast a pretty neutral assumption.
