For example, the dollar’s path is almost like a random walk, and the stock market will just go up at the risk-adjusted return that one can get in the bond market. Now, it’s quite conceivable to me that participants in the market might be factoring in some combination of developments in those areas that, with the kind of funds rate path we’ve written down, could produce more stimulus either because spending will be more interest sensitive or because the transmission of the funds rate into asset markets could be more powerful than we’re assuming in our forecast. So we write down our funds rate path as sort of a conditioned assumption and, obviously, as developments change, we react to that. But in your considerations as a policymaker and in thinking about the full range of the risks that you’re confronted with, you might want to ask, Could it be that the setting of the funds rate would produce either considerably more spending or inflation than the staff has incorporated? I also noted that it’s still the case—and it’s one of the factors that we are struggling with and don’t really have a clear answer to—that we haven’t over the past two years gotten as much activity out of very, very low real interest rates as we would have anticipated. As I noted in response to a question by President Yellen last time, some of that is apparent in housing and some in business investment also where, even though the growth rates have recovered, the level of spending as a share of GDP looks to us pretty darn depressed relative to the trends that one might have anticipated in response to these low rates. And in our forecast we’re carrying forward some of that unexplained weakness. Obviously, if that unexplained weakness were to diminish more rapidly than we envision—if we get more spending and more output—we’d have a higher funds rate path associated with the outlook as well.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Further questions? Karen, I noticed that the unit import price excluding petroleum for the month of October, which was released today, is negative for the first time this year. Do you have any notions as to what that is about?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I’m afraid you’ve caught me without an answer. I’m not sure what the elements of that are.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Could you take a look at that when you have a chance?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Sure.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: If there are no other questions, who would like to start the Committee discussion? President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some updates on the Seventh District economy since our last meeting. Many of our manufacturing contacts outside the auto industry are reporting strong orders and sales, with a growing backlog, particularly in heavy equipment. Engine producers are struggling to keep up with demand and have put truck manufacturers on allocation. Caterpillar's CEO mentioned that business is the best he's seen in his thirty-two-year career. However, Ford and GM reported disappointing October results and reduced their light vehicle sales outlook for next year to around 16.5 million units.

Our area's labor markets are showing improvement as well. Businesses report more hiring, and Kelly, the temporary staffing firm, notes that orders are rising rapidly, and permanent placement fees continue to increase. They also mention that it's becoming harder to find qualified workers. Skilled tradespeople in manufacturing and even part-time retail help for the holiday season are in short supply.

Although growth is more solid, we're not seeing significant upward pressures on wages and prices that would indicate the output gap is fully closed. Some contacts report wages are rising somewhat faster, but overall growth in wages remains modest. Suppliers are finding it easier to pass higher costs along to their business customers in construction materials and heavy equipment. The weaker dollar is also raising the wholesale prices of imported goods. However, there's little evidence of a general pass-through of higher input costs to consumer prices.

Turning to the national outlook, the economy has emerged from the midyear soft patch, with consumer spending rebounding and contacts less skittish about demand. However, the data and anecdotes don't suggest a widespread surge in growth. Instead, it feels like growth that is only modestly faster than potential, despite substantial monetary accommodation. Higher energy prices and business caution are holding back growth. Businesses seem more risk-averse and are making decisions more slowly, discarding projects they might otherwise have undertaken. It's challenging to quantify the impact of these factors, but I believe they're significant. Therefore, it's appropriate to continue being measured in removing policy accommodation. If we see energy prices raising long-run inflation expectations or business caution and resource slack dissipating substantially, we may need to move more aggressively.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent conversations with industry contacts. Firstly, my Wal-Mart source reported weak demand, with August same-store sales showing the lowest growth rate in a while, and September-October sales following suit. Wal-Mart projects same-store sales growth of 2-4% moving forward, with a current estimate of 2.9%. My trucking contact mentioned an okay economy but no boom, with some issues in rail and trucking capacity. A truck assembly plant is currently shipping trucks without tires due to a shortage, but this seems to be a spot issue.

On a positive note, my FedEx contact reported strong demand across all sectors. Their ground business is up 16% year over year, and domestic freight is up 14%. FedEx anticipates a 20-25% increase in capital expenditures next year. They've seen no wage pressures yet but expect some. The IT labor market has tightened, with UPS hiring aggressively for the holiday season and even bringing on new pilots. UPS has revised up its volume projections for next year, particularly in international business and shipments from Asia.

A contact at a West Coast software firm reported a modest PC forecast, around 7-10%, and a brutally more competitive labor market for hiring software engineers and general management talent. They've also been affected by the reduction in visas for foreign talent.

Regarding the "normal" real federal funds rate, I've been pondering this issue. Historically, it's quite variable, but we might be entering the range of what could be considered "low normal." However, we're not quite there yet. The data since the Korean War shows just how variable the short-term real federal funds rate can be. That's all for now, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to express my gratitude for the opportunity to speak today. The Red Sox win was certainly a morale booster, but I believe the economy in New England is expanding independently as well. Employment is growing or stable in all six states, and the unemployment rate is down. Inflation rates are trending down, and Boston's inflation rate is decreasing more than the national average. Consumer and business confidence have improved, and tourism in Boston has been strong, although it's been affected by cool and rainy weather elsewhere in New England. Residential real estate markets in major metropolitan areas remain strong, particularly for low to moderately priced homes. Manufacturing and retail contacts were generally upbeat, projecting reasonable to strong demand and revenue growth.

However, concerns and uncertainty remain. Growth is variable, with firms in the health care and defense industries growing more rapidly than others. We've been quite busy at the Boston Fed since our last meeting. We've held forums for local bankers and businessmen, met with our Academic Advisory Council and software companies, and had two meetings of our directors.

Manufacturers, particularly, expressed concern about rising oil and natural gas prices and commodity prices such as copper, aluminum, and resin. One contact in electrical and electronics manufacturing described an environment of "hyperinflation," noting that price increases could sometimes be passed on to big box retailers. Most manufacturers and retailers expressed concern about the impact of higher oil prices on both demand and costs.

Contacts remain cautious about hiring, even when expected growth and net revenue gains are positive. They view a new employee as an addition to "fixed costs," given their past experiences during the recession. Sarbanes-Oxley is also contributing to caution, particularly in the hiring and spending arenas. Finding new employees with the right technical skills is both difficult and expensive. And most contacts mentioned rising benefit costs as a further deterrent to hiring.

Plans for increased capital spending range from modest to relatively strong, with a continued focus on reducing costs and improving productivity. Most contacts expect profit margins to remain solid, especially in the biotech area. The region's exporters have benefited from the slowly falling dollar. One paper and pulp producer reported record overseas sales and the ability to make price increases stick in high-grade products. High-tech contacts in software

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on the current economic landscape in the District. Our economic expansion continues, but it's not as exuberant as before, aligning closely with the national trend. Housing markets are generally robust, except for a few exceptions. Consumer spending remains solid in retail and travel sectors. Our ports are quite busy, with some even being overburdened due to heightened import and export activity. However, there are signs of slowing demand growth in manufacturing, as some of our contacts report. Job gains remain modest, and unemployment has only decreased slightly, resulting in ample labor supply and no significant worker shortages or wage increases.

Turning to the national economy, recent data have shown mixed signals, but overall, growth appears sustainable, though not as robust as last year. The October employment report was reassuring, with strong job growth and a decline in labor market slack. However, the Greenbook forecast suggests only a meager acceleration in activity next year, despite continued monetary stimulus and a shallow upward path to the funds rate.

The forecast is plausible due to several reasons, including high oil prices, weaker fiscal stimulus, a potential rise in the private saving rate, and a persistent trade gap. Business investment in equipment and software is not expected to rebound at a pace that would lead to robust growth.

The IT sector poses risks to the forecast, as the Greenbook forecast for IT investment has been revised down significantly due to disappointing data. However, I consider this forecast strong, as it anticipates a high share of IT investment in total equipment investment.

Inflation remains contained, with favorable recent data and well-contained core inflation and longer-term inflation expectations. The Greenbook forecast for core PCE and CPI inflation of 1.5% and 1.8% respectively, seems reasonable, but there are upside risks from oil and the dollar.

Given the contained inflation, lack of real momentum in output growth, and remaining economic slack, I don't see a need for a more rapid tightening of monetary policy without further evidence. Today's move to raise the funds rate by 25 basis points seems sensible, but the appropriate move in December is still uncertain. We should craft a statement that leaves all options open.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Fifth District economy's recent performance. Manufacturing growth is still expanding, but it's showing signs of slowing down. Our shipments index dropped a bit but remains positive. However, the new orders index is approaching neutral, and our manufacturing employment index remains neutral as well. Many manufacturing contacts mentioned higher material prices. A manager at Rohm and Haas in North Carolina reported ethylene and propylene price increases but held the line on product prices. A G.E. manager in South Carolina noted rising raw material prices but said turbine prices were increasing even faster.

Our service sector continues to indicate strong growth in revenues and employment. Retail activity seems to have slowed since our last report, with big ticket sales particularly weak. Most auto dealers reported sales were down. One energy business director believes energy prices are unsustainably high and expects them to come down significantly soon. He also mentioned that many CEOs are complaining about the costs of complying with Sarbanes-Oxley and the lack of skilled personnel to implement those systems.

The October jobs report shows stronger employment growth than previously anticipated, indicating a better labor market outlook. Aggregate hours and average hourly earnings are both up, providing a boost to personal income and supporting consumer spending. Rising wages and slower productivity growth have pushed up unit labor costs, but inflation and inflation expectations remain anchored.

The economy appears to be doing better than expected, and if it continues to strengthen, we'll need to keep raising real interest rates. We'll likely face two concerns moving forward: the weak business investment spending and the discrepancy between market expectations and our forecast. We'll want to keep real interest rate increases measured to encourage investment spending but may need to act decisively if inflation pressures develop. We should avoid signaling a steeper expected funds rate path only in response to inflation expectations to maintain price stability. It's challenging to determine the necessary interest rate tightening in the period ahead, but we must avoid encouraging the belief that we'll only adjust rates in response to tangible signs of inflation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to begin by discussing the region's economy, which has shown steady expansion since our last meeting. Although there was some hesitance from businesses and consumers due to the election, we're witnessing some positive developments. Consumer spending has increased slightly, while auto manufacturing experienced a slowdown due to the withdrawal of incentives. However, I anticipate sales incentives will return as the year progresses.

Energy activity in our region has surged due to higher oil and natural gas prices, leading to increased activity. With the election behind us, businesses and consumers are optimistic about a pickup in activity. Furthermore, small manufacturing has shown significant growth, which is encouraging.

Looking ahead, we anticipate a healthy economic growth rate of 3½ to 4 percent in 2005 and 2006, as others have suggested. The accommodative monetary policy and easing energy prices should contribute to this growth. The recent payroll numbers provide evidence of a more positive forecast. Prices may rise by a tenth or two in the first part of next year, but there are upside and downside risks to consider.

On the upside, the removal of market uncertainty is a significant factor. The labor market's strength is evident, with an average monthly increase of about 190,000 over the last four months. This could lead to increased consumer spending. Additionally, adjustments in the dollar may result in a reduction of the current account deficit, which would be less of a drag on GDP numbers.

However, there are downside risks, such as an increase in personal saving, which may offset consumer spending. Energy prices remain a wild card and could impact the economy as we move forward.

In conclusion, the outlook is favorable, and I recommend continuing to remove policy accommodation and moving towards the neutral stance of policy. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to express my optimism regarding the current state of the national economy. Over the past six months, real GDP has grown at a rate above 4%, which is higher than the average growth rate of 3.6% during the 1990s expansion and with less volatility. Our economic models suggest that this growth is above the projected trend growth rate of around 3¼%, indicating no significant output gaps. Consumer spending remains strong, and investment spending is contributing positively to growth.

However, we cannot ignore potential risks such as oil prices, geopolitical tensions, and fiscal policy. While the recent unexpectedly strong job growth number is a bonus, we should not overemphasize it or expect a return to the 300,000 plus monthly job growth of the 1990s. Productivity growth may have been overestimated in the past, and we should focus on getting the funds rate back to a more neutral stance.

In our district, consumer spending is good, and state government tax collections are increasing. Housing construction remains high, and the manufacturing sector's structural downsizing seems to be mostly behind us. However, hurricanes have had an impact, particularly on tourism and the airline industry. Major tourist destinations have been largely unaffected, but smaller markets have been hit hard. Advance bookings for next fall are down, and high fuel prices have caused significant losses for airlines like Delta and AirTran. Housing sales may be leveling off as one-time effects wind down.

Additionally, Hurricane Ivan destroyed a significant number of oil and gas pipelines in the Gulf of Mexico, resulting in a loss of around 4½% of yearly oil production. This represents about a third of total U.S. production, and refineries have struggled to make up for the loss. Despite these challenges, I believe we are on a very acceptable track with accommodative policy, election uncertainty behind us, and a promising employment picture. I hope we will continue to remove accommodation as financial markets expect.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from our recent discussions with advisory boards and data collection about the economic activity in the Third District. Manufacturing remains a strong performer, despite a dip in the September business outlook survey index of general activity to 13.4. However, the sharp rebound to 28 in October suggests that this was an anomaly. New orders and shipments remain healthy, and the employment index has strengthened. About 40% of firms plan to increase production in Q4, while 40% will maintain current levels, and only 20% plan reductions. Inventories of finished goods are expected to remain stable.

Manufacturing is expected to continue its strength through the end of the year. Retail sales of general merchandise are growing moderately, with Christmas sales forecasted to be good but not excessively strong. Home sales and housing-price appreciation remain robust in our three states, but residential construction activity has moderated. Nonresidential construction is still soft, but there's a pickup in the commercial sector.

Employment data are a bit old, but based on unrevised numbers, our three-state region's employment growth was stronger than the nation's. The labor markets in New Jersey are the strongest, while those in Pennsylvania are the weakest, with Philadelphia experiencing particular weakness. Payrolls declined in the city over the past quarter, but businesses continue to report difficulties finding qualified workers.

Nationally, the economy has bounced back from the summer soft patch, with payroll employment data now consistent with this view. Growth this year is expected to be in the 3½ to 4% range, despite rising oil prices. Inflation remains low, with payrolls growing at about 200,000 jobs per month.

Given the economic data and market expectations, I suggest we continue our path of removing accommodation at a measured pace and maintain most of the language in our last announcement. However, we should begin considering our longer-term policy strategy. The employment report suggests the expansion may now be on a solid footing, but we need to be prepared for potential risks such as higher oil prices and a sharper depreciation of the dollar, which could put pressure on inflation in the future. If data between now and our December meeting remains consistent, the Committee may want to consider preparing the markets for a

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on the economic conditions in our district, which includes Ohio. While Ohio may have played a significant role in determining the presidential election, its impact on the economy's direction is less clear. I'm afraid my report may not align with the overall optimism.

Businesspeople I've spoken to in our district have reported modest growth in production and sales. There's a noticeable divide between those who sell locally and those who sell nationally and internationally. The former group expresses pessimism, while the latter group is more optimistic.

Despite the strong October employment report, our district is undergoing an industrial realignment that might not reflect national trends. Manufacturing jobs continue to decline, with a loss of 5,000 jobs in October following a decline of 14,000 jobs in September. Employment in our district remains 4% lower than at the peak of the last business cycle, while national employment is up only 1%.

The October employment report aligns with the improving national picture, but I'm cautious about reading too much into one month's data. Most business contacts, regardless of where they do business, expect to meet rising demand without adding substantially to their payrolls. This suggests productivity could be higher than the baseline forecast.

Businesses continue to report cost pressures, particularly from oil prices and higher prices for nonpetroleum materials. However, I haven't seen evidence of these cost pressures leading to generalized inflation. Retail prices and wage growth remain subdued, and inflation expectations are steady.

Businesses are finding it difficult to pass on these price increases to their customers, especially those producing finished goods. As a result, they're focusing on production process adjustments to substitute away from expensive commodities. One of my directors, a large international producer of vinyl siding and decking materials, is investing in raw material substitution.

Given these circumstances, I believe our monetary policy stance is appropriate at the moment. However, my future policy recommendations will depend on incoming data on economic activity. I also want to maintain the necessary flexibility to adjust policy as economic developments unfold. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on the District's economy and the housing market in particular. We've seen some mixed signals in consumer spending, with strong sales in auto and non-auto retail, followed by a slower month. However, tourism has picked up due to better weather, and manufacturing and agriculture are doing well.

Recently, we held a meeting at the Bank to discuss housing and residential construction. With concerns of a potential housing bubble and the high level of construction activity, we invited developers, lenders, consultants, and economists to share their perspectives. While there was little worry about a bubble, they did anticipate a slowdown in the pace of increase.

Three key takeaways from the meeting: first, the strength in housing is largely due to favorable financial conditions, including low interest rates and lower down-payment requirements. Some lenders expressed concern that credit conditions have become too easy. Second, in some markets, up to 30% of new unit purchases are by investors, not intending to occupy the property immediately. Lastly, there's an acceleration of purchases by first-time homebuyers, fearing they'll be priced out of the market.

On a national level, the economy remains favorable, with growth on an upward trend and low inflation expected. I believe the economy will grow faster next year than projected, and the real federal funds rate is likely lower than what's needed in the long run.

That's a brief summary of the meeting and the current economic outlook. I'm open to any questions or comments.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Shall we take a coffee break and return in fifteen minutes, please.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: First Vice President Holcomb.

Speaker: MS. HOLCOMB
Statement: Thank you. I appreciate the opportunity to represent the Dallas Federal Reserve Bank here today. It has given me the opportunity to have some very high quality meetings with our economists over the three short weeks since Bob McTeer indicated that he was leaving the Bank. First, looking at our District economy, over the period since the last FOMC meeting economic growth in the Eleventh District appears to have continued at a moderate pace. Payroll employment in June, July, August, and September showed quite modest gains. Like the nation, other indicators showed more strength than the payroll numbers. Consumption, as reflected by sales tax receipts, is growing at its most rapid pace since 1998. Personal income is up for the seventh consecutive quarter, and surveys of business leaders across Texas show that business confidence is gaining strength. However, these positive statistics seem to be somewhat at odds with the anecdotal reports that we have received from our head office and Branch boards of directors. For example, I mentioned that consumption has shown considerable strength, but from our directors we’ve heard numerous stories about the damping impact of higher gasoline prices on consumption. One of our El Paso directors is a rancher. He offered a story about how the four cowboys he hires all showed up to work last spring, each driving his own pickup truck and pulling his own horse trailer. The same group showed up a few weeks ago, but all four shared a ride in one pickup and pulled one trailer for all of the horses. @laugh They said that the cost of gasoline made the difference. I can assure you that in West Texas no one worries about running out of street space.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I thought there was more horsepower. @laugh

Speaker: MS. HOLCOMB
Statement: That’s as close to a Bob McTeer story as you’ll get from me. @laugh

Speaker: SPEAKER
Statement: It’s better than some of his! @laugh

Speaker: MS. HOLCOMB
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to share some positive news about the impact of higher energy prices on our District economy. Although oil and gas activity had been expanding slowly earlier this year due to the price increase, there are now signs of a change. In the last two months, we've seen an uptick in exploration and improvement projects announced by both major and independent companies. Even in the Dallas–Fort Worth metropolitan area, where production had been virtually shuttered for the past eighteen years, energy production is expanding.

Moreover, the office markets in Dallas and Austin are showing declining vacancy rates, indicating a sense of optimism that began last summer. Commercial loan activity at Texas banks has also grown nicely in the last two quarters, which could be a reflection of improved business confidence and the start of expansion activities.

Two additional sectors that have shown improvement are worth mentioning. First, our directors and Beige Book contacts report an absence of wage pressures but some modest uptick in price pressures. Second, a development that may alleviate price pressures over the next few quarters is the drop in medical malpractice insurance premiums due to the $250,000 cap on non-economic damages in medical malpractice suits, which was approved by Texas voters in 2002. Thirteen companies providing malpractice insurance have reentered the state, and premiums have dropped by 8 to 17 percent in the last year.

Turning to the national economy, it's doing well and is expected to grow despite several headwinds. These include sagging consumer confidence, less business confidence, high energy prices, declining leading indicators, the expiration of bonus depreciation, and slower demand growth in the rest of the world. However, the primary tailwind is coming from monetary policy, and the strengthened balance sheets of businesses, along with good profitability growth, provide another tailwind that can support higher investment spending in the future.

The inflation outlook is mixed, with annualized CPI inflation at 0.6 percent over the last three months and PCE at 0.2 percent. Core measures of inflation are in the 1.5 to 2 percent range, and one-year inflation expectations range from 2.3 percent to over 3 percent. Long-term inflation expectations are anchored at or above 2.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement:
During our meeting, I'd like to share my perspective on the national economic outlook. While anecdotal reports suggest some uncertainty about demand growth, the national numbers have remained solid. Surveys indicate a modest decline in expansion expectations, but this is still a relatively small decrease from high levels. Inflation concerns persist, despite deceleration in price indexes. Overall, the economy has shown remarkable resilience in the face of rising oil prices, terror concerns, and election uncertainty.

Our forecast for real GDP growth is between 3.5 and 4 percent for 2005 and 2006, with core PCE around 1.5 percent. We see risks as balanced around this forecast, but our forecast is slightly less positive on growth compared to the Greenbook, primarily due to net exports. Our quarterly pattern shows some deceleration, but if we average the two quarters, there's also a decline.

We have less confidence in the now-prevailing equilibrium rate of interest being as low as many believe and are uncertain about the need to lean against potential headwinds, such as a higher household saving rate, fiscal contraction, negative net exports, and sustained business caution. The Greenbook path for monetary policy is significantly lower than the market path, so we assume a different path, closer to the one priced into markets.

Employment and hours are expected to grow moderately, leading to healthy compensation growth and some deceleration in consumption expenditures. Businesses continue to invest strongly. Growth outside the United States has moderated but remains on a path around estimates of potential.

Productivity growth is expected to slow before returning to the medium-term trend of around 2.5 percent. Associated unit labor costs will be absorbed by shrinking profit margins as competition holds down pricing power. With output growth close to potential, inflation should stay contained even as the gap narrows.

However, this view is exceptionally benign, and there are risks of higher inflation. We may face more dollar depreciation, increased pass-through, and higher energy price pass-through. Core finished goods in the PPI are still showing high rates of increase, and some models suggest more-elevated rates of increase and a higher probability of increases above our comfort range. Inflation expectations have risen, but the dollar's resumed decline has not yet caused significant negative

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the current economic situation. The economy has continued to grow solidly, with inflation remaining low, despite the increase in oil prices and the fading fiscal stimulus. The accommodative monetary and fiscal policies, along with anchored inflation expectations, have helped mitigate the negative impact on both output and prices.

However, the rise in oil prices and the waning fiscal impetus will restrain demand for a few quarters. Nevertheless, the stabilization of oil prices and favorable financial conditions should support growth in the long term. Financial conditions have become even more accommodative since our last meeting, with the fall of the dollar, rise in equity prices, and narrowing of credit spreads.

Even if productivity growth slows down, as forecasted, I believe that resource slack and elevated profit margins will keep inflation expectations in check. The alternative simulations in the Greenbook show growth and inflation not far from the baseline, which I don't attribute solely to a lack of imagination on the part of the staff. Instead, it reflects the strong underlying fundamentals and reduced risks around a desirable economic outcome.

The expectations of gradual and low interest rates, as seen in both the market and the staff forecast, are crucial for this outcome. The market anticipates a rise in interest rates to only around 3% by the end of next year, and the staff sees a shallow incline to 2¾% at the end of 2006. This implies fewer than half of the future meetings with rate increases through the end of next year.

The low inflation expectations and slow rise in real interest rates are a reflection of the recent economic experience. Businesses and households have not responded to low interest rates as expected, even after accounting for the effect of higher oil prices. The expansion has been dampened relative to the degree of monetary ease, and the output gap has not closed much since early this year.

Household spending is unlikely to be a source of strength due to historically low saving rates, and investment spending is also not expected to significantly increase. This could be due to governance issues and businesses' cautious behavior. Profits, productivity, and capacity utilization data do not suggest that sales or profit opportunities are slipping away because firms have been slow to add labor or capital capacity.

The effective neutral rate may be lower than historical levels, making policy appear more accommodative

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share my perspective on the current economic situation. Despite the increase in oil prices and the widening trade deficit, I believe the economy will continue to grow above trend and maintain low and stable core inflation. The solid growth this year is a testament to this. Even if oil prices and the trade deficit do not improve next year, as long as they don't worsen significantly, growth will still receive a net boost.

The labor market also shows signs of improvement, with substantial job creation last month. However, the labor force increased even more, keeping the employment-to-population ratio low. The number of individuals not in the labor force but wanting jobs also increased by half a million last month. Wage growth does not indicate much pressure on labor supply.

Regarding inflation, unit labor costs have only risen by 0.6% over the past year, and the underlying trend is consistent with core inflation at current levels. Non-energy commodity prices have declined since our last meeting, and the markup of prices over unit labor costs remains close to its 1997 peak, leaving room for businesses to absorb higher labor and energy costs.

The recent increase in the TIPS spread is puzzling, but nominal bond yields and TIPS inflation compensation have been negatively correlated with oil prices, suggesting that investors are more concerned about the growth effect of higher oil prices than any possible inflationary effects.

Looking forward, I suggest a goal for policy to keep PCE core inflation at about its current level in 2005 and 2006. This will help anchor inflation expectations and provide a sound basis for the sustained expansion. However, we must be aware of the possibility of overkill and the risks of disinflation.

The range of forecasts for the number of policy increases next year goes from two to about five. While policy will depend on incoming data, it seems plausible that we will choose to stand pat at some meetings next year while continuing to remove policy accommodation at a measured pace. It's important to signal to the public that pauses may occur and that our future funds rate increases will not be automatic or independent of the state of the economy. Instead, we should focus on the overall thrust of monetary policy reflected in the entire term structure. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss inventory levels and productivity based on recent conversations I've had with business executives. Over the past few years, I've observed the significant improvements in inventory management that have led to lower inventory levels relative to sales. These firms have made small adjustments to keep inventory in check and have seen a decrease in inventory volatility in the economy.

Business contacts continue to expect further reductions in inventory-sales ratios. They've improved internal information flows and linked sales, procurement, work scheduling, and shipping to make real-time adjustments. Suppliers have also improved their processes, enabling firms to reduce inventory needs even more. These trends support the forecast in the Greenbook, which anticipates a continued downward trend in inventory-sales ratios.

Regarding productivity, the Greenbook discusses two scenarios—one with higher productivity and one with lower productivity. The baseline forecast for the second half of this year shows slower productivity growth compared to the unusually high levels we've seen recently. Business contacts indicate a productivity dichotomy—they're focusing on improving productivity for competitive and quality reasons, but they're also managing staff carefully due to high fixed costs and morale issues. Some firms are reaching the limits of their existing workforce and are beginning to add employees, which could lead to slower short-term productivity growth.

Lastly, I've heard increased focus on Sarbanes-Oxley from business contacts, particularly as they prepare to file their third-quarter 10-Q reports next week. They're aware of the upcoming assessments under auditing standard no. 2 and want to be transparent about their internal control structures. CEOs are concerned about admitting major weaknesses in their internal controls just six weeks later, so we may see some references to Sarbanes-Oxley in the 10-Qs next week. Overall, management at all levels is highly focused on Sarbanes-Oxley compliance as we approach the year-end.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some perspective to the standard forecast presented today, which aligns closely with the Greenbook baseline. This forecast assumes a moderate decline in the dollar and a federal budget policy in line with current projections. The funds rate is projected to rise less than suggested by the fed funds futures market, and output expansion is expected to be just above potential, with a slow closing of the output gap. Inflation remains stable, but a negative shock could prevent the output gap from closing entirely.

Many of you have suggested that there might be pauses in our rate-raising process, that the equilibrium funds rate might not be as high as previously thought, and that we will continue to monitor data in our policy decisions. I agree with these sentiments, but I'd also like to discuss some potential longer-term reasons that could push us to raise the funds rate more than the Greenbook path suggests.

First, let's discuss the dollar. The international co-dependency truce, where the US can over-consume while foreign central banks support the dollar by buying US Treasuries, may be unraveling. Some countries, like Japan and China, have seen their currencies appreciate relative to the dollar. The US dollar has dropped since our last meeting, and the current account deficit remains high. A memo from our staff suggests that a 50% drop in the dollar would be necessary to stabilize the debt-to-GDP ratio. While this result may not be surprising, I was taken aback by the memo's finding that a falling dollar could lead to steady upward trends in core PCE inflation rates, even with low pass-through rates. This could be a significant issue we should keep an eye on.

My second concern is fiscal policy. I've long been a fiscal hawk, but political gridlock, which has kept budgets in check, has taken a hit in the recent elections. With four budget-conscious Republican Senators losing their blocking power, there's a risk of more irresponsible budget-busting policies. The Administration has announced two priorities for the next term: Social Security reform and tax reform. While there are various ways to approach Social Security reform, I believe the most likely scenario is that some payroll tax revenue will be diverted to individual accounts, with benefit payments to be cut only in the distant future. This could lead to increased consumption and decreased national saving

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to build on Governor Gramlich's points regarding fiscal policy. I'm glad to see the President and his team focusing on issues like Social Security and Medicare. It's a positive sign, but returning to a budget surplus or neutral will require both revenue and spending discipline.

The last time we achieved a surplus, it was primarily due to high revenue, not spending cuts. However, our forecast doesn't suggest an economy that will generate such high tax revenues in the near future. But we can start by reinstating the Pay-As-You-Go (PAYGO) provisions. This would impose spending discipline and send a signal of a return to fiscal management.

As for monetary policy, when we began to move away from accommodative policies, there were concerns about the impact on the economy. Some feared rising rates could derail the recovery. But as Dino mentioned earlier, longer-term interest rates have actually declined, and the economy has continued to recover despite hurricanes and rising oil prices.

The markets expect a continued reduction in monetary policy accommodation and a return to neutral. I believe we should stick with this course.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to bring up some complexities in the current economic situation that the charts we're handing out now address. While the risk of dollar depreciation is clear, the possibility of a more persistent pool of underutilized resources creates a more intricate set of risks. Despite the October employment surprise, changes in payroll employment have been remarkably subdued during this economic turnaround. This observation led me to ask Andy Figura and Bill Wascher to analyze labor market developments during the recession and post-recession period. Specifically, I was interested in understanding whether this labor market performance is more easily explained by increased productivity or a shortfall of demand.

To begin this analysis, I asked them to identify industries that have had unusually poor labor market performance since the 2001 business-cycle peak. They measured both absolute net industry employment changes and net industry employment changes relative to changes for a comparable length preceding the business-cycle peak. Four industries stood out as noticeable underperformers in both absolute and relative terms: durable manufacturing, information, transportation and warehousing, and professional and technical services.

One of the unusual features of the recent downturn is the significant and persistent increase in the share of layoffs that have been permanent. Job destruction increased during the recession and then fell back, while job creation fell during the recession and has remained low. The four underperforming industries accounted for the overall increase in the average pace of job destruction between 1998 and 2000 and in the years 2001 and 2002. Job creation was more evenly distributed across industries.

The most intriguing question is whether the slow net job creation, particularly in these four underperforming industries, is due to demand shocks, which would be negative, or supply shocks, which would be more positive. Chart 5 indicates a strong positive correlation between changes in value-added share and changes in employment share. The actual correlation coefficient is 0.77, and it is significant at a 5 percent level. The four underperforming industries, which are clustered in the lower, left-hand quadrant, all experienced losses in both employment and output shares.

However, there is not a strong relationship between the change in employment share and the change in productivity growth. There is a mild, very small negative relationship, which is significant

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Mr. Reinhart.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to begin by thanking Carol for distributing the materials I'll be referring to. The recent intermeeting period served as a reminder of the transient nature of our work here. Following the distribution of the Bluebook, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported an impressive gain of 450,000 new payroll jobs, with revisions adding an additional 113,000. This employment report led to a shift in policy expectations, causing the net intermeeting rise in federal funds futures rates to reach 10 to 40 basis points.

The market now anticipates a 3 percent funds rate by the end of 2005, suggesting that you plan to tighten policy in ¼ point increments at approximately half of the FOMC meetings between now and then. Market participants have redefined "measured" as "carefully calibrated" rather than "regular and rhythmic."

According to the Desk's survey of primary dealers, respondents universally predict a ¼ point firming of policy today, with balanced risks to the Committee's goals, and the retention of "measured" language in the statement. A minority of dealers still expect a pause in December, but the number expecting such a pause has dwindled since Friday morning.

As a result of this revision in policy expectations, nominal Treasury yields have risen 14 to 38 basis points over the intermeeting period, with the largest increases occurring at shorter maturities. However, this should not be interpreted as a tightening in financial market conditions, as indexed debt yields have fallen, and equity prices have gained 4¼ percent, while the dollar has depreciated 6 percent against a basket of major currencies.

Oil prices dominated global financial markets during much of the period, with West Texas intermediate crude ending yesterday only $1 above its level at the time of the September meeting but varying in a $10 range. Energy prices have three main channels of influence on the economy: a direct contribution to consumer prices, an increase in an excise tax, and a tilt in the production mix toward other inputs, lowering their marginal products.

As long as inflation expectations remain contained, market participants might expect policymakers to focus on the effects of energy prices on aggregate demand, which could lead to

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. Questions for Vincent? President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I just had a quick question. Have we given some thought to whether by being clearer about what we expect to do in the future we are, in effect, getting information back from the market that is what we told them, as opposed to what the market’s own expectations are? Do we know what the market thinks with regard to the need, in this case, for an increase in interest rates? Or are we involved in a self-fulfilling situation here where we are clear and, therefore, the market says, “The Fed is saying this, so we’re going to give them back the same information they’re giving us,” without a lot of added market intelligence?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: That’s the reverse of what we used to talk about as “looking in the mirror.” That was the issue of whether we were being guided by market signals because, if the market was guided by our signals, then again there was this positive feedback question. Also one worries that market participants are not investing independently in the effort to forecast the economy and to decide what is the appropriate monetary policy. In that regard, I think a good example is not this intermeeting period but the prior two where data came out that seemed to suggest that aggregate demand would be weaker than previously thought. Market participants didn’t change their view of the monetary policy actions over the next five months because the Committee’s statements seemed to suggest that you would be removing policy accommodation. Everything that moved was at a further-ahead time horizon. So the whole structure of the term structure of fed funds futures rates shifted down past four or five months out, whereas the near-term track of tightening seemed to be baked in the cake. That said, volatilities are low, but market participants seem to understand the conditioning nature of the Committee’s decisions. In support of that notion, as we’ve noted in past briefings, implied volatilities are low but the reaction to surprises in the data have been much larger than in the prior decade. So if you look at the reaction to surprises in the employment report or consumer prices, you see that markets are repricing their expectations for policy action. There just seems to be less idiosyncratic noise about where policy will be.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: [ERROR]

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I agree with your recommendation. I wanted to say, notwithstanding your comments on productivity, with which I don’t disagree, that the fact is that the real fed funds rate today is zero. I’d be more inclined to the view that we need to pause if the real fed funds rate were in fact positive—or to put it another way if the nominal rate were in the 2½ to 3 percent range, which is what I think you may be suggesting. So as long as the rate is below what I would consider a reasonable, neutral rate—and zero fits that criterion—then I don’t think we should be pausing. That’s not to say that we should move every time we meet next year—I realize that’s not the case—but I think the rate needs to be at a positive level, as you’re suggesting. In my view, the discussion at our meeting in February of next year will be important, and I think changing the language at that point will be important as well.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Maybe we ought to circulate the potential statement so that, if you find that any of the language is inconsistent with your views, you can comment on it. Why don’t we do that? It’s the one labeled B′. People may misinterpret what is actually being proposed, so let’s not leave that to chance. President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Thank you very much. I’m in strong agreement with what you have proposed, Mr. Chairman, both the move of 25 basis points and the selection of the language that does not attempt to signal anything for December. I understand the logic in the Greenbook. We in Boston have the same kind of perspective about the future. So I realize that there is an identifiable—maybe even baseline—probability that even with a relatively flat fed funds rate over the next year or so, we’ll still have an output gap. I recognize all that economic logic. But my basic instinct says that we need to get out of the zero zone on the real fed funds rate and to a positive rate for any number of reasons, which you’ve done a better job of touching on than I ever could. I also would like to say that I think our use of language over the last year or so was prompted by a set of extraordinary circumstances. Whether one views those circumstances as related to the extraordinary productivity performance or to the unusual market volatility when deflation concerns were very prominent, we needed to do something to calm things down. We did calm things down for a good period of time. I really feel that in our communication we now need to move back to something that says what we did, why we did it, maybe gives some perspective on the balance of risks, and leaves it at that. Whether we move there in December or in February—or in March, April, or May of next year—I don’t care, but I’d like us to move there. To be explicit, I’d get rid of paragraph 6. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I support your recommendation both for the increase in the funds rate and the statement. I’d like to offer just a couple of other observations. The reason the communications process is so important is that it’s highly desirable that the market and the Federal Reserve read incoming data the same way and that the interpretation is a correct assessment of the significance of the incoming data. That’s not so easy to do but, as Cathy Minehan pointed out earlier, we certainly don’t want to run the risk that the market’s assessment is simply flowing from what we have said about future policy actions, which when we get to the point of taking action may not reflect our own assessment of the data that have come in. I think that the statement will have to evolve, as you suggest. I’d also like to mention that I think the greater diversity of views around the table reflects the fact that the probability distribution of where we’re going to go has become considerably more spread out. Or I’ll state it this way: My own probability distribution is considerably more spread out. If I think about where we were six months ago, I thought it was extraordinarily unlikely that we were going to remain at 1 percent on the funds rate. But my own view of where we’re going to go has become more spread out, and where we’re going to end up six months from now, I think, is going to depend much more on the data that we get in the next six months than anything that I can now put into my forecast. So I think that that divergence of views probably reflects each one of us becoming more uncertain as to where we’re going to go.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: A divergence of both the mean and the aggregate variance. Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I agree with your recommendations on both the 25 basis point move today and also language that doesn’t hint very much one way or the other about future policy. There are a couple of other things I’d like to add, though. One is that I don’t want to forget what exhibit 2 in Vincent’s presentation showed or what President Yellen talked about, which is that, if we move to 2 percent today, we are well up into the range of estimated equilibrium real rates historically. So while, yes, the rate is low, let’s not get ourselves too wrapped up in the thought that we are outside an acceptable range. We may be in the low part of it, but we seem to be in the range. Second, obviously having thought about this issue of a slowdown in productivity and in high-tech investments—I’ve talked about it here and publicly—I’d make a couple of other points. One is that what we do with respect to the possibility of a slowing of productivity growth depends very much on how quickly the market picks it up and it is reflected in asset prices and other things. If, in fact, the market doesn’t pick this up and if asset prices continue to rise inappropriately, we have to do something to slow aggregate demand down to the new expectation of slower increases in aggregate supply. On the other hand, if the markets pick it up relatively quickly, asset prices won’t move up as quickly, aggregate demand won’t be out of line, and we still may be in a mode where we want to raise rates for a variety of reasons, but we may not feel as pressed because of the productivity uncertainty you talked about. So I think in both cases we need to recognize the probable direction, but I at least am still very much open minded, as I think President Poole suggested we should be, about the pace at which we’re going to move in this direction. In essence, I don’t think that the market necessarily has it wrong, nor do I think that the Greenbook has it wrong either. So I am very much of a pragmatic view about what our policy stance next year might be.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I support your recommendation to increase the funds rate 25 basis points, but I am concerned about adopting the proposed statement. When I opened the Bluebook this time, I was actually pleased to see in alternative B what I thought was a well-crafted statement that hinted at a possible pause in a way that was rather artful and subtle, without foreclosing any option in the future whatsoever. And I thought the reasoning in the Bluebook in support of “B” was strong and convincing. As Vincent stated, both before and after the employment report, market participants expected some hint in the statement of a possible pause. After we received the employment report, there was an update to the Bluebook that contained alternative B′ as the proposed statement. So I took B′ as a response to the employment report, and to my mind that was an unwarranted shift. I would note that the Greenbook assessment of the appropriate path for the funds rate had not changed as a consequence of the employment report. But we observed a large and, if the Greenbook is right, excessive market response to the employment report. We saw that in the immediate move in the fed funds futures. I suppose if one interprets the employment report as a very strong signal—and perhaps this is the view that you were expressing—that structural productivity growth is lower than we had been assuming, then this B′ statement would be an appropriate response. But it seems to me that the Greenbook has already incorporated an expected decline in productivity growth. I suppose in the Greenbook the decline is cyclical not structural, but it is built into the Greenbook forecast. The employment report simply confirmed what the staff had earlier been expecting when they had been surprised by the low employment growth. So it seems to me that adopting B′ reinforces the market expectation about the future path of monetary policy in a way that I would not like to see it reinforced. My own preference would be to move today but to adopt alternative B rather than B′.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I agree with your recommendation in all parts. I’m madly looking around for the sentence that Janet is referring to, and I can’t find it.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It’s the first sentence that relates to alternative B—the very top one.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: Oh, they’re putting in a reference to the cumulative increase.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: In other words, by stipulating how much we have done already, it suggests that that may be enough.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: Right. So I’m for B′— the statement that was passed out. But I agree with some combination of the views expressed by Janet, Cathy, and you that soon we should change this sentence that says, with inflation expected to be low, “the Committee believes that policy accommodation can be removed at a pace that is likely to be measured.” At some level, that statement is inoffensive and all-inclusive, but the problem is that the more we move up 25 points per meeting, the more we define what measured is. We’ve now done that four times, so we are coding that sentence, and we probably have to change it pretty soon. I wouldn’t want to change it this time; I have at least some hawkish elements in my head, as you heard before. I think next time may be the time to change the sentence in a way that undoes the coding we have given it.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: I support the two parts of your recommendation, Mr. Chairman. I think alternative B′ is preferable to B. I appreciate your observation on the diversity around the table, and I think President Poole is correct that it’s because each of us has a wider distribution in our heads regarding what is likely to happen. We’re just talking about where our mean is, I guess, or maybe where our insecurities are. In reality, my thought is that there is a good deal of uncertainty on both sides, as I pointed out in my formal comments. There is uncertainty on the weak side as well as the strong side, for the reasons you noted. Accordingly, I think it is time for us to look at this language issue again. We enjoyed it so much the first time, and the second, and the third. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I support the recommendation of B′. It seems to me that there is no need to change the language, at least at this point, because I think market participants understand the conditional nature of what’s going on here. So in that sense, I think we’re positioned rather well. I remain open as to when we may want to change the language and how we may want to change it, but for now I think we’re still reasonably well positioned.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I support your recommendation to increase the fed funds rate by 25 basis points. On the language question, I think it is time to start moving away from the term “measured.” It has worked well—quite frankly, better than I thought it would. I was concerned about it when we started using a series of forward-looking statements. So I would like to move away from the forward-looking statements—obviously not at this meeting, but possibly at the December meeting. At the same time, however, we should also seriously consider the balance of risk statement that we had previously. After two task forces working on this with Roger Ferguson, I’m not going to suggest a third task force. But the balance of risk language has evolved over the ten years that I’ve been here. It has been useful at times, but I think it is getting increasingly difficult to craft a balance of risk statement, given the complexity of the economy.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I support both parts of your recommendation. I have just two comments. One is that I think you overstate a bit the idea that the current funds rate is outside the range of historical experience. As President Poole noted, it’s not so unusual in real terms—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I’m sorry—I was referring to the 1 percent.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: Oh, the 1 percent, okay. I don’t think there’s a basis to move just on an allergy to a 2 percent funds rate; I think it should be based on the data and on the economy. I would just note additionally that the economy responds most directly to overall financial conditions, and there I don’t think we’re anywhere close to the border of what has been experienced in history. My second comment is on communications. As everyone knows, I disagree with President Minehan; I think we should be trying to provide guidance. In my view it has worked very well the last couple of years. I don’t think it was surprising that it worked well. Precisely because we are assessing such complex matters—for example, we’re trying to assess the course of productivity— it’s very difficult for the market to read our minds. We need to provide some kind of guidance; whether it’s conditional or whatever it may be, I think we should. I’m personally comfortable with dropping the “measured” language or modifying the language, but I hope we do not go back to a completely uninformative statement, at least without some thorough discussion of what the options might be. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I support both parts of your recommendation, raising the funds rate and keeping the statement essentially as it was last time. I also hope we reconsider the language in December. To some extent I may have a different focus than some others who want to do that. I hope we leave open the possibility that we could pause from needing to move—that we’re not locked in to tightening at each meeting. My concern about “measured” and about the current language is that it does seem to lock us in one meeting ahead, but I don’t think it has really constrained the market when it looks much beyond the next couple meetings. I think we see that we’re getting good feedback from the market; our “measured” language hasn’t impaired that feedback except for the very short term maturities because market participants see our announcement as triggering the next action. So I believe we need to think about how to do this in December, if we’re going to keep an open mind for February. If we remove “measured,” I hope we don’t do so in a way that suggests we’re on an even steeper upward track and our pace of tightening is not going to be measured. I don’t think that’s consistent with the economic outlook. You seemed to imply that the inflation risks were quite skewed because of the productivity risks, and I admit that productivity growth may be slower than we had anticipated. The staff has a slowdown built into the Greenbook and it could—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I’m not saying that. I’m saying that the cost of a mistake in policy is skewed, which is a different statement. I’m acknowledging that the probability distribution is symmetrical on the outcome of the event, but the cost–benefit analysis on a mistake is not.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: Perhaps. I think there is a cost to being too high as well as being too low.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Absolutely.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: Maybe the cost of being too high is that we have a more sluggish economy with fewer people put back to work and too low an inflation rate if some of these upside risks to inflation don’t occur. So I think there are costs on both sides here. If we take out “measured,” I hope we give some sense, even if it’s a balance of risk sentence, of our assessment looking forward, as Governor Bernanke said. I hope we don’t induce people to raise intermediate- and long-term rates by a considerable amount because they think we’re no longer going to be measured in removing policy accommodation. I think that would be a problem, given the outlook. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I also am in strong agreement with your recommendations both to increase the fed funds rate and on the statement. My thinking on how we would remove our accommodation has evolved over the past few months, along the line that President Poole has already articulated, and I’m just not certain when a pause may be necessary. So I am supportive of language that gives us that flexibility. And I was encouraged by Vincent’s comments that some market participants do think that the current language gives us that flexibility. So I’m in support of your recommendations.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I also support both parts of the recommendation. With respect to the statement, I also think it is appropriate at this time to make as little change in the statement as is necessary, for a couple of reasons. First of all, the markets understand that the absence of change is not a result of inattention; they know it is the net result of a great deal of thought and consideration. So any change whatsoever is interpreted, and occasionally over- interpreted, and I don’t think we are at the point yet where we need to open that possibility. With respect to communication in the future, I’m with Governor Bernanke. I don’t think we can go back to communicating in the fashion that we were doing even a year ago. I think we have set an expectation that we will make a real effort to communicate some of our underlying sense of what’s happening with the economy, and I think we will be called on to continue that trend.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement: I support your recommendation, Mr. Chairman, including the language.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I support your recommendation, including the language, for this meeting, but I do share some of the concerns that several of my colleagues around the table have expressed. I think some of the language may not be seen as particularly consistent with our policy of improved communications. If the words are seen as boilerplate every time, in a way it may take away from our flexibility going forward. So at the December meeting, I’d like us to look at some alternatives. If the word “measured” continues to be part of our statement, I think, as others have said, it will end up tying our hands. Another part of the statement is the second sentence, in which we’ve talked about this “robust underlying growth in productivity.” We saw in the ’90s that we moved to a different level of long-term productivity growth, but as I said in my comments, we may be in a part of this business cycle or stage of the recovery when productivity growth is going to look weaker. We need to think about how that is worded. I think that language could raise some questions about what productivity we are focusing on—the long-term versus the cyclical—if productivity turns out to be weakening, as the forecast in the Greenbook lays out. So as the recovery continues to move forward, there are aspects of the statement that we have to be careful about in terms of leaving in some of the language for such a long period of time. We need to think through and begin to anticipate prior to a meeting how to make those kinds of changes in order to give us flexibility.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: I agree with your proposal, Mr. Chairman, about raising the rate and about the statement. My own sense is that the funds rate is more likely to lie on the path of the market’s forecast than that in the Greenbook, and I don’t think it’s the right time to pull down the yield curve. With regard to our statement, I agree with Governor Bernanke that we shouldn’t be looking to turn back the hands of time and increase the variance in the market’s forecast for the future fed funds rate.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: I support your recommendations. I wish I knew what I thought about the desired statement! @laugh I would just point out not only that the market now expects us to slow the pace of increase significantly from what we’ve been doing but also that the minutes from our last meeting have some nice artful language near the end—which is probably relevant to some of the concerns many of you expressed—saying that the market has not misinterpreted us as signaling a steady reflexive march up at 25 basis points per meeting. I think the minutes are helpful against that risk of being misinterpreted, and I look forward to a discussion about the future structure of the statement in December and beyond.

Speaker: SPEAKER
Statement: You may be the only one! @laugh

Statement: Are you volunteering to be the chairman of a committee to look at that issue? Do you want to make a statement, Ms. Holcomb?

Speaker: MS. HOLCOMB
Statement: Well, I thought maybe my silence could serve as assent, but I do support your recommendation. And our expectation is that there will be a need for pause and that at the next meeting the language might need to be changed.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Would you read the appropriate language?

Speaker: MS. DANKER
Statement: This is from page 13 of the Bluebook, on the directive: “The Federal Open Market Committee seeks monetary and financial conditions that will foster price stability and promote sustainable growth in output. To further its long-run objectives, the Committee in the immediate future seeks conditions in reserve markets consistent with increasing the federal funds rate to an average of around 2 percent.” And then the risk paragraph from the press release on the assessment of risks: “The Committee perceives the upside and downside risks to the attainment of both sustainable growth and price stability for the next few quarters to be roughly equal. With underlying inflation expected to be relatively low, the Committee believes that policy accommodation can be removed at a pace that is likely to be measured. Nonetheless, the Committee will respond to changes in economic prospects as needed to fulfill its obligation to maintain price stability.”
