Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the recent intermeeting period. With the market's reaction to this meeting's outcome, there was heightened focus on exchange rate developments. Dollar sentiment was bearish for most of the time, but last week's sudden appreciation took markets by surprise. Asian central banks' actions, both in terms of policy and portfolio management, have been a significant market driver.

Let me show you some data on this. The top panel on page 1 compares three-month U.S. and euro-denominated deposit rates, with Libor fixings represented by solid lines and forward rates by dashed lines. The U.S. short-term rates have risen due to the tightening cycle, but the more intriguing development is the shift in forward rates. In September, nine-month forward rates for both currencies were around 2.6%. Since then, dollar forward rates have risen, while euro forward rates have declined and are now close to the cash rate. This suggests that the market is pricing in a possible ECB easing due to Europe's economic sluggishness and the adverse effect of the strong euro on exports.

Looking at a longer time frame, the bottom left panel on page 1 shows U.S.-German three-month interest rate differentials since the beginning of the year. Dollar interest rates have gone from being 120 basis points lower to being almost ¼ percentage point higher. At the long end of the curve, the relationship is more volatile, but dollar interest rates are still higher by about ½ percentage point. This favorable shift in interest rate differentials might have helped the dollar, but it hasn't prevented the dollar's downward trend.

The dollar's fall has affected returns on U.S. assets for foreigners, as shown in the bottom panel on page 1. Most asset classes have returned around 5%, but for foreign investors, returns are closer to zero due to the nearly 5% fall in the dollar.

Another focus of market attention has been the behavior of Asian central banks. The top panel on page 3 graphs Chinese yuan spot and forward rates, showing that forward discounts have widened due to speculation about a possible revaluation of the yuan. Meanwhile, other Asian countries have allowed some appreciation against the dollar, but the pace

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: With respect to the direct net currency shipments abroad, recently I have seen reconciliations between other techniques that endeavor to get to the aggregate amount of currency held abroad and the direct currency shipments. Are those other techniques indicating much the same story? I presume, with the growth of currency in circulation slowing and with the foreign piece such a huge part of that, that a direct measure of shipments looks much like what we estimate is the total. Is that accurate?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: I believe so. But as I said, we don’t see the currency that leaves the country—or that can come back in—by other means. There seems to be a correlation between the middle panel and the bottom panel, but again, there is a lot of slippage because of other ways currency can leave the country. But I have not seen a firm reconciliation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The way we estimate foreign holdings from the stock of currency is not a bad technique. Indeed, as I recall, the several different measures yield roughly the same result. So, the implication is that the net change in that estimated stock less the direct shipments is, indeed, a presumed “other” component plus statistical error. Does that tend to be a small number or a large number?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: I can’t say that I can tell you.

Speaker: MR. MADIGAN
Statement: I don’t have that either, Mr. Chairman, but I would note that our estimates of growth in foreign-held currency are actually still somewhat larger for this year than for domestic currency, despite the negative net shipments that Dino referred to. For instance, we are projecting that foreign currency is growing about 6¾ percent for 2004 as a whole, whereas domestic currency growth we estimate at about 4 percent.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: So that suggests that there’s an unexplained significant residual. On the IMM traders report, to what extent are the net long and short positions useful in anticipating changes in rates? My presumption is that, historically, if there was a relationship, it disappeared as soon as somebody observed it. What are the facts?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: We actually did some work on this. Bob, do you remember the paper that we did a few years ago?

Speaker: MR. ELSASSER
Statement: I think the basic conclusion there was that those data do seem to have some predictive power, but they are released with such a long lag that they are not useful for traders on a real-time basis.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: But there is other evidence that does suggest that, when everybody becomes absolutely committed to the view that a currency is going to move in one direction or the other, one can always make money on the other side of that transaction. At least that’s my recollection of history. But I guess since nobody knows what these data are in real time, one can’t make that statement.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: People do use these data sometimes as a contrary indicator—for the very reasons that you note.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: But if they do see a relationship, it disappears.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: That’s the risk.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Other questions for Dino?

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: With the possible exception of these positions vis-à-vis the dollar, there seem to be a remarkable number of indications—spreads are narrower, the yield curve is flatter, and so forth—that suggest that market participants see less risk. Yet the risks seem to be out there. We hear people talking about that all the time. Some of this betting against the dollar reflects the international risks having to do with the size of our deficit and the way the currency markets are going. There is a potential for markets to get upset about fiscal deficits going forward. There seem to be a lot of risks out there, but we don’t see it in the curves much. Do people talk about that at all?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Yes. The short answer is yes. They have observed—especially over the last year —any number of things that should have and did create uncertainty. Yet there was this dichotomy where volatilities were low and spreads were low. So yes, there has been this dichotomy, and people talked about it. There’s a certain amount of frustration about it as well, I think, because it doesn’t make sense in terms of most people’s mental models of how these things work.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Yes. A couple of people I talked to in the investment world characterized the situation as a sort of tight equilibrium that was balanced off against risk on both sides. They approach it with a sense of unease because they view it as not necessarily stable.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Any further comments or questions? If not, would somebody like to move to ratify the domestic open market operations?

Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection, they are approved. We move on to Dave Stockton.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: [ERROR]

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement:
At this time of year, around the holiday season, I can't help but share the news that the International Division's forecasters have been eagerly anticipating: global oil prices have indeed fallen more than we predicted in our previous Greenbook, leading to a decrease in our forecast for U.S. oil import prices by about $5 per barrel in total for 2005 and 2006. This development has resulted in a flatter futures curve for WTI prices and a projected path for the average price of imported oil, with only a small further change expected in oil prices through the end of 2006. However, uncertainty about future oil prices remains high, with expected prices for WTI for the January and June contracts next year both around $41, but a wide probability distribution around the mean price for June, ranging from $32 to $50 per barrel.

The implications of these oil price developments for growth and inflation abroad are mainly the future effects of the price increases that have already occurred. Despite their recent declines, oil prices remain elevated compared to last year, and the slowing of activity in several foreign economies in the third quarter was due in part to the net increase in oil prices this year. The negative effects on output growth were particularly pronounced in Asian emerging market economies, which are more dependent on imported oil than other regions. Over time, we expect the transitory influence of the spike in oil prices to dissipate, and average real GDP growth abroad to return to an annual rate of about 3¼ percent.

In other international news, the dollar continued its downward trend during the intermeeting period, reaching a nine-year low early last week. Market participants cited concerns about the financing burden of the widening U.S. trade deficit as a major factor driving the dollar's slide. However, the dollar has since significantly reversed its intermeeting move in a sharp appreciation, with no particular data release or event seeming to have triggered the climb. Market participants point to incentives for traders to take profits and eliminate previously established short dollar positions ahead of the year-end.

Our baseline December forecast incorporates the lower path projected for the dollar directly in U.S. trade prices and volumes, and the outlook for domestic activity and inflation in a complex way. Daily fluctuations in the dollar cannot be anticipated and could easily put us back at the December Greenbook starting level.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Dave, you put down finger-crossing as a serious econometric technique. I want to communicate that in my experience the t-value is quite high. @laugh

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: I’m so glad to hear that. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Questions for our colleagues? You realize that, if there are no questions, this is a historic occasion! @laugh Hearing none, who would like to start our discussion? President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some insights from our latest economic data in the Seventh District. Our economy is showing signs of improvement, with businesses reporting increased production, strong new orders, and growing backlogs. However, labor market conditions are not yet robust, and temporary help firms indicate that demand for professional and technical help is strong, but growth is slower in light industrial and clerical sectors.

At our recent Economic Outlook Symposium, twenty-six economists submitted forecasts, which were slightly more pessimistic than the Blue Chip forecast. The consensus outlook is for a 3.1% GDP growth in 2005 and a 2.4% increase in the CPI. The auto industry, which employs many of our directors, anticipates light vehicle sales of 16.8 million units in 2005, a figure similar to 2004. However, if the Big Three continue to lose market share, this could lead to declines in their sales, which would be a challenge for our district given the significant presence of Big Three production.

Two of our directors, who are major parts suppliers, predict that Big Three production in the first quarter will be 7% below that of the previous year, but they wouldn't be surprised if the actual cuts were as much as 10%.

Regarding the national outlook, we have not received any significant new data that would change our views about the economy. We continue to expect growth to average above potential over the forecast period. However, there is uncertainty about the size of resource gaps, and it's possible that there is less resource slack in the economy than we think. This could lead to inflation concerns, which is a risk we cannot ignore.

Despite this risk, I believe we should continue our current monetary policy course. It's too early to signal an imminent pause, which will be a key question for next year.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent conversations with industry contacts regarding the current state of the labor market and economic outlook. The situation remains favorable, with demand for skilled labor, particularly in IT, accounting, and MBA programs, remaining strong.

I spoke with placement directors at two MBA programs, one considered a first-tier program, and the other a second-tier one. The first-tier program saw a significant increase in the number of companies recruiting on their campus, up from 126 in 2002 to 169 this year. The second-tier program reported a similar trend, with a 12-15% increase in firms recruiting compared to last year. Notably, consulting firms have returned to the market, which had been absent for some time.

At the lower skill level, my contact at Wal-Mart reports continued high demand for associates when opening new stores. This indicates that the labor market is beginning to tighten at the upper skill levels, suggesting companies are gearing up for a solid outlook going forward.

In terms of the overall economic outlook, I concur with the Greenbook, but with the usual caveats. The expansion is not fragile, and business confidence is growing, with early signs of exuberance in the form of IPOs and merger and acquisition activity. However, inflation risks are tilted slightly to the upside, although I don't foresee an outbreak of inflation.

Oil prices and exchange rates should be viewed as endogenous variables, reflecting an environment of expansionary policy continued over a sustained period of time. While recent oil prices may have dropped from their highs, long-term oil futures are up significantly, indicating a fundamental change in the economics of the market.

Pass-throughs occur slowly but eventually, so we should not be complacent about the current oil prices. The long-term oil futures are up by 50% or more from a few years ago, signaling a significant shift in the economic landscape. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on the economic activity in the District and the national economy. Our district is experiencing a moderate expansion, similar to the nation as a whole. Housing markets are particularly robust, and consumer spending on retail and travel is solid. However, there are challenges in our ports in southern California due to high international trade volumes, resulting in backlogs and increased shipping prices.

Nationally, I've been encouraged by recent economic data, which have been mostly positive. While the November employment report was disappointing, the overall trend of job growth and other data have made me more optimistic about the economy's footing. The decline in the dollar and the recent retreat in oil prices add further support to aggregate demand.

However, it's worth noting that output growth has only moderately exceeded potential despite significant fiscal and monetary stimulus. The Greenbook forecasts a similar pattern for 2005, even with a gradual removal of monetary accommodation.

Regarding inflation, the Greenbook forecast of core PCE inflation at about 1½ percent and core CPI inflation at 2 percent seems reasonable. Our contacts report that consumer prices remain stable, and there's continued pressure on firms to control costs.

However, there are risks to inflation from uncertainty about the future paths of the dollar and oil prices. Another risk is the high markup of prices over unit labor costs, which could result in a significant reduction in the rate of inflation if it reverts to more normal levels.

Productivity growth is also crucial for the inflation outlook. The recent moderation in price declines for high-tech goods and weakness in high-tech investment last quarter hints at the possibility of less-robust productivity growth going forward. However, there's a real chance that productivity growth over the next few years will be higher than anticipated in the Greenbook.

Despite some concerns about the recent productivity trend, there are reasons for optimism. Businesses are continuing to adapt and reorganize their practices to take full advantage of new ICT technologies. For example, one major bank has been able to support growth in customers and services with fewer staff, and the expansion of big box stores has had a dramatic effect on productivity growth in the retail and wholesale sectors.

Given the potential upside productivity and associated downside risks to inflation, it seems sensible to raise the funds rate 25 basis

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Third District's economic landscape. Manufacturing activity is expanding at a moderate pace, with mostly positive reports from our manufacturers. Our November Business Outlook Survey (BOS) index of general activity was down slightly to 20.7, but the December BOS rebounded to 29.6, indicating continued expansion. New orders and shipments remain strong, and employment is continuing to improve.

Retail sales of general merchandise are showing moderate growth during the holiday season, with retailers expecting a 4% increase in sales this year, consistent with the National Retail Federation's forecast. However, sales of luxury goods are growing at a faster rate than other items.

Construction remains soft, with nonresidential construction showing little improvement. Commercial real estate firms anticipate a decline in office vacancy rates in the year ahead, but effective rents continue to decline as landlords compete for tenants. Some investors are concerned that building prices have risen to unsustainable levels.

Residential real estate prices are accelerating in all three states, with builders raising prices for new homes. Employment growth was slower in October but remains strong in all major industry groups except manufacturing and information services. New Jersey and Delaware continue to outpace the nation in employment growth, while Pennsylvania exhibits the weakest labor market.

Looking ahead, our staff forecast calls for moderate acceleration in employment growth and a decline in unemployment rates in each of our three states. Inflation pressures are increasing, with industrial goods price pressure reported by our contacts and high price indexes in our BOS. Some firms expect raw material prices, including energy, to increase next year.

The outlook among our business contacts remains positive, with growth expected to continue at its current pace through the winter. Nationally, data since our last meeting has been generally positive, with household spending, business investment, retail sales, and housing construction all showing improvement. Payroll growth has averaged over 185,000 jobs per month this year, and the labor market is showing continued improvement, although there is still some slack.

I expect economic growth to continue at around 4% next year, and I'm more comfortable with the fed funds rate path in the Greenbook than I was with the previous one. However, there is some inflation risk, and I would prefer to maintain

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from our recent meetings with the Sixth District boards of directors. The economic outlook was notably positive, with many directors expressing optimism not felt in some time. There's a sense that once the initial adjustments to Sarbanes-Oxley are complete, business caution may begin to ease.

Secondly, there was a lot of discussion about price increases, some attributed to energy costs, but others just a determination to adjust prices. I found it interesting that one of our New Orleans directors, a senior executive at a large US chemical company, shared that Chinese companies were using oil prices that were significantly lower than West Texas intermediate crude due to their use of sour crude. This temporary energy price advantage could impact global markets.

Thirdly, the economy seems to be growing at a more robust pace than previously stated, with an average of over 4% GDP growth for the past six quarters. While job growth remains a concern, we've seen an average of 185,000 new jobs a month over the past eleven months, allowing the unemployment rate to decrease.

However, there are signs of potential price increases and a shift in inflation prospects, which gives me pause. Five-to-ten-year inflation expectations have been steadily rising, and the substantial run-up in house prices, influenced by low mortgage rates, could lead to further study and thought as we decide on policy posture.

Despite these concerns, I'm generally comfortable with our current policy path. However, with real fed funds rates near zero, there's a risk of unintentionally encouraging imbalances in the inflation environment and international sector. I hope we won't signal a pause in removing policy accommodation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on New England's economy. We're seeing steady improvement, with employment growing and job levels above last year's levels in all states. Unemployment dropped significantly in September and remained below the national rate in October. However, concerns persist due to a recent softening in consumer and business confidence. Major industries like manufacturing and information services have experienced job losses compared to last year, while financial activities posted a small loss and the large professional and business services sector was flat.

Manufacturing export growth slowed in the third quarter, despite a declining dollar. Downtown office vacancy rates in Boston widened due to mergers like Fleet/Bank of America and Manulife/John Hancock. On a positive note, the fall tourism season was strong, and retail sales and housing markets remain robust.

At the national level, growth is strong, with consumer spending and business investment showing considerable strength. Labor markets are delivering an average monthly level of job growth, which should reduce slack in the labor market over the next year and a half. Financial markets are supportive of continued growth, but liquidity could have downsides.

Despite this generally positive outlook, risks remain. There's increased pricing pressure due to rising import prices and labor costs. The fiscal deficit may also be a concern, as the latest government appropriations bill only temporarily reins in spending. The value of the dollar has declined, which could lead to increased inflation or market instability.

Given these risks, it's important for us to be cautious and consider the potential for unpleasant surprises. We may need to prepare for increased inflation, slower growth, or market instability. I believe the U.S. economy is on an upward trajectory, but we need to be mindful of these risks and adjust our policies accordingly. Let's keep our focus on clear and clean communication as we move forward.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, good morning. I'd like to begin by sharing some concerning news from the PNC Bank's Twelve Days of Christmas price index. It saw a 2.4% increase, with French hen prices driving the surge, despite a modest drop in turtle dove costs. The core index, excluding the volatile swans-a-swimming component, rose by 3.1% year-over-year. @laugh

Moving on to my district report, I mentioned in our last meeting that business activity in the Fourth District seemed out of sync with the national picture. The latest Beige Book suggests my region is still lagging behind. During the intermeeting period, I spoke extensively with local businesses. While they report growth in orders and production, job growth remains elusive. Some firms are managing to maintain profits by increasing productivity, while others are absorbing cost pressures. However, many are expanding production capacity abroad instead of locally. This global realignment could mean a prolonged period of weak employment gains in our area. @laugh

The staff's projections have had to be adjusted due to faster demand growth and slower cyclical productivity. I'm more optimistic about productivity based on feedback from my business contacts, but I also hear they're having more success passing costs along to customers. These factors have opposing effects on inflation. We've been gradually reducing policy accommodation, and I believe the economy will continue to expand at a consistent pace. I recommend sticking to the market's expectations and making minimal changes to our statement. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement:
Chairperson, I'd like to begin by sharing my perspective on the national economy. Based on our analysis, I believe the economy will continue to grow above trend, with growth projected to be around 3.75% this year and next. This trajectory suggests we're approaching our long-term potential GDP. Factors contributing to this growth include accommodative monetary policy, robust consumer spending, improved labor markets, higher wealth due to the stock market and housing prices, and lower oil prices.

In our District, retailers have reported a positive start to the holiday shopping season, with traffic and sales up several percentage points from last year. Travel and tourism activity have also been strong, and Rocky Mountain resort owners anticipate a significant increase in foreign ski visits due to the lower dollar. Manufacturing firms remain optimistic about future activity and plan to increase capital spending and employment during the first half of next year.

Our agricultural sector is experiencing a record-breaking year, with farm income 25% above last year's record high and 50% above previous 2004 estimates. Livestock prices have been a significant contributor to this increase, along with a 12-25% increase in crop production for major crops. Despite these large supplies, prices remain well above their ten-year average. This record farm income has led to higher land values, with ranch land values up 10%, non-irrigated cropland values up 7%, and irrigated cropland values up 5%. Equipment sales are also surging, with large equipment sales up 25-50% in October.

Regarding inflation, it remains modest, but there are signs it may be rising. Core PCE inflation is 1.5%, and core CPI inflation is 2%. We've seen some increases in core inflation over the last couple of months, and five-year inflation compensation has increased. Although oil prices have decreased, the dollar continues to depreciate, putting upward pressure on producer prices. Manufacturers have reported a net positive percentage of firms raising output prices for the twelfth month in a row, and the future finished goods price index reached its second-highest reading on record in November. Wage pressures remain slight, but the risk of higher inflation is a concern.

Given this outlook, I believe we should continue to gradually increase the federal funds rate

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to provide an update on the economic conditions in the Fifth District. Our service sector has seen steady growth in revenues over the past two months, while retail sales have remained stagnant. Big ticket sales and car sales, in particular, have been weak. Manufacturers reported a decline in growth in shipments and new orders last month, with shipments being flat in November. Building permits were down compared to last year, but the housing market remains strong.

Employment in services and retail sectors showed gains, while manufacturing employment remained unchanged in November. Price increases at district firms have been trending up, but remain below 2 percent. Manufacturers have reported that they can now pass on price increases to their customers, unlike earlier in the year.

On a national level, the economic recovery has continued at a steady pace. Consumer spending, capital spending, and housing have all shown positive growth. The Greenbook forecasts real GDP growth of around 3½ to 4 percent through 2006, with the output gap gradually closing by then. Inflation is expected to remain stable at 1½ percent.

I'm pleased to see that the disconnect between the Greenbook's projection and market expectations regarding the future path of the federal funds rate has been resolved. The Greenbook now projects a rate path that is closer to market expectations. However, there is still a disconnect between the Greenbook's projection of CPI inflation and market expectations. The public's expectation of inflation is higher than the Greenbook's projection, and this disconnect will need to be resolved. It's unclear whether market expectations will drift down to align with the Greenbook's projection or if we'll need to maintain tighter monetary policy to ensure that the disconnect is resolved in our favor. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Comments from directors and from a wide range of contacts indicate further improvement in the District economy—indeed some acceleration recently—and the improvement is quite broadly based. Given that, let me just comment on a couple of developments that seem particularly relevant. There have been in the District sustained, albeit moderate, gains in employment for better than a year now. Hiring plans are positive, according to our most recent survey, and some skills are now in short supply. The investment outlook appears to have improved recently. Some projects that were on the back burner, according to business contacts, have now been moved forward. In general, I think businessmen are feeling a bit more upbeat, and that is fueling some enthusiasm for further investment. The final point I would mention about the District economy is that, despite tightening labor markets, wage increases remain subdued—generally in the 2 to 3 percent range—and price pressures are modest as well. As far as the national economy is concerned, I think it’s perhaps at least worth noting that the current expansion is now into its fourth year, and recently at least things have been turning out pretty much as we expected. Now, that might be a reason to worry, but I prefer to look on the bright side of that. I agree with a point that Dave Stockton mentioned—that the fundamentals supporting further economic growth from here forward are distinctly positive. If I were writing down a GDP forecast at the moment, I might write down something a little stronger than the Greenbook forecast for both 2005 and 2006. On the inflation side, I do expect inflation to remain low, but I must admit that I’m a little less confident about that than I was a few months ago. If I ask myself why, I think it has mostly to do with the dollar. It’s not so much that I’m concerned about the pass-through, narrowly defined; it’s really more a concern about the changing nature of the competitive environment implied by the declining dollar. And that gives me a bit of pause as I contemplate inflation prospects. But I think that’s a pretty slim reed to hang all that much on, so I personally would stick with a low inflation forecast for now. Having said all that, it seems to me that the policy course is pretty clear here. We should continue with the policy path that we’ve been on. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: First Vice President Holcomb.

Speaker: MS. HOLCOMB
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some positive updates on the economic recovery in Texas since our last meeting. October employment growth was robust, with the strongest increase since the end of last year. The latest Beige Book report indicates increased optimism among businesses in our district. Our directors, who represent various regions and industries, echoed this sentiment during our recent board meeting.

Texas employment has grown at an annualized rate of 1.3% through October, slightly below the national average. Temporary employment saw a significant increase in October, which is a good sign for future job growth. In fact, Beige Book contacts reported an unusual number of temporary workers finding permanent positions. Additionally, our directors reported increased hiring by firms of all sizes.

However, the high-tech sector has seen some layoffs at semiconductor production, telecommunications, and computer services companies. But this appears to be part of ongoing industry restructuring. A tech sector director reported improved profitability and an expectation of manageable growth of 5-10% in 2005.

The airline industry's ongoing restructuring is also impacting Texas, with major carriers reducing employment and wages. This is due to both industry restructuring and efforts to offset high fuel costs.

Housing sales and prices have softened slightly in the last six weeks, but housing industry contacts remain optimistic for next year. They believe a stronger rate of job growth is needed to meet sales expectations. Office markets have continued to improve, and energy drilling activity has increased, with higher prices, growing backlogs, and some worker and equipment shortages.

Oil prices have not been high enough to absorb the increase in world oil output, and mild weather in natural gas-consuming regions has pushed natural gas prices below $6.00. Natural gas inventories are also very high. If we have a relatively mild winter, natural gas prices could fall further.

The Texas leading index was down in October but has increased strongly over the last three months. It's forecasting 2004 employment growth of just under 1.5% and 2005 job growth of about 1.9%. While these rates are below historical levels, they reflect the improving trend that will allow job growth to eventually meet or exceed the national average.

We generally agree with the Greenbook forecast of continued growth during the upcoming year, and the

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'm truly impressed by the team's insight and creativity, @laugh. Our perspective on the economic outlook hasn't shifted significantly since our last meeting. We're nearly there with the Greenbook forecast on most fronts. New York's sentiment has brightened a bit in recent months. Our upcoming December Empire survey indicates robust readings across the board - orders, shipments, employment, and business conditions. Nationally, we anticipate stronger demand growth heading into the new year. The fundamental drivers of expansion, slightly above trend growth, remain intact. Global demand growth is a tad softer but still solid. Our real GDP growth projection for '05 and '06 remains between 3½ and 4 percent, with core PCE around 1½ percent. Our monetary policy path is close to current market expectations.

However, we're entering a new phase of inflation factors. Productivity growth is slowing, unit labor costs are rising, and margins are compressing. These factors, along with other cost pressures, a declining dollar, a smaller output gap, and above-trend demand growth, suggest a higher inflation risk. Inflation expectations are above our comfort level, but they've eased slightly since our last meeting. Given this context, I propose maintaining the current monetary policy stance, with a gradual increase in the real fed funds rate. I don't see a compelling reason to alter market expectations at this time.

Leaving market expectations unchanged could potentially result in a tighter monetary policy stance, but I believe this risk is minimal. There's a chance we may need to move more aggressively in '05 than currently priced into the futures market. However, the probability of a steeper path seems to outweigh the probability of a softer one. We should continue to move at a pace that minimizes the risk of having to make larger adjustments in response to rising expectations. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to begin by acknowledging the generally positive news on current and anticipated demand since our last meeting. Surprisingly, consumption has remained robust despite higher energy prices, fueled by rising housing and equity wealth, and low intermediate- and long-term interest rates. These factors should continue to support growth moving forward.

However, the more pressing concern for many of us today is the inflation outlook that might accompany this output path. As growth becomes more entrenched and the output gap narrows, our focus shifts away from supporting growth and towards maintaining stable inflation. Monetary policy plays a crucial role in this regard. Our policy tightening, both actual and expected, is designed to keep inflation in check and balance the risks. I believe we have a good chance of success with a measured pace of tightening.

Concerns about higher inflation stem from pipeline pressures, the effects of the lower dollar on import prices, and the possibility of slower trend productivity growth, as well as recent core CPI data. However, staff briefings have shown that only a small portion of pipeline pressures and dollar depreciation translates to core consumer prices. There's a lot of uncertainty surrounding productivity trends, and even a few quarters of slower growth would tell us very little about underlying trends.

Additionally, there are several factors that could help keep inflation in check. Wages and compensation have remained moderate despite strong productivity growth and higher inflation. Markups of prices over unit labor costs have remained high, and these markups would absorb the initial cost effects of a slowdown in trend productivity. Long-term inflation expectations have remained stable.

That being said, there are risks, and I want to acknowledge them. One significant risk is a substantial fall in the dollar that is much larger than assumed in the staff forecast. This could put upward pressure on prices, and I would be cautious about tightening policy in anticipation of such a development. The timing and dimension of foreign exchange movements are difficult to predict, and the appropriate policy response would depend on the response of other asset prices.

Another risk is the recent behavior of bond yields, which have declined despite the expansion's growing strength. I don't believe this decline represents expectations of central bank buying, and a backup in bond rates in response to unexpected strength in activity or prices would play an important stabilizing role. However, there's a risk that something else could trigger a reassessment

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some thoughts to the discussion regarding the latest economic data and its implications for our forecast. The data we've received recently has been better than anticipated, with real consumer spending, excluding motor vehicles, increasing at a solid pace. Yesterday's retail sales report further reinforces this notion, indicating that households are still willing to drive the economy forward.

On the investment side, housing sales and starts have remained strong, and orders and shipments of nondefense capital goods have been trending upward. Internationally, the dollar has fallen, which should give a boost to the export sector once we get past the usual J-curve effects. Additionally, moderating oil prices will help reduce a potential drag on future growth.

However, while the data has been generally positive, there are some concerns that we should not ignore. I've been particularly worried about the pace of technological change and the amount of labor market slack, with implications for productivity growth and inflationary pressures. The staff has captured these concerns in a scenario titled "greater cost pass-through with lower structural productivity."

This scenario is given some credibility by the fact that the forecast for real high-tech investment growth has been marked down to only its 1980-96 average. The current orders survey also indicates that two major manufacturers of productivity-enhancing equipment have reported recent increases in orders as falling below expectations. Furthermore, only 30 percent of NFIB survey respondents are planning to make capital expenditures, which is near the low end of its ten-year range. I've also heard from a private-sector contact of my own about a sudden weakness in demand for cutting-edge automated machine tools.

Additionally, the slowing in Japan and emerging Asia may be indicative of a slowdown in the productivity-improving high-tech sector. Unit labor costs in the nonfarm business sector have also experienced a pickup in the most recent four-quarter period, after several successive four-quarter declines. The data shows quarter-over-quarter increases in unit labor costs of 1.9 percent in Q2 of this year and 2.2 percent in Q3, both at an annual rate.

Market signals, such as the flattening of the yield curve and the increase in inflation compensation over the next five years, may also be consistent with

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Why don’t we break for coffee at this time.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the data we've gathered since our last meeting. The economic expansion seems to be picking up steam, with fewer risks to growth on the horizon. Inflation is a concern, but falling oil prices should keep any significant increase in check. I don't believe these developments warrant a change in our current policy just yet.

I'd also like to touch on the current account and the dollar. The large current account deficit and slow adjustment of the dollar and real interest rates raise questions about sustainability. If this situation is due to cyclical factors or manipulation, the adjustment process might be short-lived. However, I believe the U.S. external deficit is a secular phenomenon that will evolve slowly.

The primary cause of the U.S. external deficit is excess global savings. Advanced industrial nations, such as Japan and Western Europe, need to save for demographic reasons. With insufficient investment opportunities at home, they can only do so by running current account surpluses. Ideally, their savings would fund investment in developing and emerging-market economies. However, due to financial crises in the 1990s, these countries are unable or unwilling to accept large capital inflows, resulting in the United States becoming the recipient of massive savings inflows.

The situation is likely to persist due to the demographic factors driving saving in mature industrial economies and the inability of emerging markets to absorb large capital inflows. While this situation can be sustained for some time, it's essential to consider its sustainability.

Despite the perception of an unsustainable consumption binge in the U.S., Americans are not consuming beyond their means. In fact, U.S. net national wealth has increased by around $3.3 trillion over the past year, or about 30% of GDP. This increase in net national wealth explains the continued strength of consumption spending.

My first conclusion for monetary policy is that a U.S. current account adjustment may play out gradually over several years. We should not attempt to forecast swings in the dollar or base our policy on a presumption of a rapid depreciation in the near term. Instead, we should monitor the situation and respond as necessary to fulfill our mandate.

However, given the potential for herding and momentum trading in financial markets

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to weigh in on the ongoing discussion regarding our monetary policy and the pace at which we're raising the funds rate. Some have suggested that we're being too cautious, while others argue that we need to consider our ultimate goal before making a decision. I believe the ultimate target is not the equilibrium funds rate itself, but rather the economic conditions it represents. We could reach that equilibrium through various means, including raising rates above or below it.

To determine our strategy, we must consider the economic considerations at play. I propose focusing on global economics over a slightly longer period, as former Fed Chairman Bernanke did. My objective is to rebalance the world economy, stabilizing U.S. international debt-to-GDP ratios while maintaining price stability and proximate full employment around the globe.

A more ambitious goal would be to let capital flow away from the United States and toward emerging markets, but that's not feasible at this time. Instead, I aim to limit the inflow to the United States to a sustainable long-term pace. This rebalancing requires the dollar to fall enough to eliminate the U.S. trade deficit, a necessary condition for stabilizing debt-to-GDP ratios.

European economies are already operating below full employment, and their employment problems worsen when their currency rises. To stimulate their economies, they should employ a combination of monetary and fiscal policy, even if it means modifying existing rules. Structural policies may also boost demand in Europe, despite the anecdote about their neutral to negative impact.

Asia's situation is more complex. Asian economies should let their currencies appreciate to allow the normal adjustment process to occur. If they don't, the ultimate adjustment could be even more costly. Japan should stimulate demand through fiscal measures, such as tax cuts, if they cannot set interest rates below zero. Other Asian economies should also stimulate their domestic spending to preserve high employment.

For the United States, my preferred approach would be fiscal tightening to raise national saving and monetary accommodation to maintain full employment. However, fiscal tightening would likely lead to a falling dollar, and the two are complements rather than substitutes. If fiscal tightening does not occur, monetary tightening in the United States would be necessary to offset the new export demand from the fallen dollar.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to bring up two perspectives today, one from the banking sector and another on the fiscal issue. Based on my conversations with bankers, there seems to be a noticeable difference in their outlook on the economy compared to the discussions we've had in this room. While some have described the economy as "decidedly positive," bankers lean more towards "reasonable."

Regarding business expansion, there's significant growth happening outside the banking industry, particularly in small entities. Larger entities are more likely to expand offshore if they have that option, while those without that choice are expanding through domestic borrowing. US banks have taken on more M&A lending due to low demand for typical C&I loans, with the exception of commercial real estate, where growth in new condo starts is reportedly unsustainable.

As for fiscal discipline in Washington, there are a few positive signs. The re-appointment of John Snow as Treasury Secretary indicates a commitment to moving forward with the agenda, which includes tax reform. The President's discussions on Social Security reform also suggest a willingness to address fundamental issues.

Additionally, the recent passage of intelligence reform legislation is a positive sign that the Republican-led Congress and White House can work together. This bodes well for the enactment of fiscal responsibility measures such as tax reform, Social Security, and Medicare.

Given these observations, I believe we should continue removing monetary policy accommodation at the same pace. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies has laryngitis, as I’m sure you’re all aware, and Debbie Danker will read her prepared statement.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement: The aspect of the economy that I want to focus on is the improving outlook for consumer spending. After a significant slowing in the second quarter, real consumer spending grew by a healthy 5.1 percent annual rate in the third quarter and advanced strongly in the last two months. While early indicators of holiday spending imply not as bright a time as retailers have anticipated, particularly for lower-price retailers, inventories appear to be closely managed. The expectation for a strong year in 2005 for retail sales seems more firmly grounded than earlier this fall. Since the last FOMC meeting, oil prices have fallen significantly, which should put more discretionary spending power in the wallets of consumers and help the cash flow of businesses that are heavy users of petroleum products. Over the past three months, payroll employment gains have exceeded their average pace of the past twelve months. The stronger pace of employment gains will expand the income base to support continued consumption growth. While consumers should increase saving out of current income for their long-term financial health, there are few signs that they are willing to build their assets and defer spending at the current time. Long-term interest rates are still low by historical standards and have not provided enough incentive to encourage significant growth in longer-term bank deposits. Strongly rising housing prices have increased assets, and the resumption in growth of equity prices in the stock market will provide another leg to support increased net worth. If recent behavior patterns continue, therefore, consumers will be able to increase spending and show growth of assets, without significant increases in the short-term saving rate. Thus, I have greater confidence in the pace of economic growth in the next few quarters, and I believe that we should continue to remove accommodation by raising the fed funds rate by 25 basis points today.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN

Speaker: MR. MADIGAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the recent developments in financial markets during the intermeeting period. While there were some crosscurrents, most financial market prices showed only modest net movements. Yields on short-term Treasury coupon issues rose slightly, but yields on ten-year Treasuries and TIPS declined slightly. Implied inflation expectations for the next five years continued to rise, while those for the following five years remained unchanged. The Wilshire index and Nasdaq both saw gains of 2.5% and 4%, respectively, with equity prices supported by falling oil prices. Risk spreads on corporate, GSE, and emerging-market debt all declined.

Money market futures rates suggest that the expected path of monetary policy edged up for the next year but declined slightly over a longer period, consistent with the flattening of the Treasury yield curve. Dealers expect a 25 basis point move today, and none anticipate a pause in tightening going forward. However, they don't think you will tighten ¼ point at each meeting indefinitely into the new year. Two dealers expect a pause in February, and seven anticipate a pause in March.

The economy's expansion seems well-entrenched, with improving labor market conditions. However, there are concerns about inflationary pressures. The staff's revised estimates of equilibrium real interest rates suggest continued monetary tightening over time. The Committee may interpret these estimates as pointing toward a trajectory of policy over the next several years, but they should be weighed against other factors in setting policy.

Some policymakers may believe that the recent pace of tightening can now proceed more slowly, while others may think it should be maintained or even accelerated. The degree of policy accommodation remains substantial, and policymakers may be concerned about the potential for inflationary pressures to build. If the Committee wishes to signal a possible pause, it could do so by acknowledging the significant reduction in monetary policy accommodation while noting that the stance remains somewhat accommodative. If it believes policy tightening should continue, only modest changes to the statement would be necessary. The Committee could also hint at continued policy tightening by referring to the potential for upward pressure on prices from energy and business costs, and by noting that labor market conditions continue to improve. Ultimately, the Committee may choose not to signal a pause in advance through its announcement, as

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
President Hoenig: I'd like to add a few thoughts to our discussion. Our current economic policy is largely a response to the unique circumstances of a shallow recession and an atypical recovery. In the past, we've had clearer signs of economic rebound, making forecasting easier. However, this time around, we're dealing with endogenous and exogenous forces that have resulted in minimal changes to the economy.

Looking back at earlier forecasts, the economy doesn't appear to have changed significantly since then. Given the lack of a clear historical precedent, it's challenging to make accurate judgments about potential outcomes. Our forecasts may not hold up well in retrospect.

Given these uncertainties, I propose we continue our "opportunistic de-accommodation" approach. This means bringing the funds rate up to a level that puts us in a strong position to respond to any economic developments. It's like a tennis player moving to one side to hit a shot and quickly returning to the center to be ready for the next shot.

I believe we'll find it difficult to fine-tune policy based on forecasts. History shows that forecasts are not always accurate. Therefore, I recommend we adopt alternative C, which involves increasing the funds rate by 25 basis points and making only essential updates to paragraph 3. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the ongoing discussion about expediting the release of FOMC meeting minutes. Many of us have brought up the topic of minutes in our past communications policy discussions. Those in favor of expediting the minutes' release argue that it would provide more current and nuanced information about the economic outlook and monetary policy decisions. Releasing the minutes before the next meeting would prevent market participants from misinterpreting lagged minutes as reflecting current Committee thinking. The minutes also convey the range of views within the Committee, and expediting their release could lead to improved public communications. After their release, market participants could put individual statements by policymakers into perspective based on the minutes' description of the spectrum of members' preferences. Policymakers might also be more willing to speak publicly about a wider range of policy possibilities if they can reference those possibilities in the minutes. Furthermore, if the expedited minutes provide a complete and timely explanation of the policy decision, the policy statement released after the meeting could potentially be shortened.

However, some members might not be convinced by these arguments. Expedited release could lead to undue market attention to the minutes, potentially complicating the Committee's deliberations. Members might alter their discussion at the meeting to ensure certain views are represented in the minutes or offer support for a given policy decision in exchange for a particular reference. If an adverse dynamic of increased market attention and increased contention within the Committee sets in, the minutes could become sanitized and less helpful over time. Additionally, it might be operationally difficult to manage expediting the minutes' release.

To address concerns about operational difficulty, the Committee directed its Secretariat to conduct an experiment in expediting the minutes' release this January. The experiment involved producing a first draft seven working days after the meeting, gathering feedback, and aiming for a publication deadline of 2 p.m. on Friday afternoon. To meet this schedule, staff changed the process of producing the minutes by contracting with a private firm to produce transcripts quickly, distributing drafting responsibilities among more staff, and providing more systematic feedback on comments to make the revision process more likely to come to closure by the deadline. The experiment also involved developing a procedure for secure, chad-free voting and collaborating with Public Affairs staff at the Board and the Markets Group at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York to assess the likely market reaction to early release.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to begin by expressing my appreciation for the well-executed conduct of the recent experiment. I believe we've gained valuable insights from it, and I'm confident that the initial draft of the minutes will endure. I'd also like to acknowledge that I'm not in favor of releasing the minutes prematurely. However, I want to clarify that my opposition isn't based on a slippery slope argument. I've made this decision based on the merits of the case. Early release may foster a positive image of openness, but it doesn't significantly enhance communication. Furthermore, it carries risks and could potentially result in vague and less useful minutes.

Our objective in communications policy should be to optimize the rational expectations macroeconomic equilibrium. To achieve political accountability through better communications, we must prioritize policy success. Any policy change that may seem to enhance accountability but undermines policy success will ultimately fall short. Miscommunication with markets can harm rather than help political accountability.

Our goal is to encourage market reaction to new information that aligns with the FOMC's reaction. This isn't just about market response between meetings but over a longer period. We want the market and the Committee to assess monetary policy implications of information in the same way. The flow of new information is continuous, while the Committee meets at discrete intervals. When the market responds appropriately to new information, the economy benefits from an essential stabilizer.

The minutes should be faithful to the transcript and not disturb markets. Markets may react to the minutes, but the reaction should be beneficial and not detrimental to policy goals. My concern isn't about excessive attention to the minutes but misinterpretation of them. The issue isn't whether interest rates will respond to the minutes but whether the response will be accurate and proportionate. If there's never a market response to the minutes, it suggests that the minutes aren't providing valuable information.

There are instances when the FOMC may benefit from remaining quiet. Minutes released before the next FOMC meeting will be a significant event, while minutes released on the current schedule are a minor one. I'm concerned about situations like the fall of 1998, when market instability was high. Revealing a close split in the Committee or discussing the possibility of intermeeting policy actions could be problematic

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to propose that we bring forward the release of the minutes to around three weeks after the meeting, as suggested by Vincent. I understand the risks and costs involved, but I believe they are manageable and outweighed by the benefits. These benefits include a more timely release of additional information, which will help key market reactions align better with the FOMC's reaction to the same information.

The minutes provide a more complete explanation of our decision-making process, including our views on future developments and risks. They offer a finer reading on the Committee's outlook and provide a sense of alternative perspectives, which has been lacking in recent years due to the decline in dissents. This diversity of views helps fill out the picture of the issues the Committee is focused on and where it sees risks.

Earlier release of the minutes will also help the public interpret the economic situation as it develops over the intermeeting period and our reactions to incoming data. It will be another news event, and markets may not always react in desirable ways, but on average, over time, market reactions to the information in the minutes will reinforce our policy and help us achieve our objectives.

Additionally, the minutes will assist markets in putting our individual speeches and interviews in better perspective, making it less likely that they will misinterpret our statements. I've found it useful in my speeches and public discussions to reference the minutes or the announcement, and having this document published earlier will give us more opportunities to utilize this method of putting our own remarks in the overall context and reinforcing the Committee's message.

I would not want to trade early release of the minutes for a shorter, less forward-looking announcement on the day of the meeting. I believe there are useful things we can say about the future in that statement. The experience of the last five years, including the last year and a half of interest rate guidance, has been successful in this regard. We've provided information that has helped the markets make accurate policy predictions without constraining our ability to take needed policy action.

The main negatives, as Bill mentioned, are the potential feedback on the minutes and the deliberations of the Committee. However, we cannot adopt a new transparency tool that would make the Committee discussion less useful and productive. The experiment, along with the extra time embedded in Vincent's proposal, should address concerns about the Committee's ability to

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. The floor is now open for discussion.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to share my perspective on the issue of releasing the committee minutes earlier. Having worked closely with Vincent for the past five years, I've seen us make significant progress towards our goals. I used to express reservations about expediting the release of minutes, but after careful consideration, I've shifted my stance.

Don has made some compelling points about the benefits of earlier release. The minutes will be more accurate and detailed, providing a more nuanced representation of our discussions. They will also serve as a touchstone for evaluating other commentary and may help to clarify certain issues.

Of course, there are costs associated with this decision. The high attention paid to the minutes could potentially overshadow other news that may be more relevant to future policy deliberations. There's also the risk that the minutes may not be a smooth and polished reflection of what transpired at the meeting.

Despite these concerns, I've come to believe that the benefits outweigh the costs. We're already interacting with the markets between meetings, and the current set of minutes may not accurately reflect the most recent developments. The markets often react to the information in the minutes, whether appropriately or not, and we can't control this reaction.

Given that we're already in a position where the minutes are treated as a reflection of the Committee's views, I think it makes sense to have more contemporary and relevant minutes. This will help to ensure that the markets have a clearer understanding of our current stance, rather than relying on outdated information.

In conclusion, my views on this issue have evolved, and I now support the earlier release of the minutes. This experiment has been valuable in helping me to understand the implications of this decision and has provided us with valuable insights into how the markets react to our communications.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I’ve felt for a long time that the minutes, given the way we currently handle the timing of their release, are an unappreciated or at least underappreciated asset. So I continue to strongly favor moving up the release date. I think Don basically made the case, so let me just quickly summarize things as I see them. In my experience, the minutes are accurate, they are comprehensive, and they are valuable. They illustrate the thinking of the Committee, and they get into the issues that we discuss when we consider policy. I think all of that is important to communicate to the public. In short, I believe it would improve communication to release the minutes earlier, when people would have an incentive to take a real interest in them. And that, to me, is the reason for doing it. I agree that there are some potential downsides, and I don’t want to overstate or exaggerate the benefits. But I do believe that, on balance, the benefits are rather compelling. I also think that it’s certainly possible from time to time that market participants will read something in the minutes that we believe is innocuous and make a lot more of it than we would. But in my view we shouldn’t be so paternalistic and worry that, if market participants don’t get it right every time, something has gone seriously wrong. Most market participants are smart, sophisticated people, and I think, on balance, they will get it right most of the time; and when they don’t, that’s their business.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: I support the expedited release of the minutes. I think we should point out that on a number of occasions this year the delayed release of the minutes has actually confused the market—stepped on our message from the most recent meeting by sending a somewhat different signal. And although I don’t have a lot of market contacts, a number of them did contact me at those times and asked, “What’s going on here?” Second, I would put more weight on the notion that we all have to go out and give speeches and talks and answer questions and we worry about giving the wrong signal and confusing the market. It would be a comfort to have a Committee document out there to which we can refer and which the market can use for putting our remarks in perspective. That will reduce the risk that we all face of inadvertently moving the market or creating a misimpression. And finally, I would just note, as Vincent’s study showed, that a number of other central banks have used this practice effectively and have not had serious problems with it.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: I support early release. We don’t have to do this, but I think it’s the sensible thing to do, and I think it’s the sensible time to do it. I can’t improve on the arguments made in favor of it by Don, Gary, and Ben. I would say, Bill, that the argument you led off with is sort of an argument for extending the delay and taking release of the minutes out of the time frame when they would be viewed as having any signal value for the future stance of monetary policy. You weren’t advocating that, but I think it’s awkward to suggest that we should release the minutes outside the time period of the meeting to which they relate. In my view it’s better to take the risk of some noise added to the signal by releasing the minutes between meetings, closer to the relevant meeting, than to have the risk that the release comes after a new policy decision has been made and thus creates broad confusion about how to interpret the minutes in that context. Generally I think early release will add more perspective to our views about the outlook for inflation and output growth and will provide more context to the range of views across the Committee. Therefore, I believe it will give people a better sense of the factors shaping our decision, and in that sense, I think it would be helpful.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement: I support early release of the minutes, Mr. Chairman. I have for some time. I think it will give a better sense of the Committee’s views and the process of its thinking with regard to policy, and I believe that will serve the market well.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
During this meeting, I believe the experiment we've been conducting has been successful. The closer proximity of the product creation to our discussions has resulted in a higher quality initial product for both the transcript and minutes. I've managed to keep up with the tight schedule, despite a few late comments from my end. I'd like to commend the team for their efforts in streamlining the process. Regardless of the outcome of this discussion, I'd advocate continuing with our current approach.

Regarding the early release of minutes, I agree with President Poole that we've been conducting this experiment at an opportune time. Our statements have provided a sense of near-term assurance to the market, and releasing the minutes early probably wouldn't have had a significant impact. They would have confirmed what the market already knew.

I've viewed the early release of minutes as a trade-off for removing forward-looking commentary from our statements. Over the years, we've used language like "tilt" and "measured pace" to give the markets a sense of our policy direction. I believed that, with the minutes' earlier release, the markets would receive their conditioning from the minutes instead of relying on our statements.

Going forward, I don't anticipate the need for such explicit forward-looking commentary in our statements. The future direction of policy will be less clear to us, and the market should be forming its own opinions and taking appropriate action. I see the two communications as a package.

I'd prefer to change both the statement and the minutes at the same time, but if it takes us a while to decide on a change to the statement, I'd continue the experiment with the minutes in the meantime. I recognize that this is a challenging decision, and I wouldn't make it now. I'd make it when we're ready to alter the statement. In the interim, I'd advocate for the middle ground.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: I would support early release. I would do it unequivocally. Unlike Cathy, I don’t view this as something under the conservation of law and information here. So if we give out more information sooner, I don’t think we have to take anything back. In particular, I think the statements are working very well now, and I wouldn’t change those in the least. I just plain support early release. Let me make three other quick points. Bill mentioned that, if we didn’t have early release of the minutes, we’d still have a number of speeches as a communication tool. Well, member speeches are member speeches; they are not statements from the whole Committee. I don’t think the two are equivalent, and I’d rather have something from the whole Committee. On the question of whether the market would misinterpret the data, I can’t see how bringing the minutes closer to when the data came out would increase misinterpretation. This is a point Don made. I think it would reduce misinterpretation. And, third, I’d like to support a point that Ben made, which is that, when the minutes come out after we’ve given the statement for the next meeting, there’s inevitably confusion about that. It has happened several times, and with early release of the minutes we get away from that. So, to me, it’s becoming clearer and clearer that we ought to go to early release and that we shouldn’t do anything to our statement.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I was initially skeptical about releasing the minutes earlier, like Roger and some others in the group. However, I've come around to the idea that it could be beneficial. I'd like to add a few thoughts, building on what others have said. I've noticed that we've been using error bands in our forecasts more frequently over the last few years, and it seems to me that there are also opinion bands among us during our meetings. I've found it challenging to summarize our post-meeting statements into a few phrases, and I agree with Cathy that we might consider a tradeoff if we're going to release the minutes earlier.

Ben, Roger, and Tim made valid points about how we're making speeches anyway, and the market is analyzing our words. I've often wished that the minutes were available earlier when I go back out to the street to discuss the meeting's outcomes. Having the minutes in the public domain earlier is a significant advantage.

I'd like to mention a couple of nuances. First, I'm a bit uneasy about not knowing how comments are resolved after they're submitted. Vincent has been transparent about the disposition of my comments, but I don't know what other comments came in or how they were handled. I trust Vincent and you, Mr. Chairman, but I'd be uncomfortable with other Secretariats or Chairmen without some insight into the process. You suggested that a small editing committee could help address this concern.

Lastly, if we're not going to release the minutes earlier but continue to produce them early, I wonder if we leave ourselves open to criticism if someone discovers that we've already produced them but haven't released them. If the minutes are in final form and people find out about that, we could face criticism for not making them public. I'm unsure if the halfway house of producing them early but not releasing them is a viable solution. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I think that the experiment was very helpful. My own feeling is that releasing the minutes early will provide useful information to the markets. I think it will help us communicate better. I agree, as others have said, that it will be very helpful in our speeches and interviews. And other central banks are doing it. Also, strictly from the standpoint of logic, we are producing minutes, and we are going to release them at some point. We now know that we have the technical ability to release them earlier than we have been. It’s rather difficult to argue that we should delay releasing them because we think people are going to misuse the information. That just doesn’t hang together from a logical standpoint. I don’t know that we could pass the “red face” test in making that argument in public. I agree with Cathy in that I would also very much like to take out the forward-looking statements in our announcements. But as for the idea of packaging these two changes together, I think it would be very difficult to accomplish just in terms of trying to get agreement among the Committee. It’s difficult enough to get agreement on one piece of this let alone two pieces at the same time. So I just don’t know if it’s practical to try to do that. If we decide to go this route, I do have one minor suggestion on the announcement. But why don’t I hold off on that until we decide whether we’re going to do this or not.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: I strongly support expedited release for a lot of the reasons that Governor Kohn and others have expressed. I have a general predilection toward more transparency; the more information in the markets, the better. I just want to make three points. One is that bringing this information out earlier in time would resolve uncertainty earlier. If we see any market reaction to this, for the most part it would just represent a shift in the timing of market volatility. It doesn’t seem to me likely that on net, in any global sense, it’s going to add any market volatility. So we shouldn’t let the magnitude of perceived market reactions dissuade us or give us second thoughts down the road. The second point I want to make is that I do see this as alleviating some of the pressure on the Committee with regard to the drafting of the statement. But, Cathy, I think this cuts both ways. After expedited release of the minutes, the statement will stand as the Committee’s latest communication for only three weeks. To some extent, that could make it easier to communicate and agree on phrasing rather than harder, since only three weeks are at stake rather than an entire intermeeting period. I agree with Don. I wouldn’t want to see the extent of the communication we do in the statement cut down. Finally, there’s one tiny detail that I hesitate to bring up, but it sort of surprised me. If it were up to me, I’d like to see it changed. Apparently, it’s the tradition that nonmembers’ policy statements are excluded from the minutes as they’re written. Now, I don’t remember ever noticing this before, and I can’t remember seeing it in the orientation package I got. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Is that true?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: In the last several paragraphs, which bear only on the policy decision, a statement refers only to comments made by members. Before that, in the discussion of the outlook, the minutes reflect the entire go-around, so it includes all participants at the table.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think that’s the logically necessary position for this Committee to take.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Which one?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The one just enunciated by Vincent.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: In the material Debbie circulated last week, the proposal was to make the distinction sharper by talking about members and other participants in the earlier section. But, again, when it comes to who voted on the policy decision, it’s only members.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Suppose we had a situation in which the nonmembers held different views from those who voted but then we show a unanimous vote. We’d get a lot of comments as to why the indicated vote was wrong. We can’t do it that way. The voting members have one privilege and one privilege only: They get to vote on the policy at that particular point and on the nature of the statement. The discussion of what is going on in the economy and the issues that are on the table and the relevance for potential future policy changes is a full Committee discussion. But the specific policy decision that is reported is the vote of the then-Committee. And the membership of the Committee changes from time to time.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Mr. Chairman, let me just say a couple of things. First, there is this mechanism for distinguishing between members and nonmembers. So a view about policy could easily be attributed to a nonmember. Second, many statements about the economy by members as well as by other participants seem integrally linked with their statements about policy. Today’s meeting, I think, was an excellent example of that.
