Third, I view the purpose of the minutes as a means to communicate to the public the deliberations that occurred at the meeting, rather than the deliberations of the Committee—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: No, that is not the purpose. The purpose is essentially to give the reasons why we voted the way we did.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Well, that’s a different philosophy; I’ll grant that.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It has always been that way. The point is that, if you divide the Committee into two separate groups and then discuss what those who voted on the particular statement thought and what those who didn’t vote thought, we are actually no longer talking about the Federal Open Market Committee. The Federal Open Market Committee is composed of the twelve members who have the right to vote at a particular time. That’s the law. We can’t get around it.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: May I ask a clarifying question?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Vincent, in the discussion in the minutes that refers to the first part of the meeting on the outlook, where you use the terms “most,” “many,” and “several,” is the denominator—

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: It is nineteen.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Could I just say something? We are really discussing two things here. One is the issue of members versus nonmembers—I would use the terms voting members and other members, but be that as it may—and whether you discuss all of their perspectives in the last part of the minutes on the policy discussion. Then there’s the other question, which relates to the change that Vincent and Debbie proposed, about whether to call certain people “other participants” in the first part of the discussion. I think that’s pushing it a bit. I don’t mind, if I’m not a voting member, not having my views included in the comments on policy. That doesn’t bother me. But to draw a distinction between members and other participants in—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, you don’t have to do that. The basic statement is that in that last section of the minutes we are merely stipulating “the reasons for the policy decision.” In that context, only the views of those who actually voted on the policy are included, and we don’t make reference to anybody else.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: That’s exactly what I am suggesting to you to do.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: That’s indeed what we do.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: In trying to actually write down what was the oral tradition, we did find that not everybody agreed on what it was. One thing was clear: As the Chairman said, the last two paragraphs essentially are viewed as explaining the vote of the Committee. Hence, in those paragraphs, the “members” we talk about are the members of the Committee.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Right.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: The problem is if you want to represent everyone’s views in the discussion of the economy several paragraphs earlier and you refer to them as “members.” Then you’ve used the same word to describe two different sets of people.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Are you using “members” in that different way now?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Yes. Unfortunately, it turned out that the rule was that anyone in the discussion was called a member up to the last couple of paragraphs, and then only members were called members in the last couple of paragraphs. That seemed to be an unsupportable inconsistency. We didn’t want to exclude nonmembers’ characterizations of the economy and the risks to the outlook, and one way to do that would be to call them “participants.”

Speaker: SPEAKER
Statement: Can’t you call the group in the last few paragraphs voting members?

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Yes, right. Why not call them voting members as opposed to members?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The definition of the Federal Open Market Committee is statutory, and there is no such thing as a nonvoting member of the Federal Open Market Committee.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I was rapped on the knuckles by Joe Coyne fifteen years ago on exactly that point. The membership of the Committee is twelve people, and there is no such thing as a nonvoting member.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: Why not call everyone participants in the first part of the—?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: We’re quite happy to do that, yes.

Speaker: SPEAKER
Statement: That’s what he’s proposing.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: That’s the proposal.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: That works.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: So up to paragraph 21, or whatever paragraph number it is, if you express a view you’re a participant in the meeting. If you express a view that is included in the last couple of paragraphs of the minutes, it’s there because you are a member.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: You’re a member, right. Okay.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Okay. President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement: Well, just briefly, I’d say that I definitely support the publication of the minutes on an expedited schedule, given that it is feasible. And I agree completely with Don’s analysis of the benefits. It does seem to me that, in moving toward perfecting a rational expectations kind of equilibrium, the minutes released in a timely fashion, when they’re still relevant, contain enough information about the Committee’s reaction to data and how it processes those data that it should help the market improve its understanding of the rationale for policy. And that includes the fact that there isn’t unanimity on the Committee; there is a range of opinions about the outlook and the risks. Just in the brief time that I’ve been back on the Committee, there have been occasions when the minutes were released at a point when they were actually misleading because the views of the Committee had changed. I’ve found that unfortunate. Let me respond to Cathy’s point. While I can understand that, if the Committee decides to move back to more formulaic language in the statement and to omit any reference to a consensus about future policy, some might view earlier release of the minutes as a substitute for those kinds of statements. But I would consider that both unnecessary and unfortunate. I support Ned’s comments about that, in that I think it’s actually quite helpful to have an explicit statement about the Committee’s consensus view regarding future policy. So I would not like to see this become a simultaneous decision to forgo that in favor of the earlier release of the minutes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement: I have two comments. First of all, I see the decision as, on average, a four- week acceleration of the release of the minutes, and therefore I think there are two considerations. One is, Is there a logistical problem with early release? Having had Vincent track me down when I’ve been away from the office at times, I realize that there is a burden in meeting the publication deadline. But from what I’m hearing, that is not an insurmountable problem. The second is, Will there be any significant changes in the minutes as a result of that acceleration? We won’t really know the answer to that until we get into it, and I think I’d support Don’s evaluation that it probably is a manageable issue. Also, of course, the first time that we release the minutes early it will be an early release. From there on, it will be a scheduled release. So I think we will get the early release only once. @laugh Finally, I don’t see this decision and the language in the statement as a package. I think on balance the market would tend to see a less forward-looking statement as a net reduction in our communication, and I think we would have to reverse that policy. That gets back to a point that Bill made earlier, which is that we can’t retreat. I’m an incrementalist because we can never go back. I think our communication policies will continue to evolve. There is no end game. There is no destination. We ought to make this move but do it in complete isolation and not have any other corresponding moves along with it.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement: Like President Minehan, I thought the experiment on early release of the minutes was motivated by the struggle that we were having in capturing the full sense of the Committee in the few words that are allowed in the press statement. I also have been uncomfortable with the code words that we’ve been using in that press statement. And those code words have proven awkward to remove without sending unintended signals to the markets. So I still prefer a shorter post-meeting statement with the early release of the minutes. I also agree with President Minehan—especially after reading Vincent’s list of untested aspects of the experiment—that we’ve been conducting it in the best of times. That led me to come to this meeting in favor of what Cathy referred to as the middle-of-the-road ground. Let’s extend the experiment through circumstances that might not be considered the best of times. I know that this is an unpopular position, but I thought we were going to continue to perfect our communication strategy and that this would all be one package. I can see, however, in listening to my colleagues, that I’m in a minority in that view. Nevertheless, I did want to take a more middle-of-the-road approach and continue to improve our communications.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: When I proposed the experiment on early release of the minutes, I thought that we were addressing a few questions. The first one was, Can we do it? Do we need to change the process? Can we all focus our attention on the minutes, given the time constraints? And given the new proposed process, were we comfortable with what would be released? I think that was the experiment. I think we learned a lot from that experiment, but not everything. We learned, for example, that we shouldn’t move to releasing the minutes on a Friday. We also decided that we ought to plan to publish the minutes three weeks after the meeting. Quite frankly, I think that’s a good addition of a few extra days to allow for contingencies. I believe we would have had a harder time meeting the publication goal ex post if we had started releasing the minutes on the previous Friday. But one never learns everything from an experiment. So if there are things left on the table, it shouldn’t surprise us. Nevertheless, we now have a new process for producing the minutes that is more timely. It brings more resources to the work, has hastened attention to it, and actually is a discussion that’s more symptomatic of the time when the minutes would be released. I, therefore, endorse the release of the minutes in a more timely fashion. I know there’s some concern about market reactions. Most market participants are grown-ups; they’ll sort that out. I guess I trust markets enough to think that they’ll figure out how to do it. I also share the concern addressed by President Guynn that, if we have these minutes and put them on the shelf and then take them out several weeks later, it’s just a matter of time before someone says, “Why are you doing that?” So, first of all, I think early release is the right thing to do. I now know we can do it. And I think not doing it puts us in a situation of less disclosure than seems appropriate. Having said all that, I think we should do it.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'll admit I had my doubts at the outset about the success of this operation. But I'm glad to report that I was wrong. The team presented an impressive demonstration of the feasibility of this process, and as we explored different options throughout the various stages, I believe we gained a deeper understanding of how it functions. In essence, the experiment has been a success.

Regarding Cathy's concern about the tradeoff between statements and minutes, I'm not overly worried. I view the forward-looking statements as an historical anomaly that allowed us to make projections based on our extended period of productivity growth. This gave us significant control over inflation, resulting in declining unit labor costs and enabling us to maintain a stance on inflation that was not concerned with the near term. I see this as an aberration, and I don't anticipate that the future will look the same.

Once we reach our desired destination with this "measured pace" approach, I propose that we return to a short-term, balanced focus and provide the traditional indication of Federal Reserve policy. There are risks to releasing the minutes earlier, but as Bill mentioned, it's a close call. If we make a mistake, the cost will not be significant. We can adjust our approach to the minutes or the information we provide in them if needed.

Given the general consensus of the Committee, I suggest we vote on implementing expedited release of the minutes at the February 2005 meeting and maintain a schedule of releasing minutes of regular meetings three weeks after the policy decision. Let's put this on the table for a vote. [Call the roll.]

Speaker: MS. DANKER
Statement: Chairman Greenspan Yes Vice Chairman Geithner Yes Governor Bernanke Yes Governor Bies Yes Governor Gramlich Yes President Hoenig Yes Governor Kohn Yes President Minehan Yes, I guess. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: This, incidentally, is a full vote of both the Presidents and the Governors on the grounds that it’s a decision that relates to the policy of the Committee, and all members and potential members, in my judgment, should vote.

Speaker: MS. DANKER
Statement: Okay. I’ll pick up the others in a moment. Governor Olson Yes President Pianalto I can’t repeat Cathy’s way of saying yes, but—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: “Yes, I guess” is a formal vote.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement: Okay. Yes.

Speaker: MS. DANKER
Statement: President Poole I’m persuaded. Yes President Guynn Yes President Lacker Yes President Moskow Yes President Santomero Yes President Stern Yes President Yellen Yes First Vice President Holcomb Yes

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I just want to congratulate Vincent on an extraordinarily well-done job.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Mr. Chairman? I congratulate you, too, Vincent, but may I make one minor suggestion?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Instead of waiting to announce this in the minutes that will come out right after the February meeting, I would announce it early in January for several reasons. One, I think it would give more time to the markets to be aware of this change. If we put it in the minutes of this meeting, which would be released after our February meeting, that would give market participants about twelve trading days before a new set of minutes—those for the February meeting—will be released. Second—and I hate to say this—there’s always a chance of a leak. I would hate to see this come out before we announce it.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think that’s a good point. But let me ask this: What are the reasons not to announce it earlier?

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: Yes. Let me take that a step further. What if we put something about this in our statement today?

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Exactly.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: I would strongly support that, if you can do it. I would do it as soon as possible. I thought you’d want to draft some wording and have the Committee look at it first.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let me put it this way. I heard no discussion here that in any way would preclude announcing this decision today, given that we’ve come to a conclusion and that it was effectively unanimous—with a couple of “I guess” votes. @laugh So unless there’s an objection, I would submit that it’s certainly possible to do this today. We have the time to put a sentence in the statement indicating what the Committee concluded and voted on regarding this issue today.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: One possibility, Mr. Chairman, is basically to pick up the language that you, in fact, voted on: “The Committee voted unanimously to begin expedited release of the minutes with the minutes of the February 2005 meeting and to maintain a schedule of releasing the minutes of regular meetings three weeks after the date of the policy decision.” I would also suggest that we put more detail, in terms of a paragraph or two, in the minutes of this meeting, so that market participants will know what is coming. You will have precluded the possibility of a leak, and market participants will get more explanation at a later date.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Fine. That’s excellent.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement: Mr. Chairman, will there be a question of why we’re not proceeding with early release of the minutes for this meeting?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I’d say it’s because the discussion of this issue occurred subsequent to the vote on the policy decision.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: Should there be some discussion about nineteen votes versus twelve in that statement?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let me go back. That would be the answer, but we could certainly move it up if—

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: As long as we’re into this, what’s the problem? If we can release the February minutes in three weeks, why can’t we release today’s minutes in three weeks?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: We could release these minutes in three weeks, if you so direct. I thought there were certain advantages to having a paragraph or two explaining why the Committee made this decision and giving details of how the Committee would do this going forward before those in the market actually got the first release.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Is that really necessary?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Not if you say it isn’t. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, I think the logic of our decision forces us into the position of doing precisely what Governor Gramlich is suggesting. Let me ask, Are there any dissents to actually releasing the minutes of today’s meeting on an expedited basis?

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: May I suggest one possible benefit of waiting?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: By all means, go ahead.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: You can all shout it down, but it occurs to me that one possible benefit is to give people who follow our every word—and there are many people around who do—a longer period of time to get used to the idea and to read the minutes of some previous meetings in the context of what was going on at the time. They could calm themselves down in time to get the February minutes three weeks after the meeting.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Three weeks is a long time. @laugh

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: All right. I’m willing to be shouted down on that. But that’s the only reason I can think of for waiting.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Are there any arguments that anyone can think of? The date these minutes would be released would be January 4. If it were Christmas Day, I think it would create a problem.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: Mr. Chairman, either we are going to do this or we aren’t. We can’t start out by immediately saying, “Oh, well, that date is not very convenient.”

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Let’s at least think of what we intend to do. The proposition currently on the table is (1) to announce that we’re going to release the minutes early and (2) to do it in three weeks. I haven’t heard serious objections, nor have I heard serious reasons—which are far more important—not to proceed. So unless I hear objections from anybody, I would read it as the consensus of this Committee to make the statement that we’re going to do it and to actually do it as of January 4. Is that acceptable to everybody?

Speaker: SEVERAL
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: So be it.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Mr. Chairman, just one more question or suggestion for Vincent. Maybe you’ve already considered this. When you release the minutes for the first time, you might want to consider adding some of the Secretariat’s clarifying information about the conventions in the language—an explanation of some of the terminology. I don’t know if you were intending to do that, but I think it would be helpful

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I did want to make clear the distinction between “participants” and “members.”

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: And the hierarchy of the language around the terms “majority” and “several” and so forth might be helpful, too.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: We thought of making a little index card that we could hand out to Fed watchers. @laugh

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: We can expect to be asked if we are one of the “few” or one of the “many.”

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: May we leave that issue of explaining the terminology to the Secretariat to make a judgment on?

Speaker: SEVERAL
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: In that event, I want to confirm that the dates of our next meeting are Tuesday and Wednesday, February 1 and 2, 2005. This meeting is adjourned, and we are off to have lunch with our former colleague, Bob McTeer.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Today is our regular annual organizational meeting and, as is customary, I will turn the floor over to Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman. At the first regularly scheduled Committee meeting of every year, our first obligation is to elect a Chairman and a Vice Chairman to serve until the election of their successors at the first regularly scheduled meeting of the next year. So let me now open the floor for nominations from among our members for Chairman and Vice Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: I nominate Alan Greenspan to be Chairman.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: And do you have a nomination for Vice Chairman?

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: I nominate Timothy Geithner as Vice Chairman.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: Is there a second to those nominations?

Speaker: SEVERAL
Statement: Second.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: Any other nominations? Any objections? Any comments? Well, we’re not in an era of great democracy. @laugh Let the record show a unanimous vote for Messrs. Greenspan and Geithner to be Chairman and Vice Chairman of the FOMC until its first regularly scheduled meeting of 2006. Congratulations.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you very much. I wish to note that there was no voter fraud that I could perceive. @laugh

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: And we have a turnout of 100 percent!

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The next item on the agenda is the selection of staff officers. Would you kindly read the list for us, please?

Speaker: MS. DANKER
Statement: Yes. For Secretary and Economist, Vincent Reinhart; Deputy Secretary, Deborah Danker; Assistant Secretary, Michelle Smith; General Counsel, Scott Alvarez; Deputy General Counsel, Thomas Baxter; Economists, Karen Johnson and David Stockton; Associate Economists, Thomas Connors, David Howard, Brian Madigan, Steven Oliner, David Wilcox, Charles Evans, Loretta Mester, Arthur Rolnick, Harvey Rosenblum, and Joseph Tracy.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I’ll entertain a motion for the proposed slate.

Speaker: SEVERAL
Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection, that is approved. The next item on the agenda is the selection of a Federal Reserve Bank to execute transactions for the System Open Market Account. My notes indicate to me, and I quote, “New York is the odds-on favorite.” @laugh In that event, not wishing to go against the odds, I will entertain any motion which is restricted to nominating the Federal Reserve Bank of New York!

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement: Well, consistent with the odds and tradition, I will nominate the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The nomination is approved without objection. Since we have done that, let’s move to a decision that may be far more important, which is to select the Manager of the System Open Market Account. I understand that there’s only one name on this ballot, and it’s Dino Kos. Unless anyone has an objection, I will presume that there was a nomination, a second, and a unanimous vote in the affirmative. Hearing no objection to that, I presume that that selection is now approved. Now I’d like to call on Dino in his renewed official position.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I have two subjects on which I’ll need a vote from the Committee. The first one is on the Foreign Currency Directive, the Authorization for Foreign Currency Operations, and the Procedural Instructions with Respect to Foreign Currency Operations. I recommend that they be approved with no amendments.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Questions? Objections? If not, they are approved.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: The second item is the Authorization for Domestic Open Market Operations, which I propose be approved with one amendment as described in the memo dated January 24, 2005. That amendment would eliminate the so-called leeway provisions—the limit on the amount by which holdings of securities in the System Open Market Account can change between meetings—given that the Committee has many other ways to oversee the activities of SOMA [System Open Market Account].

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Any questions? Approved without objection. We have received documents on proposed revisions to the Committee’s rules, statements, and resolutions, and I presume you have read them. I need a vote to approve the proposed revisions, but, obviously, if there are comments, we will discuss them prior to that. Hearing none, they are approved without objection. Incidentally, because of the timing, we need to determine officially as a Committee that public notice and comment on these amendments would be unnecessary or contrary to the public interest. I would accept a motion to that effect.

Speaker: SPEAKER
Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Approved without objection. We have an annual review of the Committee’s Program for the Security of FOMC Information. I’d like to emphasize a point covered on page 1, which is that individuals who have access to confidential information may provide it to or discuss it only with those Federal Reserve employees who are authorized to have access to such information. I raise these issues every year. We all vote upon these rules and agree to bind ourselves to them, and invariably we slip periodically. I trust that at some point we’ll do better than we have done, especially fairly recently. I understand that President Moskow would like to say a few words on this.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I think the leak of the topic for today’s discussion, which was the subject of an article that appeared in The Wall Street Journal, was very disappointing and troubling. I’ve been with the Federal Reserve for10 years, and we pride ourselves on being an organization with very high integrity. When we have a leak like that, I think it’s really an embarrassment to the Federal Reserve and to each of us individually. Leaks such as this have happened on rare occasions in the period that I’ve been here. But I think it is important that we emphasize every year, in line with the integrity of this organization, how crucial it is to maintain the confidentiality of what we discuss.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I’m at a loss to understand the motives of communicating obviously confidential information that could compromise the nature of our deliberations here. What is to be gained? I don’t get it. All I can do is to reiterate, as we vote on this, that we take it very seriously. I will assume everyone is in the affirmative on this. We will now move on to a rather large topic, price objectives for monetary policy. I’d like to call on David Wilcox, Doug Elmendorf, and Vincent Reinhart to make their presentations.

Speaker: MR. WILCOX
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to bring up the topic of the paper we've titled "Considerations for Setting a Specific Numerical Price Objective for Monetary Policy." Doug Elmendorf and I will provide a brief overview of the main points from the R&S staff paper that was distributed to the Committee. Afterward, Vincent will discuss the communication and governance implications of setting a numerical price objective.

The top panel of your first exhibit shows that inflation, as measured by major indexes, has been low and stable for several years. Some analysts argue that with the economy now close to price stability, it's time for more clarity and precision regarding the Federal Reserve's ultimate objectives regarding the price-related aspect of its dual mandate. Others, however, are skeptical of moving away from the current successful approach.

The paper outlines the key characteristics of a numerical price objective: it would be numerical rather than qualitative, stated in terms of a specific published index, and involve a commitment to either inflation control or price-level control. To give meaning to such an objective, the Committee would aim to achieve it on average over an extended period. The premise of the paper is that a price objective should be chosen to minimize the costs of deviations from price stability.

One argument against this view is that a smoother measure of inflation would breach a band of any given width less frequently, making communications easier. However, the width of any band would be a choice available to the Committee.

The question of whether the Committee should adopt a specific, numerical price objective is distinct from the question of whether it should adopt an inflation targeting regime. However, even the more limited step of establishing an explicit price objective would involve important changes in the Federal Reserve's relationships with the public and Congress.

Your second exhibit summarizes the potential benefits and costs of adopting a specific, numerical price objective. On the benefits side, advocates argue that it could help preserve the current commitment to price stability, anchor long-term inflation expectations, reduce inflation volatility, and improve public understanding of monetary policy. Skeptics, on the other hand, argue that the burden of proof should be on those who would disturb the status quo and see potential harm in misleading the public, inadvertently placing more emphasis on the price objective, damaging the Federal Reserve's credibility, and constraining future actions.

Speaker: MR. ELMENDORF
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to discuss some operational issues the Committee would face if we were to set a numerical price-related objective. The first issue is the choice of a price index. We recommend consumer prices for several reasons, which you can see outlined in the top right panel of the exhibit. Practically, consumer prices are the most familiar to the public and best measured. Theoretically, inflation rates generally move together in the long run, so anchoring consumer price inflation would help keep other broad measures of inflation in check.

The second issue is the choice between inflation control and price-level control. While I won't go into detail on the pros and cons here, our paper concludes that an inflation-based strategy is the best option for us at this time.

The third issue is the average inflation rate. The middle panel highlights the main considerations. One factor is measurement bias, which can overstate changes in the cost of living by nearly 1 percentage point per year for the CPI and ½ percentage point per year for PCE prices. When setting an objective, it's essential to take this bias into account.

The rationale for aiming for zero true inflation is the traditional costs of inflation, such as money illusion and relative-price confusion. However, downward nominal wage rigidity and the zero lower bound on nominal interest rates are the chief rationales for positive true inflation.

The last issue is the choice between a point objective and a range. A point objective would be simple, but a range might be preferred to communicate inflation variability, allow for uncertainty, express divergent views, or create a "zone of indifference." The economic consequences of announcing a range would depend on the reasons and explanation to the public.

Our fourth exhibit considers the accuracy with which the FOMC could achieve an inflation objective. Due to inflation's volatility and the indirect and lagged impact of monetary policy, the Committee cannot hit a point objective precisely or guarantee a narrow range. The table in the middle panel shows results from stochastic simulations of the FRB/US model, which indicate that total PCE inflation could be held within plus or minus 1 percentage point of the desired rate about 59% of the time, and core PCE inflation could be held in the same interval 64% of the time, assuming the shocks from 1968 to 2004.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to discuss the complexity of determining an explicit price objective for the Committee, taking into account the process of choosing such an objective and communicating it to the public and Congress. The decision tree in exhibit 5 may help guide our conversation, even if it seems unlikely at first. The main question at hand is whether an explicit, numerical specification of price stability would aid us in achieving our goals of maximum employment, price stability, and moderate long-term interest rates.

Based on the arguments presented by David and Doug, some of you might believe that our past behavior has already made our inflation preferences clear to the public and each other, making the benefits of this additional step small compared to the costs. In that case, we might prefer to maintain the status quo, as represented in the left branch of the tree. However, there could still be room for improvement while preserving the current structure. For instance, we could encourage more specificity about inflation preferences within the Committee and in public communications.

On the other hand, if we decide to quantify an explicit price objective, as in the right part of the schematic, we must then decide whether or not to make that objective public. We could keep it private, but I cannot conceive of how the Committee could justify doing so, nor how it could be kept secret. A decision so central to our policy decisions would have to be reported in the minutes for them to remain accurate.

If we settle on a numerical definition of price stability amongst ourselves, we must ask how the FOMC will choose that objective. We could view it as a group decision, similar to choosing ranges for monetary aggregates in the past. Alternatively, we could poll participants periodically as to their preferred specification of the Committee's price objective.

Ultimately, the key question is how we define price stability. Is it known only by inference about behavior, or by a numeric specification? If it's the latter, what price index or indexes do we prefer? Is it stated in terms of a path for its level or as a rate of change, and what are the desired point estimates or ranges for their rate of growth?

I suggest we organize our discussion around these questions.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. Let’s just open the floor to general questions and see where our conversation leads us. Who would like to raise the first issue? Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to express my gratitude for the opportunity to contribute to this discussion. I acknowledge that I don't represent the average voter on this issue, so my primary goal today is to learn from my colleagues. I want to commend the staff for their thorough analysis of the costs and benefits of a specific inflation objective. They rightly pointed out that the empirical evidence on the matter is not definitive. I believe the same uncertainty applies to the question of whether low inflation is desirable in the first place. Given the difficulty of obtaining precise empirical evidence, I think we must consider a priori reasoning as part of the argument.

Regarding the definition of price stability, I've previously referred to it as a range of 1 to 2 percent based on the core PCE deflator. I'd like to emphasize two principles. First, the lower bound of the range should not be zero. I think maintaining some distance from the zero lower bound and our interest rates is crucial, and I'd be uneasy with a lower range that frequently brings us to zero. Second, whether to use a core or total measure depends on the time horizon. For a short-term perspective, I believe the core measure is preferable due to its stability and representation of underlying trends. For a long-term objective, a total measure is acceptable.

Personally, I prioritize reaching a consensus and do not hold a dogmatic stance on this measure. If we were to adopt an explicit numerical objective, I think it would be a significant step forward to define our long-term inflation objective as the rate that best achieves our dual mandate. If we were to take this step, I would not advocate for further steps towards a short-term inflation targeting regime.

Moreover, I believe one advantage of this approach would be to offer a clear and quantifiable method of communicating our intentions and plans to the public. If we were to pursue this path, I think we should combine an inflation objective with more frequent forecasts to help the public assess the economy's progress towards the stable equilibrium. In summary, I would be open to removing the forward-looking language in the statement and transitioning to a more quantitative communication style if we were to adopt an explicit inflation objective. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think you’re raising a very important first question. Are we, in fact, all in agreement that low inflation is a desirable goal? If we don’t come to an agreement on that, the rest of the conversation, in my judgment, is moot. So it might not be a bad idea to put that question on the table. I have always presumed, judging from the experience especially of recent years, that we have all concluded that low inflation—having created a certain degree of lower risk and greater stability—has enhanced the real economy in a manner which our experiences with high inflation did not. If that conclusion is not generic around this table, then we have a much broader question to be discussed among this Committee. So I’d just like to put that on the table to be sure that we don’t end up with a majority vote on the other side of this question. If there is anyone here who feels even modestly in this direction, please take the floor and make your case. Governor Bies, please.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I would just add one perspective on that, which relates to how businesses reacted when the Fed really started to bring inflation down in ’79, ’80, and ’81. One of the challenges that a lot of companies faced in that period was that they had gotten so accustomed to being able to raise prices automatically—because the climate was such that everybody was raising them—that the business processes around inflation anticipation were very different than what we have today. So to answer the question of whether low inflation is a desirable goal, one would have to look at it based on where we are today. Today I think companies understand that a major challenge in this very competitive environment is to find ways to enhance productivity. They realize that it’s a very tough world to operate in compared to the period of easy price increases that they might have experienced in the early ’80s. I think companies have learned that. So in today’s environment, since low inflation lowers the cost of capital and forces them to focus on service quality and their processes and controls, overall I think businesses would agree that low inflation is a desirable objective. But I think we would have heard a different answer from them if we had asked that in 1980.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
Person 1: I believe the actions taken in October 1979 were aimed at curbing inflation and preventing an unstable economic system. The business community's belief that they could pass on cost increases was a major concern, as it could lead to a serious problem. Since then, we've established a stable price environment, which is ideal for long-term growth. However, we should not take this for granted.

Person 1: Regarding inflation targeting, I'm unsure if focusing solely on price inflation is the best approach. It could potentially skew our analysis. If the evidence were compelling, we'd have to consider it. But for now, I see no value in a specific numeric target. Price stability is crucial, but whether we define it by a specific index is less important.

Person 1: We also need to consider the role of asset prices in macroeconomic stability. If we decide to factor in asset prices, it could lead to a new approach in policy decision-making. This is a complex issue, and we should consider it in the context of an ever-changing economic environment.

Person 1: I've been observing the economy for decades, and I've never seen a period like this. We've had a good understanding of the underlying forces driving inflation, unemployment, and productivity. However, I fear that this recent period might be an exception. We should keep this in mind as we consider changing our procedures.

Person 1: That's all I have to say for now. Anyone else want to add to the discussion? @laugh

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to express my strong belief that a low and consistent inflation rate is crucial for an economy's long-term health. Having witnessed the consequences of inflationary cycles in our region, I've come to this conviction. I found the papers on adopting an explicit price target insightful. While there isn't clear evidence of negative effects from explicit objectives, there is some weak evidence suggesting they may help anchor long-term inflation expectations.

Considering this, I'd lean towards supporting a range for consumer price inflation as an objective. Given its widespread recognition, I'd suggest using the Consumer Price Index (CPI) for this purpose. My proposed range would be around 1 to 2 percent or 1½ to 2½ percent. I believe the medium term should be the horizon for this objective, making total CPI the preferred choice. If the horizon were shorter, I'd agree with Governor Bernanke that the objective should be stated in terms of the core CPI.

However, I acknowledge the concern that an explicit inflation objective might emphasize one component of our dual mandate too much. To mitigate this, we should always discuss both objectives when discussing monetary policy strategy and operations. We cannot set a target for the growth side, but we should ensure we don't overlook it.

Regarding introducing an explicit numerical goal, I don't believe we need congressional approval but would defer to an expert on that matter. I could support having the Committee vote on a medium-term inflation objective annually, allowing us to adapt to a changing environment. While the objective might change depending on developments, I doubt it would do so significantly or frequently. We'd likely reaffirm the previous year's objective or modify it slightly based on new circumstances.

In conclusion, I believe an explicit inflation objective would help us maintain focus on our primary goal of long-term price stability. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. I’d like to confine my remarks at this time to the points that you raised a few moments ago. The importance of the long-term nominal anchor is great, and I think it has a lot to do with enhancing productivity and the other things that Governor Bies was talking about. But I think it’s very important for stability in the short run that expectations be closely tied to that long-term anchor—that is, expectations about long-run inflation—and that those expectations not be pushed one way or another by current events. One of the things that was so clear in the 1970s was that short-term monetary policy actions or inaction led to a destabilization of the longer-term expectational environment. When that happens, it puts severe constraints on the short-run policy options available to the Committee. One of the great benefits of very solidly established long-term expectations is that they provide the Committee with a lot more freedom to react to short-run disturbances of all different sorts without upsetting the expectational environment. I think that’s a very great benefit in terms of short-run stability. To me, one of the advantages of an explicit FOMC inflation objective is that it would assist in anchoring long-run expectations and make them less reactive or potentially reactive to short-term events, including policy decisions that are made for purposes of short-term stabilization.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: May I ask what evidence there is that that is indeed the case? Do you have any evidence that knowledge of a central bank committee’s specific numerical judgment as to the appropriate range for inflation does, in fact, anchor longer-term expectations? Obviously, if it does, it’s tremendously important. Is there evidence that you find persuasive?

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: No, I agree with Ben Bernanke that evidence in this area is very, very hard to come by. But I’m saying that is one of the benefits I would see, basing my view mainly on—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: If it were to happen, it would be an obvious benefit, but—

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: One thing that I do know, and I can speak to evidence in this sense, is that since the Korean War, meetings of this Committee have often focused at particular times on a concern that policy actions would be viewed by the market as upsetting the inflationary environment. When you go back and look at the minutes of these meetings—particularly around business cycle peaks, when the economy was already beginning to turn down—the Committee was typically slow to ease policy even though there was an understanding that the business cycle peak was at hand or at risk or had even passed. The reason, and you see this again and again in the discussions around this table, was concern that a premature easing—what the FOMC participants would call a premature easing—of policy would affect expectations in the market and would, therefore, aggravate the inflation problem that the Committee had been dealing with.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I remember saying exactly that.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: So I can point to evidence that that happened numerous times around this table.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, no. The reason—and I remember it at these meetings—was that, indeed, we were looking at the question of inflationary imbalances building up. The point is that we knew in retrospect that the economy was tilting downward, but there were innumerable occasions when inflationary imbalances were building up and the economy didn’t turn down. We have an anti-inflation bias in this Committee, which is as it should be. And our judgment on policy has always involved a cost-benefit analysis—with particular concern about the cost of being wrong and going back to the 1979 experience. But I don’t consider that evidence. How does that control long-term expectations?

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: It is not evidence that an announced target would help to anchor expectations. I’m making that judgment on the basis of what I think I understand about the process.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, you obviously have opinions or else you wouldn’t be making the statement.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: But I do think that well before 1979—in the whole period since the Korean War—one sees over and over again at business cycle peaks that this Committee was very concerned that it would upset the inflation expectations environment by acting prematurely. If you’re asking for evidence, I think there is one case, strangely enough, on the other side. That was when we were getting the funds rate down to 1 percent. The inflation rate was low and there was uncertainty in the marketplace about what the Committee’s inflation objectives were. And it was at that point that the language came into the statement regarding an “unwelcome further decline” in inflation, and—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: That’s qualitative.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: That was qualitative. That’s correct.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I don’t think anybody disagrees on the notion of making our conviction of price stability on both sides clear.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Right.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The issue is whether there is a critical advantage of a specific number rather than a range or probabilistic view or some qualitative view. That’s what we’re discussing. I think we have agreed that low inflation is superior, and I think we all have a general view of where low inflation is with respect to the indexes. The question is: Do we get an incremental advantage by specificity whereby it alters expectations in a way that clearly would be favorable? That’s the real question.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: I think in that case that if we had previously announced a range, the market— well before inflation got close to or below the bottom of the range—would have been saying that the FOMC will be responding. The market would have been expecting us to move the funds rate down because inflation was getting down to or below the bottom of the announced range. The one other case is not a U.S. case but Japan. I think the Japanese have also suffered from not being very clear that they do not want deflation, particularly as the situation developed in the early 1990s. The markets were left quite at sea in trying to figure out where Japanese monetary policy was going to go.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I think they still are.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Yes, they probably still are.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Let me make just a few broad comments. Normally, when I start thinking about these kinds of issues, I ask myself what is broken that we’re trying to fix. Here I ask myself: Do we want to be more explicit about a price or inflation objective? My answer to that is “yes,” but it’s not because I’m trying to fix something. I view it as the next logical step in more or less formalizing what we really have been doing over the past decade or more—what I would call trying to lock in past successes. So to me that’s the most compelling rationale for seriously considering taking this step. As I said, I’ve already come down pretty favorably on it, but I have two reservations that give me some pause. Let me just mention them. They’ve already come up. One is: Will we send the wrong signal about our dual mandate? I think Tom Hoenig already addressed that in suggesting that we can clarify that through communication. Many of us already address that when we’re out in public by emphasizing the significance of price stability or low inflation, but indicating that the underlying reason for our commitment to price stability is how it affects economic performance. The second concern that gives me a bit of pause is: Would this inappropriately tie our hands under certain circumstances? There I concluded that, first of all, if there were a legitimate crisis, we wouldn’t have any difficulty explaining why we were deviating from whatever inflation objective we had announced. If we were concerned about deflation, I don’t see any conflict because people would see what we were doing and the reasons for it would line up. So it seems to me that would be a concern only if we had a strange confluence of circumstances where inflation was an issue but simultaneously we were experiencing subpar growth or something like that. In such a case, I think Bill Poole’s comments about the 1970s are relevant. First of all, I don’t know what the probability is of the confluence of those kinds of events. It’s probably going to be low. But secondly, I think it is worthwhile to have our hands tied at a time such as that.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to express my viewpoint on the significance of establishing a clear inflation goal for the FOMC. I strongly advocate for a numeric definition of price stability moving forward. In my recent speech, I suggested a target band of 1-3% annual inflation based on the 12-month moving average rate of change in the core PCE deflator. However, I'm open to various approaches for designing an effective inflation targeting framework.

The key attributes of such a framework include: a range versus a single number, the time interval for the target, and the measure of inflation. My proposal covers these aspects, but I'm not married to the specifics.

The benefits of being precise about our price stability goal are substantial. An inflation targeting regime would:

1. Foster open discussion
2. Clarify our intentions to the public
3. Provide a clear criterion for evaluating policy
4. Align public expectations with our intentions
5. Build public confidence in price stability over the long term

Moreover, I believe that an explicit inflation target would increase our flexibility and help us achieve other economic objectives. Our credibility, earned through past actions, has allowed us to lower interest rates recently without sparking inflation fears. This flexibility was crucial in mitigating the shallowness of the last recession.

By implementing an inflation targeting regime, we can pass on our hard-earned credibility to future Committees, ensuring they can benefit from our past successes in combating inflation and maintaining price stability for the past 20 years.

I understand there are arguments against inflation targeting, but I find them less compelling. For instance, I believe that a well-designed inflation targeting framework can coexist with other economic objectives. In conclusion, I believe that an explicit inflation target would be a valuable step towards enhancing public trust and ensuring long-term price stability.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: You’re using the term “inflation targeting” to mean?

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: Well, this broad notion of saying that we have a numerical target and here’s what the number or the range is.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: But you’re going beyond what’s in the text. Are you using the terms interchangeably or are you talking—

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: Yes, I am using it to mean this broad notion of our saying that we as a group have in mind a number that we want for inflation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I ask because inflation targeting has a very specific operational meaning.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: I understand your point. That’s why I think, quite frankly, that by arguing about the details we lose sight of what we’re trying to do. What we have done up to this point is to agree on a rather open notion that we think the economy operates most effectively in a stable, low-inflation environment, and we have made that clear to the public. In my view, the next step is to say, “this is what we think we’re looking for in terms of price stability.” And as I said at the outset, I think the way to do this is by setting a range for an inflation objective over a period of time. That adds information. It is not so strict in the purest sense of an inflation target, but rather an inflation targeting framework or regime—those are the words I would use. I’d like to talk for just a moment or two about the arguments that we shouldn’t move toward inflation targeting. For starters, I believe inflation targeting is totally consistent with the FOMC’s mandate. I agree with the view expressed by others around this table that we don’t need Congress to set a definition of price stability. It is a technical aspect of what our mandate is. Some of the discussions concerning the cost of inflation targeting to me actually serve to highlight the problem of discretionary policy. There will be times when the commitment to inflation targeting in my sense of the term will be constraining. If this were not the case, there would be no need for institutionalizing our commitment, because discretion and the commitment would yield the same policy. Therefore, an explicit inflation targeting environment will give the FOMC stronger incentives to act as it says it intends. Recognizing this, the public will consider the commitment to low and stable inflation more credible so that the full benefit of a stable price environment can be realized. But will inflation targeting in this sense be too constraining? I don’t believe so. I recognize that all contingencies for any situation cannot be pre-specified. In special circumstances, the FOMC must be able to act appropriately. Any miss in the inflation target because of extraordinary events that occur could easily be communicated to the public and, therefore, would not result in an inappropriate monetary policy or loss of credibility. The experience of other countries and the fact that we are already implicitly targeting inflation without hindering economic performance indicate to me that it’s not a major challenge to adopt an inflation targeting framework.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
During our meeting today, Vincent raised the question of whether an explicit numerical specification of price stability would be beneficial for us in achieving our objectives of price stability and maximum sustainable employment, as well as in our internal and external communications. My response to this question is "no, I don't think so." I understand the value of setting inflation targets or goals, especially for countries with high inflation rates or those just transitioning to market economies. It can help establish policy intent and build credibility. However, the United States is not in such a situation. We've had a stable inflation environment for decades, and I believe our definition of price stability, which is the absence of rising prices as a major concern for businesses and consumers, has been successful.

Some of you might argue that stating a range of acceptable inflation would make our policy more transparent and forthright. I agree that transparency is important, but I also believe that setting a numerical goal could potentially be less transparent or even lead to poor policy decisions. If we commit to an inflation goal but don't always adhere to it due to economic conditions, stating the goal might not improve communication. Instead, it could create confusion.

Moreover, inflation is not the only goal we have. Sustainable employment is equally important. Our policy preferences for a given level or path of inflation would depend on the state of the real economy. In the short run, there can be tradeoffs between growth and inflation. We need flexibility to set policy with a balance between the two goals.

Inflation targets or goals are similar to performance metrics used to evaluate effectiveness. While they can be useful, they can also be tricky. Focusing too much on one metric can lead to unintended consequences. Inflation targets need to be multidimensional and balanced with robust control systems to be effective.

I trust our ongoing commitment to price stability, regardless of who is in this room. What I am concerned about is the potential impact on the balance and discretion that is essential for effective policymaking. In conclusion, I view inflation targets or goals as a potential solution in search of a problem and with the potential to create one. I am not in favor of adopting an inflation goal at this time. However, if we decide to do so in the future, I have some thoughts to share.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement:
During today's meeting, I'd like to begin by expressing my appreciation for the comprehensive and well-balanced background papers presented by the staff. The topic at hand is whether the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) should adopt an explicit numerical price-related objective, and if so, how. I believe this is an essential question, despite its seemingly narrow presentation. While announcing such an objective may not require significant changes in our current policymaking practices, I strongly believe it's a crucial step we need to take.

Regarding the operational issues raised in Wilcox and company's memo, I concur with their recommendation to target either the Consumer Price Index (CPI) with a 2% midpoint or the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index with a 1.5% midpoint. These midpoints would account for an upward measurement bias and provide an additional 1% cushion against the zero bound. I personally prefer targeting the PCE index due to its methodological advantages and our internal focus on it. Additionally, I favor targeting the core measure to anchor headline inflation over time while allowing food and energy prices to fluctuate. I also propose a 1 percentage point range, from 1 to 2%, rather than a point target, to provide a reasonable safe harbor for inflation fluctuations. A narrow safe harbor would encourage us to explain these fluctuations and our plans to reverse them.

In response to Vincent's memo, I suggest announcing our objective to the public, emphasizing that it's an incremental step and not a dramatic departure from our current monetary policy approach. We should also highlight the benefits of this step, such as enhancing the operational power of monetary policy to stabilize both employment and inflation. This would be achieved by better anchoring long-term inflation expectations and improving our ability to communicate in the short run.

Some may argue that monetary policy has been working well lately, and we're already credited with maintaining low inflation. However, I believe there are three compelling reasons to announce an explicit long-run inflation objective now. First, we all agree that we want inflation to remain close to its current level. Second, an announced objective would not limit our flexibility, as we've already given up significant flexibility in establishing our reputation for price stability. An announced objective would guide our actions in the long run and help us respond to short-term

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to begin by expressing my appreciation for the team's impressive work on the paper and presentation. When I joined this committee back in 1994, I believed that inflation was above the comfort level of every FOMC participant, and reducing it was a widely shared goal. At that time, a qualitative definition of price stability was sufficient to communicate our objective to the public. However, with underlying inflation now at a level many of us consider optimal, the costs of lowering it further have become insignificant. I think articulating a numerical long-term inflation objective could facilitate internal discussions and help anchor public expectations, enhancing our ability to avoid deflation near the zero interest rate bound. It could also reduce the potential for destabilizing inflation scares following adverse supply shocks, allowing monetary policy to respond to their real effects.

However, I'm aware of the potential costs and unintended consequences of such a policy. Some argue it could lead to a slippery slope that undermines our mandates for maximum employment and broader financial stability. Therefore, any enunciation of a numerical inflation goal must be in the context of clear communication of our multiple objectives and the policies we use to achieve them.

In 1996, there was a considerable consensus among participants, including myself, for a 2% long-run objective for CPI inflation. Since then, several developments have occurred. The potential negative impact of downward nominal wage rigidity on real economic performance has diminished due to rising productivity and average wage growth. However, the experience of very low inflation both here and abroad has heightened my concern regarding the zero lower bound. Considering these factors, I find myself still comfortable with the numerical objective we discussed a decade ago.

Instead of adopting an objective based on the CPI, I would now enunciate an objective based on the core PCE. This seems sensible given the methodological advantages of that index and the Committee's recent emphasis on it. My preference would be for a long-run inflation objective of 1.5% for core PCE inflation.

Regarding communication, I suggest combining the polling and surveying elements of Vincent's alternative 3 with the communication strategy of alternative 1. If a consensus on a numerical objective is reached, it should be expressed in the minutes using carefully crafted language, emphasizing the potential for conflicts

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
During our meeting, Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective on the topic of price stability and inflation. I believe there's a shared understanding among us that maintaining low, stable inflation is the long-term goal of this Committee. This consensus, as you mentioned, Mr. Chairman, and as Governor Bies agreed, enables us to promote sustainable growth by keeping inflation out of the equation. I'm confident that this consensus will endure, regardless of time or personnel changes.

Regarding the idea of locking in past performance, I think it holds less weight in the argument for defining price stability or setting an inflation target. In my opinion, price stability is already a given, and I'd be surprised if it were to disappear. There's a range of measured inflation rates that can promote efficiency and not significantly impact private decisions. The policy response to a projected inflation rate depends on the overall economic conditions and the dynamics of the price process. For instance, a steady 1% inflation rate may not be as concerning in the presence of strong productivity and demand, as opposed to weak conditions.

Moreover, the attitude towards an inflation goal could be influenced by our perceptions of financial stability. In my view, we've been conducting policy effectively, producing price stability and stabilizing economic cycles. President Lacker stated that we wouldn't lose much flexibility by adopting a definition. I agree, and I don't see a reason to give up this flexibility.

However, I do believe we could enhance the clarity of our price stability objective. For instance, we could extend our February projections for a year and communicate the sorts of inflation situations that would concern us. If necessary, we could consider an explicit numerical range from the FOMC as a Committee to anchor expectations. I would suggest a broad measure, such as total PCE inflation, and a fairly wide range, like 1 to 3 percent. I think it's essential to clarify that this definition is a long-term one and that choosing a range or a definition does not imply a downgrading of our output stabilization goal.

Lastly, if we were to adopt a numerical definition, I think it's crucial that we consult extensively with the Administration and Congress to explain our reasoning and the implications for future policy conduct. In all inflation targeting countries, the government has been involved in some way in the adoption of their regimes or definitions. We're not discussing

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. Let’s take a break now for maybe 10 or 15 minutes. We’ll come back and see if we can get fully around the table by the end of the day.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to express my gratitude to the team for the insightful work on this topic. I strongly agree that maintaining price stability is crucial for the country's long-term economic health, and I commend the FOMC for its success in achieving and sustaining it over the past two decades. However, I do believe there's room for improvement in our current practices.

I propose that we be more explicit about our inflation objective to enhance our credibility and internal communication. While an explicit numerical price objective isn't a silver bullet, it could help clarify our intentions and reduce confusion. By being clearer, we can project a more credible image and potentially instill some additional self-discipline when needed.

My personal price stability objective is for the PCE index to increase at an average rate of 1½ percent per year, which I expect to achieve by having the PCE index itself increasing at that rate over five to ten years. If we were to use the CPI instead, my objective would be in the 2 percent range. I suggest putting a tolerance zone around the objective, such as a 1 percentage point spread above and below it, to indicate some flexibility.

Regarding the role of the price objective in our policy process, I'm open to exploring the idea of a Committee objective and range, but I think it's prudent to take a gradual approach. I strongly support modifying our semiannual economic projections, as outlined in Alternative 3, by having each participant submit projections for the next two years and an additional set for the average of the next three to five years. These projections, based on each participant's definition of price stability and the policies that support it, would be made public, allowing for a more transparent policy process.

I believe this adjustment to our current practice would be consistent with the congressional mandate and should not be perceived as radical. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to express my gratitude for your leadership and the insightful papers presented today. I find myself aligning most closely with Cathy Minehan and Don Kohn's perspectives. We've accomplished a great deal in maintaining a long-term record of stable and low inflation. I believe we've also achieved a significant level of transparency regarding our inflation preferences. I think it's safe to assume that everyone around this table shares a general understanding of our target, and this understanding is not only held by those in this room but also by external parties, including financial markets.

I don't see the need for additional transparency by announcing a specific numerical objective. There's a risk in formalizing our objective, especially given the size of our group. Agreeing on a single price measure and adhering to a less flexible adjustment process could prove challenging, if not counterproductive. We risk losing the depth of discussion we've had in the past.

I recall a time when we were overly focused on the fear of deflation and may have missed opportunities to examine various measures of inflation in detail. By focusing on a single price measure, we could potentially overlook the underlying factors at play. While I agree with President Stern that a specific objective wouldn't tie our hands, I believe it would limit our flexibility. I see no reason to give up this flexibility.

One point that hasn't been discussed extensively is the potential reaction of Congress to our attempts to set a price-related objective. I concur with others that we have an obligation to consult with Congress. However, there's a risk that Congress, with the best intentions, could attempt to impose its judgment on our objective. They might even propose multiple, potentially inconsistent objectives that could be beyond our control. I won't reiterate the arguments others have made about the potential communications challenges that could arise from a formal objective. Instead, I believe we should celebrate our successful approach and process. I agree with the suggestion to continue explaining our inflation preferences and intentions. However, I don't believe our current process is "broken." I have faith in the ability of those around this table and those who will follow us to maintain its integrity. Mr. Chairman, I propose that we remain where we are for the time being, but this issue certainly warrants continued discussion. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my support to the importance of maintaining low and stable inflation, having had similar experiences in both private industry and government. I recall the challenges we faced during the wage and price controls in the late '60s and early '70s, and we must do everything in our power to prevent a repeat of that period.

Regarding the definition of price stability, I agree that behavior is a key indicator. If people are devoting minimal resources to dealing with inflation costs and risks, then we have price stability. However, it would be helpful for this committee to have an explicit number or range to gauge our progress. I suggest using the PCE index as it is technically the best, even if it's less familiar to the public.

Personally, I lean towards a core inflation rate of 1.5%, but anything between 1 and 2% would yield similar economic performance. Anything lower could pose issues with the zero bound. I also want to caution against a wide range, such as 1 to 3%, as we would need to clarify acceptable outcomes within that range. For instance, consistently high inflation within that range would not be considered price stability.

I currently favor alternative 1, the status quo, and would supplement it with additional transparency measures. We've made incremental improvements in transparency, and I believe this approach has served us well.

The international experience discussed in the staff paper was intriguing, but our situation is unique. We don't face a crisis like Brazil or Mexico, and our inflation expectations are likely to form differently due to our size and closed economy. I recommend taking small steps and proceeding with caution, considering our dual mandate and potential challenges in communicating our decisions to Congress and the public.

Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. A lot of points have been put on the table already, so I feel a bit like I’m taking an oral exam, but here goes. First question, is low inflation desirable? Yes, I definitely think it is.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: That’s one right answer! @laugh

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement:
Person 1: I'd like to move on to more substantial matters, @laughs. Should we have an inflation anchor? I believe we should, as Bill Poole explained, because it gives us more room to address unemployment when we have a clear anchor. However, I'm glad no one's advocating for a full-blown inflation targeting system like the one proposed in the International Finance Division's document. Our current system isn't broken, and we've made it clear that we prioritize low inflation. We might consider some minor adjustments, but we should proceed with caution.

Chairman: What about asset prices? Nobody's mentioned that yet. I'd like to. Asset prices, such as stock prices, exchange rates, and housing prices, are part of the monetary transmission mechanism. We should consider them when making forecasts and analyzing the economy, but I don't think they should be part of our target index. Asset prices are a different beast.

Person 1: I agree with the staff's assessment. I prefer a core index for stability reasons. If we have an index and want to keep it within a zone, I'd like it to be as stable as possible. Consumer prices and the rate of inflation seem like the best options for our target. Most of us agree on a bottom line of 1 percent, and I'm on board with that. However, some of you seem a bit too hawkish on the upper limit. I'd like to propose an experiment: ask people if they'd prefer $10,000 today or the same amount five years ago, and see which option they choose. People consistently choose the current option, indicating that even higher core PCE rates might be consistent with price stability.

Chairman: What about the range or point issue? Janet raised a valid point about the point, and I agree that we should consider a range or zone instead of a specific point. For instance, if inflation is within a 1-2 percent range, we might not need to explain it. I think we should be prepared to explain why inflation leaves the zone when it does.

Person 1: I agree. We should take a low-key approach and make it clear that this is part of our broader agenda. We have a dual mandate, and an inflation anchor could help us fight unemployment more effectively. We don

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the ongoing discussion. Having started as a young Turk in this committee and now being part of the old guard, I'd like to speak in favor of maintaining our current approach to price stability. I believe that the experience we've gained over the past 20 years has instilled a strong commitment to price stability among us. It's essential that we don't forget this, regardless of who holds which chair in the room.

Regarding the definition of price stability, I agree with the staff's efforts, but I think they've presented us with a false dilemma. Price stability can be defined both qualitatively and quantitatively. Qualitatively, it means inflation isn't affecting household behavior. Quantitatively, I believe the core PCE index is currently the better indicator, but that could change in the future.

As for the objective, I think it should be based on a range rather than a point estimate. While Janet has presented some compelling arguments for a point estimate, I believe it's wiser to consider a range. However, if we announce a range as a "zone of indifference," we must consider whether we've added anything new to the public's understanding.

I don't think we need to engage in a broad external discussion about our objectives at this time. While some may argue that our current range isn't explicitly defined, I believe most observers understand our bottom end to be 1%, and the upper end to be around 2-2.5%. We've managed to communicate our views effectively without announcing a target.

However, if we were to announce a quantitative target or range, we'd need to explain how it aligns with our mandate for maximum sustainable growth and employment. We'd also need to address what we'd do if inflation falls outside the range and how quickly we'd respond. We'd likely face questions about why we don't provide a more meaningful inflation forecast.
