Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection, I think we can go to a recess at this stage. The Presidents have a dinner to attend. I look forward to seeing you all tomorrow at 8:59 a.m. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Good morning, everyone. We’ll continue our meeting with the chart show. Larry Slifman, Sandy Struckmeyer, and Karen Johnson will give the presentation.

Speaker: MR. SLIFMAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the "Staff Presentation on the Economic Outlook" that we have before us. The Greenbook's only change since the December FOMC meeting is the near-term outlook. I'll first go over some recent high-frequency indicators and then focus on the fundamental forces driving economic activity over the next two years.

In the very near term, the first chart shows various data series that have informed our judgments. The upper panels highlight two production side indicators. Private nonfarm payroll employment rose 181,000 per month, on average, in the fourth quarter, a noticeable pickup from the third quarter. The Board's index of industrial production continued to show above-trend gains in manufacturing sector activity. Most forward-looking indicators, such as regional business surveys and the ISM manufacturing report, also point to continued near-term gains. Real PCE excluding motor vehicles and motor vehicles sales are also quite favorable.

Our estimate of fourth-quarter real GDP growth was fairly close to the BEA's estimate, with no reason to alter our first-quarter forecast. The next chart presents an overview of the forecast. We expect the federal funds rate to reach 3% in the fourth quarter of this year and 3½% in the latter part of 2006. Regarding fiscal policy, we've reduced the deficit projection for fiscal year 2005, but it has virtually no effect on our preferred indicator of fiscal stimulus.

Monetary policy and other financial market conditions are expected to be supportive. The real funds rate is projected to remain below its long-run average and on the stimulative side of short-term measures of r*. Nominal long-term rates have been well-anchored and are projected to be little changed over the projection period. Corporate balance sheets are quite strong, with cash abundant and interest expenses relative to cash flow at very low levels.

We estimate that higher oil prices in 2004 reduced GDP growth by three-fourths of a percentage point last year, but the negative effects wane to a quarter of a percentage point in 2005. The housing market is projected to remain close to the 1.6 million unit mark over the next two years, with favorable mortgage rates providing ongoing support.

Business investment is

Speaker: MR. STRUCKMEYER
Statement:
In our upcoming chart, we'll discuss the labor market outlook. With real GDP projected to grow slightly above its potential rate, we anticipate nonfarm payrolls expanding at an average pace of around 200,000 per month through 2026. This pace is higher than last year's rate, as businesses have become more confident in the economic expansion and plan to hire more aggressively. However, they're not abandoning their focus on efficiency. We expect structural labor productivity to continue rising at a brisk pace, though not as fast as it did from 2001 to 2003. The increase in hiring and slightly slower productivity growth will bring actual labor productivity back in line with structural productivity by the end of next year.

Additionally, we anticipate that the more favorable labor market conditions will attract workers back into the labor force, causing a modest increase in the labor force participation rate. The unemployment rate is projected to gradually decline, reaching 5% by the end of next year. The employment-to-population ratio will also increase slightly over the projection period.

Regarding labor compensation, recent data has come in below expectations, and we'll adjust our forecast accordingly. We expect compensation growth to remain at its 2004 rate. One-year-ahead inflation expectations have risen slightly due to energy price increases, but this will be reflected in wage demands this year. The declining labor market slack is also expected to put less downward pressure on wages and prices.

Inflation is projected to slow to a 1¼% pace in 2025 and 2026. Energy prices are expected to retrace some of last year's run-up, while food price growth will slow by about ¾ percentage point. Core inflation is projected to remain stable at a 1½% rate.

We've also considered risks to the inflation forecast. In a higher-inflation scenario, we assume the NAIRU is 5½% instead of 5%, and employers' health insurance contributions rise to 10% in 2025 and 2026. Under these circumstances, core PCE inflation rises to almost 2½% in 2026. In a lower-inflation alternative, we assume structural multifactor productivity growth doesn't slow as projected and holds at a

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: [ERROR]

Speaker: MR. STRUCKMEYER
Statement: The final chart presents your economic projections for 2005 and 2006. As shown in the upper panel, the central tendency of your forecasts for real GDP this year is 3¾ to 4 percent. The unemployment rate is projected to be 5¼ percent, while the central tendency for core PCE inflation is 1½ to 1¾ percent. As indicated in the lower panel, you expect real GDP growth to slow to 3½ percent in 2006 and the unemployment rate to fall to between 5 percent and 5¼ percent. For core inflation in 2006, the central tendency remains at 1½ to 1¾ percent. Mr. Chairman, that completes our presentation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Do you have a forecast of current account balances for the world, including the discrepancies?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I do. Do you want me to give you some figures?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I’d actually like to see the whole table. Does it have data for individual countries?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: We have several tables that cover all of the countries we forecast and then we have the global—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Could somebody just bring it around to me rather than have you read it? Other questions for our colleagues? President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I was a little surprised at the forecast of no decline in the value of the dollar. Your alternative simulation seems as though it’s relatively close to what we’ve experienced since 2002. You had mentioned that the dollar is down 27 percent since the peak in 2002. That’s roughly three years, and that’s not terribly different from 10 percent at an annual pace, which is what I understood you to say in talking about the simulation. Maybe I understood you wrong.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: No, no. You might think of our Greenbook forecast for the dollar as a technical assumption. We have adopted a “random walk” frame of mind when it comes to forecasting the dollar, based on two things. First of all, the research that we ourselves have done in the articles that members of the Board staff have written shows that a random walk assumption outperforms any structural model over long periods of time. Moreover, the profession has engaged for years in trying to forecast the dollar. And the random walk—that is, no change from today—in a statistical sense outperforms on average any of the alternatives that have been suggested. One possibility, an alternative assumption, might be to go with the implied futures rates that come out of trading. But those rates have been shown to be particularly bad forecasters. So there’s no real empirical support for making that alternative choice. We used to attempt to infer the consequences of what we knew—the “maintained” assumption of the federal funds rate, so to speak. That was based on the view that we knew something about the future course of policy that the market didn’t know; that is, we were conditioning the forecast on something that the market didn’t necessarily know, and we thought we would sense other forces, if you will, at work. So we had a somewhat more activist forecast for many years until we were wrong for such a long time that we gave up, basically. It troubles me in some sense that our forecast suggests that import inflation will drop from being a fairly significant number to virtually nothing—tomorrow—and will continue that way throughout the forecast period. That result is very much affected by this maintained assumption that the dollar isn’t going to change much more. So my explanation is simply that we try to make that assumption very explicit to you. You understand that we have not taken a position on changes in the value of the dollar, which very much determines our import price forecast. And we let it go at that. We do not take a position on the dollar because our actual ability to tell you when the dollar is going to go up and when it is going to go down is just nonexistent.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: So, depending on how one feels about the likely path of the dollar, there is some definite sense of risk to the forecast associated with that.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Yes. But I would say that over any short time horizon, like the next two years, there’s as much upside risk as downside risk. The dollar rose substantially in the first half of 2004; it rose in the early part of this year. Even though many of us have argued at great length that there are reasons to think that over time the dollar has to go down, for any period you care to name—for a year or even as long as two or three years—it could well go up before it goes down.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I have one further question. I’ve been surprised to read in some of the market publications—the newsletters from J.P. Morgan and so forth—that a number of market participants appear to be getting more convinced, based on their reading of the political tea leaves, that a move on the Chinese currency is going to occur sooner rather than later. Have we thought about the implications for this forecast if something like that happens?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: We haven’t taken the step of, say, putting that into an alternative simulation in the Greenbook, which maybe we should do. But we have been thinking about the issue and what alternatives might be good—that is, effective and productive—and what alternatives might be counterproductive. There was a time about three weeks ago when the amount of chatter about the Chinese regime was very elevated. And there were some remarks that came out of the meetings in Davos, just five days ago, that made it seem as if something was imminent. And then the talk would subside and it would go quiet. Do I think that nothing is going to happen in this regard between now and the end of 2006? Personally, my answer would be “No, I don’t.” I think something will happen before that. But we would have a very hard time specifying exactly what will be done or when and putting it in the baseline forecast.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Do you have a bigger-than-a-bread-box feeling about how that will work?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I could make it bigger than a bread box, depending on whether the rest of Asia reacts in some explicit manner. I say that because the pegging to the dollar is a device for stabilizing exchange rates among the Asian trading partners. So if China moves in certain ways, that might trigger a reaction on the part of its trading partners. But I could imagine some steps that China might take that wouldn’t do that. Now, I think we can, and probably should, take a chance and commit to writing what we think those relationships are and what the implications might be. If all of the Asian currencies move, I think you would see some consequences. If China revalues by 5 percent and pegs again and nobody else does anything, I think it will be almost inconsequential. It will be politically helpful to a variety of people, but economically I don’t believe it will mean a lot. If China not only moves but also floats—if it defines a band in which the currency will fluctuate or goes to a weighted average or, in other words, introduces the potential of movement going forward—and that triggers a comparable decision on the part of other Asian countries, it’s a different story. If the currencies of Asian countries that now in one way or another stabilize their exchange rates with China’s become more flexible vis-à-vis their bilateral rate with the dollar, I think the implications could be greater.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Thank you.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I guess I will commit to trying to do some sort of analysis of this issue or an alternative simulation in the next Greenbook. I think that would be a helpful thing to do.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Maybe not on my say-so alone.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: Just as a derivative question, relating to chart 13, Karen. You were commenting on the disappointing performance of exports, and one of your stories was the rather sharp downturn in exports to “Other Asia.” Could you elaborate on that a little bit? Is that, in your view, importantly or unimportantly affected by the Chinese peg to the dollar?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: I don’t know that I would link it to that particularly. I think it is in part, but I can’t tell you how much, a result of the integration of trade—or reintegration of trade—that’s going on in Asia, so that exports that previously came to us from Japan now pass through China for some final stage of processing. There’s a sense in which the gains that China is making are coming at the expense of other Asian trading partners and are not of much significance. On the other hand, if the China and Hong Kong line had gone up at the same time that this “Other Asia” line went down, I think I would have had to say that I shouldn’t show them separately but should add them together or something. But it did not. I’d point more to the high-tech sector as a factor. In terms of the risks to this global forecast, we have oil prices, our basic standard risk. But the issue of whether the high-tech sector is going through a temporary inventory lull and is going to rebound or not is the biggest question mark for the Asian emerging-market countries. I think that accounts for the falloff in trade at the end of last year—that is, the falloff in exports more broadly and in IP [industrial production]. I think that’s all wrapped up in the high-tech question.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: Does your simulation of the effect of the 10 percent decline in the dollar include the effects on foreign countries’ income from facing a more appreciated exchange rate?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Yes, we did that in our global model. And those countries—given that prices in their currency for the goods they sell to the United States go up because the dollar is depreciating—actually earn some income, which is then spent on U.S. exports. So in that sense it accounts for all the money that is spent on trade. It goes somewhere; it feeds back to the model.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: It also affects their import demands.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Yes, that’s what I’m saying.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: That’s from the income effects.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Yes. Now, the model has the usual Houthakker and Magee asymmetry in it, but the move down of the dollar is very stimulative. In our model it would be a very stimulative shock to the United States, and it would be a contractionary shock to almost all of our trading partners.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement: The reason I ask is because your simulation raises the question of whether the derivative is the right sign on the current account with respect to the dollar. Given the effect that the falling dollar has on our interest rates and, therefore, on our capital income payments, there seems to be so very little effect on the current account from the weakening dollar.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Right, but because of the interest rate effect. Now, it’s possible that the model doesn’t fully capture valuation effects. In the direct investment account, the fact that the dollar is depreciating means that every euro earned by a U.S. subsidiary is now worth more dollars rather than the same number of dollars. So this current account effect may be too conservative. It’s possible that the current account would improve more via that valuation effect than the model is fully able to capture.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Why can’t you capture it? It seems like a straightforward—

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Well, our people do their best, but the financial flow part of the model is less fully articulated than is the real side of the model.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It’s not that you don’t have the data. You do.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Yes. It’s just a question of how many equations—or how many independent variables—you can actually manage.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Any further questions for our colleagues? If not, who would like to start the Committee discussion? President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. The story from the Seventh District is much the same as last time. We’re seeing a broad-based improvement in business conditions. I would characterize it as solid growth, but not spectacular. Most of the sectors that were strong last year continue to do very well and expect good results for 2005. These include steel, heavy trucks, construction machinery, and commercial printing. A number of firms in these industries have been able to raise prices without seeing a reduction in their order backlogs. We’ve also heard some upbeat forward-looking reports on commercial aircraft. And agriculture has been very strong. Farm incomes were up sharply last year, and that has been supporting big gains in the sale of agricultural equipment. We also saw the largest annual increase in District farmland values in 15 years. On the other hand, the outlook for the Big Three automakers, whose operations and those of their suppliers are heavily concentrated in our District, is not so good. Most analysts expect light vehicle sales to be similar to last year, but the Big Three are concerned about losing even more market share. And their profit outlook is not good; we continue to hear that they have significant cost disadvantages relative to the transplants. Moreover, foreign nameplates have built out their product lines to the point where the Big Three face stiff competition in just about every market segment. One result of the relatively poor performance of the Big Three is that Michigan now has the highest unemployment rate in the nation.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement: You got to be first in something! @laugh

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to share some updates on a few key topics. Firstly, Illinois is currently leading the Big Ten in basketball with an undefeated record. @laugh

Moving on to business matters, there's been some chatter about rising costs for plastics and energy-related inputs, but we're not seeing widespread reports of significant price increases yet.

Regarding labor markets, our two primary temp staffing firms have reported a 10% overall growth compared to last year. The demand for skilled workers is increasing at the fastest rate. Both firms anticipate a period of stronger employment growth, but they're starting to accept that the growth rates we've seen over the last year might be the peak for this cycle.

The president of one temp firm made an intriguing observation about businesses' reluctance to hire. He mentioned that reasons for caution are now industry-specific, which is an improvement from a year ago when many firms were concerned about the overall economy.

As for the national outlook, our data since the last meeting hasn't significantly altered our expectations for 2005. We still anticipate output expanding at a rate above potential with minimal change in core inflation. The alternative simulations in the Greenbook provided a good overview of potential risks, and I believe these risks are balanced.

One risk that I'm still concerned about is the possibility that instead of substantial slack remaining in the economy, we might already be close to or at potential. Some participants in our recent academic advisors and Chicago-area business economists meeting raised this concern, although it was a minority view.

When it comes to monetary policy, there was a roughly even split between those who thought the path implied by futures markets was appropriate and those who believed we should be raising rates faster. Personally, I think we'll likely need to increase rates this year to levels higher than anticipated by futures markets.

Lastly, as we progress through the year, communication challenges may become more critical. It's not a pressing issue at the moment, but it's something we'll need to keep in mind.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to begin by sharing some positive news about the Twelfth District economy. Consumer spending is growing strongly, driven by gains in travel, services, retail goods, and especially housing. Businesses are also investing, with contacts expecting to increase spending in the coming months. The weaker dollar is benefiting District exporters, who report high demand for their products. While international trade volumes remain high, some bottlenecks persist in ground transportation networks due to storms, mudslides, and infrastructure issues, but these are improving.

On a national level, recent economic data have been encouraging, suggesting that the expansion has found a solid footing. The December employment report showed moderate improvement, and I agree with the general outlook in the Greenbook. However, I want to emphasize that this forecast depends on a gradual pace of monetary tightening. The Greenbook only calls for three more 25 basis point increases in the funds rate this year, but I believe longer-term interest rates may rise, which could slow growth.

Inflation data since our last meeting have been favorable, and I expect core PCE price index to rise by about 1½ percent in 2005. However, there are risks, such as the future paths of the dollar and oil prices, labor market slack, productivity growth, and trend productivity. In December, I argued that the risks to inflation from productivity developments were balanced. I've since reviewed research on the structural shifts hypothesis, which suggests an unusually high degree of labor market mismatch. However, after chairing a session on this topic at the AEA meetings, I emerged a skeptic. The evidence does not support the case that NAIRU has increased due to unusual structural shifts.

In conclusion, I believe it's time to remove a bit more accommodation at this meeting, but we'll need to slow down the process soon. The economy is showing signs of strength, and it's important that we proceed carefully to ensure sustainable growth.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Third District's economic landscape. Our district is experiencing a moderate expansion, but there's some variation among New Jersey, Delaware, and Pennsylvania. New Jersey and Delaware are signaling continued solid growth, while Pennsylvania's growth is more modest. Payroll employment grew at a 1.25% pace in the fourth quarter, down from earlier in the year. Pennsylvania has the slowest job growth and the highest unemployment rate among the three states.

Our business outlook survey indicated a considerably slower pace of expansion in regional manufacturing in January. The general activities, new orders, and shipment indexes all reached their lowest levels in 18 months. This is a concerning trend, as it's not just limited to Philadelphia. New York's manufacturing survey also weakened in January.

However, it's important to remember that these are just one-month readings, and other indicators in the January survey are less negative. For example, current and future employment indexes are near their highest levels in the current expansion.

Regarding future capital spending, our results from the Board staff's survey are quite similar to the nation's results, as reported in Larry Slifman's memo.

Furthermore, the economic data and feedback from firms in our district suggest that the expiration of the partial-expensing provisions had a smaller impact on firms than assumed in the baseline Greenbook forecast.

Moving on to other sectors, consumers continue to spend at a moderate pace. Holiday sales in the region generally met expectations, with luxury items showing the strongest performance. Nonresidential construction remains soft, but residential construction has remained healthy, albeit down from its peak. House appreciation continues, especially on the New Jersey shore, where prices were up over 20% in the past year.

In summary, the economic expansion continues at a moderate pace in the Third District, and the outlook among business contacts remains positive. My outlook for the nation is similar to that of the Greenbook, with real growth averaging about 3.75% over the next two years. Consumer spending is expected to expand at a similar pace.

Despite the low personal saving rate, which averaged 1% last year compared to an average of over 7% since 1959, there's little evidence to suggest that consumers will hold

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights on the current state of the New England economy. We're seeing a gradual growth pace, despite the challenging winter weather. Employment is growing steadily in all states except Massachusetts, which is in line with the national average. Consumer and business confidence, as well as spending, are on the rise. Manufacturers are cautiously optimistic about 2005.

However, there are some concerns worth mentioning. First, energy prices have surged due to the weather and oil prices, leading to a 16% annual increase in fuel and utility costs in the Boston CPI region. This could be a long-term issue, as New England's energy infrastructure is vulnerable to shortages and higher costs.

Second, while employment declined in Massachusetts, the unemployment rate dropped to a record low of 4.3%. However, this is due to a shrinking labor force, with workers leaving the region or dropping out of the workforce. This trend won't bode well for future hiring needs.

Businesses remain optimistic, but are concerned about geopolitical issues, including fiscal and trade deficits and the implementation of Sarbanes-Oxley financial controls. The costs of compliance are a major concern.

The national economy is expected to continue growing, with similar forecasts from ourselves and the Greenbook. However, there are risks, such as rising inflation and financial fragility due to large trade and fiscal deficits, accommodative financial markets, and low interest rates.

In conclusion, while the economy is showing positive signs, there are challenges that need to be addressed to ensure sustainable growth. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'm glad to report that things are moving in the right direction based on the latest data. Our output is expanding at a steady pace, and we're seeing solid investment spending and sustained consumer spending. Job creation is also sufficient to gradually lower unemployment, and inflation remains low.

The Southeast region is a prime example of this positive trend. Key sectors such as consumer spending, housing, manufacturing, retail, tourism, and auto sales are all performing well. Businesses are investing due to anticipated sales growth, as well as a desire to upgrade technology and improve competitiveness.

Consumer spending in the region is particularly strong, with tourism being a standout performer. Florida theme parks are full, hotel bookings are up, and hotel room tax collections are 8% ahead of last year. Housing growth has eased but remains high, and new job creation has been sufficient to surpass pre-recession levels. The District added 264,000 jobs last year, with Florida accounting for 172,000 of those new jobs. Since the end of the recession, Florida has accounted for 27% of net new jobs created in the country.

The District unemployment rate of 4.9% is below the national rate, and I'm keeping a close eye on potential imbalances and their implications for inflation. Housing prices are rising sharply in some areas, particularly in Florida real estate markets. Equipment shortages in trucking are leading to price increases, and fuel surcharges are being passed on. Railroads are declining to renew some contracts for bulk chemicals, which are less profitable than containers and piggyback loads.

As for policy, the data from both our region and the national level doesn't indicate a need to change the current path of removing accommodation. Markets expect further moves, and our models suggest that even with several more increases in the fed funds target rate, the real economy should expand between 3½ to almost 4% next year. Unemployment is expected to continue to decline, and the CPI should average between 2½ and 3%.

Of course, there are risks to these forecasts, but I'm comfortable with the path we're on for now. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some context to President Guynn's report regarding the economic conditions in the Fourth District. Our region's economy hasn't seen significant changes since December, as indicated by the Beige Book report. While my business contacts remain cautiously optimistic about the country's economic growth in 2005, they're expressing less focus on productivity gains as the reason for limited hiring. Instead, I'm hearing about potential staff additions, albeit in specific industries.

These developments align with the Greenbook baseline projection of GDP expansion in the 3½ to 4 percent range, modest employment gains, and robust capital spending. However, my contacts continue to express concerns over high input prices, particularly raw materials, and the need to maintain their margins. They've had some success in passing on price increases to their customers, but it's challenging to quantify the extent of this.

The flat markup scenario reported in the Greenbook is a significant risk, despite my belief in the baseline projection. Last year's realized rate of headline inflation was higher than desirable, and while energy sector issues have likely subsided, the Blue Chip forecasters predict higher-than-expected inflation as a potential surprise in 2005. I believe we should avoid contributing to continued price level performance from the previous year by setting the fed funds rate too low.

I appreciate that we've been able to remove accommodation at a measured pace in moving the fed funds rate back toward a more comfortable zone. If we don't adjust the fed funds rate now, we risk losing the ability to continue with moderate steps. Our ultimate goal is to move the fed funds rate back to a neutral neighborhood, and I'd prefer not to be at the lower end of that range. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement: Thank you. The broad-based expansion in our District continues. Employment gains have been modest but persistent. In talking to employers, the general impression I get is that they are finding labor availability to be ample, although occasionally they’ve expressed some concern about the shortage of particular skills. At the same time, they have mentioned some apprehension about labor supply going forward. And I’ve noticed that a couple of large employers recently have returned to running job fairs—something that we haven’t seen in recent years. Outside the labor market, the manufacturing sector in our District is strong. Housing continues to operate at a high level. Mining, energy, and agriculture, outside of the areas that were affected by drought, all had good years and appear to be in good shape. We recently queried some of our directors and other business leaders about inflation, particularly inflation over the past three months—essentially, the fourth quarter of last year. The reports we got back were uniformly along the following lines: There has been no detectable acceleration of inflation, and, if anything, there has been a diminution of inflationary pressures. As far as the national economy is concerned, my forecast is very close to the central tendency reported in the chart show. I took the tenor of the chart show to mean, as I thought about it, that there’s not really very much wrong with the economy at the moment—depending on the significance one might attach to the current account deficit. Beyond that, it seems to me that inflationary expectations remain essentially very well anchored. All of this suggests to me that the policy path we’ve been on is appropriate and that we should continue on that path.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: First Vice President Holcomb.

Speaker: MS. HOLCOMB
Statement: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. This is only the third FOMC meeting that I’ve had the privilege of attending, and I cannot help but notice how much the U.S. economy has improved in that short time. @laugh

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: You’re welcome to attend any time!

Speaker: MS. HOLCOMB
Statement:
During our recent meeting, I expressed my belief that luck plays a significant role in the current economic situation. Following a brief economic downturn last autumn, which could have been due to energy developments or election-related uncertainty, most economic indicators have improved over the past few months. Job gains were robust during the last quarter, and durable goods orders showed strong gains in November and December. Retail sales ended the year on a positive note as well. Despite the Federal Open Market Committee's five consecutive rate hikes in the federal funds rate, long-term mortgage and corporate bond rates have decreased slightly, potentially contributing to the economic tailwinds.

When preparing our economic projections for the next two years, my team and I met with several economists from the Dallas Fed. We all agreed that real GDP growth would fall within the 3½ to 4 percent range. Our projections for the unemployment rate were also similar to those of the Board staff. However, we differed on the trajectory for core inflation. The Dallas economic staff predicts that core PCE inflation will reach 1.8 percent this year and remain stable in 2024. Several factors contribute to this difference. First, the Greenbook assumes that domestic profit margins will continue to narrow, foreign firms will not attempt to rebuild their squeezed profit margins, and benefit cost increases will remain moderate. If any of these assumptions are relaxed, the risks of higher inflation and faster output growth increase.

The results of the special Beige Book survey on capital investment plans further complicate the inflation outlook. Many companies are planning to increase their spending due to strong demand and emerging capacity constraints. The question remains whether this capacity will come online in time to alleviate the inflationary pressures that have emerged in the past year.

I've observed a notable shift in the tone of FOMC meetings and Dallas Fed board of directors meetings. In November, the focus was on economic headwinds, while in December, the discussion shifted to economic tailwinds. This trend was also evident in the directors' economic reports, which were uniformly downbeat in November but optimistic in December. While most data for the Eleventh District has not yet caught up with these reports, Texas employment growth has strengthened but remains below the national average. The Texas unemployment rate ticked up in November and December and remains above the national rate. Almost all sectors of the Texas economy are gaining employment, and the recovery is

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some thoughts on the current state of the economy. We're seeing growth above trend, and it's expected to stay that way for several quarters. This means we're getting closer to reaching the economy's long-term potential GDP. I anticipate growth will be around 4% this year.

There are several reasons for this. Monetary policy remains accommodative, consumer spending on goods and housing is strong, and business equipment spending is robust. Although fourth-quarter growth was below expectations, final sales to domestic purchasers grew at a healthy 4.3% pace. The labor market is also improving. Retailers reported moderately higher holiday sales than last year, and many exceeded their plans. Ski resorts in our region experienced sharp increases in hotel occupancy and a near-record volume of ski visits, many from foreigners. Job growth picked up in December, with hiring announcements outpacing layoff announcements by a 2:1 margin. Small and midsized manufacturers plan to increase employment in the coming months.

However, there are reasons to be cautious. Core inflation is expected to rise, and with the federal funds rate below most estimates for the neutral rate, the risk of inflation is increasing. Other factors, such as the return of pricing power, a greater pass-through of higher commodity prices, the possibility of continued dollar depreciation, and slowing productivity growth, are also contributing to this risk.

In our District, we're seeing evidence of labor shortages, with 53% of businesses reporting shortages in January, up significantly from last quarter and last year. Wage pressures have also increased, with 26% of employers reporting they had to boost wages more than normal compared to 17% last quarter and 11% a year ago. Our manufacturing survey shows evidence of greater pricing power, with a larger share of businesses reporting higher output prices among those reporting higher input prices.

Given these factors, I believe we should be thinking about raising interest rates more aggressively than in the past to mitigate the risk of inflation. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bernanke.

Speaker: MR. BERNANKE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some thoughts on the economic outlook. The recovery appears to be solidifying, and domestic demand remains robust, indicating a promising growth trajectory for 2005. I don't foresee significant inflation risks changing in the recent months. Labor costs have remained surprisingly subdued.

However, as the recovery gains strength, it's essential to continue reducing monetary accommodation at a measured pace. Some argue that a potential slowdown in labor productivity growth could lead to inflationary pressures. While lower productivity does result in higher unit labor costs, the relationship between productivity growth and inflation, as well as the policy implications, are complex.

First, it's important to acknowledge that a slower productivity growth rate is already factored into the Greenbook forecast. The staff projects a 1.7% annual growth rate for output per hour in the nonfarm business sector in 2005, which is below recent experience and below the profession's long-term consensus estimate. This slowdown is due to both cyclical factors and the assumption that some of the recent gains will be reversed. Given the history of productivity surprises, the risks for the Greenbook productivity projections should be considered balanced.

The deceleration in the cyclical component of output per hour is expected to have little impact on inflation but will lower profit margins. Although a slowdown in structural productivity growth will put upward pressure on prices, the staff anticipates that other factors, such as declining energy prices and a stabilized dollar, will offset this impact.

In summary, the Greenbook's baseline forecast suggests that some productivity growth slowdown is consistent with stable inflation. The critical question is what happens if productivity growth in 2005 is even lower than the 1.7% projected by the staff? If firms perceive this as a permanent change, they may pass on the increased labor costs to consumers, leading to short-term inflationary pressures. However, the appropriate policy response depends on the reaction of aggregate demand to this change in productivity growth. If aggregate demand declines significantly in response to the productivity slowdown, it could offset the inflationary impacts, and a slower pace of tightening may be warranted. Conversely, if productivity growth slows substantially but aggregate demand does not respond, a judgment call would be required about the permanence of the slowdown. If it's temporary, no significant

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some recent economic developments in the Fifth District. Retail sales have shown some improvement, with shopper traffic stronger in January compared to December. Big-ticket sales have also picked up after a weak month. In the service sector, revenue growth remains robust. Manufacturing activity generally firmed up in January, with new orders and factory hiring on the rise.

However, there are some concerns regarding price increases. Our survey shows that prices rose at a quicker pace in January compared to December, but the increases for final goods and services were less than 2%. Some manufacturers are reporting that higher costs are squeezing their margins, but they're passing on these costs to consumers by raising prices. The stronger economic outlook and customer acceptance of higher prices are helping with this.

On a national level, the economic recovery seems to be on track based on the data we've received since the December FOMC meeting. Inflation and economic activity projections remain unchanged, with a real GDP growth of between 3½ to 4% over the next two years.

The most intriguing development since December has been the flattening of the yield curve. Despite a 26 basis point increase in the two-year Treasury rate, the 10-year rate barely moved, and the 30-year rate even declined by 17 basis points. This is a sign of confidence in the low-inflation commitment and the belief that long-term interest rates will remain low. However, we'll need to keep a close eye on the term structure for any signs of emerging market concerns about inflation or deflation.

Overall, the economic conditions and the yield curve suggest that the policy path we're assuming for this outlook is well-balanced, taking into account both upside and downside risks.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. Let’s break for coffee. We could extend this for a little longer than usual, since we do have a lot of time. So let’s break for 20 minutes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share my perspective on the economic outlook for 2005 and 2006. Like many of you, I forecasted a growth rate slightly faster than the trend rate, reflecting the dissipation of previous growth inhibitors and the continued impact of stimulative financial conditions. However, I believe growth will remain moderate, not explosive.

Factors such as monetary and fiscal policy will keep growth in check. The fiscal stimulus from partial expensing provisions may have brought forward some capital expenditures, and monetary policy's gradual withdrawal of stimulus will lead to rising real interest rates. These rising rates will dampen consumption and investment spending, as well as house and equity prices.

However, there's a downside risk to this forecast: a significant increase in longer-term real interest rates. Such a jump could have a substantial impact on house prices and consumption. But for now, the low level of long-term real rates and stable equity prices suggest that caution among savers and spenders remains.

Additionally, the trade deficit could dampen demand on U.S. resources. The staff forecast assumes a pickup in foreign demand, but we haven't seen that in the data yet. As people become less willing to send us their savings, the deficit will have to shrink, putting pressure on U.S. productive capacity.

Regarding inflation, I forecast a slightly higher rate than the staff, but below 2%. The recent data from the second half of 2004 suggests that the pickup in core inflation in the first half was due to special factors, such as the reversal of the unexplainable undershoot in 2003 and the pass-through of higher energy and commodity prices. Energy prices should not be a factor in 2005, and labor markets show that slack is still present.

In conclusion, I believe the risks to the economic outlook are balanced, with low, stable inflation being the most likely outcome. However, I'll be monitoring developments closely to assess whether the skews around the modal outcomes will become significant enough to influence the central tendencies and the path of policy removal. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent conversations with business contacts. The two main topics that come up are Sarbanes-Oxley and rising healthcare costs. Sarbanes-Oxley is causing a significant increase in accounting and audit budgets, but the costs aren't reflected in unit labor costs. It's more of a distraction than a major concern.

When I asked about labor availability, I didn't get much concern, except for IT professionals. The overall impression is that labor is readily available, except for some specialties. However, there are modest upward revisions in expectations for company growth. A contact from a large computer firm reported an upward revision in global growth expectations, and package delivery businesses are ordering new aircraft due to strong growth in Asian traffic.

The forecast for real GDP growth this year is 3.9%, with a confidence interval of 2.2-5.5%. A 5.5% or 2.2% growth rate would have significant implications for our business. It's important to keep our minds open to various shocks and communicate this uncertainty to the markets. We're on a sensible path, but we can't assume we're locked into it without considering potential surprises. I've jotted down some potential shocks, but the ones that will impact us most are likely to be unexpected.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement:
At our latest meeting, we haven't made any major adjustments to our national outlook. Our forecast aligns closely with the Greenbook and the central tendency of other forecasts. I don't have any significant updates regarding the Second District economy that would impact the national outlook. Assuming monetary policy follows the current market expectations, we anticipate growth to exceed potential, around 3.5%, with core PCE inflation hovering around 1.5%. We're more confident in our forecast this time compared to the last FOMC meeting. There's less downside risk to the growth forecast than previously.

Despite some concerns about global economic fragility and volatility, the overall sentiment is more positive. Productivity growth is slowing, resource utilization is increasing, and unit labor costs are accelerating, which could put pressure on inflation. Profit margins are substantial enough to absorb these cost increases, but firms are reporting increased pricing power. With markets confident that we'll continue to raise the fed funds rate, we've managed to keep inflation expectations stable.

However, this forecast might seem overly optimistic. It's unlikely that the economy will follow the consensus path between now and the end of 2023. The low risk premia and expectations of a stable fed funds rate could leave us vulnerable to an adverse shock that could negatively impact consumer spending. The greatest risk to the forecast involves our large external imbalance, uncertainty about fiscal improvements, and the sustainability of high structural productivity growth. We should be cautious in our actions to avoid reinforcing an unhealthy degree of confidence in the future path of the fed funds rate.

Our monetary policy should continue to aim at raising the fed funds rate towards equilibrium and conveying our commitment to doing so at a pace determined by the evolving outlook. We'll need to strike a balance between maintaining neutrality to market expectations and avoiding actions that could make the fed funds rate path less responsive to data and the outlook. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to bring up a few points regarding the chart on Equipment and Software (E&S) expenditures and the "no investment pothole" scenario. Based on my experience, significant incentives like the partial-expensing provisions attract capital loudly. Since we haven't seen any indication of this from E&S manufacturers or lenders, it seems unlikely that there will be a deceleration in E&S spending due to the expiration of these incentives. This is a risk to the forecast's accuracy, but not an unwelcome development.

Moving on, I've noticed two themes emerging from bankers' comments in January following the release of fourth-quarter earnings reports. They mentioned that asset quality has peaked and can only decline, and they highlighted additional provisioning for loan losses. This suggests a cyclicality that could impact our forecast of growth above potential through 2006. However, based on historical data, nonperforming assets and net charge-offs are reliable lagging indicators of economic downturns. Loan loss provisions, on the other hand, don't necessarily indicate an inflection point. From the current analysis of asset quality, I don't see any risks to our forecast.

Lastly, I'd like to touch on the Social Security reform issue. The focus in Washington, D.C. is on personal/private accounts, and it's unlikely that this will change. The Republicans see these accounts as transformational, and they're willing to endure painful adjustments to make it happen. If the package doesn't include personal accounts, there won't be any Social Security reform. The window for action is short, and if it doesn't happen in 2005, it's unlikely to happen at all. The President has staked a significant part of his reputation on achieving Social Security reform, so we should expect this to be a high-profile issue for the next several months.

In conclusion, there's nothing in the current situation that would warrant altering our current path of removing accommodation.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share my perspective on the current economic situation. In my opinion, we're in a mid-cycle sweet spot, with inflation under control and growth around potential. The staff's outlook and consensus forecast align with this benign outlook. While Q4 GDP growth was slightly below expectations, domestic demand grew solidly at a 4.3% pace. Private sector growth was particularly encouraging, as government consumption grew less than 1%.

Real disposable income increased by 6.2%, and both PCE expenditures and E&S expenditures contributed to growth. Orders and shipments of durable goods excluding aircraft rose briskly in December. Weekly jobless claims remain low, and consumer and business confidence surveys are holding steady. Our survey of capital spending plans also indicates gradual business expansion.

However, there are concerns that should give us pause. The trade deficit remains stubbornly high, despite dollar depreciation. The drag from net exports is likely to continue, and the domestic demand situation overseas is not particularly attractive. Another risk is the pace of growth in unit labor costs, which has quickened and could lead to businesses raising prices. The profit share has risen to about 11% of GDP, and if businesses condition their expectations on the last cycle, we could see more widespread price increases.

These are the worries that keep us up at night. The trade deficit and the pace of growth in unit labor costs are the main risks I see. We should continue with our gradual removal of policy accommodation but review it seriously at each meeting. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the economic outlook for 2005 as presented in the Greenbook and the private sector forecasts. In my view, these forecasts depict a robust economic growth scenario with a declining unemployment rate. I'm confident that our gradual policy accommodation withdrawal is in line with this growth trajectory.

However, with such a positive forecast, it's essential to consider potential risks. Two concerns that I've been pondering are inflation and long-term interest rates.

Firstly, the Greenbook's inflation projection is lower than many private-sector forecasts. While I hope the Greenbook is accurate, the recent inflation data volatility warrants close monitoring. Inflation numbers have shown significant fluctuations in the last two years, even in core measures. Therefore, we must keep a close eye on incoming data to better understand the situation.

Secondly, the mystery surrounding the current low long-term interest rates is intriguing. While there are explanations, such as record corporate profit margins, fewer accounting scandals, and reduced downgrades by rating agencies, we might be at the peak of the credit cycle.

If long-term interest rates rise, the primary concern is the potential impact on business investment. Profit margins could narrow, and cash flow might shift from internal funds to external credit. On the household side, consumers have been borrowing extensively, and a rise in interest rates could lead to a decrease in credit usage, which could negatively affect consumption and, consequently, GDP growth.

Consumption has been a significant driver of this economic cycle, and a slowdown in housing market activity or even a plateau could have repercussions. The low saving rate and high net worth to income ratio are factors contributing to the current consumption trend. However, if net worth stabilizes and consumers are unable to increase cash flow through refinancings or home equity lines, this could lead to a decrease in consumer spending and less GDP growth than forecasted.

In conclusion, while the economic outlook is promising, it's crucial to remain vigilant about potential risks, particularly inflation and the impact of long-term interest rates on consumption. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to begin by acknowledging that this past month in Lake Wobegon has been relatively calm. The expansion of aggregate demand has been steady, and output growth has been reasonable, albeit with a desire for more growth in exports and import-competing sectors. Core inflation remains within my target range, and I share the sentiments of those advocating for a lower NAIRU. Our current monetary policy has been effective in lifting rates off the floor without hindering the expansion, and I see no reason to alter it at this time.

However, I want to reiterate my concerns regarding the low national saving rate in the US. Personal saving rates have been declining, and recent budget deficits have added to this issue. While some believe that personal saving rates will rise and budget deficits will shrink, I remain skeptical. The Congressional Budget Office's latest report on the budget deficit forecast indicates that assuming continued spending for Iraq, extended tax cuts, and AMT indexing could result in large-scale deficits through 2015, with the debt-to-GDP ratio approaching 45 percent by that time. This is concerning, as we could certainly improve our financial situation before the baby-boomer generation begins retiring in large numbers.

Low national saving means either that investment will decrease or that international borrowing must continue. We have discussed the challenges of falling investment at length. The continued borrowing, however, is a less well-understood issue. A recent staff memo highlighted the challenges of prolonged international borrowing in other countries. The data suggest that no high-income country has experienced more than four or five years of continuous trade account borrowing. Our trade account borrowing has already lasted three times as long as any other episode, and it continues to grow in Karen's forecast.

There are both good and bad explanations for this phenomenon. The bad explanation is the codependency thesis, where we are over-consuming, and other countries are inappropriately supporting their export industries by intervening to strengthen the dollar. The good explanation is that other countries have aging populations and need a good place to invest their savings due to our strong productivity advance. This benign explanation may be correct, but it has limited duration. When the older workers in these countries retire, they will start to draw down their savings and pull their funds back. At that point, our

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Thank you. Vincent.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, based on the information available to most investors, it seems that the Committee's decision today to raise the federal funds rate by ¼ point to 2½ percent and issue a similar statement to the one released after the December 14 meeting is a foregone conclusion. This belief is likely due to the Committee's recent statements, including the minutes from the December meeting, and incoming economic data that does not appear to present any significant obstacles to the Committee's path of reducing policy accommodation.

Market participants expect a series of ¼ point rate hikes until the summer, followed by a slower pace of tightening that puts the funds rate at around 3¾ percent by the end of 2006. This trajectory is consistent with working down resource slack and maintaining about steady inflation.

However, some concerns have been raised about the potential for excessive risk-taking in various asset markets due to favorable liquidity conditions, such as low long-term interest rates. Indications of a relaxed attitude toward risk include narrow spreads in corporate and sovereign debt markets, low implied volatilities of financial prices, and high and rising home values.

While asset prices can influence economic behavior, it is not clear that policymakers can identify asset price bubbles in real time or what actions they should take if they were confident that some prices were misaligned. Additionally, there may be other policy instruments better suited to dealing with potential asset market imbalances.

Therefore, it is recommended that asset prices influence monetary policy decisions only to the extent that they have a material effect on output and inflation. Any concerns about potential asset market imbalances might be tempered by the range of policy prescriptions based solely on macroeconomic objectives.

The Committee has already moved the policy rate toward the high end of the range of standard recommendations and is anticipated in futures markets to move even higher relative to that range in the quarters to come. Market participants expect a median probability that the current firming cycle will be halted by the September meeting, which would put the federal funds rate at 3½ percent by August.

There are three reasons why this alternative explanation of futures rates is presented. First, it underscores the imprecision of our understanding of financial prices and the existence of multiple plausible interpretations. Second, if the Committee would prefer that market participants not anticipate an unbroken string of policy moves,

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Questions for Vincent?

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Vincent, in exhibit 2, this is a measure of the point on the calendar where the market expects us to stop?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: To stop tightening, yes.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Meaning it’s the point where the future path of the fed funds rate from that point on is perfectly flat. So it’s not the probability of when we will first pause.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Right. This is an alternative explanation that says you will not pause but will stop. This views the expected federal funds futures curve as a weighted average of a succession of moves followed by a flat funds rate.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: I interpreted you as saying that an identifying assumption more or less built in is that we move, move, move, move, move and then stop—that we don’t pause and then continue moving again.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Right. But I would point out that our understanding of expectations formation is such that you could also direct it to mean that you’ll do a ¼ point move every other meeting and fit the probability of those events. It’s just what identifying assumption you use.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Does this use options at all?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: No, this just uses the futures rate. But, actually, that has been an extension we’re looking at to see what this model does imply about the distribution, the pdf [probability density function] from options prices.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: Well, with enough pdf you wouldn’t need the identifying assumption.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Right.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Again, I’m sorry to ask this; I should know the answer. But are you projecting forward the range of estimated equilibrium real rates?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I’m assuming that those “rivers” just move sideways.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: That they move sideways? Does that make sense?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Probably not. A number of you have articulated over the course of this meeting reasons why they’d probably drift up over time, one of which is that as the output gap closes, you have less room to go and you presumably would have a higher real rate.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Wouldn’t that significantly alter the path in the upper panels if that were true?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: If you follow the staff forecast, the answer is “not really.” It could shift these colors over a bar or two but it would not materially influence the story.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Further questions?

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Vincent, have you drafted a sentence to see what alternative 2 would look like on exhibit 3 using probability?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: Yes, I circulated that to the Committee back in August, I think. Essentially it says that the Committee judges that if the current level of the federal funds rate were to be maintained, the probability of output growth being above its sustainable pace about equals the probability that it would be below. Also, the probability that inflation would be above that consistent with price stability is about equal to that of it being below. That is, you essentially call out a point on the fan chart and characterize where you think the outcomes are relative to that. And that sentence would characterize it in terms of the next few quarters. It said “If the current amount of policy accommodation were maintained over the next few quarters, then the Committee assesses that….” It was stated in those terms.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Thank you. I have a question, Vincent. My memory may not serve me well, but I recall our going through the communications process step-by-step. One of the reasons— though maybe not the only reason—we got into the discussion of risks for the future had to do with the fact that we had previously used a bias formulation, in part, to get consensus around the table. And that was thought to be information that ought to be communicated to the markets and, in fact, it was communicated, as I recall, inadvertently or advertently. Anyway, we ended up moving into the more formulaic approach to talking about the balance of risks. What is your impression about whether we really have the option to do alternative 1? Has this long period we’ve had of speculating on probabilities gotten us into a bind? Communication is a very tricky thing. Is this one of those cases where after we’ve done it, we can’t take it back without looking as if we’re returning to the Dark Ages of nontransparency? Do we really have the option of saying only what we did and why we did it, even recognizing that the explanation of why we did it would always have some forward-looking aspect to it because, of course, monetary policy works with a lag, and so forth. Is alternative 1 really something we can do?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I would say that there are tradeoffs possible. That is, if all you did was cut out the last paragraph, it would be seen and interpreted by markets as cutting back on the degree of transparency in a retrograde step. But if you wanted to, you could expand your characterization of the outlook in the previous paragraph, implicitly providing some sort of forecast and, therefore, hinting at the future direction of rates. You could be more numeric in your characterization of the outlook, say, by releasing your central tendency surveys more frequently. So, there are some possibilities. But I think that just dropping it outright would probably not be well received.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: I’ve sensed from time to time that a couple of people around the table have agreed with the general premise that trying to tell the markets where policy is going—except when we really needed to do it, which is how I would characterize the situation in 2003 and 2004— isn’t something they’re necessarily comfortable with. I know I’m not comfortable with it.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: But telling people you’re not sure of the future direction of rates is giving them information, and it may be superior to being silent about that and leaving them to form potentially inappropriate expectations.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: Should we infer from your presentation that you would not change the structure of this paragraph until we reach the point where the probability of a move up equals the probability of a move down? Would you say that the point at which the desirable path of the fed funds rate is flat is the optimal time for evolution or would it be ahead of that point?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I think there are going to be a couple of speed bumps in the process along the way. The first is earlier in the rationale portion of the paragraph in your characterization of the degree of policy accommodation. One could imagine a succession of steps, as you continue to move the funds rate up, that will get you to a point where you can no longer confidently assert that policy is accommodative. You’d therefore have to make that a qualified statement rather than an explicit statement.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: You’d say that would be an obvious intermediate change.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: That’s the first speed bump that you think about if you try to walk through the changes that might need to be made in the statement going forward given, say, the Greenbook outlook. If you wanted to introduce new language, an opportunity to do so would come when you’re just not quite sure of the direction of rates—when the funds rate is in a zone where the Committee can no longer be confident that it can remove policy accommodation at a measured pace. When you reach the point where that is no longer true, you’re going to have to drop that sentence. You’re going to have to ask the question: Should we try to change the risk assessment or try to write another sentence that characterizes the Committee’s view that it is not sure of the future direction of rates?

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: I have one more question. Should one also infer from your presentation that you think the optimal path of monetary policy in the period ahead is to get behind us all the moves necessary to get the funds rate closer to the midpoint of the range of its equilibrium? Are you saying that we should steadily get those moves done, so we get more quickly to the point where one could expect a flatter path going forward, or are you not suggesting that?

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I don’t think there was advocacy in the two different interpretations of the federal funds curve, only a suggestion that if you are not of the view that the path will involve a ¼ point move at every meeting, it would be helpful to define “measured” once again. I say that because there is a real risk that market participants, having extrapolated from six policy firmings by the end of today, are going to say that you are on a path to do so. Indeed, it may have come to the point where they wouldn’t expect a pause unless you signaled a pause—that is, that you wouldn’t feel that you could keep policy unchanged with the existing statement.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: And yet if you repeat that language in the minutes again, as you suggested, reasonable people might interpret that as a conscious decision to try to signal in some sense that a pause is approaching. That’s a little awkward to justify, given that we’re in a period of time where I think the probability the market is attributing to the next three moves is higher than it has been over the last six to nine months.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: But you could make it a symmetric statement. You could say that the measured pace language is not inconsistent with a pause in the policy of 25 basis point moves at each meeting nor with a larger move. There was at least one member who indicated a sentiment for a firmer policy.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
During our meeting, I brought up the topic of accommodation when we reached a 1.75 funds rate on the way down. I'm curious, when do we start seeing responses from others regarding the apparent asymmetry in our judgments about accommodation? I haven't heard anyone raise this question, except perhaps as a moving target. While there may not be an inconsistency yet, we need to address this issue because it will come up with the first economic hiccup you mentioned.

I'd also like to touch on another point. The likelihood of us making it through the end of 2006 with the positive economic outcomes we're forecasting is quite low. Our econometric models and market functions give us a continuous, smooth path coming off the "sweet spot," but history suggests this won't last. When an economy is growing, the probability of continued growth in the subsequent six months is lower in the early stages of the recovery. This is because the economy may not yet be out of the woods, and the market may not be fully confident in the recovery.

However, as confidence grows, capital investment increases, stock markets rise, and yield spreads fall. The problem is that this scenario is unsustainable, as history shows that it always comes to an end. When risk premiums are low, they can only go in one direction. This is the inverse of what happens when the economy is in a downturn. We've been able to handle these adjustments smoothly since the early 1980s, but we can't ignore the fact that the current situation is built on the assumption that stability will continue.

I'm not suggesting we have a better alternative to the Greenbook forecast, but I am concerned that it may be underestimating the potential for an inflation pickup. We need to be aware of this possibility and prepare for it.

In conclusion, I recommend another 25 basis points on the funds rate and a slightly modified statement. I believe we've had a successful run, but this won't last forever. When it ends, our ability to forecast what's coming next will be greatly diminished. We've seen pauses and un-pauses throughout this cycle, and the next pause could be the one that signals a larger problem. I suggest we be prepared for this eventuality. Any thoughts, President Guynn?

Statement: Karen, could you give us a fair and balanced view— @laugh—of the significance of the changes to the Stability and Growth Pact? On net, are they really that bad? Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Well, I have not read it or thought about it in sufficient detail to truly answer your question. My sense is that, in part, it was a recognition of the inevitable. That is, it was a political solution to the fact that certain realities of life were confronting several European countries. So rather than continue with the political tension at meetings about who was going to be penalized and allow the stresses between the larger countries and the smaller countries to keep playing out in the media, they revised the agreement. They felt that if they changed the language, they could accept what was going to happen in any event and reduce the damage it might cause, because countries would be complying in some sense with the revised Stability and Growth Pact rather than the old one. As for the actual details of the changes, they largely took the form of redefining what goes into the measures against which targets are set and against which penalties are levied for violating the targets. To be honest, I don’t have the list complete in my head, but one change had to do with German reunification. For example, certain expenditures get put in a different category and are not part of the relevant measure of the budget deficit against which they are supposed to meet the target.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: That in itself is enough to bring the German deficit well down.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Other kinds of restructuring expenditures were included. So there was an effort made to—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The labor reform expenditures were also allowed to be taken out.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Yes, the pension reform.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: In addition to sort of recognizing the inevitable, such as the German reunification piece, many of the other categories that were accorded new status I would describe as things that everybody agreed should be done. They agreed that it would be regrettable if, in the name of the Stability and Growth Pact, political pressure were exerted to cut back on restructuring so as to meet the goal. Now, we all know the Stability and Growth Pact was intended to tell these countries that they should cut other things so they could achieve the target, but that wasn’t happening. The ECB [European Central Bank] made a guarded statement about remaining concerned. The markets didn’t seem to react negatively. Down deep the problem, in my mind, is that the way the Stability and Growth Pact was originally structured—and possibly even the way it is still structured—was appropriate for countries that had already re-centered their fiscal activity more or less at a zero balance. The various ways that the limits were stated and the symmetry, and so forth, all assumed each country started from a good place. And then it made perfect sense. Actually, if that had been the case, one would be indifferent to whether it should be the structurally adjusted balance or the actual balance; that is irrelevant if you’re starting at zero. Some of the big countries never got there, and that has been the part of the story that has produced tension ever since 1999, really. These reforms didn’t undo that. They didn’t say: We wrote a pact centered on zero and that isn’t working, so we’re going to scrap that and start all over. But they did make these exceptions, which are going to ease the pressures on some of the big countries. So, those countries will not be forced, in a macroeconomic sense, to tighten when it’s not such a good idea; nor will pressure be put on them not to do some structural things that they should be doing, because those specifically are the things that have been exempted.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: But they didn’t accommodate greater cyclical divergence on their path.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: No.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: It really is accommodating structural changes that go in the wrong direction to the objectives even if those changes otherwise make some sense.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Right. But by setting aside a class of expenditures that are going to happen, they have allowed the band, as defined, to embrace the countries. So the fact that they didn’t change the cyclical thing doesn’t matter so much anymore.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: PAYGO does not apply. @laugh

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN

The presumption that everything is going to be fine beyond the election has no basis, because the next set of pressures will create different effects. What it demonstrates is that the general view that the system would hold together and work for the big countries is flawed. It worked for the small countries, because the big countries insisted; they essentially have the capacity to give the smaller countries an either/or ultimatum, so to speak. With Luxembourg in the Chair—and they were very strongly against any changes—it took a couple of months to erode a lot of that language and finally get everybody on board, which tells you that this is a very flawed system.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON

Had they done those things, the old Stability and Growth Pact would have probably been just fine. It’s not obvious to me that the changes they agreed to just recently are going to move us very far in the direction of the Lisbon Agenda. But these changes will at least eliminate the uncertainty that has been out there in the market about whether these countries will actually be contracting fiscal policy at a time when unemployment is rising, and that kind of thing.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
