Statement: The very process itself would produce an effect. As soon as these issues begin to move off what would be a sort of constant BBB yield, it automatically drops out—

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: Not entirely, but it gets downweighted by a significant amount.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: You’re weighting with respect to the position on the spread?

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: Yes.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: As the paper moves from BBB to BB, for example, the weights fall and then they rise again as they cross the border?

Speaker: MR. OLINER
Statement: Yes, exactly.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: That’s really quite interesting. I didn’t know we did it that way. Other questions for Dino? If not, would someone move to ratify the domestic operations?

Speaker: SPEAKER
Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection, they are approved. You wanted to discuss the swaps issue next?

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: Yes, my favorite issue.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I didn’t want to ask for a quick, routine vote without allowing time for at least a defensive comment.

Speaker: MR. KOS
Statement: It’s May, which means that it’s the time of year that I come to this Committee and ask for a vote on the renewal of the swap facilities for Mexico and Canada. They get renewed in December, but six months’ notice would need to be given if the Committee chose not to renew them. That’s why we vote on it at this meeting.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The arguments in favor remain as valid as they have been. @laugh

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Is that defensive or offensive?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: I refuse to comment further. @laugh Would somebody like to move to renew these?

Speaker: SPEAKER
Statement: So moved.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Without objection. You have another year of grace. Let’s move on to Dave Stockton.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: [ERROR]

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement:
During our recent staff review, we noticed only minor adjustments to our projection for average foreign real GDP growth, despite significant shifts in global oil prices, commodity prices, and exchange rates, as well as a notable revision to the U.S. growth outlook. However, these seemingly small changes mask substantial differences among countries and regions. I'd like to discuss some unexpected developments during the intermeeting period and their potential implications for the global economy and the U.S. external sector.

One notable development is the significant drop in long-term sovereign interest rates in major foreign industrial countries since the last FOMC meeting. For instance, the German 10-year rate fell from approximately 3.7% at the time of the March meeting to an all-time low of 3.38% on April 29. Rates in Canada and the United Kingdom decreased nearly as much, and even Japanese rates dropped nearly 20 basis points. Although lower interest rates might typically be expected to boost equity prices, major stock price indexes abroad have instead fallen between 2% and 7%.

These asset market developments reflect participants' reactions to recent data, which suggest that the foreign industrial countries are facing additional headwinds that are impeding growth during the current quarter and likely will do so over the remainder of 2005. As a result, we have revised down our near-term projections for the major foreign industrial countries. We believe that both elevated oil prices and less favorable news for the U.S. outlook are contributing factors.

Euro-area economic sentiment fell to its lowest level in a year and a half in April, and the Tankan survey in Japan dropped noticeably in the first quarter. In contrast, recent developments in emerging-market economies, although mixed, are generally positive, and we have revised up slightly our outlook for average growth there for this year. This outcome is due to several emerging-market economies exporting oil and other primary commodities whose prices have remained high, as well as the unexpectedly strong first-quarter growth recorded in China.

Exchange market developments also contained some surprises. The dollar appreciated broadly over the intermeeting period, particularly against the Canadian dollar and the euro. Domestic political tensions appear to be weighing on the Canadian dollar, and we continue to build into the Greenbook baseline a slight real dollar depreci

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It strikes me that if we’re going to get that significant a slowdown in imports, we should be seeing it in some upward pressure on U.S. industrial production, provided the composition of the import change is industrial materials, which theoretically would be shifted to domestic sources—that is, the share from domestic sources would be higher. Do you have any insight into the details at all? Or is it just that the extraordinarily outsized surge in the first quarter—beyond anything reasonable—requires a reversion to the mean?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: Well, we have numbers written down for the pieces, but not ones that I think are ready for exposure.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It has to affect domestic GDP because, obviously, the imports of capital goods will have an impact on the producers’ durable equipment purchases, which theoretically are split into domestic and foreign producers.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: A very big chunk of the Q2 story is oil and relates to the seasonal adjustment procedure that is now in place for describing real oil, which we question. We think that may not translate into real-time evidence of this sort, because it is, in some sense, a statistical treatment of the data as opposed to an actual real-world event. Other than that, consumer goods have been a big piece of the surprise in imports, so I don’t think they would immediately have implications for U.S. industrial production. There is nothing specific I can point to, and I admit—in fact, it’s one of the reasons I highlighted this—that a change from double-digit growth to 1+ percent growth this quarter is a huge drop. But that is our best estimate.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: It really depends on what the monthly pattern is. It could very well be, for example, that January was up sharply, February was down, and March was down, and the average is still up 15 percent. And you keep going down, so you can’t really see the difference.

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: The people who construct these numbers write down notional placeholders for the different components for the very, very near term, but I don’t think they would regard them as grounded in a lot of information. They do that to see if the number they write down for the longer term is a sensible number.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: We certainly haven’t seen any surge in orders to domestic producers that would be indicative of that.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: On the spread that’s opening up between average hourly compensation of employees and the ECI [Employment Cost Index], theoretically that is supposed to mean that the mix is changing. It implies that the mix is changing toward higher-skilled components, presumably. We can check that, I would assume, by looking at least at the ratio of supervisory workers to production workers. Are the numbers moving in line with that ratio?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: I don’t think a mix story is explaining the first-quarter divergence there. The big slowdown in the benefits component came from what the ECI is measuring as employer contributions to pension plans; there was a big drop in that component. It doesn’t necessarily have to line up, in an accounting sense, with what’s happening in the national income accounts. So, I think it’s more than the wage mix side that seems to be—

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Well, the interesting point is that it should. Otherwise, the question is whether labor income is calculated correctly.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: They’re not measuring that exact same component on a quarterly basis in the same way.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: So the compensation per hour may be overestimated in that respect, if that’s true.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: It could be. But also we have seen big discrepancies in the past that haven’t necessarily ever been reconciled in later publications of the national income accounts. So it’s conceivable.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Those are the workers who live in that country called “discrepancy” in our world balance of payments accounts! @laugh

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Perhaps.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Any other questions?

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement: I have a question for David concerning investment. At our last meeting, I certainly had the feeling that the outlook for the economy and for demand was a lot more robust than I had previously thought, and the Greenbook reflected the same view. In large part that was based on rejecting the pothole theory related to partial expensing for investment. We thought that there would be a pothole in January. It didn’t occur. January data on orders were strong, and the staff decided, I think, that there was a lot of momentum in investment spending. Certainly the fundamentals seemed quite strong. But for February and March the data are really very weak. It seems to me that the response in the Greenbook this time is to stick with the view that the fundamentals are strong and the investment spending outlook remains quite strong. There hasn’t been a lot of downward revision there. I wonder if you’d just talk about that a little bit. One possibility might be that, in fact, January wasn’t a perfect test of partial expensing. Suppose that firms could take advantage of that expensing provision if they placed an order by December 30. Maybe some orders were placed and didn’t actually show up in the December data. They might have shown up in January. I don’t know the details of how that series is constructed. Another possibility is that we may be learning from the February and March data that the outlook for investment spending just isn’t as robust as we had thought, but it’s not because of partial expensing. In any event, it seems to me that there was not a lot of downward revision in the medium-term forecast for investment spending. I just wondered if you would discuss your thinking.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: First of all, on whether or not we’ve experienced the pothole, I think you’re certainly right that January isn’t necessarily the perfect test. But I don’t think that the hypothesis you suggested would work, in the sense that the equipment had to have been installed by the end of December to qualify for partial expensing.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement: Not just ordered?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Not just ordered. There were a couple of exceptions to that general rule, but the equipment was supposed to have already been installed. So the timing doesn’t look good for the pothole story. But that doesn’t necessarily mean, given how noisy those data are, that we couldn’t still be seeing some pothole effect now. To be honest, we took out most of our pothole effect at the time of the last forecast, but we didn’t take all of it out. So we certainly think there’s some consistency between what we’ve seen and our assumption that there would be a pothole effect. You’re right that we certainly didn’t view the March reading as indicating a huge turning point. We just basically fed those data through our near-term statistical filters, and then, beyond the near term, allowed our econometric model to “speak,” if you will. And the econometric models still revise down the level of investment spending over this forecast horizon. So it isn’t the case that we offset the weakness that we saw; we’ve actually carried that forward.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement: It’s a level effect.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: It’s a level effect. We had a drop in long-term interest rates, which tended to cushion, through the cost of capital, some of the downward revision that we would have had otherwise. And we’ve allowed a little bit of the accelerator effect to show through. As we look at the situation, it is still the case, if one believes that underlying final sales are continuing to expand at a reasonable clip—and we recognize that that’s an open question but that’s the view that we have taken in the forecast—that the cost of capital is low. And corporations’ balance sheets still look pretty strong to us. Corporations are sitting on a very big pile of liquid assets, so we don’t think there’s going to be much difficulty financing this amount of investment spending. So, this is one of those areas where we have read the recent news as probably more noise than signal. We have given it some signal, though, and that’s why we have the level a bit lower by the end of this forecast than we did last time.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: You now have higher energy prices built into the forecast—significantly higher energy prices—and the futures market does, too. I’m just wondering if you could talk about how that factors into your thinking about potential output going forward.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: The rise in energy prices that we’ve had suggests to us—and especially as we look at those far-dated futures contracts—that this probably is going to be a drag on the level of potential output going forward. We made a more discrete adjustment in the fall to reflect that. And when we update our entire supply-side portion of the forecast in June, we’re going to have to revisit that issue. We do see this as a factor. While we haven’t heard a lot of reports of firms talking about increasing scrappage of capital equipment, given the significant change in relative factor prices that there has been, we’d expect to see some of that going forward. So, we look at the rise in the relative price of energy as a negative for potential output. It would be a small negative, because those effects occur over very long periods of time. It isn’t usually reflected as a very large discrete change in the level of potential output, but it could be a factor weighing on it.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: If we had significantly higher energy prices than we have now, what would the sensitivity be?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: You’re raising a good question, and I don’t know the answer. There could conceivably be some nonlinearity in that process. But I think we’re talking about effects that are measured in tenths of a percentage point—not quarters or halves of a percentage point—in terms of the effects on multifactor productivity, or, in essence, capital input going forward.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement: David, you referred in your remarks to the exercise shown in the package of materials for Monday’s Board briefing. I’m looking at the pass-through into core inflation of the rise in energy prices, import prices, and commodity prices. If back in December ’03, which is your baseline for this exercise, you had had a forecast for energy prices, commodity prices, import prices, and the dollar that is consistent with what happened, would you have expected your model to show this much pass-through to core inflation prices? Or would this result have been surprising?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: The result of that exercise suggests, if anything, a little surprise that we didn’t get a touch more pass-through. Actually, I was surprised when I first saw those results but not after I sat back and thought more carefully. Certainly, if you had told me in late 2003, when oil prices were $25 to $28 a barrel, that they were going to be something north of $50, that the markets would perceive that as being very largely a permanent change, that we would see an ongoing depreciation of the dollar and a very significant acceleration in intermediate materials prices, I think I would have said core consumer price inflation now of just 1¾ percent would be a pretty good outcome. So, as I thought more about it, I was more comfortable with the results that we presented yesterday. Now, as we indicated in the Greenbook in an alternative simulation, while we are comfortable with our inflation forecast, we could certainly understand it if you were less comfortable with our assessment of the pass-through to core inflation, given that this has been an area where we have made persistent errors. It’s very hard to estimate these pass-through effects. I know of work done over the years at the Board and by Reserve Bank staff aimed at trying to estimate indirect energy price effects or import price effects. Sometimes the coefficients are zero and sometimes they are large. It looks to us as if, more recently, the size of those coefficients has diminished, but that could simply be a function of the fact that many of those variables were relatively stable over a long period of time. It’s just very hard to estimate precisely what the effects are. Our alternative simulation, where we doubled the size of those pass-through effects, is intended to give you a sense of how far things could get out of line if we made a rather big error in the size of the pass-through. And, as we noted, there would be a very noticeable effect going forward. So, it does seem reasonable to wonder not only about whether we have the underlying forecast right for oil, imports, and other commodities but also whether we have the pass-through of that correct.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: The first quote of NIPA [national income and product accounts] data showed a large increase in inventory accumulation outside of what we talked about in terms of oil effects. I’m rather curious about the inventory issues. Where are these inventories? Are they in final goods? Are they in intermediate goods? Are the inventories produced domestically or abroad? What is the implication, in terms of the future industrial impact, of this accumulation of inventories? I was just wondering if you could expand a bit on your view of that.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: First, I’d note that we are not necessarily buying into the BEA’s March assumption, which showed another very large inventory accumulation. In fact, that’s one of the areas where, in writing down our forecast, we differed—to the tune of about $6 or $7 billion—with the BEA. We have a lower level of inventory investment. So, that’s just their forecast against ours. At least on our assessment, the BEA’s figures overstate the extent to which inventories were accumulated in the first quarter. Now, in terms of looking for areas where there are inventory imbalances, it’s quite clear that one problem area is the motor vehicle industry. They’re still sitting with a very large days’ supply of cars and especially of trucks. We do think the implication of that would be a drag on GDP growth going forward, and we’re already seeing signs that the auto manufacturers are cutting back production. The other problem areas are a little harder to identify. Supplies of some types of chemicals look a little high at this point, and in paper and printing and a few other areas there apparently are some higher levels of inventory–sales ratios. We think that is part of the reason why we’re in a bit of a soft period for industrial production. When we look at that flow-of-goods system that we report occasionally in the Greenbook—which doesn’t use the Census Bureau and BEA data on inventories but infers inventories from what we think is going on with production and consumption—that shows not much going on at all. If anything, the weakness in industrial production suggests that maybe the inventory investment in the Census Bureau data may be overstated to some extent. So, it’s an area we’re watching, but it doesn’t seem to us as if a significant problem has developed. I might note also that yesterday’s purchasing managers’ report showed a drop in the number of purchasing managers who viewed their customers’ inventories as too high. That had inched up, and it’s come back some. So at this point, we’re inclined to think that some of what has happened in the first quarter was probably unintended inventory accumulation. That is a negative for the second quarter’s GDP growth, but it isn’t what I would call a significant inventory cycle at this point.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: The flattening of industrial production in March in that flow- of-goods system seems to reflect a rather large liquidation, seasonally adjusted, of motor vehicle inventories. I was curious whether there was any awareness of that on the part of BEA, because clearly those data were available at the time when BEA made the GDP forecast. Do they show a separate inventory for motor vehicles?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: They do. I don’t actually have that figure in front of me. In motor vehicles, they are basically doing a unit accounting. They use different seasonals than we do, but they should be showing something like that.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: So they’re either missing that, or they’re showing a significantly larger increase in non-automotive inventories than we have.

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Right.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Fisher.

Speaker: MR. FISHER
Statement: I’d like to ask a question of Karen about import price growth, particularly in the retail sector. In terms of imported goods sold through our retail chains and the timing of the increasing price pressures we’ve seen, is that weighted heavily toward March and recent activity in April? Could you expand on that a little bit?

Speaker: MS. JOHNSON
Statement: We collect import prices at the border, so to speak. So I can’t speak to the question of this showing through to retail prices. David might want to comment on that; I don’t know. Import price inflation has been surprising us a bit on the upside, and we have attributed part of that to higher global commodities prices. It shows up in our case in the industrial supplies types of categories, but we’ve seen some in the manufacturing area as well. When we break out the actual data for how import prices have moved, we’re getting contributions from both those pieces into import price inflation. So, relative to the last Greenbook, we have extended the period of elevated import price increases into the second quarter and a little into the third quarter because the global indexes of commodity prices—based on futures at this point for those periods—show the prices not rising further but remaining high, and we think it will take some time for that to be fully reflected in our import prices. But one factor that seemed to cause the timing to be off a little bit in our forecast last year was that commodity prices tended to be showing up more rapidly in U.S. import prices than our models would suggest is the historical average. We have that as a second term, in addition to foreign prices and the exchange rate and so forth. So embedded in our story is that import prices were high in the first quarter and will decelerate somewhat in the second and third quarters, but not hugely and not as much as we had thought before. It involves a somewhat more extensive pass-through of high global commodity prices into import prices, and a little bit is probably still the effect of dollar depreciation at the end of 2004. Those things, in essence, go away thereafter, and we have import price inflation dropping down very much—to less than 1 percent in 2006. That is conditioned on two assumptions: the assumption we have been making routinely for the dollar of late—because we don’t know a better one to make—which is that it will change only little going forward; and the futures path for commodity prices. So the projection that import prices will decelerate in stepwise fashion through the second half of this year and into 2006 is very much a reflection of these two conditioning assumptions. And we don’t have a whole lot of information other than that.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Any further questions for our economists?

Speaker: MR. STOCKTON
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I would note that I do have in front of me a table showing the BEA’s assumptions for March, and the BEA is showing a liquidation of motor vehicle stocks but very large accumulations elsewhere.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: If there are no further questions, who would like to start off the Committee discussion? President Moskow.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from our recent calls regarding the current economic soft patch and its potential connection to higher energy prices. In the Seventh District, growth has moderated slightly, with the manufacturing sector still strong but showing signs of slowing orders. The Big Three auto manufacturers are still struggling, and retail sales have softened, particularly for large SUVs and recreational vehicles. Higher gasoline prices are impacting consumer behavior, with middle-income consumers shifting to discounters and lower-income consumers cutting back more significantly.

However, business spending remains solid, with the heavy equipment market experiencing record demand. One manufacturer in this sector is increasing capital expenditures by 40% this year to address production bottlenecks. In contrast, a large diversified manufacturer has seen softening orders in the last few weeks, but overall business remains good.

Energy prices continue to be a major concern, with a more pervasive sense that they will remain high. The auto industry, retailers, and energy sector are all reporting this trend. However, a major oil company remains cautious in their investment planning, using a $20 a barrel assumption for new production projects. This suggests that oil prices will need to remain elevated for an extended period before the industry increases supply through significant long-term investments.

Additionally, cost pressures persist in areas such as construction materials, rubber, and plastics. Some of these pressures may be creeping into expectations. For example, a manufacturing firm budgeted for a 1% decline in material costs but experienced a 3% increase last year, and is budgeting for another 3% increase this year.

Looking ahead, we expect price pressures to remain a concern, but growth should pick up in the second half of the year. Core inflation is expected to reach around 2% this year, but should stabilize or edge off slightly in 2006. However, these assessments are uncertain, and we need to monitor the impact of the current soft patch on the output gap. If it reflects reduced policy stimulus or increased caution, we may need to remove accommodation gradually. But if it's due to higher energy prices, we risk accommodating an adverse supply shock and unhinging inflationary pressures.

Overall, we expect long-term expectations to remain anchored, but we'll need to monitor the impact

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Lacker.

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to share some insights about the economic activity in the Fifth District. After experiencing a soft patch in March, we've seen a slight rebound in April, particularly in manufacturing and retailing sectors. Manufacturers report solid demand and have successfully passed on higher energy and materials costs. Our shipments and new orders indexes have both increased. Most manufacturers plan to maintain their capital spending and remain optimistic about future prospects, except for those in textile, apparel, and furniture industries, who continue to struggle with pricing power and limited capacity expansion.

In the retail sector, we've seen a surge in big-ticket sales, with the strongest reading since July 2003. Retail employment and wages have also increased. Housing activity remains strong, with robust home sales in many areas.

However, the national economic picture has been disappointing since March, with both real growth and inflation decelerating. The slowdown in capital spending is particularly concerning, given expectations that business investment would drive this expansion. Core PCE inflation has risen to a 2.9 percent annual rate over the last three months, above the 1.7 percent rate over the past year. This could present a challenge for monetary policy, as we face weakening real growth and rising inflation.

Despite these concerns, I believe the economic fundamentals underlying this recovery are sound, with prospects for growth in real household income looking good. The Greenbook forecast suggests a more protracted narrowing of the output gap, which should help push core PCE inflation back below 2 percent. Market participants expect inflation to remain contained, and TIPS inflation compensation has declined since March. The yield curve is expected to react sensitively to data if inflation risks pick up, and we should ensure our communications don't inhibit this process.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Minehan.

Speaker: MS. MINEHAN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some thoughts on the current economic situation in New England and the nation. While we've encountered a few bumps in the road to steady growth, these are not expected to turn into major obstacles. However, there's a degree of uncertainty surrounding various economic variables.

In New England, contacts in early April reported solid growth, with employers adding jobs in February and March. Unemployment rose slightly due to a stabilization in the labor force after a period of decline. Tourism and business travel have been strong, particularly in Boston. Productivity improvements in the hotel industry are also noteworthy, with average staffing dropping from 80 per 100 rooms in 2000 to 62 per 100 rooms in 2004 due to technological advancements and online booking.

Retail and manufacturing contacts were generally optimistic, with strong demand and pricing power keeping margins robust. However, higher costs for oil and skilled labor were a concern, as was the more inflationary business environment.

On the negative side, consumer confidence took a hit in April, with concerns about rising oil prices and home heating oil prices doubling the national average. Residential real estate markets leveled off, and a regional index of leading economic indicators pointed to slower growth ahead.

Nationally, data have been disappointing, with subdued demand and rising prices. The labor market data for March was less than expected, and durable goods orders and manufacturing output saw contractions. Advance GDP data suggest a slowdown in final demand and a drag from net exports.

These indications of a slowdown could be temporary, and some moderation in demand may be beneficial given the stronger pricing environment. However, it's important to keep an eye on incoming data and adjust policy accordingly.

I believe it's prudent to continue moving policy to a point that is not stimulative by the time the output gap closes, and doing so gradually will help minimize the impact on the economy. If data continue to disappoint, a pause may be necessary. Conversely, upside surprises could lead to a faster pace of upward policy movement.

Given the increased uncertainty in the economy, I suggest we drop the "measured pace" language and simply communicate what we're doing and why. This will allow the market to assign its own probabilities to future

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Guynn.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share my perspective on the recent economic data and its potential implications for output growth and inflation. The softness in some of the latest data releases may suggest an increased risk to the average real output growth of 3.5% since the recession and 4.3% over the last eight quarters. I believe consumer and business confidence may still be more fragile than anticipated, with uncertainty around high energy prices being a significant factor.

Our Southeast business leaders, from both large corporations like Georgia Pacific and smaller companies, have reported some caution when it comes to spending and hiring decisions. For instance, a senior executive at Georgia Pacific mentioned a slight decline in box sales, while the CFO of UPS reported steady but unspectacular business. At a recent Small Business Advisory Council meeting, there was a consensus that persistently high energy prices were negatively impacting profits and delaying capital spending plans.

Despite these concerns, I still believe the most likely path for output growth is near or above potential, as indicated by the Greenbook. However, I am more concerned about the recent price developments, which have shown an upward trend in measures of inflation, regardless of the preferred index.

Price pressures in our region are uneven, with some intended and actual price increases becoming more widespread and sticking, while others face resistance. For example, our Georgia Pacific executive reported that price increases on consumer products have become less successful in the last two months. At the same time, prices for building materials are clearly reflecting the higher energy costs involved in their production.

Residential construction in Florida remains strong, but there are concerns about speculation in the market. Additionally, there are signs of potential shortages for concrete and drywall. Our staff's analysis of CPI inflation data indicates that the largest contributors to increased inflation have come from factors other than the pass-through of energy costs, such as new and used car prices, lodging costs away from home, and the rental equivalence measure for housing.

In summary, while the risks to the real economy have increased, the risks to inflation are now clearly to the upside. Markets are expecting another 25 basis points of tightening today, and I believe this is the appropriate path for us to take. However, given the increased uncertainty around the real economy, we need to carefully consider just how much we truly know and what to communicate about the future path of policy. I suggest we

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Stern.

Speaker: MR. STERN
Statement:
During our meeting, I'd like to bring up two concerns that have been on my mind, which align with the issues Dave Stockton addressed. Firstly, is the recent decrease in the Greenbook forecast an accurate reflection of the economy, or will it perform worse or better than anticipated? Secondly, is the forecast of moderate inflation still valid, given the increase in most core measures we've seen?

I believe there's a risk of overanalyzing the statistics that have emerged in the past few weeks in April, considering their reliability and the fact that not all of them were surprising to me. However, I wouldn't completely disregard them. Consequently, I'm inclined to lower my growth forecast, although it remains slightly above the Greenbook's projection of 3.5 percent. Our internal forecasting model suggests 3 percent growth consistently, and I'm more optimistic than that for three reasons: the fundamentals, anecdotes, and history.

Regarding the fundamentals, we have the familiar factors we've discussed at this table for several years: favorable financial market conditions, productivity expansion, a lack of supply-side constraints, and healthy consumer and corporate balance sheets. The anecdotes from business leaders in our district and District economic data have been mostly positive. Manufacturing is growing, employment is increasing, and labor markets are tightening, particularly in areas with strong mining and energy activity. Consumer spending is expanding, and capital spending reports and comments from directors are quite strong overall. However, there's a note of caution from firms with significant transportation sector exposure. The anecdotes, on the whole, have been quite positive.

Lastly, I look to history, specifically the long expansions of the 1980s and 1990s. In my opinion, these expansions were not solely due to good luck or astute policies but were largely due to the economy's inherent strength, flexibility, and resilience. I don't believe these traits have changed or diminished just because we're in a new decade. This gives me confidence that the outlook remains favorable and that the expansion remains solidly grounded.

As for inflation, I still believe moderate inflation will persist, with core prices likely maintaining a pace similar to the last five or six years on average. However, I acknowledge that core inflation measures have been increasing steadily since late

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Fisher.

Speaker: MR. FISHER
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to share some observations I've made after reviewing the minutes from previous sessions and attending my first FOMC meeting. I understand that I'm joining an ongoing conversation, but I believe my fresh perspective could provide some valuable insights.

Mr. Stockton, I noticed your sense of humor in the minutes, which was a pleasant surprise. However, I've detected a noticeable dissonance between recent meetings and the current state of the Bluebook, Greenbook, and the overall discussion tone. I'd like to use a baseball analogy to describe this situation.

Previously, the FOMC faced consistent pitches from the mound, with predictable inflation rates between 1% and 2%. We all assumed a batter's stance accordingly and managed to progress without creating imbalances. Now, the data and anecdotal evidence suggest that the economy has changed pitchers, and we're encountering various types of pitches, such as curveballs, knuckleballs, and split-finger fastballs. The question is, should we adjust our batter's stance or stick with what worked for the previous pitcher?

I'd like to focus on the kinds of pitches we're seeing in the Eleventh District, as well as nationally and globally. Given our District's second-largest manufacturing state status, significant employment in the high-tech sector, and major transshipment point with substantial retailing activity, I believe there's some proxy value in our District's performance.

In the first quarter, economic activity in our region grew around 3%, with manufacturing and high-tech manufacturing picking up pace. Energy-related activity, which is a stereotype for our District, shows price pressures from the employment standpoint but no significant pass-through activity in terms of pricing power. We're not hearing reports of increased pricing power from retailers either, except for accountants due to Sarbanes-Oxley.

Based on my background as a strategic advisor to companies like UPS, Exxon, EDS, and Wal-Mart, I've reached out to get a feel for how our local situation corresponds with the broader economy. The consensus is a 3% to 3.5% growth rate for the domestic economy, and people are adjusting to this expectation. One CEO I spoke with

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Yellen.

Speaker: MS. YELLEN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, it's been barely a year since I've been back at the table, and I'm getting a sense of déjà vu. @laugh The recent trend of higher inflation and softer spending feels eerily similar to what we experienced last spring and summer. After a spike in energy prices, we saw a slowdown in consumer spending and an increase in inflation. But, as last time, I remain cautiously optimistic that both the growth slowdown and the inflation uptick will prove transitory.

Last year's energy price shock delivered a significant blow to consumers and businesses. However, after the initial jolt, consumer spending and economic growth rebounded. With oil prices coming down lately, I anticipate a similar recovery in spending this year.

The lesson from last year's experience is that we shouldn't overreact to the present soft patch or short-term fluctuations in real-side data when the fundamentals for growth appear to remain relatively strong. The forecast beyond the current quarter has been revised only slightly, with major components of demand projected to continue expanding at a healthy pace. Our business contacts, outside of the Bay Area and Los Angeles, remain upbeat about the economy.

Last summer's experience also advises a cautious policy reaction to inflation data. The recent uptick in core inflation likely reflects pass-through of cost pressures due to higher energy, commodities, and import prices. If so, the upturn is likely to be transitory.

Several fundamentals driving the outlook for inflation are worth highlighting. Labor markets still show considerable slack, and our contacts report little trouble filling job openings, except in some skilled trades. Productivity remains solid, and labor cost pressures appear restrained. Firms report continued scope for productivity improvements.

While "pricing power" is a phrase frequently mentioned by our contacts, it's important to note that it refers exclusively to pass-through of cost increases due to energy, materials, and transportation. In construction, agriculture, and transportation, firms report considerable success in passing these cost increases along. In other sectors, the experience has been more mixed.

I support a 25 basis point increase at this meeting and maintaining the "measured pace" description of the likely path for policy going forward. However, weak real-side data might suggest a pause to evaluate the extent and likely duration of the slowdown

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Pianalto.

Speaker: MS. PIANALTO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, at our previous gathering, I expressed optimism about the Fourth District's economic improvement and its alignment with the national economy as reported in the Beige Book. However, this positive trend seems to have waned. Since then, economic activity in my district has taken a downturn. During the Beige Book process and my discussions with business contacts, I've been paying close attention to pricing pressures and the reemergence of pricing power. The tone regarding pricing power has softened, and while some contacts can pass through higher raw material costs, most are finding it challenging to increase prices. I'm concerned about the potential for ongoing inflation, given the recent trend of upside surprises.

The Greenbook projection views energy shocks as temporary disturbances that dissipate over time. Last year, energy shocks caused a ripple effect on inflation, which then declined. However, without these energy shocks, I believe the Greenbook inflation projections for this year would have remained stable. I think the current projections are reasonable, but the risk of more supply shocks cannot be ignored.

There's little evidence of accelerating unit labor costs or problematic prices unaffected by energy. This gives me some confidence in the Greenbook baseline inflation projection. However, I acknowledge that inflation dynamics could be more persistent than anticipated. Throughout this tightening cycle, I've focused on returning the fed funds rate to the neutral range. With the recent economic data, I'm less certain about aiming for the higher end of that range. The equilibrium real rate of interest could be notably higher or lower by our June meeting.

For now, I still believe a sequence of small incremental increases in the fed funds rate is the most likely course of action. However, the increased uncertainty about the economic and inflation outlook necessitates a change in communication strategy. I suggest removing the "measured pace" language and adding words that convey greater uncertainty about the real growth and inflation outlook and the timing and magnitude of future policy actions. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Hoenig.

Speaker: MR. HOENIG
Statement:
Chairperson, I'd like to begin by acknowledging some unexpected developments since our last FOMC meeting. The economy has experienced a deceleration following the first quarter, and core inflation has persisted in its upward trend. Consequently, I've revised my growth forecast for this year to approximately 3.5%. I anticipate growth in the second half to be around 3.75%, and it may drop back to 3.5% in the following year. With a trend growth rate of about 3.25%, the output gap and labor market slack are expected to continue shrinking, as previously stated.

I concur with those attributing the slowdown to the sustained rise in energy prices, which has negatively impacted consumer spending and confidence. Furthermore, higher energy costs have also hindered growth in other countries, contributing to a worsening trade balance. However, we need more data to determine if these factors are temporary or more permanent.

If temporary, I believe the economy will recover as energy prices stabilize or decline. Monetary policy, in my opinion, remains accommodative, and financial conditions are highly accommodative. I'd like to share an anecdote from a major banking executive I spoke with recently. He described a large condominium project where the construction cost was $100 million, and the developers had only $3 million in equity. Lenders financed $97 million based on a projected sale price of $140 million, indicating significant speculation in the market.

Despite this, labor and capital markets are strengthening. The Tenth District's economy is mostly showing signs of improvement, although energy prices remain a concern. Consumer spending, including travel and tourism, rose solidly in March and early April, and housing activity has turned up. However, preliminary data from our manufacturing survey suggests a potential slowdown, with production and shipments indexes both falling in April. Additionally, some firms have expressed concerns about higher energy prices, delaying shipments and considering production cuts.

Regarding inflation, I expect core inflation to continue rising, with core PCE inflation averaging around 2% in 2005 and core CPI inflation averaging 2.5%. However, unlike the Greenbook forecast, I believe core inflation will remain elevated in 2006 rather than falling back. Energy price increases may be a contributing factor, but

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Santomero.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from the Third District's economic conditions in the first quarter. Retail sales saw a slight increase in March, but were dampened by unseasonably cold and rainy weather. Auto dealers, on the other hand, reported a healthy rebound, thanks to strong manufacturer incentives. Both retailers and auto dealers anticipate sales to improve this spring.

Employment continued to grow in the region, with a larger increase at small firms than at large ones. The unemployment rate declined, and the construction sector remained soft, with nonresidential construction remaining weak. Residential construction, however, remained strong, with a high value of contracts and robust home sales and house price appreciation.

Our business outlook survey indicated that manufacturing activity is expanding, with a significant rebound in the general activity index. About half of the firms plan to increase capacity in 2005, with most expecting increases in the 5 to 10 percent range.

However, there are concerns about increasing price pressures in the District. Consumer prices in greater Philadelphia are rising faster than the national average, and industrial price pressures have increased. The price indexes in our manufacturing survey have rebounded, but the indexes of future prices paid and received have stabilized at relatively high levels. Employment cost inflation in the Northeast region has not accelerated.

In summary, the economic expansion appears to be continuing at a moderate pace in the Third District. While there are concerns about price pressures, it's too early to alter policy based on recent data. The economy is expected to have growth quarters above and below the sustainable pace, and some softness is typical during an expansion.

I agree with the assessment that the economy continues to have positive momentum, but there are downside risks for near-term growth. The acceleration in inflation is a concern, and it's important for us to craft a statement that gives us flexibility in policy actions going forward. We should convey less certainty about policy actions and consider saying less for greater transparency. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: President Poole.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights from my recent conversations with contacts in the logistics industry. My contacts at FedEx and UPS report contrasting results. FedEx's contact indicates no signs of a slowdown, with both their business and customer activity remaining strong. UPS, however, is experiencing a slight dip, particularly in their express business, which they attribute to a decrease in mortgage refinancing documents. Both companies are pushing forward with significant expansion plans. FedEx plans to increase capital expenditures by 20% for their fiscal year starting in June, while UPS is hiring 20-25 pilots monthly, with five being replacements.

My trucking industry contact, on the other hand, reports soft business, with a 2% decline in volume year-over-year. The intermodal business has also seen a 5% decrease, marking the first decline in its 15-year history. These declines are widespread and affect various industries. My Wal-Mart contact shares that same-store sales are coming in below expectations, primarily due to the impact of high energy prices on the purchasing power of their customer base.

Labor market pressures are minimal, with the exception of specific skill sets, such as accountants, and the ongoing shortage of truck drivers. Inflation data has been higher than anticipated, while output data has been lower. My uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased, and while the Greenbook forecast remains a reasonable point estimate, I believe the inflation risk is asymmetrically skewed to the upside. The fundamentals for inflation appear solid, with modest wage increases and good productivity growth. Financial conditions do not indicate a highly expansionary monetary policy, as M2 growth has lagged behind nominal GDP and MZM has remained flat in the last six months with a 2% year-over-year increase. Overall, I think it's essential to remain cautious about the inflation risks. Additionally, the lengthy discussion about the staff's forecast underscores the increased uncertainty surrounding the economic outlook.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Coffee is available, so why don’t we take a break?

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Vice Chair.

Speaker: MR. GEITHNER
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share our thoughts on the economic outlook. While we believe the foundation for a solid expansion with moderate inflation remains strong, we're less certain than before. Based on current assumptions, we anticipate the economy to grow at a 3.5% rate for the rest of this year and in 2006. Core PCE inflation is expected to come in below 2%, but we think it will follow a slightly higher path.

We're close to the Greenbook projection in terms of the overall forecast contour and its components. However, we face greater uncertainty, with potential downside risks to growth and upside risks to inflation. The probability of inflation being higher than expected seems greater than the probability of growth falling short.

Growth indicators have weakened, and sentiment seems fragile. While these numbers may not be reliable indicators of the future, they suggest a possible deceleration. Despite these concerns, the economy's near-term fundamentals appear strong. Labor market improvements, healthy investment growth, and favorable credit conditions support continued expansion.

On the inflation front, we've seen unexpected core price acceleration and rising inflation expectations. The economy's growth rate, increasing unit labor costs, and reports of pricing power suggest a higher inflation path. Some measures of intermediate-term inflation expectations are above the desired level.

We acknowledge the recent moderation in growth and higher inflation numbers. However, we don't see a need to alter our expectations for the fed funds rate path at this time. The market's increased uncertainty about the forecast reflects a wider range of possible fed funds rate paths. We believe our statement from the last meeting struck the right balance in signaling a positive slope for the funds rate path while acknowledging uncertainty.

In conclusion, we recommend continuing to move the real fed funds rate higher, as we did at our last meeting. We'll maintain our uncertainty about the exact slope and shape of the path, but we don't see a need to introduce greater uncertainty into our statement at this time. Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Kohn.

Speaker: MR. KOHN
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add some thoughts to the table based on recent economic developments. The economic landscape has become more complex since our last meeting. We initially believed we were dealing with an emerging inflation issue, but now we're also confronted with weaker-than-expected economic activity. Both consumption and investment fell short of expectations.

I suspect the primary cause of these surprises is the greater-than-anticipated reaction to previous increases in energy prices. Perhaps we underestimated the impact of these price increases on people's behavior and businesses' decision-making. It seems that people don't change their spending habits until prices have been high for an extended period and they perceive the need to adjust their relative prices and disposable income. Businesses, in turn, may be cutting back on hiring and capital outlays due to their perception that energy prices are taking a larger share of household spending or increasing uncertainty about the future of that spending.

The recent economic weakness in several industrial economies supports this explanation, as does the ease of cost pass-throughs when everyone is facing similar shocks. If energy prices remain stable, growth should pick up, and core inflation should decrease. We're likely looking at a downward adjustment in demand and an upward adjustment in prices, not persistent effects on growth rates.

Despite the uncertainty, the fundamental economic conditions remain favorable. Financial conditions remain accommodative and supportive of growth, and foreign demand should strengthen as the energy price shock is absorbed. Labor and product markets should help keep a lid on price increases, but the recent data have increased the level of uncertainty about the outlook.

It's unclear what else, aside from energy prices, could be unexpectedly dampening demand. Confidence has eroded, which could intensify and prolong energy-related shortfalls. Financial conditions are a bit tighter than they were, which will restrain spending to some extent. The slowdown in investment spending is particularly concerning if it persists, as we were counting on it to sustain solid growth and declining slack over the next few quarters.

The recent data suggest that the economy continues to expand at a moderate pace, but there's a greater possibility that growth will not be sufficient to raise resource utilization as policy accommodation is removed further. On inflation, the decline in both short- and long-run inflation compensation and in financial markets since the last meeting indicates that energy price increases aren

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Gramlich.

Speaker: MR. GRAMLICH
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective on the current monetary situation. The recent oil market demand shock, which is causing inflation and unemployment to worsen simultaneously, presents a challenging dilemma. In the past, we've experienced the reverse situation, but the choices are harder now. Should we focus on inflation or unemployment? The answer depends on which factor is more likely to have a stronger and more pervasive impact in the medium term.

Initially, I leaned towards tightening due to concerns about inflation. However, the worsening data over the past six weeks has shifted my stance. I now believe we should ease our rhetoric moderately.

The spending data has been consistently weak, with GDP and final sales coming in below expectations. Consumption growth is expected to slow due to dropping personal saving rates and potential reversion to higher levels. The housing market and business investment are also showing signs of weakness.

Fiscal policy is neutral, and the recent budget agreement may contain some fiscal tightening, but its implementation is uncertain. Export growth is likely to slow due to trading partner slowdowns, and a sharp dollar decline could further impact exports.

Given these factors, I believe unusual importance attaches to capital investment. Business fixed investment has decelerated, and durable goods orders, housing starts, and the ISM index of factory activity are all down. I'm not yet sure if this is a trend, but we did express concerns about an investment pothole not long ago.

In the short term, I agree with the consensus that we should maintain our current stance. However, in the medium term, I'm becoming increasingly concerned about the weak spending data and its potential impact on inflation. Many of the price increases seem to be one-shot energy shocks that are unlikely to persist. I think it's important to keep an eye on wages, which have remained moderate, and overall price pressures should wane if aggregate demand weakens and output gaps fail to close.

In conclusion, I'd like to suggest that we shift our rhetoric towards the A column in the Bluebook, and I recommend that everyone carefully study the simulation labeled "Sentiment slump with financial spillover" over the next six to eight weeks.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Olson.

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to share some insights I gained from speaking with contacts at two major banks. As we've discussed, bank asset quality improved significantly in 2003 and 2004, with many bankers reporting in their year-end statements that it couldn't get better. However, the initial reports from this quarter suggest that it may have, in fact, continued to improve. Yet, both banks I spoke to expressed concerns about the marginal credits and the possibility of rewrites due to intense pressure on prices, underwriting standards, and liquidity.

Regarding today's agenda, I was struck by the contrast between the Bluebook and Greenbook information. I had planned to delve deeper into this topic, but David and Karen's summaries covered the same ground in just a few sentences. I also noticed a striking lack of variance between the Beige Book reports and the comments of Reserve Bank Presidents. While there may be a lag in the data we gather in Washington, the contemporaneous information from the Presidents seems to provide a more accurate picture. I believe we should pay close attention to this trend. In my three-and-a-half years on this committee, I've never seen the Beige Book information lag so significantly behind the Greenbook data. It's possible we're approaching a pothole or even a false move, like the Chicago Bulls' infamous head fake by Juan Dixon last night.

Speaker: MR. MOSKOW
Statement: That’s unfair. @laugh

Speaker: MR. OLSON
Statement: It has been 17 years, Michael. It has been a long time. I’m inclined to support those of you who have already spoken to the strength of the underlying fundamentals and who believe the output gap is continuing to close slightly. I think we clearly communicated in the last meeting—and the market interpreted it correctly—our heightened concern about the level of inflation. With respect to the likely direction of the underlying economy, I think the information is sufficiently contradictory or unclear that it is difficult to make a positive statement. However, we may choose to do as Susan and Roger and I did in our announcement with respect to the Basel II QIS4 [Quantitative Impact Study] information. Some of you may know that as we were looking at the QIS4 data, which involved the study of the impact of the new Basel II Accord on the banks, we decided that either the information reflected a wider disparity of risk exposure or that there were some additional fundamental inconsistencies in the underlying data that would require us to postpone the release of our notice of proposed rulemaking. So we did that. That we simply postpone, actually, would be my first choice for today. @laugh Since that is not really being an appropriate option for us, I would suggest that we not significantly revise the manner in which we have communicated our views regarding the removal of policy accommodation until we get a clearer sense of what the underlying data are telling us.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Bies.

Speaker: MS. BIES
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the discussion, although I know some of what I'll say may overlap with others. I want to express my concerns regarding inflation, which seems to be a less-discussed topic at the table. While the first-quarter GDP growth of 3.1% was weaker than anticipated, the underlying data reveals some positive signs. Private domestic final sales grew at a high 3% rate, indicating strong consumer and business spending.

However, the weakness in the quarter was primarily due to net exports and government spending. The imports surge and the government spending slowdown are not necessarily cause for alarm, as they may be temporary.

The accommodative monetary policy and robust corporate profits, which are still coming in, further support this optimistic outlook. The market conditions remain favorable for business expansion and consumer borrowing.

Regarding Sarbanes-Oxley compliance, the market has shown remarkable resilience, with investors distinguishing between companies with isolated control weaknesses and those with more pervasive issues.

My primary concern, however, is the inflation trend. Although the lagging indicators show inflation rates in the mid-1% range, the trend in the first quarter is concerning. Core inflation measures have risen significantly, with core CPI up 1.3 percentage points to 3.3%, and core PCE up a full point to 2.6%.

The goods sector has seen the most significant price increases, with goods prices going from declining at a 3% pace to being unchanged. The employment cost index showed a slight slowing, but this was due to the pension funding slowdown. Health care costs remain the top concern for corporate CFOs, which may lead to subdued wage increases and potential impacts on hiring and consumption.

Given these developments, I believe the risks of inflation are now clearly on the upside.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Governor Ferguson.

Speaker: MR. FERGUSON
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to add my perspective to the ongoing discussion. I share the concerns expressed about the recent data showing signs of a soft patch in growth and a rough patch in inflation. However, I trust that the Greenbook team has taken these developments into account, as they have a proven track record of handling such situations with wisdom and expertise.

Given the uncertainties we face, I believe the baseline remains a reasonable starting point for policymaking today. However, I must admit that I anticipate the baseline will ultimately prove to be incorrect, though I cannot yet predict in which direction. The range of risks is indeed quite large, as many have pointed out.

Regarding the language we use today, I suggest maintaining the phrase "measured pace" in our description. Given the current uncertainties, I believe this approach will create less uncertainty and provide a clearer path forward for markets.

Looking beyond today's meeting, it's worth noting that market expectations for the fed funds rate have shifted, with many anticipating a rate closer to 3.75% by the end of this year and next. This leads me to question whether the equilibrium real interest rate, or neutral rate, may have shifted as well.

The Greenbook suggests two reasons for the softness in recent data: higher energy prices and the impact of monetary policy tightening. While many have already discussed the implications of energy prices for inflation, I'd like to focus on the second point. If the equilibrium real rate has indeed come down, it could be due to several factors, including the drag on domestic demand from high energy prices, lingering hesitancy among businesses to invest, and the trade deficit.

The staff's simulation suggests that the run-up in oil prices since late 2003 may have taken a half percentage point off growth in 2004 and three-quarters of a percentage point off growth this year. This indicates a dampening effect on domestic demand due to high and consistently high energy prices.

Equipment and software spending has been growing relatively strongly, but both nominal high-tech and non-high-tech expenditures as a share of GDP are lower than historical averages. This suggests a lingering drag on investment following the 1990s.

The trade deficit is another factor that could be pulling the equilibrium rate down. The European Central

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Mr. Reinhart.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement:
Mr. Chairman, I'd like to discuss the materials we received during the coffee break. The market sees today's decision as another step in your process of reducing policy accommodation. According to the data in the first panel, futures prices and survey responses indicate a ¼ point increase is a certainty, and the statement will retain the assessment that the risks to both objectives are balanced. However, there's uncertainty about whether the "measured pace" language will survive the day. Some surveys suggest that one in four participants believe it won't.

The market's expectations were largely set by your March statement, as shown in the second panel. The May federal funds futures contract has remained around 3% throughout the intermeeting period. However, investors understood that your actions in the coming quarters depend on economic conditions. They revised down their year-end rate expectations by ¼ point, which is roughly in line with the staff's baseline.

Your March statement was viewed as hawkish on inflation, causing a significant swing in the two-year Treasury yield, as shown in the third panel. Some of this was later revised in the minutes released three weeks later. The two-year yield rose around major economic data releases, particularly those related to consumer price inflation. However, it was also influenced by negative sentiment due to problems at prominent corporations and declines in equity prices.

Equity prices fell 2½% net during the intermeeting period, and risk spreads on debt instruments widened. Triple-B yields edged lower, but speculative-grade yields actually rose. The value of the dollar appreciated slightly, leading to tighter financial conditions.

If you believe that these financial conditions, which are based on market expectations of policy action today and in the future, will preserve satisfactory economic expansion while containing inflation, you may be comfortable with raising the federal funds rate to 3%. However, the economic outlook, as summarized in exhibit 2, has worsened, with the output gap narrowing more gradually and core PCE inflation running along a slightly higher path. This fits the description of an adverse supply shock, likely due to sustained higher oil prices.

In this new economic environment, a ¼ point increase in the nominal funds rate would help prevent an erosion in the real funds rate due to inflation and might even close some of the gap between the actual real rate and estimates of its equilibrium.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Questions for Mr. Reinhart?

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: I have just one question on the inflation swap quotes. You talked a little bit about them when you first introduced them in February. How good are those data? Is that market deep? Are the quotes indicative? Are they real transaction quotes? I’m just curious; it’s intriguing stuff.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: It’s material that was in the last Bluebook, and it is repeated again in this one, where we talk about the changes in forward-looking inflation compensation. In fact, the inflation swap market developed mostly in Europe. That’s where the market is deeper, in part because in Europe apparently there are participants on both sides of that trade—mainly utilities that have legislated rates and, therefore, are willing to pay for getting that protection. In the United States, the volume of trading has increased over the last year or so, and we do think that it is a useful market. One mystery in that market, which still prevails, is that at least at the front part of the TIPS yield curve and the inflation swap yield curve, there’s a bit of an arbitrage opportunity. Dealers we talk to say that, yes, they know it exists, but to exploit it would involve basically exploiting their customers, and they are reluctant to do that while the market is still limited; it hasn’t deepened enough.

Speaker: MR. SANTOMERO
Statement: Thank you.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Further questions?

Speaker: MR. LACKER
Statement: I have a comment on process. I appreciate the earlier distribution of Table 1, which is a procedure we decided on at the last meeting. On reflection, I’d like to register some reservations about it. It has the effect of shifting substantive deliberations out of the Committee, and it prevents us as Committee members from benefiting from the reasoning and thoughts of our colleagues on the FOMC. We don’t know what other members are communicating during the period leading up to the meeting. I’m not sure exactly the right way to get around this, and I hope we can revisit this topic sometime soon. But one possibility would be to have members submit their suggested changes to Table 1 in writing. Then, after the close of some suitable comment period, the staff could distribute those suggestions so that we could glean what people are thinking when they suggest changes to the language rather than having to reverse engineer via exegesis.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: Certainly some progress can be made in that direction, but let’s be careful not to bring it to the table at this point in the meeting, because the length of the discussion is indeterminate, and we still have our 2:15 deadline. Therefore, I think it’s critical that whatever we do with respect to enhancing the interaction on the wording of the statement be done before the meeting. And the Internet, especially e-mail appropriately encrypted, is a rather significant vehicle that can readily be employed if the members perceive that there is inadequate interaction on the statement itself. In my view, that can fairly easily be improved upon, if necessary, and I think Vincent is the central point here. So I would suggest that those of you who agree with President Lacker communicate that, or indicate to Vincent that you think the current process is perfectly adequate, which I always find a difficult position to support.

Speaker: MR. REINHART
Statement: I would note with some irony, Mr. Chairman, that in the December 1999 memo that introduced the balance-of-risk language, it was taken for granted—I think this was on the first page—that draft language for the statement would not be circulated before the meeting because it had the potential to stifle the discussion at the meeting. So, the views of the Committee have changed in this regard. I would say it’s an open issue of where the pendulum is now relative to perpendicular. I welcome everybody’s comments. I look forward to those e-mails. And one possibility, if there’s enough interest, is that we can figure out when to have a Committee discussion.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: You know, there’s a difference from five years ago. The difference basically is that the general notion that we can all come here, sit down and have a discussion in a limited number of hours, and in that period come up with a consensus on the best wording for our statement, has proven false. It’s a much more detailed deliberation; there’s far more involved than merely a statement of where the funds rate is going to be. What we are learning is that the process requires a great deal of deliberation prior to the meeting. So, even though we officially meet only on fixed days, the amount of interaction in the intermeeting period is increasing. It’s largely, of course, as a consequence of a statement that is ever more elaborate. I think we’re finding that the old notion about not discussing in advance what our views are—that we should just come to the table and work them out—would be fine if we had an open-ended time frame, which we don’t. We issue a statement at 2:15 p.m. on the day of our meeting. So, unless we want to change that, which has a lot of consequences, we have to recognize that a goodly part of what we discuss and conclude has to result from our pre-meeting discussions. Now, to the extent that you wish to enhance the amount of interaction among members, there clearly are vehicles to do that which we do not at this stage employ. There has to be an ultimate arbiter, and it still has to be Vincent in one way or another, because it is conceivable, if we are all interacting and not taking votes, that we could have a stalemate. And a stalemate is not permissible in this Committee, because we have to come to conclusions. So, we can move to a greater degree of interaction, if you choose. And I think that can be done very readily, especially with the marvelous advances in technology that have emerged in the last five years.

Speaker: MR. GUYNN
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I was going to raise this point later, or maybe at another meeting, but I think it’s consistent with the discussion we’re having. I would hope that we don’t foreclose the possibility, long term, of rethinking this entire process. It seems backwards to me to be trying to work on the statement before we have the benefit of a Committee discussion in which we hear each other’s views. I don’t think we can replicate that in one-on-one discussions. Don, you know far better than I do the process at the Bank of England, but Bob Eisenbeis and I had occasion to visit there, and I believe their process still involves a meeting that encompasses two days. They meet one day to talk about their views on the economic outlook and on policy. They spend the next day talking about what to do and how to describe it. It seems to me that this work is important enough that at some point—again, not today—we might want to reconsider the whole process. If we to want to craft these kinds of statements, they may be better done by the Committee after we’ve had our discussion about the economy and policy. I just hope we wouldn’t rule that out as a possibility somewhere down the road.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement:
During our meeting today, I'd like to bring up a point that President Lacker raised earlier, which I believe is worth repeating. Despite the current uncertainty, I think we've done a good job of communicating our priorities to the marketplace. The market now anticipates our responses to events and developments, and they've been relatively accurate. While there are challenges, such as the eight-week gap between meetings, I'm not overly concerned. I suggest we continue to convey our priorities to the marketplace and let them help us until we meet again.

Now, I understand that the markets are indicating that we may pause in some of our upcoming meetings. While it's one thing to forecast a pause, it's another to actually implement it. We could either stipulate that we won't act in the current meeting but will at the next one, or we could continue increasing rates until we reach what we perceive to be the end. I find it challenging to craft a statement that effectively communicates the latter option with credibility.

Moreover, history shows that the last move in a process of tightening or easing is often a mistake. Our goal should be to minimize the disruption when we make that mistake. We're constantly faced with the question of whether taking a particular action and having it prove to be a mistake is more costly than taking the alternative action and having that turn out to be a mistake.

Currently, we're deciding between pausing or making a significant change in our statement. If we choose the former and it's wrong, it could create a more significant problem for us than if we assume inflation is the more critical ongoing problem and continue with a 25 basis point increase.

Additionally, recent developments in the oil market may be contributing to the pause. The Saudis increased production, creating a temporary glut of lower-gravity, more sulfurous crude oils. This has led to a significant drop in spot prices relative to forward prices, making it profitable to accumulate oil inventories. As inventories rise, the spot price falls further, creating a self-reinforcing cycle.

Gasoline prices are also likely to decline as they're largely a function of the weighted average crude price. If this pattern continues, we'll learn soon enough whether or not declining gasoline prices are undercutting grave concerns about the outlook.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Mr. Chairman, I have an alternative D, which may not be appropriate for today, but it might be helpful in some future meeting, so let me pass this language around. While that’s going around, I’d like to say that I agree with Jack Guynn that we have a real problem in terms of the coordination of the statement and the minutes. When the minutes come out in three weeks, they’re going to have to reflect the fact that there is widespread uncertainty around the table. And somehow I think that ought to be in the statement, because that is really part of the outcome of this meeting.

Speaker: MR. GREENSPAN
Statement: That will appear in the minutes.

Speaker: MR. POOLE
Statement: Oh, it will appear in the minutes. But I’m saying that I think it ought to appear in the statement, because, otherwise, the statement would not truly reflect what happened at the meeting. Anyway, I wanted to make that point—that the minutes coming out in three weeks could give a different impression from the statement. In my view, coordinating the two is very important. And I think that’s the point Jack Guynn was raising a few minutes ago. Some elements of my alternative D are in the alternative C language in the staff handout. As you know from the memo I distributed, I think the “measured pace” language is becoming less and less useful. And I believe that accurate communication is both terribly important and terribly difficult. I would like to see us come out—if not today, then at some point—with language making it very clear that, depending on the incoming information, we would be open to either a 50 basis point increase, if the inflation environment seems compelling, or a pause. I’m concerned that the market expectation we are creating of a move of 25 basis points at our next meeting is going to get in the way of either of those outcomes.
